syllabus.title: Functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Represent, interpret, and analyse functions, including domain, range, transformations, intersections, and graphical features.

syllabus.title: Graphing techniques
syllabus.content: Use transformations and graphing techniques to analyse, sketch, and interpret functions.

syllabus.title: Logarithms and exponentials
syllabus.content: Use logarithmic and exponential functions, equations, and transformations in algebraic and applied contexts.

syllabus.title: Differential calculus
syllabus.content: Use derivatives to determine rates of change, gradients, stationary points, tangents, normals, and optimisation results.

syllabus.title: Applications of differentiation
syllabus.content: Apply differentiation to rates of change, curve features, optimisation, and contextual modelling problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use definite and indefinite integrals, areas under curves, and accumulation ideas to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use integration methods, definite integrals, areas, and accumulation to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Modelling financial situations
syllabus.content: Use sequences, series, interest factors, annuities, loans, and recurrence relationships to model financial situations.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions
syllabus.content: Work with trigonometric functions, identities, exact values, equations, graphs, and practical angle relationships.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and identities
syllabus.content: Use trigonometric identities and algebraic manipulation to prove identities and solve trigonometric problems.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Analyse trigonometric functions, graphs, equations, periodic behaviour, and transformations.

syllabus.title: Probability and discrete probability distributions
syllabus.content: Use probability rules, conditional probability, discrete distributions, and contextual probability models.

syllabus.title: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
syllabus.content: Use scatterplots, association, least-squares regression lines, interpolation, extrapolation, and contextual interpretation to analyse bivariate datasets.

syllabus.title: Random variables
syllabus.content: Use discrete and continuous random variables, probability density functions, normal models, percentiles, and expected-value reasoning.

questions.title: Probability distribution of a discrete random variable
questions.promptLatex:
The probability distribution table for a discrete random variable \(X\) is shown.

| \(x\) | \(P(X = x)\) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0.4 |
| 2 | 0.2 |
| 3 | |

What is the value of \(P(X = 3)\)?

A. 0.2
B. 0.4
C. 1.2
D. 2.0

questions.title: Estimating probability of a biased die
questions.promptLatex:
A ten-sided die has faces numbered 1 to 10.

The die is constructed so that the probability of obtaining the number 1 is greater than the probability of obtaining any of the other numbers. The numbers 2 to 10 are equally likely to occur.

When the die is rolled 153 times, a 1 is obtained 72 times.

By using the relative frequency of rolling a 1, which of the following is the best estimate for the probability of rolling a 10?

A. \(\frac{1}{17}\)

B. \(\frac{1}{11}\)

C. \(\frac{1}{10}\)

D. \(\frac{1}{9}\)

questions.title: Normal distribution temperature probability
questions.promptLatex:
The minimum daily temperature, in degrees, of a town each year follows a normal distribution with its mean equal to its standard deviation. The minimum daily temperature was recorded over one year.

What percentage of the recorded minimum daily temperatures was above zero degrees?

A. 16%
B. 50%
C. 68%
D. 84%

questions.title: Turning point and intercepts of a quadratic
questions.promptLatex:
The graph of a quadratic function represented by the equation \(h = t^2 - 8t + 12\) is shown.

(a) Find the values of \(t\) and \(h\) at the turning point of the graph. (2 marks)

(b) The graph shows \(h = 12\) when \(t = 0\). What is the other value of \(t\) for which \(h = 12\)? (1 mark)

betterResponses: (a) find \(t\) using the axis of symmetry
(a) substitute the value of \(t\) into the equation of the quadratic to find \(h\)
(b) use symmetry to find the value of \(t\)
(b) substitute \(h= 12\) and correctly solve for \(t\)
improvementAreas: (a) recognising the difference between intercepts and the vertex
(b) recognising the symmetrical shape of a quadratic
(b) solving a quadratic by factorising

criteria.criterion: Finds correct value of \( t \), or equivalent merit

assets.label: Graph of h = t^2 - 8t + 12
assets.alt: A parabola opening upwards with a y-intercept at 12 and x-intercepts at 2 and 6.

questions.title: Equation of the tangent
questions.promptLatex: Find the equation of the tangent to \( y = 5x^3 - \frac{2}{x^2} - 9 \) at the point \((1, -6)\). (3 marks)

betterResponses: differentiate correctly, particularly the term with the negative index
find the equation of the tangent by collecting the like terms of the equation correctly
substitute the point and gradient into the equation of a straight line to find \(y= 19x-25\) using either form of the equation of a line
improvementAreas: identifying the most appropriate method of differentiation – working with negative indices
recognising the difference between finding the gradient of a tangent and locating a maximum or minimum point
knowing the difference between finding the derivative at a point (\(f’(a) = m\)) and solving to find value of \(x\) that produces a particular derivative, \(f′(x) = a\)
correctly substituting values into \(y-y_1 = m(x-x_1)\)

criteria.criterion: Finds the correct gradient of the tangent, or equivalent merit
Attempts to find the gradient of the tangent, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Geometric sequence terms
questions.promptLatex:
The numbers, \(75, p, q, 2025\), form a geometric sequence.

Find the values of \(p\) and \(q\).

betterResponses: form three equations connecting the terms of the geometric sequence, or form two equations connecting the terms \(p\) and \(q\) to solve for the required pronumerals
use the formula for the \(n^{th}\) term of a geometric sequence to establish the common ratio \(r\) and the values of \(p\) and \(q\)
use the formula for geometric sequence
improvementAreas: recognising a sequence as an arithmetic or geometric sequence
writing a pattern with the information given to try to establish a common ratio
using the Reference Sheet to determine the \(n^{th}\) term of a geometric sequence

criteria.criterion: Shows understanding that the sequence is geometric, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Bivariate data and regression analysis
questions.promptLatex:
In a research study, participants were asked to record the number of minutes they spent watching television and the number of minutes they spent exercising each day over a period of 3 months. The averages for each participant were recorded and graphed.

(a) Describe the bivariate dataset in terms of its form and direction. (2 marks)
Form: ...................................................................................................................................
Direction: ...........................................................................................................................

The equation of the least-squares regression line for this dataset is
\[ y = 64.3 - 0.7x. \]

(b) Interpret the values of the slope and \(y\)-intercept of the regression line in the context of this dataset. (2 marks)

(c) Jo spends an average of 42 minutes per day watching television.
Use the equation of the regression line to determine how many minutes on average Jo is expected to exercise each day. (1 mark)

(d) Explain why it is NOT appropriate to extrapolate the regression line to predict the average number of minutes of exercise per day for someone who watches an average of 2 hours of television per day. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Form: Linear, Direction: Negative (b) Slope: For every 1 minute increase in time spent watching television per day the time spent exercising decreases by 0.7 minutes per day. y-intercept: If someone doesn't watch television they are expected to exercise for 64.3 minutes per day. (c) 34.9 minutes (d) 2 hours or 120 minutes of television suggests -19.7 minutes of exercise and can't do negative minutes of exercise. (Alternative: 2 hours or 120 minutes is outside the dataset so extrapolating is not reliable.)
workingLatex: (b) Slope: For every 1 minute increase in time spent watching television per day the time spent exercising decreases by 0.7 minutes per day. y-intercept: If someone doesn't watch television they are expected to exercise for 64.3 minutes per day.

(d) 2 hours or 120 minutes of television suggests -19.7 minutes of exercise and can't do negative minutes of exercise. (Alternative: 2 hours or 120 minutes is outside the dataset so extrapolating is not reliable.)

betterResponses: (a) demonstrate a strong understanding through accurately selecting the correct terminology to describe both form and direction
(a) identify that the form is linear and the distribution has a negative direction
(b) identify the slope and \(y\)-intercept for the equation of the least-squares regression line
(b) clearly interpret the meaning of the slope and \(y\)-intercept within the context of the scenario, rather than simply explaining what slope and \(y\)-intercepts represent in general terms
(c) correctly substitute \(x= 42\) into the equation to calculate the answer
(d) convert 2 hours to minutes and substitute \(x= 120\) minutes into the formula
(d) identify that the graph goes below zero and cannot have a negative number of minutes of exercise
(d) recognise 2 hours is outside the dataset
(d) understand the meaning of the key word ‘extrapolate’
improvementAreas: (a) using the correct language for form and/or direction
(a) refining their knowledge of statistical terms and key words used in the syllabus for bivariate data analysis
(b) interpreting the slope and \(y\)-intercept in context of the question with values shown
(b) interpreting the slope and \(y\)-intercept correctly, not just identifying the values
(b) understanding that the slope is not the same as the correlation coefficient
(c) showing correct substitution into the formula
(c) understanding what each variable in an equation represents
(d) understanding that the values outside the dataset are not reliable
(d) knowing extending the line of regression gives the negative answer for time
(d) knowing the meaning of the key word extrapolate

criteria.criterion: Provides correct direction, or equivalent merit
Provides correct interpretation of both slope and y-intercept
Provides correct interpretation of y-intercept, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Modelling sound waves with trigonometric functions
questions.promptLatex:
A sound wave can be modelled using a function \(P(t) = k \sin at\), where \(P\) is air pressure in Pascals, \(t\) is time in milliseconds (ms) and \(k\) and \(a\) are constants.

(a) Write the equation for a sound wave \(P_1(t)\) that has an amplitude of 2 Pascals and a period of 5 ms. (2 marks)

(b) The graph of \(P_1(t)\) from part (a) is shown.
On the diagram, sketch the graph of \(P_2(t) = 4 \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{10} t \right)\) for \(0 \le t \le 10\). (2 marks)

(c) Hence, find the values of \(t\), where \(0 < t < 10\), for which functions \(P_1(t)\) and \(P_2(t)\) are BOTH decreasing. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(P_1(t) = 2 \sin \frac{2\pi}{5}t\) (b) [Graph showing a sine wave with amplitude \(2\) and period \(5\)] (c) \(6.25 < t < 8.75\)

questions.title: Normal distribution of sheep weights
questions.promptLatex:
(a) In a flock of 12 600 sheep, the ratio of males to females is 1 : 20.

The weights of the male sheep are normally distributed with a mean of 76.2 kg and a standard deviation of 6.8 kg.

In the flock, 15 of the male sheep each weigh more than \(x\) kg.

Find the value of \(x\). (4 marks)

(b) The weights of the female sheep are also normally distributed but have a smaller mean and smaller standard deviation than the weights of male sheep.

Explain whether it could be expected that 300 of the females from the flock each weigh more than \(x\) kg, where \(x\) is the value found in part (a). (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(x = 89.8 \text{ kg}\) (b) The percentage of female sheep with weight greater than \(x \text{ kg}\) will be smaller than \(2.5\%\), so less than \(300\) females will weigh more than \(x \text{ kg}\).
workingLatex: (a) \(\text{Percentage of males weighing more than } x \text{ kg} = \frac{15}{600} \times 100 = 2.5\%\)
(a) \(\text{Since } 2.5\% \text{ corresponds to a } z\text{-score of } 2, \text{ we have } x = 76.2 + 2 \times 6.8 = 89.8 \text{ kg}\)
(b) \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \text{ will be larger than } 2 \text{ as the mean and standard deviation for females is smaller.}\)
(b) \(\text{The percentage of female sheep with weight greater than } x \text{ kg will be smaller than } 2.5\%.\)
(b) \(\text{So less than } 300 \text{ females will weigh more than } x \text{ kg.}\)

betterResponses: (a) find the number of male sheep correctly using the given information: \(\frac{12600}{21} = 600\)
(a) express 15 male sheep as a percentage of 600 male sheep: \(\frac{15}{600} = 0.025 = 2.5\%\)
(a) use the percentage to equate to a \(z\)-score of 2
(a) calculate \(x\) kg using the normal distribution curve or the \(z\)-score formula
(b) express 300 female sheep as a percentage of the 12 000 female sheep correctly
(b) recognise the female percentage (or \(z\)-score) is the same as the male percentage from part (a) of the question

(b) recognise and state that with a smaller mean and standard deviation, the percentage of female sheep greater than \(x\) would be less than 2.5%, or that the \(z\)-score of \(x\) would be greater than 2
improvementAreas: (a) calculating the number of male sheep correctly according to the given ratio
(a) identifying the difference between a \(z\)-score and a percentage
(a) using and labelling a diagram to help to understand where the values are placed within the normal distribution
(a) using the Reference Sheet to match the percentage break-up to a \(z\)-score
(b) calculating the correct number and percentage of female sheep
(b) understanding the concepts, \(z\)-score, mean, standard deviation in more detail, and how these concepts affect each other

questions.title: Tangent to logarithmic and quadratic functions

workingLatex: The gradients of \(y = e \ln x\) and \(y = ax^2 + c\) at point of intersection are both equal to \(1\).
\(y = ax^2 + c has gradient of 1 at point (e, e).\)
\(y = ax^2 + c = e at x = e \implies e = \frac{1}{2e} \cdot e^2 + c \implies e = \frac{e}{2} + c \implies c = \frac{e}{2}\)

questions.title: Integration of x sin x
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Show that
\[ \frac{d}{dx}(\sin x - x \cos x) = x \sin x. \]
(2 marks)

(b) Hence, find the value of
\[ \int_{0}^{2025\pi} x \sin x \, dx. \]
(2 marks)

(c) The regions bounded by the x-axis and the graph of \( y = x \sin x \) for \( x \ge 0 \) are shown.

Let \( A_n = \int_{(n-1)\pi}^{n\pi} x \sin x \, dx \), where \( n \) is a positive integer.

It can be shown that \( |A_n| = (2n-1)\pi \). (Do NOT prove this.)

Find the exact total area of the regions bounded by the curve \( y = x \sin x \), and the x-axis between \( x = 0 \) and \( x = 2025\pi \).
(2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x - x \cos x) = x \sin x \text{ (b) } 2025\pi \text{ (c) } (2025)^2 \pi \text{ units}^2\)
workingLatex: (a) \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x - x \cos x) = \cos x - [x \times (-\sin x) + \cos x] = \cos x + x \sin x - \cos x = x \sin x \text{ as required}\)

(c) \(\text{Exact area of regions} = \pi + 3\pi + 5\pi + \dots + (2 \times 2025 - 1)\pi = \pi + 3\pi + 5\pi + \dots + 4049\pi. \text{ This is an arithmetic series with } a = \pi, d = 2\pi, n = 2025 \text{ and } l = 4049\pi. S_n = \frac{n}{2}(a + l) = \frac{2025}{2}(\pi + 4049\pi) = 2025 \times 2025 \times \pi = (2025)^2 \pi \text{ units}^2\)

betterResponses: (a) apply correctly the product rule \(\frac{d}{dx}(x \cos x) = \cos x - x \sin x\) or \(\frac{d}{dx}(-x \cos x) = \sin x - \cos x\)
(a) simplify expressions involving positive and negative terms correctly
(b) recognise and use the result from part (a) of the question \(\int_0^{2025\pi} \sin x dx = [\sin x - x \cos x]_0^{2025\pi}\)
(b) substitute correctly into the given function \(x = 2025\pi\) and \(x = 0\) into \([\sin x - x \cos x]_0^{2025\pi}\)
(c) use \(A_n = (2n-1)\pi\) to determine the terms \(\pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \dots, (2 \times 2025 - 1)\pi\)
(c) recognise that the total area is given by the sum of an arithmetic sequence
(c) correctly apply the sum of an arithmetic sequence formula \(S_n = \frac{2025}{2}(\pi + 4049\pi)\) or \(S_n = \frac{2025}{2}[2\pi + (2025 - 1)2\pi]\)
improvementAreas: (a) differentiating trigonometric functions correctly
(b) recognising that ‘hence’ implies the use of a previous result
(b) recording of the function \([\sin x - x \cos x]_0^{2025\pi}\) and appropriate substitution accurately
(b) checking calculator mode (radians) when performing calculus operations
(c) engaging with the formula provided as a guide in the question \(A_n = (2n-1)\pi\)
(c) considering absolute value notation negates concerns about areas below the \(x\)-axis \(A_n = (2n-1)\pi\)
(c) identifying the values of \(a, d, n\) and/or \(l\) clearly before substituting into the arithmetic sequence formula

questions.title: Solutions to a trigonometric equation
questions.promptLatex:
The equation \(\cos px = \frac{1}{2}\) has 2 solutions where \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\) and \(p > 0\).

Find all possible values of \(p\).
workingLatex: For \(y = \cos px\), the graph is dilated horizontally by a factor of \(\frac{1}{p}\).
For the lowest value of \(p\) to get 2 solutions, the second solution occurs at \(x = 2\pi\): \(\frac{5\pi}{3} \times \frac{1}{p} = 2\pi \implies p = \frac{5\pi}{3 \times 2\pi} = \frac{5}{6}\).
For the highest value of \(p\) to get 2 solutions, the third solution occurs at \(x = 2\pi\): \(\frac{7\pi}{3} \times \frac{1}{p} = 2\pi \implies p = \frac{7\pi}{3 \times 2\pi} = \frac{7}{6}\).

questions.title: Calculus, Trigonometry, and Algebra Fundamentals
questions.subtopic: Inverse functions, trigonometric equations, and integration
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find the inverse function, \(f^{-1}(x)\), of the function \(f(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{x - 2}\). (2 marks)

(b) Solve \(\sin 2\theta - \sin \theta = 0\) for \(0 \le \theta \le \pi\). (3 marks)

(c) Find \(\int \sin 3x \cos x \, dx\). (2 marks)

(d) Sketch the graph of \(y = \frac{1}{3} \cos^{-1}(2x)\). (2 marks)

(e) For what value of \(m\) is the vector \(\binom{1}{m}\) parallel to the vector \(\binom{2}{6}\)? (1 mark)

(f) The roots of \(2x^3 + 6x^2 + x - 1 = 0\) are \(\alpha, \beta\) and \(\gamma\).

What is the value of \(\frac{1}{\alpha\beta} + \frac{1}{\alpha\gamma} + \frac{1}{\beta\gamma}\)? (2 marks)

(g) Evaluate \(\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \cos^2(3x) \, dx\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) Let \(y = f(x)\). Then \(y = 1 - \frac{1}{x-2} \implies y - 1 = -\frac{1}{x-2} \implies x - 2 = -\frac{1}{y-1} \implies x = 2 - \frac{1}{y-1}\). Swap \(y\) and \(x\) for the inverse function, \(y = 2 - \frac{1}{x-1}\). \(\therefore f^{-1}(x) = 2 - \frac{1}{x-1}\).
(d) [Graph shows a curve passing through \((0, \frac{\pi}{6})\) with endpoints at \((-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\pi}{3})\) and \((\frac{1}{2}, 0)\)]

questions.title: Calculus, Induction, and Trigonometric Equations
questions.subtopic: Related rates, volumes, induction, and trig equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The radius, \(r\) cm, and angle, \(\theta\) radians, of a sector vary in such a way that its area remains a constant \(10 \text{ cm}^2\).

[Diagram of a sector with radius \(r\) and angle \(\theta\), labelled with Area = \(10 \text{ cm}^2\)]

The angle \(\theta\) is increasing at a constant rate of \(2\) radians per second.

Find the rate at which the radius is changing when the radius is \(4\) cm. (3 marks)

(b) Consider the region bounded by the hyperbola \(y = \frac{1}{x}\), the \(y\)-axis and the lines \(y = 1\) and \(y = a\) for \(a > 1\).

Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed when the region is rotated about the \(y\)-axis. (2 marks)

(c) Prove by mathematical induction that

\[ 1 \times (1!) + 2 \times (2!) + \dots + n \times (n!) = (n + 1)! - 1 \]

for integers \(n \ge 1\). (3 marks)

(d) Find the solution of \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{(2 - y)(2 + y)}\), given that \(y = 1\) when \(x = 0\). (3 marks)

(e) (i) Express \(\sqrt{3} \sin x - \cos x\) in the form \(2 \sin(x - \alpha)\), where \(0 < \alpha < \frac{\pi}{2}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, solve \(\sqrt{3} \sin x = \cos x + 1\), where \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(-3.2 \text{ cm s}^{-1} (b) \pi - \frac{\pi}{a} (c) \text{Proof} (d) x = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) - \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ or } y = 2\sin\left(x + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) (e)(i) 2\sin\left(x - \frac{\pi}{6}\right) (e)(ii) x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \pi\)
workingLatex: (d) \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{4-y^2} \implies \int \frac{dy}{\sqrt{4-y^2}} = \int dx \implies x = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) + C. \text{ At } (0,1), 0 = \sin^{-1}(0.5) + C \implies C = -\frac{\pi}{6}. \text{ Thus } x = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right) - \frac{\pi}{6}.\)

(e)\((ii) \sqrt{3}\sin x - \cos x = 1 \implies 2\sin\left(x - \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = 1 \implies \sin\left(x - \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}. x - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ or } \frac{5\pi}{6} \implies x = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ or } \pi.\)

questions.title: Calculus, Combinatorics, Vectors, and Statistics
questions.subtopic: Differential equations, permutations, and binomial distribution
questions.promptLatex:
(a) It is given that \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{5}{y}\) and \(y = -4\) when \(x = 0\).

Find \(y\) as a function of \(x\). (3 marks)

(b) Eight guests are to be seated at a round table. If two of these guests refuse to sit next to each other, how many seating arrangements are possible? (2 marks)

(c) At time \(t\), a particle has position vector \(\underset{\sim}{r}(t) = t\underset{\sim}{i} + \frac{t^2}{9}\underset{\sim}{j}\), velocity vector \(\underset{\sim}{v}(t)\) and acceleration vector \(\underset{\sim}{a}(t)\).

Find the time when the angle between \(\underset{\sim}{v}(t)\) and \(\underset{\sim}{a}(t)\) is \(\frac{\pi}{4}\). (4 marks)

(d) A bag contains counters, some of which are green.

One hundred trials of an experiment are run. In each trial, one counter is selected from the bag at random and its colour noted. The counter is returned to the bag after each trial.

Let \(X\) be the random variable representing the number of times that a green counter is selected.

Given that \(E(X) = 20\) and \(P(X \ge k) = 0.0668\), find the value of \(k\). You may use the standard normal approximation and the information on page 13. (4 marks)

(e) (i) The Pascal's triangle relation can be expressed as

\[ \binom{n}{r} = \binom{n-1}{r-1} + \binom{n-1}{r}  (\text{Do NOT prove this.}) \]

Show that \(\binom{m}{R} = \binom{m+1}{R+1} - \binom{m}{R+1}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, prove that

\[ \binom{2000}{2000} + \binom{2001}{2000} + \binom{2002}{2000} + \dots + \binom{2050}{2000} = \binom{2051}{2001} \]. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(\int y \, dy = \int 5 \, dx \implies \frac{y^2}{2} = 5x + C. Using x = 0, y = -4, \frac{16}{2} = 0 + C \implies C = 8. \frac{y^2}{2} = 5x + 8 \implies y^2 = 10x + 16. Since y = -4 at x = 0, y = -\sqrt{10x + 16}.\)
(b) Total arrangements = \(7!\). Arrangements with two people together = \(6! \times 2\). Arrangements not together = \(7! - 6! \times 2 = 3600\).

(a) use the initial conditions to calculate the constant of integration
(a) investigate and use the given point to obtain the correct expression for \(y\).
(b) correctly identify \((n-1)!\) seating arrangements when sitting in a circle
(b) recognise the number of arrangements when grouped together
(b) consider seating arrangements by allocating and considering options.
(c) derive the velocity and acceleration functions
(c) correctly apply the scalar (dot) product using vector components

(d) calculate the \(z\)-score from the given data

(e) (i) apply the correct substitutions \(r= R+ 1\) and \(n= m+ 1\) into the given Pascal’s triangle relation
(e) (i) use the expansion of \(\binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!r!}\) to prove LHS = RHS.
(e) (ii) apply Pascal’s triangle relation to the given expression
(e) (ii) simplify a series

questions.title: Mixed Topics in Mathematics Extension 1
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove that the product of any seven distinct factors of 60 must be a multiple of 60. (2 marks)

(b) Points \(A\) and \(B\) lie vertically above the origin. Point \(A\) is higher than point \(B\) such that \(\frac{OA}{OB} = k\), where \(k > 1\).

A particle is projected horizontally from point \(A\) with velocity \(U \text{ m s}^{-1}\). After \(T\) seconds, another particle is projected horizontally from point \(B\) with velocity \(V \text{ m s}^{-1}\). The two particles land on the ground in the same place.

Show that the ratio \(\frac{V}{U}\) depends only on \(k\). (4 marks)

(c) The hands of an analogue clock are \(OA\) and \(OB\), where \(A\) is \(\left(\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{360}\right), \cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{360}\right)\right)\), \(B\) is \(\left(2\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{30}\right), 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{30}\right)\right)\), \(O\) is the origin, and \(t \ge 0\) is the number of minutes past midnight.

Find the values of \(t\) when the hands are perpendicular for the first and second time after midnight. Give your answers to 3 decimal places. (3 marks)

(d) The function \(f(x)\) is defined by \(f(x) = \cos^{-1}(\sin x)\) in the domain \((0, \pi)\).

Find \(f'(x)\) for those values of \(x\) where it is defined. (3 marks)

(e) It is given that \(\tan \alpha\), \(\tan \beta\) and \(\tan \gamma\) are the three real roots of the polynomial equation \(x^3 + bx^2 + cx - 1 + b + c = 0\), where \(b\) and \(c\) are real numbers and \(c \ne 1\).

Find the smallest positive value of \(\alpha + \beta + \gamma\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Create 6 buckets \([1, 60], [2, 30], [3, 20], [4, 15], [5, 12], [6, 10]\). If we choose 7 numbers, then by the pigeonhole principle two numbers must be chosen from the same bucket. These two numbers multiply to 60. Hence, the product of all 7 numbers is a multiple of 60. (b) \(\frac{V}{U} = \sqrt{k}\) (c) \(t \approx 16.364 \text{ minutes and } t \approx 49.091 \text{ minutes}\) (d) \(f'(x) = -1 \text{ for } x \in (0, \frac{\pi}{2}) \text{ and } f'(x) = 1 \text{ for } x \in (\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi)\) (e) \(\frac{3\pi}{4}\)
workingLatex: (a) Create 6 buckets [1, 60], [2, 30], [3, 20], [4, 15], [5, 12], [6, 10]. If we choose 7 numbers, then by the pigeonhole principle two numbers must be chosen from the same bucket. These two numbers multiply to 60. Hence, the product of all 7 numbers is a multiple of 60.

(b) Let \(t\) be the time after the first particle is projected, let the time difference be \(T\) seconds and let \(OB = h\) metres so \(OA = kh\) metres. For the particle projected from \(A\), the equations of motion are \(x = Ut\) and \(y = -\frac{1}{2}gt^2 + kh\). For the particle projected from \(B\), the equations of motion are \(x = V(t - T)\) and \(y = -\frac{1}{2}g(t - T)^2 + h\). The particles land in the same place, so \(x = Ut = V(t - T) \implies \frac{V}{U} = \frac{t}{t - T}\). Also, they land on the ground, so \(y = -\frac{1}{2}gt^2 + kh = 0 \implies kh = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \implies \frac{2kh}{g} = t^2\), and \(y = -\frac{1}{2}g(t - T)^2 + h = 0 \implies h = \frac{1}{2}g(t - T)^2 \implies \frac{2h}{g} = (t - T)^2\). Thus, \(t^2 = k(t - T)^2 \implies \frac{t^2}{(t - T)^2} = k \implies \frac{t}{t - T} = \sqrt{k}\) since \(t > T > 0\). Therefore, \(\frac{V}{U} = \sqrt{k}\).

(c) The hands are perpendicular when the scalar product is zero, which leads to \(\sin(\frac{\pi t}{360})\sin(\frac{\pi t}{30}) + \cos(\frac{\pi t}{360})\cos(\frac{\pi t}{30}) = 0\), which simplifies to \(\cos(\frac{\pi t}{30} - \frac{\pi t}{360}) = \cos(\frac{11\pi t}{360}) = 0\). This means \(\frac{11\pi t}{360} = \dots, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \dots\). The first time that the hands are perpendicular is when \(\frac{11\pi t}{360} = \frac{\pi}{2}\), which gives \(t = \frac{360 \times \pi}{2 \times 11 \times \pi} \approx 16.364\) minutes. The second time that the hands are perpendicular is when \(\frac{11\pi t}{360} = \frac{3\pi}{2}\), which gives \(t = \frac{3 \times 360 \times \pi}{2 \times 11 \times \pi} \approx 49.091\) minutes.

(d) \(f(x) = \cos^{-1}(\sin x)\). \(f'(x) = \frac{-\cos x}{\sqrt{1 - \sin^2 x}} = \frac{-\cos x}{|\cos x|} = \pm 1\). For \(0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}\), \(\cos x > 0 \implies f'(x) = -1\). For \(\frac{\pi}{2} < x < \pi\), \(\cos x < 0 \implies f'(x) = 1\). When \(x = \frac{\pi}{2}\), \(f'(x)\) is undefined. Thus, \(f'(x) = -1\) for \(x \in (0, \frac{\pi}{2})\) and \(f'(x) = 1\) for \(x \in (\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi)\).

(e) \(\tan \alpha + \tan \beta + \tan \gamma = -b\), \(\tan \alpha \tan \beta + \tan \alpha \tan \gamma + \tan \beta \tan \gamma = c\), \(\tan \alpha \tan \beta \tan \gamma = 1 - b - c\). \(\tan(\alpha + \beta + \gamma) = \frac{\tan \alpha + \tan(\beta + \gamma)}{1 - \tan \alpha \tan(\beta + \gamma)} = \frac{\tan \alpha + \frac{\tan \beta + \tan \gamma}{1 - \tan \beta \tan \gamma}}{1 - \tan \alpha \left(\frac{\tan \beta + \tan \gamma}{1 - \tan \beta \tan \gamma}\right)} = \frac{\tan \alpha - \tan \alpha \tan \beta \tan \gamma + \tan \beta + \tan \gamma}{1 - \tan \beta \tan \gamma - \tan \alpha \tan \beta - \tan \alpha \tan \gamma} = \frac{(\tan \alpha + \tan \beta + \tan \gamma) - \tan \alpha \tan \beta \tan \gamma}{1 - (\tan \alpha \tan \beta + \tan \alpha \tan \gamma + \tan \beta \tan \gamma)} = \frac{-b - (1 - b - c)}{1 - c} = \frac{-(1 - c)}{1 - c} = -1\). \(\therefore \alpha + \beta + \gamma = \dots, -\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3\pi}{4}, \dots\). Smallest positive value is \(\frac{3\pi}{4}\).

betterResponses: (a) recognise that there were 6 factor pairs, each with a product of 60, and treat them as pigeonholes
(a) explain that in choosing 7 factors, there would be at least one pair chosen
(a) identify the prime factors of 60 as 2,2,3,5 and then use the pigeonhole principle to explain clearly why the prime factors of at least 7 factors of 60 were needed in this case.
(b) provide the correct position vectors of both particles, considering that the angle of projection was zero and the particles were not projected from the origin
(b) recognise that the time of flight was different for particles A and B

(c) recognise that the perpendicular clock hands meant that \(\vec{OA} \cdot \vec{OB} = 0\) and were able to deftly convert the scaler (dot) product result into a compound angle result

(d) differentiate the given function correctly and realise from the denominator that the gradient was undefined at \(x=\frac{\pi}{2}\)

(e) understand how to find the smallest positive angle of \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma\) from a negative ratio.

(b) writing clearly to distinguish U from V, T from t, r from v, g from 9.

questions.title: Complex numbers, vectors, and calculus
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The location of the complex number \(z\) is shown on the diagram on page 1 of the Question 11 Writing Booklet.

On the diagram provided in the writing booklet, indicate the locations of \(\bar{z}\) and \(i\bar{z}\). (2 marks)

(b) The complex numbers \(w\) and \(z\) are given by \(w = 2e^{\frac{i\pi}{6}}\) and \(z = 3e^{\frac{i\pi}{6}}\).

Find the modulus and argument of \(wz\). (2 marks)

(c) The complex number \(z\) is given by \(x + iy\).

Find, in Cartesian form:

(i) \(z^2\) (1 mark)

(ii) \(\frac{1}{z}\). (2 marks)

(d) (i) Force \(\underset{\sim}{F_1}\) has magnitude 12 newtons in the direction of vector \(2\underset{\sim}{i} - 2\underset{\sim}{j} + \underset{\sim}{k}\).

Show that \(\underset{\sim}{F_1} = 8\underset{\sim}{i} - 8\underset{\sim}{j} + 4\underset{\sim}{k}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Force \(\underset{\sim}{F_1}\) from part (i) and a second force, \(\underset{\sim}{F_2} = -6\underset{\sim}{i} + 12\underset{\sim}{j} + 4\underset{\sim}{k}\), both act upon a particle.

Show that the resultant force acting on the particle is given by:

\[\underset{\sim}{F_3} = 2\underset{\sim}{i} + 4\underset{\sim}{j} + 8\underset{\sim}{k}.\] (1 mark)

(iii) Calculate \(\underset{\sim}{F_3} \cdot \underset{\sim}{d}\), where \(\underset{\sim}{F_3}\) is the resultant force from part (ii) and \(\underset{\sim}{d} = \underset{\sim}{i} + \underset{\sim}{j} + 2\underset{\sim}{k}\). (1 mark)

(e) Prove by contradiction that \(\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{5} > \sqrt{11}\). (2 marks)

(f) Find \(\int \frac{5}{\sqrt{7 - x^2 - 6x}} dx\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(See diagram in sample answer. (b) Modulus = 6, Argument = \frac{\pi}{3}. (c)(i) x^2 - y^2 + 2xyi. (c)(ii) \frac{x}{x^2 + y^2} - \frac{y}{x^2 + y^2}i. (d)(i) 8i - 8j + 4k. (d)(ii) 2i + 4j + 8k. (d)(iii) 22. (e) \sqrt{3} + \sqrt{5} > \sqrt{11}. (f) 5 \sin^{-1} \left( \frac{x+3}{4} \right) + C.\)
workingLatex: (a) Let \(z = a + ib\), then \(\bar{z} = a - ib\). \(i\bar{z} = i(a - ib) = -i^2b + ia = b + ia\).
(c)(ii) \(\frac{1}{z} = \frac{1}{x + iy} = \frac{x - iy}{(x + iy)(x - iy)} = \frac{x - iy}{x^2 + y^2} = \frac{x}{x^2 + y^2} - \frac{y}{x^2 + y^2}i\).

(e) Assume \(\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{5} \le \sqrt{11}\). Squaring: \((\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{5})^2 \le 11 \implies 8 + 2\sqrt{15} \le 11 \implies 2\sqrt{15} \le 3\). Squaring again: \(60 \le 9\), which is not true. Thus \(\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{5} > \sqrt{11}\).

questions.title: Calculus, Induction, Complex Loci, and Dynamics
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Using integration by parts, evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x \sin x \, dx\). (3 marks)

(b) Given the function \(y = xe^{2x}\), use mathematical induction to prove that \(\frac{d^n y}{dx^n} = (2^n x + n2^{n-1})e^{2x}\) for all positive integers \(n\), where \(\frac{d^n y}{dx^n}\) is the \(n\)th derivative of \(y\) and \(\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{d^n y}{dx^n}\right) = \frac{d^{n+1} y}{dx^{n+1}}\). (3 marks)

(c) Sketch the region of the complex plane defined by \(|z + 5 - i| > |z - 3 + 3i|\). (3 marks)

(d) Find \(\int \frac{x^2 - 2x + 9}{(4 - x)(x^2 + 1)} \, dx\). (4 marks)

(e) A particle of mass \(m \text{ kg}\) moves along a horizontal line with an initial velocity of \(V_0 \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

The motion of the particle is resisted by a constant force of \(mk \text{ newtons}\) and a variable force of \(mv^2 \text{ newtons}\), where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\) is the velocity of the particle at \(t \text{ seconds}\).

Show that the distance travelled when the particle is brought to rest is \(\frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{k + V_0^2}{k}\right) \text{ metres}\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(1 (b) See working (c) y < 2x + 1 (d) -\ln|4 - x| + 2\tan^{-1}x + c (e) x = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{k + V_0^2}{k}\right)\)
workingLatex: (a) \(Let u = x, u' = 1. Let v' = \sin x, v = -\cos x. \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x \sin x dx = [-x \cos x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} + \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos x dx = (-\frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \cos(\frac{\pi}{2}) + 0 \cdot \cos(0)) + [\sin x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} = (0 + 0) + (\sin(\frac{\pi}{2}) - \sin(0)) = 1\)

(e) \(m\ddot{x} = -mk - mv^2 \implies v\frac{dv}{dx} = -(k+v^2) \implies \int \frac{v}{k+v^2} dv = -\int dx \implies \frac{1}{2}\ln(k+v^2) = -x + C. At x=0, v=V_0, C = \frac{1}{2}\ln(k+V_0^2). Thus x = \frac{1}{2}\ln(\frac{k+V_0^2}{k+v^2}). When v=0, x = \frac{1}{2}\ln(\frac{k+V_0^2}{k})\)

questions.title: Calculus, Vectors, and Inequalities
questions.promptLatex:
(a) It is given that \( A = \int_{2}^{4} \frac{e^x}{x-1} dx \).
Show that \( \int_{m-4}^{m-2} \frac{e^{-x}}{x-m+1} dx = kA \), where \( k \) and \( m \) are constants. (3 marks)

(b) Let \( \underset{\sim}{c} = x\underset{\sim}{i} + y\underset{\sim}{j} + z\underset{\sim}{k} \) be a unit vector that is perpendicular to both \( \underset{\sim}{a} = 2\underset{\sim}{i} + 4\underset{\sim}{j} - 3\underset{\sim}{k} \) and \( \underset{\sim}{b} = -4\underset{\sim}{i} - 5\underset{\sim}{j} + 3\underset{\sim}{k} \).
Find all possible vectors \( \underset{\sim}{c} \). (4 marks)

(c) (i) For positive real numbers \( a \) and \( b \), prove that \( \frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab} \). (2 marks)
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, show that \( \frac{2n+1}{2n+2} < \sqrt{\frac{2n+1}{2n+3}} \) for any integer \( n \ge 0 \). (2 marks)

(d) Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{u}{1 + \sin u + \cos u} du \), by first using the substitution \( u = \frac{\pi}{2} - x \). (4 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(Let u = m - x, then du/dx = -1. When x = m - 2, u = 2; when x = m - 4, u = 4. I = \int_{4}^{2} \frac{e^{u-m}}{1-u}(-1)du = -e^{-m} \int_{4}^{2} \frac{e^u}{1-u}du = -e^{-m} \int_{2}^{4} \frac{e^u}{u-1}du = kA where k = -e^{-m}.\)

(b) Since \( c \) is perpendicular to \( a \) and \( b \), \( a \cdot c = 2x + 4y - 3z = 0 \) and \( b \cdot c = -4x - 5y + 3z = 0 \). Adding gives \( -2x - y = 0 \), so \( y = -2x \). Substituting into the first equation gives \( 2x - 8x - 3z = 0 \), so \( z = -2x \). Since \( c \) is a unit vector, \( x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1 \), so \( x^2 + (-2x)^2 + (-2x)^2 = 1 \), which gives \( 9x^2 = 1 \), \( x = \pm 1/3 \). Thus \( y = z = \mp 2/3 \).
(c)\((i) (a - b)^2 \ge 0 \implies a^2 - 2ab + b^2 \ge 0 \implies a^2 + 2ab + b^2 \ge 4ab \implies (a+b)^2 \ge 4ab \implies a+b \ge 2\sqrt{ab} \implies \frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab}.\)

(c)(ii) Apply AM-GM: \( \sqrt{ab} < \frac{a+b}{2} \) for \( a \ne b \). Let \( a = 2n+1 \), \( b = 2n+3 \). Then \( \sqrt{(2n+1)(2n+3)} < \frac{(2n+1)+(2n+3)}{2} = 2n+2 \). Dividing by \( (2n+1) \) gives \( \sqrt{\frac{2n+3}{2n+1}} < \frac{2n+2}{2n+1} \). Taking the reciprocal gives \( \frac{2n+1}{2n+2} < \sqrt{\frac{2n+1}{2n+3}} \).

(d) \(Let u = \pi/2 - x. The integral I = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{u}{1+\sin u + \cos u} du = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{\pi/2 - x}{1+\cos x + \sin x} dx = \frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{dx}{1+\cos x + \sin x} - I. Thus 2I = \frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{dx}{1+\cos x + \sin x}. Using t-substitution t = \tan(x/2), I = \frac{\pi}{4} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{2dt/(1+t^2)}{1 + (1-t^2)/(1+t^2) + 2t/(1+t^2)} = \frac{\pi}{4} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{2}{2+2t} dt = \frac{\pi}{4} [\ln|1+t|]_0^1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \ln 2.\)

questions.title: Calculus, Kinematics, Complex Numbers, and Inequalities
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(I_n = \int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cot^{2n} \theta \, d\theta\) for integers \(n \ge 0\).

(i) Show that \(I_n = \frac{1}{2n-1} - I_{n-1}\) for \(n > 0\), given that \(\frac{d}{d\theta} \cot \theta = -\csc^2 \theta\). (3 marks)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, calculate \(I_2\). (1 mark)

(b) The acceleration of a particle is given by \(\ddot{x} = 32x(x^2 + 3)\), where \(x\) is the displacement of the particle from a fixed-point \(O\) after \(t\) seconds, in metres. Initially the particle is at \(O\) and has a velocity of \(12 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) in the negative direction.

(i) Show that the velocity of the particle is given by \(v = -4(x^2 + 3)\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find the time taken for the particle to travel 3 metres from the origin. (2 marks)

(c) Let \(w\) be a complex number such that \(1 + w + w^2 + \dots + w^6 = 0\).

(i) Show that \(w\) is a 7th root of unity. (1 mark)

The complex number \(\alpha = w + w^2 + w^4\) is a root of the equation \(x^2 + bx + c = 0\), where \(b\) and \(c\) are real and \(\alpha\) is not real.

(ii) Find the other root of \(x^2 + bx + c = 0\) in terms of positive powers of \(w\). (2 marks)

(iii) Find the numerical value of \(c\). (1 mark)

(d) Positive real numbers \(a, b, c\) and \(d\) are chosen such that \(\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, \frac{1}{c}\) and \(\frac{1}{d}\) are consecutive terms in an arithmetic sequence with common difference \(k\), where \(k \in \mathbb{R}, k > 0\).

Show that \(b + c < a + d\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \((i) I_n = \int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cot^{2n}\theta d\theta = \int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cot^{2(n-1)}\theta(\csc^2\theta - 1) d\theta = \int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cot^{2(n-1)}\theta \csc^2\theta d\theta - I_{n-1} = \left[ -\frac{\cot^{2n-1}\theta}{2n-1} \right]_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} - I_{n-1} = \frac{1}{2n-1} - I_{n-1}\)

(b) \((i) \int v dv = \int 32x(x^2+3) dx \implies \frac{v^2}{2} = 8(x^2+3)^2 + C. \text{ Given } v=-12, \frac{144}{2} = 8(3)^2 + C \implies 72 = 72 + C \implies C=0. v^2 = 16(x^2+3)^2 \implies v = \pm 4(x^2+3). \text{ Since } v < 0, v = -4(x^2+3).\)

(d) \(b = \frac{a}{1+ak}, c = \frac{a}{1+2ak}, d = \frac{a}{1+3ak}. a+d = a + \frac{a}{1+3ak} = \frac{2a+3a^2k}{1+3ak}. b+c = \frac{a}{1+ak} + \frac{a}{1+2ak} = \frac{2a+3a^2k}{1+3ak+2a^2k^2}. \text{ Since } 1+3ak+2a^2k^2 > 1+3ak, \text{ then } b+c < a+d.\)

questions.title: Vectors, SHM, and Complex Roots
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The adjacent sides of a parallelogram are represented by the vectors \(\underset{\sim}{a} = 4\underset{\sim}{i} + 3\underset{\sim}{j} - \underset{\sim}{k}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b} = 2\underset{\sim}{i} - \underset{\sim}{j} + 3\underset{\sim}{k}\). (4 marks)
Show that the area of the parallelogram is \(6\sqrt{10}\) square units.

(b) A particle moves in simple harmonic motion about the origin with amplitude \(A\), and it completes two cycles per second. When it is \(\frac{1}{4}\) metres from the origin, its speed is half its maximum speed. (4 marks)
Find the maximum positive acceleration of the particle during its motion.

(c) (i) Show that (3 marks)
\[ \frac{1 + \cos \theta + i \sin \theta}{1 - \cos \theta - i \sin \theta} = i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}. \]

(ii) Use De Moivre's theorem to show that the sixth roots of \(-1\) are given by \(\cos \left( \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6} \right) + i \sin \left( \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6} \right)\) for \(k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\). (2 marks)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, show the solutions to \(\left( \frac{z-1}{z+1} \right)^6 = -1\) are (2 marks)
\[ z = i \cot \left( \frac{\pi}{12} \right), i \cot \left( \frac{3\pi}{12} \right), i \cot \left( \frac{5\pi}{12} \right), i \cot \left( \frac{7\pi}{12} \right), i \cot \left( \frac{9\pi}{12} \right), \text{ and } i \cot \left( \frac{11\pi}{12} \right). \]
questions.answerLatex:
(a) \(6\sqrt{10} \text{ square units}\)
 (b) \(\frac{8\pi^2}{\sqrt{3}} \text{ m s}^{-2}\)
 (c)\((i) i \cot \frac{\theta}{2}\)
 (c)\((ii) w = \cos \left( \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6} \right) + i \sin \left( \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6} \right), k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\)
 (c)\((iii) z = i \cot \left( \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{12} \right) \text{ for } k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\)
workingLatex: (b) \(\text{Let } x(t) = A \cos(4\pi t + \alpha) \implies v(t) = -4\pi A \sin(4\pi t + \alpha) \implies \text{max speed} = 4\pi A\)
(b) \(\text{At } t=0, x = \frac{1}{4} \text{ and } v = \frac{1}{2} \times 4\pi A = 2\pi A \implies A \cos \alpha = \frac{1}{4} \text{ and } 4\pi A \sin \alpha = 2\pi A\)
(c)\((ii) w^6 = -1 \implies |w|=1. \text{ Let } w = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta. \cos 6\theta + i \sin 6\theta = \cos \pi + i \sin \pi \implies 6\theta = \pi + 2k\pi\)

questions.title: Complex numbers, resistive motion, and vectors
questions.subtopic: Complex numbers, resisted motion, and vector geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the equation

\[ z^n \cos[n\theta] + z^{n-1} \cos[(n-1)\theta] + z^{n-2} \cos[(n-2)\theta] + \dots + z \cos[\theta] = 1 \]

where \( z \in \mathbb{C} \), \( \theta \in \mathbb{R} \), and \( n \) is a positive integer.

Using a proof by contradiction and the triangle inequality, or otherwise, prove that all the solutions to the equation lie outside the circle \( |z| = \frac{1}{2} \) on the complex plane. (4 marks)

(b) A particle of mass 1 kg is projected from the origin with a speed of 50 m s\(^{-1}\), at an angle of \( \theta \) below the horizontal into a resistive medium.

The position of the particle \( t \) seconds after projection is \( (x, y) \), and the velocity of the particle at that time is \( \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} \).

The resistive force, \( \underset{\sim}{R} \), is proportional to the velocity of the particle, so that \( \underset{\sim}{R} = -k\underset{\sim}{v} \), where \( k \) is a positive constant.

Taking the acceleration due to gravity to be 10 m s\(^{-2}\), and the upwards vertical direction to be positive, the acceleration of the particle at time \( t \) is given by:

\[ \underset{\sim}{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -k\dot{x} \\ -k\dot{y} - 10 \end{pmatrix}  \text{(Do NOT prove this.)} \]

Derive the Cartesian equation of the motion of the particle, given \( \sin \theta = \frac{3}{5} \). (5 marks)

(c) Consider the point \( B \) with three-dimensional position vector \( \underset{\sim}{b} \) and the line \( \ell: \underset{\sim}{a} + \lambda \underset{\sim}{d} \), where \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) and \( \underset{\sim}{d} \) are three-dimensional vectors, \( |\underset{\sim}{d}| = 1 \) and \( \lambda \) is a parameter.

Let \( f(\lambda) \) be the distance between a point on the line \( \ell \) and the point \( B \).

(i) Find \( \lambda_0 \), the value of \( \lambda \) that minimises \( f \), in terms of \( \underset{\sim}{a} \), \( \underset{\sim}{b} \) and \( \underset{\sim}{d} \). (2 marks)

(ii) Let \( P \) be the point with position vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} + \lambda_0 \underset{\sim}{d} \).

Show that \( PB \) is perpendicular to the direction of the line \( \ell \). (1 mark)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, find the shortest distance between the line \( \ell \) and the sphere of radius 1 unit, centred at the origin \( O \), in terms of \( \underset{\sim}{d} \) and \( \underset{\sim}{a} \).

You may assume that if \( B \) is the point on the sphere closest to \( \ell \), then \( OBP \) is a straight line. (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) All solutions lie outside the circle \( |z| = \frac{1}{2} \). (b) \( x = \frac{40}{k}(1 - e^{-kt}) \), \( y = \frac{10}{k^2} \left( \frac{(1 - 3k)kx}{40} + \ln \left( \frac{40 - kx}{40} \right) \right) \). (c) (i) \( \lambda_0 = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}) \cdot \mathbf{d} \). (ii) \( \vec{PB} \cdot \mathbf{d} = 0 \). (iii) \( |\vec{PB}| = \sqrt{|\mathbf{a}|^2 - (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{d})^2} - 1 \).
workingLatex: (a) Assume for contradiction that \( w \) is a solution to the equation and \( |w| \le \frac{1}{2} \). Then \( 1 = |w^n \cos n\theta + w^{n-1} \cos(n-1)\theta + \dots + w \cos \theta| \le |w|^n + |w|^{n-1} + \dots + |w| \le (\frac{1}{2})^n + \dots + \frac{1}{2} = 1 - (\frac{1}{2})^n < 1 \). This is a contradiction.

(b) Horizontal motion: \( \ddot{x} = -k\dot{x} \implies \dot{x} = 40e^{-kt} \implies x = \frac{40}{k}(1 - e^{-kt}) \). Vertical motion: \( \ddot{y} = -k\dot{y} - 10 \implies \dot{y} = \frac{1}{k}\{(10 - 30k)e^{-kt} - 10\} \implies y = \frac{10}{k} \{ \frac{(3k-1)}{k} (e^{-kt} - 1) - t \} \). Substituting \( t = \frac{1}{k} \ln(\frac{40}{40-kx}) \) yields the result.

(c) (i) The squared distance is \( g(\lambda) = |\mathbf{b} - (\mathbf{a} + \lambda \mathbf{d})|^2 = |\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}|^2 - 2(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}) \cdot \mathbf{d}\lambda + \lambda^2 \). Minimising gives \( \lambda_0 = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}) \cdot \mathbf{d} \).

(c) (iii) By Pythagoras, \( |\mathbf{a}|^2 = |\vec{OP}|^2 + |\vec{AP}|^2 = (1 + |\vec{PB}|)^2 + |\vec{AP}|^2 \). Substituting \( |\vec{AP}| = |\lambda_0| = |\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{d}| \) and solving for \( |\vec{PB}| \) gives \( |\vec{PB}| = \sqrt{|\mathbf{a}|^2 - (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{d})^2} - 1 \).

questions.title: Mass of currency calculations
questions.promptLatex:
The table provides information about a $2 coin and a $5 note.

| Coin/note | Quantity needed to make $1000 | Mass of this number of notes/coins (kg) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $2 | 500 | 3.3 |
| $5 | 200 | 0.157 |

(a) Calculate the mass of a $2 coin in grams, correct to 1 decimal place. (2 marks)

(b) Suppose the $2 coin is to be replaced with a note that has the same mass as a $5 note.

What is the mass of $1000 in $2 notes in grams? Give your answer correct to the nearest gram. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) 500 $2 coins have a mass of 3.3 kg
(a) \(Therefore, the mass of 1 $2 coin is \frac{3.3 \text{ kg}}{500} = 0.0066 \text{ kg}\)
(b) 200 notes have a mass of 157 g
(b) \(Therefore, the mass of one note is \frac{157 \text{ g}}{200} = 0.785 \text{ g}\)
(b) \(The mass of 500 notes is 0.785 \times 500 = 392.5 \text{ g} = 393 \text{ g} \text{ (to the nearest gram)}\)

betterResponses: convert units correctly between kilograms and grams
use the unitary method to find the mass of one coin, that is, divide the total mass by number of coins, and scale up, that is, multiply by 500, to get the mass of 500 coins
use ratios/rates confidently, write correct units, show clear steps, and treat separate parts of the question independently.
improvementAreas: converting between grams and kilograms
applying the unitary method consistently and show working for each step.

criteria.criterion: Finds the mass of one coin in kg, or equivalent merit
Finds the mass of one note, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Air conditioner power and cost calculation
questions.promptLatex:
A house has a reverse-cycle air conditioner which uses \(2.5 \text{ kW}\) of power for cooling and \(3.2 \text{ kW}\) of power for heating. The cost of electricity is \(29 \text{ cents per kWh}\).

(a) Find the cost, in dollars and cents, of cooling the house for \(6 \text{ hours}\). (1 mark)

(b) The cost of operating the air conditioner to heat the house during winter last year was \(\$640\). There are \(92 \text{ days}\) in winter.

Find the number of hours, to \(1 \text{ decimal place}\), that the air conditioner was used on average per day. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (b) \(\text{Cost per day} = \frac{640}{92} = 6.956\dots\), \(\text{Cost per hour} = 3.2 \times 0.29 = 0.928\), \(\text{Number of hours} = \frac{6.956\dots}{0.928} = 7.49\dots \approx 7.5 \text{ hours}\)

betterResponses: write an equation and solve to find the number of hours
give a clear, labelled expression for the cost of electricity used
convert cents into dollars before calculation.
improvementAreas: recognising the requirement for a specific cooling or heating cost/time period, not a combined one
providing all steps required, rather than just dividing total cost by number of days in calculating the number of hours the air conditioner was used.

criteria.criterion: Finds cost per day, or equivalent merit

workingLatex: (b)(i) Outflow of \(C = 5 + 13 + 12 = 30\). Inflow must equal outflow. Therefore, \(x = 30\)

calculate the capacity of the indicated cut
identify an actual path

questions.title: Bivariate data analysis and regression
questions.promptLatex:
In a research study, participants were asked to record the number of minutes they spent watching television and the number of minutes they spent exercising each day over a period of 3 months. The averages for each participant were recorded and graphed.

(a) Describe the bivariate dataset in terms of its form and direction. (2 marks)
Form: ...................................................................................................................................
Direction: ...........................................................................................................................

The equation of the least-squares regression line for this dataset is
\[ y = 64.3 - 0.7x \]

(b) Interpret the values of the slope and \(y\)-intercept of the regression line in the context of this dataset. (2 marks)

(c) Jo spends an average of 42 minutes per day watching television.
Use the equation of the regression line to determine how many minutes on average Jo is expected to exercise each day. (1 mark)

(d) Explain why it is NOT appropriate to extrapolate the regression line to predict the average number of minutes of exercise per day for someone who watches an average of 2 hours of television per day. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Form: Linear, Direction: Negative (b) Slope: For every 1 minute increase in time spent watching television per day the time spent exercising decreases by 0.7 minutes per day. y-intercept: If someone doesn't watch television they are expected to exercise for 64.3 minutes per day. (c) 34.9 minutes (d) 2 hours or 120 minutes of television suggests -19.7 minutes of exercise and can't do negative minutes of exercise. (Alternative: 2 hours or 120 minutes is outside the dataset so extrapolating is not reliable.)
workingLatex: (b) Slope: For every 1 minute increase in time spent watching television per day the time spent exercising decreases by 0.7 minutes per day.
(b) y-intercept: If someone doesn't watch television they are expected to exercise for 64.3 minutes per day.
(d) 2 hours or 120 minutes of television suggests -19.7 minutes of exercise and can't do negative minutes of exercise.
(d) Alternative: 2 hours or 120 minutes is outside the dataset so extrapolating is not reliable.

betterResponses: (a) demonstrate a strong understanding through accurately selecting the correct terminology to describe both form and direction
(a) identify that the form is linear and the distribution has a negative direction.
(b) identify the slope and \(y\)-intercept for the equation of the least-squares regression line
(b) clearly interpret the meaning of the slope and \(y\)-intercept within the context of the scenario, rather than simply explaining what slope and \(y\)-intercepts represent in general terms.
(c) correctly substitute \(x= 42\) into the equation to calculate the answer.
(d) convert 2 hours to minutes and substitute \(x= 120\) minutes into the formula
(d) identify that the graph goes below zero and cannot have a negative number of minutes of exercise
(d) recognise 2 hours is outside the dataset
(d) understand the meaning of the key word ‘extrapolate’.
improvementAreas: (a) using the correct language for form and/or direction
(a) refining their knowledge of statistical terms and key words used in the syllabus for bivariate data analysis.
(b) interpreting the slope and \(y\)-intercept in context of the question with values shown
(b) interpreting the slope and \(y\)-intercept correctly, not just identifying the values
(b) understanding that the slope is not the same as the correlation coefficient.
(c) showing correct substitution into the formula
(c) understanding what each variable in an equation represents.
(d) understanding that the values outside the dataset are not reliable
(d) knowing extending the line of regression gives the negative answer for time
(d) knowing the meaning of the key word extrapolate.

criteria.criterion: Provides correct direction, or equivalent merit
Provides correct interpretation of both slope and y-intercept
Provides correct interpretation of y-intercept, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Normal distribution of sheep weights
questions.promptLatex:
(a) In a flock of 12 600 sheep, the ratio of males to females is 1 : 20.

The weights of the male sheep are normally distributed with a mean of 76.2 kg and a standard deviation of 6.8 kg.

In the flock, 15 of the male sheep each weigh more than x kg.

Find the value of x. (4 marks)

(b) The weights of the female sheep are also normally distributed but have a smaller mean and smaller standard deviation than the weights of male sheep.

Explain whether it could be expected that 300 of the females from the flock each weigh more than x kg, where x is the value found in part (a). (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(x = 89.8 \text{ kg}\) (b) The percentage of female sheep with weight greater than \(x\) kg will be smaller than \(2.5\%\), so less than \(300\) females will weigh more than \(x\) kg.
workingLatex: (a) \(\text{Percentage of males weighing more than } x \text{ kg} = \frac{15}{600} \times 100 = 2.5\%\)
(b) \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \text{ will be larger than 2 as the mean and standard deviation for females is smaller.}\)
(b) \(\text{The percentage of female sheep with weight greater than } x \text{ kg will be smaller than 2.5%.}\)
(b) \(\text{So less than 300 females will weigh more than } x \text{ kg.}\)

betterResponses: (a) find the number of male sheep correctly using the given information: \(12600/21 = 600\)
(a) express 15 male sheep as a percentage of 600 male sheep: \(15/600 = 0.025 = 2.5\%\)
(a) use the percentage to equate to a \(z\)-score of 2
(a) calculate \(x\) kg using the normal distribution curve or the \(z\)-score formula.
(b) express 300 female sheep as a percentage of the 12 000 female sheep correctly
(b) recognise the female percentage (or \(z\)-score) is the same as the male percentage from part (a) of the question

(b) recognise and state that with a smaller mean and standard deviation, the percentage of female sheep greater than \(x\) would be less than 2.5%, or that the \(z\)-score of \(x\) would be greater than 2.
improvementAreas: (a) calculating the number of male sheep correctly according to the given ratio
(a) identifying the difference between a \(z\)-score and a percentage
(a) using and labelling a diagram to help to understand where the values are placed within the normal distribution
(a) using the Reference Sheet to match the percentage break-up to a \(z\)-score.
(b) calculating the correct number and percentage of female sheep
(b) understanding the concepts, \(z\)-score, mean, standard deviation in more detail, and how these concepts affect each other
(b) providing an accurate explanation, justified with mathematical reasoning and calculations.

questions.title: Curve sketching using calculus
questions.promptLatex: Sketch the curve \(y = x^4 - 2x^3 + 2\) by first finding all stationary points, checking their nature, and finding the points of inflection. (5 marks)
questions.answerLatex: \(Horizontal point of inflection at (0, 2), local minimum at (\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}), point of inflection at (1, 1).\)
workingLatex: When \(x = 0\), \(y = 2\) and \(y'' = 0\). Concavity changes sign, so horizontal point of inflection at \((0, 2)\).
\(When x = \frac{3}{2}, y = \frac{5}{16} and y'' = 9 > 0, so local minimum at (\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}).\)
\(Points of inflection: y'' = 0 \implies 12x^2 - 12x = 0 \implies 12x(x - 1) = 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ or } x = 1.\)
At \(x = 1\), \(y = 1\). Concavity changes sign, so point of inflection at \((1, 1)\).

betterResponses: find the first and second derivatives correctly

justify a change in concavity
clearly provide a sketch of the function.

justifying a change in concavity
clearly sketching graphs using calculus.

workingLatex: (b) \(\text{By symmetry the area between } 46 \text{ and } 58 \text{ is equal to the area between } 58 \text{ and } 70.\)

questions.title: Future value of an annuity
questions.promptLatex:
Jack intends to deposit $80 into a savings account on the first day of each month for 24 months. The interest rate during this time is 6% per annum, compounded monthly.

(a) Calculate how much money Jack will have in his account at the end of the 24 months. (3 marks)

(b) A table of future value interest factors could have been used to calculate how much money Jack would have in his account at the end of the 24 months.

Part of the table is shown.

| Number of periods | Interest rate per period | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| | 0.5% | |
| 24 | A | |

What is the value of A in the table? Give your answer correct to 3 decimal places. (1 mark)
workingLatex: (a) The first deposit accrues interest for 24 months, so its future value is \(80(1.005)^{24}\).
(a) Using the geometric series sum formula: \(80 \times (1.005) \left[ \frac{(1.005)^{24} - 1}{1.005 - 1} \right] = 2044.73\).

(d) Solve the differential equation \(\frac{dy}{dx} = xy\), given \(y > 0\). Express your answer in the form \(y = e^{f(x)}\). (2 marks)

(e) Differentiate the function \(f(x) = \arcsin(x^5)\). (1 mark)

(f) The volume of a sphere of radius \(r \text{ cm}\), is given by \(V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\), and the volume of the sphere is increasing at a rate of \(10 \text{ cm}^3\text{s}^{-1}\).
Show that the rate of increase of the radius is given by \(\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{5}{2\pi r^2} \text{ cm s}^{-1}\). (2 marks)

(g) The region, \(R\), is bounded by the curves \(y = \sin x\), \(y = x\) and the line \(x = \frac{\pi}{2}\) as shown in the diagram.

Find the area of the region \(R\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (d) \(\frac{dy}{dx} = xy \implies \int \frac{dy}{y} = \int x dx \implies \log_e y = \frac{x^2}{2} + C \implies y = e^{\frac{x^2}{2} + C}\)

(a) (ii) correctly apply the scalar (dot) product using vector components.
(b) factorise the quadratic and correctly test the regions to determine the solution, use acceptable notation to display their answer.

(c) transform the integral into a simpler form in terms of the given substitution, correctly simplify the integrand after substitution, integrate correctly and express their solution in terms of the original variable.

(d) separate the variables in the differential equation, integrate both sides of the differential equation correctly, give the answer in the required form ensuring that the constant term of integration was correctly handled.
(e) recognise that arcsin( \(x\)^5 ) = \(sin^{-1}(x)^5\), choose the appropriate differentiation rule to be used and applied it correctly.
(f) apply the chain rule with relevant pronumerals, recognise that \(dV/dt = 10 cm^3s^{-1}\), find the correct expression for \(dV/dr\), show all necessary working to arrive at the required result.

(g) recognise that the required area is found by taking the difference of the area between two curves, calculate the area enclosed by the line \(y = x\) and the \(x\)-axis by either using the correct definite integral or by using the area of a triangle formula, calculate the area enclosed by the curve \(y = sin x\) and the \(x\)-axis by using the correct definite integral.

(a) (ii) recognising that the scalar (dot) product of two vectors is a scalar and not a vector.
(b) demonstrating awareness of the inequality sign in the question, using graphs to solve quadratic inequalities, demonstrating proper use of inequalities or brackets to specify intervals.

(c) using brackets around binomial terms in the integrand after substitution, performing integration of terms with fractional indices, simplifying expressions using the index laws, expressing their solution in terms of the original variable and not in terms of the substituting variable.

(d) understanding that differential equations should not be integrated where there are multiple variables on one side, being mindful of the implications of the restrictions given in the question such as \(y > 0\), using logarithmic laws, such as when solving for \(y\) when \(ln y = f(x)\) and \(f(x) = x^2/2 + c\).
(e) taking care when simplifying expressions using index laws.

(f) practising problems involving related rates, using correct notations to represent rates, for example, \(dV/dr\) instead of \(V'\), showing all necessary steps and ensuring that the last line matches exactly what is being asked in the question.

questions.title: Mixed topics in calculus, vectors, and probability
questions.subtopic: Vectors, Calculus, Statistics, Induction, and Inequalities
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The vectors \(\begin{pmatrix} a^2 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix} a + 5 \\ a - 4 \end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular. (3 marks)

Find the possible values of \(a\).

(b) The region, \(R\), is bounded by the function, \(y = x^3\), the \(x\)-axis and the lines \(x = 1\) and \(x = 2\). (3 marks)

What is the volume of the solid of revolution obtained when the region \(R\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis?

(c) A charity employs a worker to collect donations. There is a 0.31 chance that when the charity worker talks to someone a donation is made to the charity. (3 marks)

Each day the charity worker must talk to exactly 100 people.

Use the standard normal distribution and the information on page 13 to approximate the probability that, on a particular day, at least 35\% of the people talked to made a donation.

(d) Use mathematical induction to prove that \(2^{3n} + 13\) is divisible by 7 for all integers \(n \ge 1\). (3 marks)

(e) The diagram shows the graph of \(y = \frac{1}{|x - 5|}\). (3 marks)

For what values of \(x\) is \(\frac{x}{6} \ge \frac{1}{|x - 5|}\)?
workingLatex: (a) \(\begin{pmatrix} a^2 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} a+5 \\ a-4 \end{pmatrix} = 0 \implies a^2(a+5) + 2(a-4) = 0 \implies a^3 + 5a^2 + 2a - 8 = 0. \text{ Testing } a=1: 1+5+2-8=0. \text{ Using sum and product of roots: } \alpha+\beta+\gamma = -5, \beta\gamma = 8. \text{ Roots are } 1, -2, -4.\)

(e) \(For x > 5: \frac{x}{6} = \frac{1}{x-5} \implies x^2-5x-6=0 \implies (x-6)(x+1)=0 \implies x=6. For x < 5: \frac{x}{6} = \frac{-1}{x-5} \implies x^2-5x+6=0 \implies (x-2)(x-3)=0 \implies x=2, 3. Solution: [2, 3] \cup [6, \infty).\)

betterResponses: (a) apply the scalar (dot) product to vectors expressed in component form, demonstrate an understanding of the properties of perpendicular vectors, apply the Factor Theorem to solve a cubic polynomial equation, use division of polynomials to factorise a cubic polynomial equation.

(b) calculate the volume of the solid of revolution formed by rotating a region in the plane about the \(x\)-axis, correctly integrate the function and manipulate the limits of integration to obtain the correct numerical solution.
(c) recognise the values needed to calculate the standard deviation, calculate the \(z\)-score from the given data, use the normal distribution table provided to find the required probability.
(d) establish the base case, use the assumption statement to prove the inductive step by manipulating the algebra without error.

(e) solve inequalities containing both absolute value and a variable in the denominator, identify that solving with absolute values results in two cases, find the critical points by considering the two cases and testing each region to obtain the correct solution, solve quadratic equations involving inequalities.
improvementAreas: (a) applying the scalar (dot) product rule to arrive at the correct cubic polynomial equation, understanding and applying the division of polynomials, factorising a cubic equation to arrive at possible values of \(a\).
(b) understanding how to find the volume of the solid of revolution using \(V = \pi \int y^2 dx\).
(c) determining which values to substitute into the \(z\)-score formula.
(d) understanding the base case occurs when \(n = 1\), rearranging the assumption to substitute into the inductive step, setting out a proof by mathematical induction including all necessary steps.

(e) understanding the sign implications if there is a variable in the denominator, strengthening their algebraic skills in multiplying by the square of the denominator, understanding that the solution cannot include the value of the variable that makes the denominator zero.

questions.title: Differential equations, trigonometry, vectors, and integration
questions.promptLatex:
(a) In an experiment, the population of insects, \(P(t)\), was modelled by the logistic differential equation
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = P(2000 - P) \]
where \(t\) is the time in days after the beginning of the experiment.

The diagram shows a direction field for this differential equation, with the point \(S\) representing the initial population.

(i) Explain why the graph of the solution that passes through the point \(S\) cannot also pass through the point \(T\). (1 mark)

(ii) On the diagram provided on page 1 of the Question 13 Writing Booklet, clearly sketch the graph of the solution that passes through the point \(S\). (1 mark)

(iii) Find the predicted value of the population, \(P(t)\), at which the rate of growth of the population is largest. (2 marks)

(b) (i) Show that \(\cos^4 x + \sin^4 x = \frac{1 + \cos^2 2x}{2}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} (\cos^4 x + \sin^4 x) dx\). (3 marks)

(c) The vector \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\) and the vector \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\).

The projection of a vector \(\underset{\sim}{x}\) onto the vector \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) is \(k\underset{\sim}{a}\), where \(k\) is a real number.

The projection of the vector \(\underset{\sim}{x}\) onto the vector \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) is \(p\underset{\sim}{b}\), where \(p\) is a real number.

Find the vector \(\underset{\sim}{x}\) in terms of \(k\) and \(p\). (4 marks)

(d) Using the substitution \(u = e^x + 2e^{-x}\), and considering \(u^2\), find
\[ \int \frac{e^{3x} - 2e^x}{4 + 8e^{2x} + e^{4x}} dx. \] (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) (i) All graphs passing through \(T\) must be decreasing to the left of \(T\). The \(y\)-intercept of such a curve must be above \(T\). The point \(S\) is not above \(T\), so \(S\) cannot be on any such curve. (a) (ii) [Sketch showing a curve passing through \((0, 2000)\) and approaching the horizontal asymptote, consistent with the direction field.] (a) (iii) \(P = 1000\). (b) (i) \(\cos^4 x + \sin^4 x = \frac{1 + \cos^2 2x}{2}\). (b) (ii) \(\frac{3\pi}{16}\). (c) \(\underset{\sim}{x} = \frac{5}{7} \begin{pmatrix} 2k + 3p \ 4k - p \end{pmatrix}\). (d) \(\frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{e^x + 2e^{-x}}{2} \right) + C\).
workingLatex: (a) \((iii) Rate of growth is a maximum when \frac{dP}{dt} is a maximum, i.e., when \frac{d^2P}{dt^2} = 0.\)

(c) Let \(\underset{\sim}{x} = \begin{pmatrix} u \ v \end{pmatrix}\). Then \(k\underset{\sim}{a} = \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{x}}{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{a}} \underset{\sim}{a} \implies k = \frac{u + 3v}{10} \implies u + 3v = 10k\). Also \(p\underset{\sim}{b} = \frac{\underset{\sim}{b} \cdot \underset{\sim}{x}}{\underset{\sim}{b} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}} \underset{\sim}{b} \implies p = \frac{2u - v}{5} \implies 2u - v = 5p\). Solving the system yields \(\underset{\sim}{x} = \frac{5}{7} \begin{pmatrix} 2k + 3p \ 4k - p \end{pmatrix}\).

(d) Let \(u = e^x + 2e^{-x}\), then \(\frac{du}{dx} = e^x - 2e^{-x}\). The integral becomes \(\int \frac{du}{u^2 + 4} = \frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1} \frac{u}{2} + C = \frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{e^x + 2e^{-x}}{2} \right) + C\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) explain explicitly that there exists a horizontal asymptote with \(S\) and \(T\) opposite it, hence \(S\) cannot reach \(T\).
(a) (ii) sketch a smooth concave down graph that follows the horizontal asymptote.

(a) (iii) find the second derivative correctly with respect to \(P\), equate the second derivative to 0 and solve correctly to get \(P = 1000\), sketch the first derivative correctly as a concave down parabola, then use the turning point to find the maximum value of \(P\).
(b) (i) prove with correct trigonometric substitution that LHS = RHS, use the double angle formulae correctly to arrive at LHS.
(b) (ii) use part (i) correctly to substitute the given integrand, find the correct anti-derivative, evaluate the limits correctly to arrive at the correct answer.

(c) understand and apply the projection formula correctly, obtain both \(k\) and \(p\) in terms of components of \(\underset{\sim}{x}\), solve simultaneous equations, arrive at the correct factorised answer of the components.

(d) differentiate correctly with respect to \(u\), expand \(u^2\) correctly, use algebra to factorise the numerator and denominator, to obtain a simple fraction which could be applied to an inverse trigonometric integration, substitute \(u\) back in terms of \(e^x\) as their final answer.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) stating that there is a horizontal asymptote at \(y = 2000\), clearly identifying that \(S\) and \(T\) are opposite sides of the asymptote.
(a) (ii) sketching smooth curves, making sure the curve follows the horizontal asymptote sufficiently, ensuring their curves do not cross the slopes perpendicularly.

(a) (iii) deriving the first derivative with respect to \(P\) rather than \(t\), applying the implications of the vertex in parabolas, using quadratic theory (\(x = -b/2a\)) to reason the maximum growth rate is half the carrying capacity.
(b) (i) knowing their trigonometric identities, squaring a binomial involving fractions correctly, working with difficult algebraic expressions.
(b) (ii) substituting part (i) correctly in the given integrand, finding the anti-derivative of \(cos^4 x\) correctly, correctly evaluating a trigonometric function.

(c) using the projection formula, recognising the component must be written as different variables, solving simultaneous equations with fractions, understanding the difference between components and vectors.

(d) differentiating correctly with respect to \(u\), expanding \(u^2\) correctly as an exponential function, recognising algebraic manipulations, in particular factorisation to present the integrand as a recognisable simple integration, rewriting an anti-derivative in terms of \(e^x\).

Obtains correct primitive, or equivalent merit
Obtains correct integral involving \cos 4x, or equivalent merit
\(Obtains both k and p in terms of the components of \underset{\sim}{x}, or equivalent merit\)
\(Obtains k or p in terms of the components of \underset{\sim}{x}, or equivalent merit\)

questions.title: Differential equations, inverse functions, and kinematics
questions.subtopic: Differential equations, inverse functions, and projectile motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find the domain and range of the function that is the solution to the differential equation
\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = e^{x+y} \]
and whose graph passes through the origin. (4 marks)

(b) For what values of the constant \( k \) would the function \( f(x) = \frac{kx}{1+x^2} + \arctan x \) have an inverse? (3 marks)

(c) (i) Explain why the equation \( \tan^{-1}(3x) + \tan^{-1}(10x) = \theta \), where \( -\pi < \theta < \pi \), has exactly one solution. (1 mark)

(ii) Solve \( \tan^{-1}(3x) + \tan^{-1}(10x) = \frac{3\pi}{4} \). (2 marks)

(d) A particle is projected from the origin, with initial speed \( V \) at an angle of \( \theta \) to the horizontal. The position vector of the particle, \( \underset{\sim}{r}(t) \), where \( t \) is the time after projection and \( g \) is the acceleration due to gravity, is given by
\[ \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} Vt \cos \theta \\ Vt \sin \theta - \frac{gt^2}{2} \end{pmatrix}  (\text{Do NOT prove this.}) \]
Let \( D(t) \) be the distance of the particle from the origin at time \( t \), so \( D(t) = |\underset{\sim}{r}(t)| \).
Show that for \( \theta < \sin^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}\right) \) the distance, \( D(t) \), is increasing for all \( t > 0 \). (4 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Domain: \(-\infty, \ln 2\), Range: \(-\ln 2, \infty\) (b) \(-1 \le k \le 1\) (c) (i) Explanation provided (ii) \(x = \frac{1}{2}\) (d) Proof provided
workingLatex: Range: As \(x \to -\infty, y \to -\ln 2\); as \(x \to \ln 2, y \to \infty\). Range is \((-\ln 2, \infty)\)
Case 1: \(f'(x) \ge 0 \implies (k+1) + (1-k)x^2 \ge 0\). This holds if \(k+1 \ge 0\) and \(1-k \ge 0\), so \(-1 \le k \le 1\)

(c) (i) Both functions are increasing with range \((-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2})\). Their sum is an increasing function with range \((-\pi, \pi)\). The line \(y = \theta\) intersects the graph at exactly one point for \(-\pi < \theta < \pi\).
Testing values, only \(x = \frac{1}{2}\) is a solution.
For \(D(t)\) to be increasing, we require the quadratic in \(t\) to have no real roots, i.e., \(\Delta < 0\)

betterResponses: (a) separate the variables, integrate correctly and find the value of the constant, determine the domain and range for the function.

(b) demonstrate an understanding of what was required of the original function in order to have an inverse function, differentiate the function correctly, recognise that, as the function could have been monotonic increasing or decreasing, they needed to investigate both cases where the derivative may have been exclusively positive or negative, understand that the numerator of the derivative was a quadratic in \(x\) and they should investigate the concavity and translation to determine possible values of \(k\).

(c) (i) explain concisely, using mathematical language, that both of \(tan^{-1}(3x)\) and \(tan^{-1}(10x)\) are monotonically increasing functions with a range of \(-\pi/2 < f(x) < \pi/2\) and so their sum would also be monotonically increasing with a range of \(-\pi < f(x) < \pi\), show that the derivative of \(tan^{-1}(3x) + tan^{-1}(10x)\) is always positive and so \(tan^{-1}(3x) + tan^{-1}(10x)\) would be monotonically increasing, graph \(y = tan^{-1}(3x) + tan^{-1}(10x)\) neatly, clearly showing the range.

(c) (ii) take the tangent of both sides of the equation and use the relevant compound angle formula to develop a quadratic equation, solve the quadratic equation to find two solutions, justify why one of the solutions was not valid in this situation and identify the correct solution.

(d) find an expression for \(D\) or \(D^2\), recognise that the use of \(D^2\) would make their ensuing differentiation easier, correctly differentiate their expressions for \(D\) or \(D^2\), understand how to show that, for \(D(t)\) to be always increasing, their derivative must always be positive.
(c)(ii) take the tangent of both sides of the equation and use the relevant compound angle formula to develop a quadratic equation
(c)(ii) solve the quadratic equation to find two solutions
(c)(ii) justify why one of the solutions was not valid in this situation and identify the correct solution.
(d) find an expression for \(D\) or \(D^2\)
(d) recognise that the use of \(D^2\) would make their ensuing differentiation easier
(d) correctly differentiate their expressions for \(D\) or \(D^2\)
(d) understand how to show that, for \(D(t)\) to be always increasing, their derivative must always be positive.

(b) explaining the requirement for a function to have an inverse function, differentiating complex functions, recognising how to show that a derivative is always positive or negative.

(c) (i) using clear mathematical language to explain a concept, addressing all components of the question, understanding that \(y = \theta\) would represent a horizontal line through which \(y = tan^{-1}(3x) + tan^{-1}(10x)\) would pass only once.

(c) (ii) understanding that the use of the calculator to provide a bold answer is not equivalent to solving an equation, using the compound angle formula, factorising complex algebra, recognising that part (i) of the question clearly stated that the equation would have only one solution.
