questions.title: Perpendicular vector calculation
questions.promptLatex:
Which of the following vectors is perpendicular to \(3\underset{\sim}{i} + 2\underset{\sim}{j} - 5\underset{\sim}{k}\)?

A. \(-\underset{\sim}{i} - \underset{\sim}{j} + \underset{\sim}{k}\)
B. \(\underset{\sim}{i} + \underset{\sim}{j} - \underset{\sim}{k}\)
C. \(-2\underset{\sim}{i} + 3\underset{\sim}{j} + \underset{\sim}{k}\)
D. \(3\underset{\sim}{i} - 2\underset{\sim}{j} + \underset{\sim}{k}\)

questions.title: Interpretation of formal logic statement
questions.promptLatex:
Consider the following statement written in the formal language of proof
\[ \forall \theta \in \left( \frac{\pi}{2}, \pi \right) \exists \phi \in \left( \pi, \frac{3\pi}{2} \right); \sin \theta = -\cos \phi. \]
Which of the following best represents this statement?

A. There exists a \(\theta\) in the second quadrant such that for all \(\phi\) in the third quadrant \(\sin \theta = -\cos \phi\).
B. There exists a \(\phi\) in the third quadrant such that for all \(\theta\) in the second quadrant \(\sin \theta = -\cos \phi\).
C. For all \(\theta\) in the second quadrant there exists a \(\phi\) in the third quadrant such that \(\sin \theta = -\cos \phi\).
D. For all \(\phi\) in the third quadrant there exists a \(\theta\) in the second quadrant such that \(\sin \theta = -\cos \phi\).

questions.title: Converse of a logical statement
questions.promptLatex:
Consider the statement:

'If a polygon is a square, then it is a rectangle.'

Which of the following is the converse of the statement above?

A. If a polygon is a rectangle, then it is a square.
B. If a polygon is a rectangle, then it is not a square.
C. If a polygon is not a rectangle, then it is not a square.
D. If a polygon is not a square, then it is not a rectangle.

questions.title: Roots of a monic cubic polynomial
questions.promptLatex:

Which of the following could be \(f(x)\)?

A. \(f(x) = x^3 - 7x^2 - 17x + 15\)
B. \(f(x) = x^3 - 7x^2 + 17x - 15\)
C. \(f(x) = x^3 + 7x^2 - 17x + 15\)
D. \(f(x) = x^3 + 7x^2 + 17x - 15\)

questions.title: Simple harmonic motion position and velocity
questions.promptLatex:

A. At the central point of motion with a velocity of \(V \text{ m s}^{-1}\)
B. At the central point of motion with a velocity of \(-V \text{ m s}^{-1}\)
C. 8 m to the left of the central point of motion with a velocity of \(0 \text{ m s}^{-1}\)
D. 8 m to the right of the central point of motion with a velocity of \(0 \text{ m s}^{-1}\)

questions.title: Angle between vectors in 3D
questions.promptLatex:
Three unit vectors \(\underset{\sim}{a}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c}\), in 3 dimensions, are to be chosen so that \(\underset{\sim}{a} \perp \underset{\sim}{b}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b} \perp \underset{\sim}{c}\) and the angle \(\theta\) between \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c}\) is as small as possible.

What is the value of \(\cos \theta\)?

A. 0
B. \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\)
C. \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\)
D. \(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\)

questions.title: Calculus, Complex Numbers, and Vectors
questions.subtopic: Integration, Complex Algebra, and Vector Geometry
questions.promptLatex:

(b) Let \(z = 2 + 3i\) and \(w = 1 - 5i\).
(i) Find \(z + \bar{w}\). (1 mark)
(ii) Find \(z^2\). (1 mark)

(c) Find the angle between the two vectors \(\underset{\sim}{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -4 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}\), giving your answer in radians, correct to 1 decimal place. (2 marks)

(d) Evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{\sin \theta + 1} d\theta\). (3 marks)

(e) (i) Write the number \(\sqrt{3} + i\) in modulus-argument form. (2 marks)
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, write \((\sqrt{3} + i)^7\) in exact Cartesian form. (2 marks)

(f) Sketch the region defined by \(|z| < 3\) and \(0 \le \arg(z - i) \le \frac{\pi}{2}\). (3 marks)

questions.title: Vectors, Calculus, Complex Numbers, and Algebra
questions.subtopic: Integration, Vector Geometry, and Complex Equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The vector \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\) and the vector \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}\).

(i) Find \(\frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{\underset{\sim}{b} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(\underset{\sim}{a} - \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{\underset{\sim}{b} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}} \underset{\sim}{b}\) is perpendicular to \(\underset{\sim}{b}\). (2 marks)

(b) Use partial fractions to find
\[ \int \frac{3x^2 + 2x + 1}{(x-1)(x^2+1)} dx. \] (3 marks)

(c) Consider the equation \(|z| = z + 8 + 12i\), where \(z = a + bi\) is a complex number and $a, b$ are real numbers.

(i) Explain why \(b = -12\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, find \(z\). (2 marks)

(d) Explain why there is no integer \(n\) such that \((n+1)^{41} - 79n^{40} = 2\). (2 marks)

(e) The line \(\ell\) passes through the points \(A(3, 5, -4)\) and \(B(7, 0, 2)\).

(i) Find a vector equation of the line \(\ell\). (1 mark)

(ii) Determine, giving reasons, whether the point \(C(10, 5, -2)\) lies on the line \(\ell\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \((i) \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} (a) (ii) \text{Vectors are perpendicular.} (b) 3 \ln|x-1| + 2 \tan^{-1}x + C (c) (i) b = -12 (c) (ii) z = 5 - 12i (d) \text{Original statement cannot be true.} (e) (i) \ell: \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 5 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -5 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix} (e) (ii) \text{Point } (10, 5, -2) \text{ is not on } \ell.\)
workingLatex: (b) \(x=1 \implies 2A = 6 \implies A=3; x=0 \implies A-C=1 \implies C=2; x=-1 \implies 2A + (-B+C)(-2) = 2 \implies 6 + 2B - 4 = 2 \implies B=0\)
(c) \((i) |z| = z + 8 + 12i; \text{LHS real so } a + bi + 8 + 12i \text{ real} \implies (b+12)i = 0 \implies b = -12\)
(d) \(\text{If } n \text{ is odd, } (n+1) \text{ is even. } (n+1)^{41} \text{ is even and } 79n^{40} \text{ is odd. Even - odd } \ne \text{ even.}\)
(d) \(\text{If } n \text{ is even, } (n+1) \text{ is odd. } (n+1)^{41} \text{ is odd and } 79n^{40} \text{ is even. Odd - even } \ne \text{ even.}\)

questions.title: Vectors, SHM, Resistive Motion, and Inequalities
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The point \(A\) has position vector \(8\mathbf{i} - 6\mathbf{j} + 5\mathbf{k}\). The line \(\ell\) has vector equation
\[x\mathbf{i} + y\mathbf{j} + z\mathbf{k} = t(\mathbf{i} + \mathbf{j} + 2\mathbf{k}).\]
The point \(B\) lies on \(\ell\) and has position vector \(p\mathbf{i} + p\mathbf{j} + 2p\mathbf{k}\).
(i) Show that \(|AB|^2 = 6p^2 - 24p + 125\). (1 mark)
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, determine the shortest distance between the point \(A\) and the line \(\ell\). (2 marks)

(b) A particle is moving in simple harmonic motion, described by \(\ddot{x} = -4(x + 1)\). (3 marks)
When the particle passes through the origin, the speed of the particle is \(4 \text{ m s}^{-1}\).
What distance does the particle travel during a full period of its motion?

(c) A particle of unit mass moves horizontally in a straight line. It experiences a resistive force proportional to \(v^2\), where \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\) is the speed of the particle, so that the acceleration is given by \(-kv^2\).
Initially the particle is at the origin and has a velocity of \(40 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) to the right. After the particle has moved \(15 \text{ m}\) to the right, its velocity is \(10 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) (to the right).
(i) Show that \(v = 40e^{-kx}\). (3 marks)
(ii) Show that \(k = \frac{\ln 4}{15}\). (1 mark)
(iii) At what time will the particle's velocity be \(30 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) to the right? (3 marks)

(d) It is known that for all positive real numbers \(x, y\)
\[x + y \ge 2\sqrt{xy}.  (\text{Do NOT prove this.})\]
Show that if \(a, b, c\) are positive real numbers with \(\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} = 1\) then \(a\sqrt{bc} + b\sqrt{ac} + c\sqrt{ab} \le abc\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) |AB|^2 = 6p^2 - 24p + 125 (a)(ii) \sqrt{101} (b) 4\sqrt{5} \text{ metres} (c)(i) v = 40e^{-kx} (c)(ii) k = \frac{\ln 4}{15} (c)(iii) t = \frac{1}{8 \ln 4} \text{ seconds} (d) \text{Proof provided}\)
workingLatex: (a)\((ii) \text{Axis of symmetry } p = \frac{-(-24)}{2 \times 6} = 2. \text{ Shortest distance when } p=2: \text{dist}^2 = 6(2)^2 - 24(2) + 125 = 101. \text{ Shortest distance is } \sqrt{101}.\)
(b) \(\ddot{x} = -4(x+1). \text{ Integrate } v \frac{dv}{dx} = -4(x+1) \text{ to get } v^2 = 4(A^2 - (x+1)^2). \text{ Using } x=0, v=4, A^2=5. \text{ Distance travelled } = 4\sqrt{5}.\)
(c)\((i) m\ddot{x} = -kv^2 \implies v \frac{dv}{dx} = -kv^2 \implies \int \frac{dv}{v} = \int -k dx. \text{ Solving with } v(0)=40 \text{ gives } v = 40e^{-kx}.\)
(c)\((iii) \frac{dv}{dt} = -kv^2 \implies \int v^{-2} dv = \int -k dt \implies \frac{1}{v} = kt + C. \text{ Using } v(0)=40, C = \frac{1}{40}. \text{ For } v=30, t = \frac{1}{8 \ln 4}.\)
(d) \(\text{Using } AM-GM: a\sqrt{bc} + b\sqrt{ac} + c\sqrt{ab} \le a \cdot \frac{1}{2}(b+c) + b \cdot \frac{1}{2}(a+c) + c \cdot \frac{1}{2}(a+b) = ab + ac + bc \le abc.\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) find the vector \(\vec{AB}\) in component form, then find \(|\vec{AB}|^2\) showing all working.

(a) (ii) use the formula for the axis of symmetry and the concavity of the parabola to show that \(|\vec{AB}|^2\) has a minimum value when \(p= 2\), then substitute into the expression in (a) to find the minimum value of \(|\vec{AB}|^2\), then take its square root to find the minimum distance

(a) (ii) use calculus to find the minimum turning point of \(|\vec{AB}|^2\), or use the dot product of \(AB\) and \(\ell\), the vector projection of \(\vec{AB}\) onto \(\ell\) or \(\vec{OA}\) onto \(\ell\), the scalar projection of \(\vec{AB}\) onto \(\ell\) or \(\vec{OA}\) onto \(\ell\), or complete the square on \(|\vec{AB}|^2\).

(b) find \(n= 2\) and \(c= -1\) from the equation of motion, then find \(a= \sqrt{5}\) by either: (1) finding an expression for \(v^2\) using definite integration using \(\frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2} v^2) = -4(x+ 1)\), (2) finding an expression for \(v^2\) using indefinite integration using \(\frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2} v^2) = -4(x+ 1)\) then rearranging into the form \(v^2 = n^2(a^2 -(x-c)^2)\), (3) using their equation for \(v^2\) to find the extremities of motion, (4) finding expressions for displacement and velocity as functions of sine and cosine
(b) find that the distance travelled in a full period of motion is \(4a= 4\sqrt{5}\), or twice the distance between the extremities of motion.

(c) (i) integrate \(v \frac{dv}{dx}= -kv^2\) or \(\frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2} v^2) = -kv^2\) to find an equation linking \(x\) and \(v\), then correctly rearrange to make \(v\) the subject, clearly explaining why only the positive solution was required.
(c) (ii) substitute correctly into \(v = 40e^{-kx}\) and rearrange the equation one step at a time to reach the given result.
(c) (iii) integrate \(\frac{dv}{dt}= -kv^2\) or \(\frac{dx}{dt}= 40e^{-kx}\) to find an expression involving \(v\) and \(t\) or \(x\) and \(t\) respectively, then correctly substitute to find \(t\).

(d) start with an expression equal to either the left-hand side, the right-hand side or the difference between the sides, of \(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}= 1\) or \(a\sqrt{bc}+ b\sqrt{ac}+ c\sqrt{ab}= abc\), then use the arithmetic mean – geometric mean (AM-GM) three times to prove the result
(d) start with three AM-GM inequalities using appropriate variables and add them, then rearrange to prove the result
(d) use three separate inequalities and add them appropriately.

questions.title: Complex Numbers, Induction, and Vector Geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove that if \(a\) is any odd integer, then \(a^2 - 1\) is divisible by 8. (2 marks)

(b) Use mathematical induction to prove that \(^{2n}C_n < 2^{2n-2}\), for all integers \(n \ge 5\). (3 marks)

(c) For the complex numbers \(z\) and \(w\), it is known that \(\arg\left(\frac{z}{w}\right) = -\frac{\pi}{2}\). Find \(\left|\frac{z-w}{z+w}\right|\). (2 marks)

(d) The following argument attempts to prove that \(0 = 1\).

We evaluate \(\int \frac{1}{x} dx\) using the method of integration by parts.

\[ \int \frac{1}{x} dx = \int \frac{1}{x} \times 1 dx \]
\[ = \frac{1}{x} \times x - \int -\frac{1}{x^2} x dx \]
\[ = 1 + \int \frac{1}{x} dx \]

So we have

\[ \int \frac{1}{x} dx = 1 + \int \frac{1}{x} dx \]

We may now subtract \(\int \frac{1}{x} dx\) from both sides to show that \(0 = 1\).

Explain what is wrong with this argument. (2 marks)

(e) The diagram shows triangle \(OQA\).

The point \(P\) lies on \(OA\) so that \(OP : OA = 3 : 5\).

The point \(B\) lies on \(OQ\) so that \(OB : OQ = 1 : 3\).

The point \(R\) is the intersection of \(AB\) and \(PQ\).

The point \(T\) is chosen on \(AQ\) so that \(O\), \(R\) and \(T\) are collinear.

Let \(\underset{\sim}{a} = \vec{OA}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b} = \vec{OB}\) and \(\vec{PR} = k\vec{PQ}\) where \(k\) is a real number.

(i) Show that \(\vec{OR} = \frac{3}{5}(1 - k)\underset{\sim}{a} + 3k\underset{\sim}{b}\). (2 marks)

Writing \(\vec{AR} = h\vec{AB}\), where \(h\) is a real number, it can be shown that \(\vec{OR} = (1 - h)\underset{\sim}{a} + h\underset{\sim}{b}\). (Do NOT prove this.)

(ii) Show that \(k = \frac{1}{6}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Find \(\vec{OT}\) in terms of \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b}\). (2 marks)

(d) understanding that indefinite integrals refer to a function whose gradient function is equal to the integrand, not to areas underneath curves
(d) understanding that an integral is only defined where the integrand is defined, so in this case the fact that \(x\) could not be equal to zero was not relevant
(d) understanding that integration by parts works for all integrals, even those that can be solved more easily using standard integrals.

(e) (i) recognising that if the question states an expression for \(\vec{PR}\), then it is more efficient to use \(\vec{OR} = \vec{OP} + \vec{PR}\) than break \(\vec{OR}\) into the sum of any other vectors

questions.title: Vectors, Integrals, and Particle Motion
questions.subtopic: Integration, Vector Geometry, and Resisted Motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the three vectors \(\underset{\sim}{a} = \vec{OA}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b} = \vec{OB}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c} = \vec{OC}\), where \(O\) is the origin and the points \(A, B\) and \(C\) are all different from each other and the origin.

The point \(M\) is the point such that \(\frac{1}{2}(\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b}) = \vec{OM}\).

(i) Show that \(M\) lies on the line passing through \(A\) and \(B\). (1 mark)

(ii) The point \(G\) is the point such that \(\frac{1}{3}(\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c}) = \vec{OG}\).
Show that \(G\) lies on the line passing through \(M\) and \(C\), and lies between \(M\) and \(C\). (2 marks)

(iii) The complex numbers \(x, w\) and \(z\) are all different and all have modulus 1.
Using part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(\frac{1}{3}(x + w + z)\) is never a cube root of \(xwz\). (2 marks)

(b) Let \(I_n = \int_{0}^{a} x^{n + \frac{1}{2}} (a - x)^{\frac{1}{2}} dx\), where \(n \ge 0\).
Show that \((2n + 4)I_n = a(2n + 1)I_{n-1}\), for \(n > 0\). (3 marks)

(c) A bar magnet is held vertically. An object that is repelled by the magnet is to be dropped from directly above the magnet and will maintain a vertical trajectory. Let \(x\) be the distance of the object above the magnet.

The object is subject to acceleration due to gravity, \(g\), and an acceleration due to the magnet \(\frac{27g}{x^3}\), so that the total acceleration of the object is given by
\[ a = \frac{27g}{x^3} - g \]

The object is released from rest at \(x = 6\).

(i) Show that \(v^2 = g\left(\frac{51}{4} - 2x - \frac{27}{x^2}\right)\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find where the object next comes to rest, giving your answer correct to 1 decimal place. (2 marks)

(d) Using a suitable substitution, find
\[ \int \frac{2x^2}{\sqrt{2x - x^2}} dx. \] (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) \vec{OM} = \vec{OA} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{AB} (a)(ii) G \text{ lies between } C \text{ and } M (a)(iii) \frac{1}{3}(x+w+z) \text{ cannot be a cube root of } xwz (b) (2n+4)I_n = a(2n+1)I_{n-1} (c)(i) v^2 = g\left(\frac{51}{4} - 2x - \frac{27}{x^2}\right) (c)(ii) x \approx 1.7 (d) -3\sin^{-1}(1-x) - (x+3)\sqrt{2x-x^2} + C\)
workingLatex: (a)\((ii) \vec{OG} = \frac{1}{3}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) = \vec{OC} + \frac{2}{3}\vec{CM}. \text{ Since } \vec{OG} \ne \vec{OC} \text{ and } \vec{OG} \ne \vec{OM}, G \text{ lies between } C \text{ and } M.\)

(a)\((iii) |z|=1. \text{ The point } G \text{ is } \frac{1}{3}(x+w+z), \text{ which lies on } CM \text{ and is inside the unit circle, so } |\frac{1}{3}(x+w+z)| < 1. \text{ Since } |xwz|=1, \text{ its cube roots have modulus } 1.\)

(b) \(I_n = \int_0^a x^{n+\frac{1}{2}}(a-x)^{\frac{1}{2}} dx. \text{ Using parts, } u=x^{n+\frac{1}{2}}, v'=(a-x)^{\frac{1}{2}}. \text{ Resulting in } I_n + \frac{1}{3}(2n+1)I_n = \frac{a}{3}(2n+1)I_{n-1} \implies (2n+4)I_n = a(2n+1)I_{n-1}.\)

(c)\((i) \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2}v^2) = \frac{27g}{x^3} - g. \text{ Integrating: } \frac{1}{2}v^2 = \frac{-27g}{2x^2} - gx + C. \text{ Using } x=6, v=0 \implies D = \frac{51}{4}g. \text{ Thus } v^2 = g(\frac{51}{4} - 2x - \frac{27}{x^2}).\)

questions.title: Trigonometry, Complex Roots, and Particle Motion
questions.subtopic: Complex roots, resisted motion, and vector geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the function \(y = \cos(kx)\), where \(k > 0\). The value of \(k\) has been chosen so that a circle can be drawn, centred at the origin, which has exactly two points of intersection with the graph of the function and so that the circle is never above the graph of the function. The point \(P(a, b)\) is the point of intersection in the first quadrant, so \(a > 0\) and \(b > 0\), as shown in the diagram.

The vector joining the origin to the point \(P(a, b)\) is perpendicular to the tangent to the graph of the function at that point. (Do NOT prove this.)

Show that \(k > 1\). (4 marks)

(b) The number \(w = e^{\frac{2\pi i}{3}}\) is a complex cube root of unity. The number \(\gamma\) is a cube root of \(w\).

(i) Show that \(\gamma + \bar{\gamma}\) is a real root of \(z^3 - 3z + 1 = 0\). (3 marks)

(ii) By using part (i) to find the exact value of \(\cos\frac{2\pi}{9}\cos\frac{4\pi}{9}\cos\frac{8\pi}{9}\), deduce the value(s) of \(\cos\frac{2^n\pi}{9}\cos\frac{2^{n+1}\pi}{9}\cos\frac{2^{n+2}\pi}{9}\) for all integers \(n \ge 1\). Justify your answer. (3 marks)

(c) Two particles, \(A\) and \(B\), each have mass \(1\text{ kg}\) and are in a medium that exerts a resistance to motion equal to \(kv\), where \(k > 0\) and \(v\) is the velocity of any particle. Both particles maintain vertical trajectories.

The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\text{ m s}^{-2}\), where \(g > 0\).

The two particles are simultaneously projected towards each other with the same speed, \(v_0\text{ m s}^{-1}\), where \(0 < v_0 < \frac{g}{k}\).

The particle \(A\) is initially \(d\) metres directly above particle \(B\), where \(d < \frac{2v_0}{k}\).

Find the time taken for the particles to meet. (4 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(k > 1 (b)(i) \gamma + \bar{\gamma} \text{ is a real root of } z^3 - 3z + 1 = 0 (b)(ii) \cos \frac{2^n \pi}{9} \cos \frac{2^{n+1} \pi}{9} \cos \frac{2^{n+2} \pi}{9} = -\frac{1}{8} (c) t = \frac{1}{k} \log \left( \frac{2v_0}{2v_0 - kd} \right)\)
workingLatex: (a) \(\text{At } P(a, b) \text{ the slope of the curve is } -k \sin ka. \text{ Since } OP \text{ is perpendicular to the tangent at } P, \frac{b}{a} \times (-k \sin ka) = -1 \implies -\frac{a}{b} = -k \sin ka. \text{ But } b = \cos ka, \text{ so } a = k \sin ka \cos ka = \frac{k}{2} \sin 2ka \implies 2a = k \sin 2ka. \text{ Let } X = 2a, \text{ then } X = k \sin kX. \text{ If } k \le 1, \text{ then as } X > 0, k \sin kX \le \sin kX < kX \le X. \text{ But } X = k \sin kX, \text{ so by contradiction } k > 1.\)

(b)\((i) w = e^{\frac{2\pi i}{3}} \text{ as } |\gamma| = |\bar{\gamma}| = 1, \gamma = e^{i\theta}, \bar{\gamma} = e^{-i\theta}, \gamma\bar{\gamma} = 1. (\gamma + \bar{\gamma})^3 - 3(\gamma + \bar{\gamma}) + 1 = \gamma^3 + 3\gamma^2\bar{\gamma} + 3\gamma\bar{\gamma}^2 + \bar{\gamma}^3 - 3\gamma - 3\bar{\gamma} + 1 = \gamma^3 + \bar{\gamma}^3 + 3\gamma + 3\bar{\gamma} - 3\gamma - 3\bar{\gamma} + 1 = \gamma^3 + \bar{\gamma}^3 + 1 = 2 \cos \frac{2\pi}{3} + 1 = 2(-\frac{1}{2}) + 1 = 0.\)

(b)\((ii) \text{The roots of } z^3 - 3z + 1 = 0 \text{ are } 2 \cos \frac{2\pi}{9}, 2 \cos \frac{4\pi}{9}, 2 \cos \frac{8\pi}{9}. \text{ The product of the roots is } -1. \text{ Hence } 8 \cos \frac{2\pi}{9} \cos \frac{4\pi}{9} \cos \frac{8\pi}{9} = -1 \implies \cos \frac{2\pi}{9} \cos \frac{4\pi}{9} \cos \frac{8\pi}{9} = -\frac{1}{8}. \text{ Using } \cos \frac{2^{k+3}\pi}{9} = \cos \frac{2^k\pi}{9}, \text{ we show } \cos \frac{2^n\pi}{9} \cos \frac{2^{n+1}\pi}{9} \cos \frac{2^{n+2}\pi}{9} = -\frac{1}{8} \text{ for all integers } n \ge 1.\)

(c) \(\text{Let } \ell = x_A - x_B. \frac{d^2\ell}{dt^2} = a_A - a_B = (-g - kv_A) - (-g - kv_B) = -k(v_A - v_B) = -k \frac{d\ell}{dt}. \frac{d\ell}{dt} = Le^{-kt}. \text{ At } t=0, \frac{d\ell}{dt} = -2v_0, \text{ so } \frac{d\ell}{dt} = -2v_0 e^{-kt}. \ell = \frac{2v_0}{k} e^{-kt} + C. \text{ At } t=0, \ell = d, \text{ so } C = d - \frac{2v_0}{k}. \ell = \frac{2v_0}{k} e^{-kt} + d - \frac{2v_0}{k}. \text{ Setting } \ell = 0 \implies 2v_0 e^{-kt} = 2v_0 - kd \implies e^{-kt} = \frac{2v_0 - kd}{2v_0} \implies t = \frac{1}{k} \log \left( \frac{2v_0}{2v_0 - kd} \right).\)

questions.title: Probability density function analysis
questions.promptLatex:
A continuous random variable \(X\) has probability density function \(f(x)\) given by

\[f(x) = \begin{cases} 12x^2(1 - x), & \text{for } 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{for all other values of } x \end{cases}\]

(a) Find the mode of \(X\). (2 marks)

(b) Find the cumulative distribution function for the given probability density function. (2 marks)

(c) Without calculating the median, show that the mode is greater than the median. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{2}{3} (b) F(x) = 4x^3 - 3x^4 (c) \text{The mode is greater than the median because } P(X < \frac{2}{3}) \approx 0.59 > 0.5.\)
workingLatex: (a) Mode of \(X\) will be when \(f(x)\) has a maximum.
(a) Discard \(x = 0\) since \(f(0) = 0\), so the mode of \(X\) is \(\frac{2}{3}\).
(c) The probability of the variable being less than \(\frac{2}{3}\) is greater than \(0.5\), therefore the mode is greater than the median.

betterResponses: (a) expand the probability density function so that the derivative can be easily determined
(a) calculate the mode as the \(x\)-coordinate of the maximum turning point of the probability density function
(a) identify the mode by substituting the possible turning point of \(x\) values into the probability density function and comparing the \(y\) values, rather than first or second derivative testing.
(b) integrate the probability density function using limits of 0 to \(x\) to obtain the cumulative distribution function
(b) expand the probability density function before integrating the expression.
(c) correctly substitute the mode into the cumulative distribution function
(c) understand that the median occurs at the point where the area under the probability density function is 0.5
(c) conclude the calculation of the cumulative distribution function for the mode is greater than the median value of the cumulative distribution function.
improvementAreas: (a) distinguishing the difference between a probability density function and a cumulative distribution function
(a) applying correct differentiation techniques and simplifying functions before differentiating

questions.title: Probability of student availability
questions.promptLatex:
Four Year 12 students want to organise a graduation party. All four students have the same probability, \(P(F)\), of being available next Friday. All four students have the same probability, \(P(S)\), of being available next Saturday.

It is given that \(P(F) = \frac{3}{10}\), \(P(S|F) = \frac{1}{3}\), and \(P(F|S) = \frac{1}{8}\).

Kim is one of the four students.

(a) Is Kim's availability next Friday independent from his availability next Saturday? Justify your answer. (1 mark)

(b) Show that the probability that Kim is available next Saturday is \(\frac{4}{5}\). (2 marks)

(c) What is the probability that at least one of the four students is NOT available next Saturday? (2 marks)

betterResponses: (a) demonstrate the difference between an independent and dependent event
(a) know how one event impacts the other
(a) use \(P(F|S) \neq P(F)\) to show that Kim’s availability next Friday changes, given Kim is available on Saturday.
(b) set out a well-structured response with the aid of conditional probability rules
(b) calculate the probability intersection of Saturday and Friday.
(c) find the complement of all four students available.
improvementAreas: (a) recognising the difference between an independent and dependent event
(a) understanding the meaning of conditional probability notation.
(b) understanding how to use conditional probability in the context of a show question
(b) identifying this dependent multi-event probability
(b) recognising that \(P(S \cap F)\) and \(P(F \cap S)\) are the same as they represent where the sets intersect.
(c) understanding the technique of complementary probability theory when the expression ‘at least one…’ is required it is equivalent to ‘1 – Probability (none)’
(c) finding the complement of four students and not just one.

questions.promptLatex:

(c) Solve the inequality 
\[ \frac{x-3}{x+2} \le 1 \] 
(2 marks)

(f) A random variable \(X\) is normally distributed with mean \(\mu = 50\) and standard deviation \(\sigma = 10\).

(ii) A sample of size \(n=16\) is taken from this population. Let \(\bar{X}\) be the sample mean. Find \(P(\bar{X} < 47.5)\). (2 marks)

questions.title: Water tank filling and projectile collision
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A hemispherical water tank has radius \(R \text{ cm}\). The tank has a hole at the bottom which allows water to drain out.

Initially the tank is empty. Water is poured into the tank at a constant rate of \(2kR \text{ cm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}\), where \(k\) is a positive constant.

After \(t\) seconds, the height of the water in the tank is \(h \text{ cm}\), as shown in the diagram, and the volume of water in the tank is \(V \text{ cm}^3\).

It is known that \(V = \pi \left( Rh^2 - \frac{h^3}{3} \right)\). (Do NOT prove this.)

While water flows into the tank and also drains out of the bottom, the rate of change of the volume of water in the tank is given by \(\frac{dV}{dt} = k(2R - h)\).

(i) Show that \(\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Show that the tank is full of water after \(T = \frac{\pi R^2}{2k}\) seconds. (2 marks)

(iii) The instant the tank is full, water stops flowing into the tank, but it continues to drain out of the hole at the bottom as before.

Show that the tank takes 3 times as long to empty as it did to fill. (3 marks)

(b) Particle \(A\) is projected from the origin with initial speed \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle \(\theta\) with the horizontal plane. At the same time, particle \(B\) is projected horizontally with initial speed \(u \text{ m s}^{-1}\) from a point that is \(H\) metres above the origin, as shown in the diagram.

The position vector of particle \(A\), \(t\) seconds after it is projected, is given by
\[ \mathbf{r}_A(t) = \begin{pmatrix} vt \cos \theta \\ vt \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix}.  \text{(Do NOT prove this.)} \]

The position vector of particle \(B\), \(t\) seconds after it is projected, is given by
\[ \mathbf{r}_B(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \\ H - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix}.  \text{(Do NOT prove this.)} \]

The angle \(\theta\) is chosen so that \(\tan \theta = 2\).

The two particles collide.

(i) By first showing that \(\cos \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\), verify that \(v = \sqrt{5}u\). (2 marks)

(ii) Show that the particles collide at time \(T = \frac{H}{2u}\). (1 mark)

(iii) When the particles collide, their velocity vectors are perpendicular.

Show that \(H = \frac{2u^2}{g}\). (3 marks)

(iv) Prior to the collision, the trajectory of particle \(A\) was a parabola. (Do NOT prove this.)

Find the height of the vertex of that parabola above the horizontal plane. Give your answer in terms of \(H\). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) \(\pi h(2R - h)\frac{dh}{dt} = k(2R - h) \implies \pi h \frac{dh}{dt} = k \implies \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h}\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) find a correct expression for \(\frac{dV}{dh}\), use their knowledge of related rates to link \(\frac{dV}{dh}\) and \(\frac{dV}{dt}\) to find \(\frac{dh}{dt}\), simplify the algebraic expression to obtain the required result.

(a) (ii) solve the differential equation from part (i) to obtain an expression relating time and the height of water in the tank, clearly explain that when the tank is full, the height of the water will equal the radius of the tank.

(a) (iii) model the situation to develop a new differential equation representing the rate at which water was draining from the tank, use their knowledge of related rates to link \(\frac{dV}{dh}\) and their new equation \(\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dh} \times \frac{dh}{dt}\), integrate the rate to obtain the required result.

(b) (i) clearly show that \(\cos \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\), use the result \(\cos \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\) and the horizontal displacements of the two projectiles to achieve the required result.
(b) (ii) use information from part (i) and the vertical displacements of the projectile to achieve the required result.

(b) (iii) find expressions for the velocities of each projectile, use the \(\vec{v}_A \cdot \vec{v}_B = 0\) result for the projectiles having perpendicular paths to obtain an equation which can be solved, solve the resulting quadratic equation to obtain the required result.
(b) (iv) find the time to maximum height for projectile A, use time to find the maximum height, express their maximum height in terms of \(H\).
improvementAreas: (a) (i) showing all steps of their processing.

(a) (ii) recognising the need to use the result generated in part (i) of the question, separating the variables of the differential equation correctly so that the terms of integration match the variable being integrated, finding the value of any constants generated in their integration, showing all steps of their processing.

(a) (iii) using the information in the question to determine a new expression for \(\frac{dV}{dt}\) when the tank is draining, separating the variables of the differential equation correctly so that the terms of integration matched the variable being integrated, finding the value of any constants generated in their integration, showing all steps of their processing.

(b) (i) using a diagram or trigonometric identities to find a value for \(\cos \theta\) in exact form, recognising that the horizontal displacements must be equal when the projectiles collide, showing all steps of their processing.

(b) (ii) recognising that the vertical displacements must be equal when the projectiles collide, finding an exact value for \(\sin \theta\) using the methods of part (i), showing all steps of their processing.

(b) (iii) differentiating with respect to time in order to find the velocities of each projectile, recognising that \(H\) is a constant, its derivative is 0 and that it does not feature as part of the expression for the vertical velocity of projectile B, knowing that the dot product of the velocities of the projectiles will be 0 since their paths are perpendicular at the point of collision, using algebraic skills to simplify the resulting quadratic equation formed from the dot product, factorising or using the quadratic formula to solve the equation, showing all steps of their processing.

(b) (iv) recognising that maximum height will occur when the vertical velocity is 0, using the correct expression for maximum height, substituting their value for time and using algebraic skills to simplify the resulting expression, understanding the intent of the phrase ‘Give your answer in terms of \(H\)’.

questions.title: Functions, Geometry, and Vector Calculus
questions.subtopic: Inverse functions, vector geometry, and polynomial roots
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(f(x) = 2x + \ln x\), for \(x > 0\).

(i) Explain why the inverse of \(f(x)\) is a function. (1 mark)

(ii) Let \(g(x) = f^{-1}(x)\). By considering the value of \(f(1)\), or otherwise, evaluate \(g'(2)\). (2 marks)

(b) Consider the hyperbola \(y = \frac{1}{x}\) and the circle \((x - c)^2 + y^2 = c^2\), where \(c\) is a constant.

(i) Show that the \(x\)-coordinates of any points of intersection of the hyperbola and circle are zeros of the polynomial \(P(x) = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\). (1 mark)

(ii) The graphs of \(y = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\) for \(c = 0.8\) and \(c = 1\) are shown.

By considering the given graphs, or otherwise, find the exact value of \(c > 0\) such that the hyperbola \(y = \frac{1}{x}\) and the circle \((x - c)^2 + y^2 = c^2\) intersect at only one point. (3 marks)

(c) (i) Given a non-zero vector \(\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix}\), it is known that the vector \(\begin{pmatrix} q \\ -p \end{pmatrix}\) is perpendicular to \(\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix}\) and has the same magnitude. (Do NOT prove this.)

Points \(A\) and \(B\) have position vectors \(\vec{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\vec{OB} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{pmatrix}\), respectively.

Using the given information, or otherwise, show that the area of triangle \(OAB\) is \(\frac{1}{2}|a_1b_2 - a_2b_1|\). (3 marks)

(ii) The point \(P\) lies on the circle centred at \(I(r, 0)\) with radius \(r > 0\), such that \(\vec{IP}\) makes an angle of \(t\) to the horizontal.

The point \(Q\) lies on the circle centred at \(J(-R, 0)\) with radius \(R > 0\), such that \(\vec{JQ}\) makes an angle of \(2t\) to the horizontal.

Note that \(\vec{OP} = \vec{OI} + \vec{IP}\) and \(\vec{OQ} = \vec{OJ} + \vec{JQ}\).

Using part (i), or otherwise, find the values of \(t\), where \(-\pi \le t \le \pi\), that maximise the area of triangle \(OPQ\). (4 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) \(f'(x) = 2 + \frac{1}{x} > 0\) for \(x > 0\). \(f\) is always increasing. Therefore since \(f\) is one-to-one for \(x > 0\), \(f\) has an inverse function.

(b)(i) The points of intersection satisfy \(y = \frac{1}{x}\) (1) and \((x - c)^2 + y^2 = c^2\) (2). Substitute (1) into (2): \((x - c)^2 + \frac{1}{x^2} = c^2\). Multiplying by \(x^2\): \(x^2(x^2 - 2cx + c^2) + 1 = c^2x^2\), which simplifies to \(x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1 = 0\).

(b)(ii) Let \(f(x) = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\). The hyperbola and circle have exactly one point of intersection if there exists \(x\) such that \(f(x) = 0\) and \(f'(x) = 0\). \(f'(x) = 4x^3 - 6cx^2 = 2x^2(2x - 3c)\). \(f'(x) = 0 \implies x = 0\) or \(x = \frac{3c}{2}\). Since \(f(0) \ne 0\), we solve \(f(\frac{3c}{2}) = 0\). \((\frac{3c}{2})^4 - 2c(\frac{3c}{2})^3 + 1 = 0 \implies \frac{81c^4}{16} - \frac{54c^4}{8} + 1 = 0\). Multiplying by 16: \(81c^4 - 108c^4 + 16 = 0 \implies -27c^4 = -16 \implies c = \frac{2}{\sqrt[4]{27}}\).

(c)(i) Let \(\theta\) be the angle between \(\vec{OA}\) and \(\vec{OB}\). Area \(= \frac{1}{2}ab\sin\theta\). Let \(\vec{OC} = \begin{pmatrix} a_2 \\ -a_1 \end{pmatrix}\) be perpendicular to \(\vec{OA}\). \(\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC} = a_2b_1 - a_1b_2\). Also \(\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC} = |\vec{OB}||\vec{OC}|\cos(\angle BOC) = ab\cos(\angle BOC)\). In both cases, \(|\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC}| = ab\sin\theta = 2 \times \text{Area}\). Thus Area \(= \frac{1}{2}|a_2b_1 - a_1b_2| = \frac{1}{2}|a_1b_2 - a_2b_1|\).

(c)(ii) \(\vec{OP} = \begin{pmatrix} r + r\cos t \\ r\sin t \end{pmatrix}\), \(\vec{OQ} = \begin{pmatrix} -R + R\cos 2t \\ R\sin 2t \end{pmatrix}\). Area \(= \frac{1}{2}|(r + r\cos t)(R\sin 2t) - (r\sin t)(-R + R\cos 2t)| = \frac{1}{2}Rr|\sin 2t + 2\sin t|\). Let \(f(t) = \sin 2t + 2\sin t\). \(f'(t) = 2\cos 2t + 2\cos t = 2(2\cos^2 t + \cos t - 1) = 2(2\cos t - 1)(\cos t + 1)\). \(f'(t) = 0 \implies \cos t = \frac{1}{2}\) (since \(\cos t + 1 \ge 0\)). This gives \(t = \pm \frac{\pi}{3}\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) provide clear explanations, express answers clearly and unambiguously using appropriate mathematical notations.
(a) (ii) correctly use the chain rule to find an expression for the gradient of the inverse function, relate the tangents to corresponding points on a function and its inverse function.
(b) (i) substitute and use the correct algebraic techniques to arrive at the given formula.

(b) (ii) correctly interpret information given in graphical form, understand that the question required locating a double root, recognise from the graph the constraints on the variables \(x\) and \(c\).

(c) (i) understand the significance of the given property and use it to arrive at the required proof, demonstrate skills in working with vectors and arrive at the required result using a variety of equivalent proofs.
(c) (ii) make effective use of the information in part (i) to facilitate the solution of the problem, use a combination of skills in trigonometry, calculus, and vectors to solve the problem.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) knowing what the term ‘explain’ means in the context of the problem, using proper mathematical terms.

(a) (ii) relating the coordinates of a point on a function to the coordinates of a corresponding point on the inverse of that function, finding derivatives of inverse functions using algebraic methods as well as the geometrical approach.

(b) (i) showing full working before arriving at the required equation, performing algebraic manipulations with directional signs correctly, realising that finding points of intersection involves solving simultaneous equations.

(b) (ii) knowing how and when to use the concept of multiple roots in polynomials, demonstrating how the interaction of geometric shapes can be depicted on a graph, estimating graphically the values of variables to meet specific conditions.

(c) (i) constructing and clearly labelling diagrams with all the relevant information, using correct vector notation, knowing that in ‘show that...’ questions the last line should be what they are asked to show, knowing how to find the projection of a vector and its application in unfamiliar situations.

(c) (ii) writing down all they know about the question in the hope that may trigger some key thought processes that they may earlier not have realised, improving problem-solving skills by practising more complex questions in vector applications, knowing how the area and its rate of changes, varies with the angle \(t\).

questions.title: Expansion of a complex number cubed
Converse of a logical statement
questions.promptLatex:
Consider the following statement.

'If an animal is a herbivore, then it does not eat meat.'

Which of the following is the converse of this statement?

A. If an animal is a herbivore, then it eats meat.
B. If an animal is not a herbivore, then it eats meat.
C. If an animal eats meat, then it is not a herbivore.
D. If an animal does not eat meat, then it is a herbivore.

questions.title: Complex number on unit circle
questions.promptLatex:
A complex number \(z\) lies on the unit circle in the complex plane, as shown in the diagram.

Which of the following complex numbers is equal to \(\bar{z}\)?

A. \(-z\)
B. \(z^2\)
C. \(-z^3\)
D. \(z^4\)

questions.title: Formal logical notation of a statement
questions.promptLatex:
Consider the following statement about real numbers.

'Whichever positive number \(r\) you pick, it is possible to find a number \(x\) greater than 1 such that \(\frac{\ln x}{x^3} < r\).'

When this statement is written in the formal language of proof, which of the following is obtained?

A. \(\forall x > 1  \exists r > 0  \frac{\ln x}{x^3} < r\)

B. \(\exists x > 1  \forall r > 0  \frac{\ln x}{x^3} < r\)

C. \(\forall r > 0  \exists x > 1  \frac{\ln x}{x^3} < r\)

D. \(\exists r > 0  \forall x > 1  \frac{\ln x}{x^3} < r\)

questions.title: Relationship between two lines in 3D
questions.promptLatex:
Which of the following is a true statement about the lines \(\ell_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\ell_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -10 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \mu \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\)?

A. \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) are the same line.
B. \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) are not parallel and they intersect.
C. \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) are parallel and they do not intersect.
D. \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) are not parallel and they do not intersect.

questions.title: Functions of simple harmonic motion
questions.promptLatex:
Which of the following functions does NOT describe simple harmonic motion?

A. \(x = \cos^2 t - \sin 2t\)
B. \(x = \sin 4t + 4 \cos 2t\)
C. \(x = 2 \sin 3t - 4 \cos 3t + 5\)
D. \(x = 4 \cos \left( 2t + \frac{\pi}{2} \right) + 5 \sin \left( 2t - \frac{\pi}{4} \right)\)

questions.title: Properties of complex numbers
questions.promptLatex:
Which of the following statements about complex numbers is true?

A. For all real numbers \(x, y, \theta\) with \(x \ne 0\),
\[ \tan \theta = \frac{y}{x} \implies x + iy = re^{i\theta}, \]
for some real number \(r\).

B. For all non-zero complex numbers \(z_1\) and \(z_2\),
\[ \text{Arg}(z_1) = \theta_1 \text{ and } \text{Arg}(z_2) = \theta_2 \implies \text{Arg}(z_1z_2) = \theta_1 + \theta_2, \]
where \(\text{Arg}\) denotes the principal argument.

C. For all real numbers \(r_1, r_2, \theta_1, \theta_2\) with \(r_1, r_2 > 0\),
\[ r_1e^{i\theta_1} = r_2e^{i\theta_2} \implies r_1 = r_2 \text{ and } \theta_1 = \theta_2. \]

D. For all real numbers \(x, y, r, \theta\) with \(r > 0\) and \(x \ne 0\),
\[ x + iy = re^{i\theta} \implies \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right). \]

questions.title: Particle motion and vector dot products
questions.promptLatex:
A particle travels along a curve from \(O\) to \(E\) in the \(xy\)-plane, as shown in the diagram.

The position vector of the particle is \(\boldsymbol{r}\), its velocity is \(\boldsymbol{v}\), and its acceleration is \(\boldsymbol{a}\).

While travelling from \(O\) to \(E\), the particle is always slowing down.

Which of the following is consistent with the motion of the particle?

A. \(\boldsymbol{r} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \le 0\) and \(\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \ge 0\)
B. \(\boldsymbol{r} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \le 0\) and \(\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \le 0\)
C. \(\boldsymbol{r} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \ge 0\) and \(\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \ge 0\)
D. \(\boldsymbol{r} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \ge 0\) and \(\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{v} \le 0\)

questions.title: Properties of dot products of three vectors
questions.promptLatex:
Consider any three-dimensional vectors \(\underset{\sim}{a} = \vec{OA}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b} = \vec{OB}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c} = \vec{OC}\) that satisfy these three conditions
\[ \underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = 1 \]
\[ \underset{\sim}{b} \cdot \underset{\sim}{c} = 2 \]
\[ \underset{\sim}{c} \cdot \underset{\sim}{a} = 3 \]

Which of the following statements about the vectors is true?

A. Two of \(\underset{\sim}{a}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c}\) could be unit vectors.
B. The points \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) could lie on a sphere centred at \(O\).
C. For any three-dimensional vector \(\underset{\sim}{a}\), vectors \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c}\) can be found so that \(\underset{\sim}{a}\), \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c}\) satisfy these three conditions.
D. \(\forall \underset{\sim}{a}, \underset{\sim}{b}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{c}\) satisfying the conditions, \(\exists r, s\) and \(t\) such that \(r, s\) and \(t\) are positive real numbers and \(r\underset{\sim}{a} + s\underset{\sim}{b} + t\underset{\sim}{c} = \underset{\sim}{0}\).

questions.title: Complex numbers, vectors, and calculus
questions.subtopic: Complex numbers, vectors, mechanics, and integration
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Solve the quadratic equation
\[ z^2 - 3z + 4 = 0, \]
where \( z \) is a complex number. Give your answers in Cartesian form. (2 marks)

(b) Find the angle between the vectors
\[ \underset{\sim}{a} = \underset{\sim}{i} + 2\underset{\sim}{j} - 3\underset{\sim}{k} \]
\[ \underset{\sim}{b} = -\underset{\sim}{i} + 4\underset{\sim}{j} + 2\underset{\sim}{k}, \]
giving your answer to the nearest degree. (3 marks)

(c) Find a vector equation of the line through the points \( A(-3, 1, 5) \) and \( B(0, 2, 3) \). (2 marks)

(d) The quadrilaterals \( ABCD \) and \( ABEF \) are parallelograms.
By considering \( \vec{AB} \), show that \( CDFE \) is also a parallelogram. (2 marks)

(e) A particle moves in simple harmonic motion described by the equation
\[ \ddot{x} = -9(x - 4). \]
Find the period and the central point of motion. (2 marks)

(f) Find \( \int_{0}^{2} \frac{5x - 3}{(x + 1)(x - 3)} dx \). (4 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \( z = \frac{3}{2} \pm \frac{\sqrt{7}}{2}i \) (b) \( \theta \approx 87^\circ \) (c) \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}, \lambda \in \mathbb{R} \) (d) Proof (e) Period \( = \frac{2\pi}{3} \), centre \( x = 4 \) (f) \( -\ln 3 \) or \( \ln \left( \frac{1}{3} \right) \)
workingLatex: (d) \( \overline{AB} = \overline{DC} \) (opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal in length and parallel). In parallelogram \( ABEF \), \( \overline{AB} = \overline{FE} \). Therefore \( \overline{DC} = \overline{FE} \), so \( CDFE \) is a parallelogram.

betterResponses: (a) use the quadratic formula appropriately, write the answer in Cartesian form.
(b) correctly calculate the scalar product, use DEG mode on the calculator, round to the nearest degree accurately.
(c) find the direction vector, showing working, write the required vector in an acceptable form, understand that there were several acceptable answers.
(d) draw an accurate diagram using the given information, use vectors to answer the question succinctly, argue the proof using both lengths of sides and parallel sides.
(e) determine the value of \(n\) and the period \(T\).
(f) correctly use partial fractions and determine constants, correctly integrate partial fractions, simplify the definite integral.
improvementAreas: (a) simplifying the discriminant, checking numbers/formula to avoid transcription errors.
(b) taking care when calculating the scalar product.
(c) taking care to not transcribe incorrect numbers from their final answer, determining the direction vector.
(d) drawing and labelling diagrams using correct cyclic naming, using vectors to make the proof easier, using clear and logical statements throughout the proof.
(e) understanding when acceleration is zero, \(x\) is the centre of motion, learning the links between \(\ddot{x} = -n^2(x-c)\) and the centre and period of motion.
(f) integrating \(\frac{2}{x+1}\) to obtain \(2\ln|x+1|\), and know that \(\ln|-1| = \ln(1) = 0\), integrating \(\frac{3}{x-3}\) to obtain \(3\ln|x-3|\), and know that \(\ln|-3| = \ln(3)\).

questions.title: Complex Numbers, Mechanics, and Proofs
questions.subtopic: Proof techniques, complex roots, and polynomials
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove that \(\sqrt{23}\) is irrational. (3 marks)

(b) Prove that for all real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), where \(x^2 + y^2 \ne 0\),
\[ \frac{(x + y)^2}{x^2 + y^2} \le 2. \] (2 marks)

(c) An object with mass \(m\) kilograms slides down a smooth inclined plane with velocity \(v(t)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after the object started sliding down the plane. The inclined plane makes an angle \(\theta\) with the horizontal, as shown in the diagram. The normal reaction force is \(R\). The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\) and has magnitude \(g\). No other forces act on the object.

The vectors \(i\) and \(j\) are unit vectors parallel and perpendicular, respectively, to the plane, as shown in the diagram.

(i) Show that the resultant force on the object is \(F = -(mg \sin \theta)i\). (2 marks)

(ii) Given that the object is initially at rest, find its velocity \(v(t)\) in terms of \(g\), \(\theta\), \(t\) and \(i\). (2 marks)

(d) Find the cube roots of \(2 - 2i\). Give your answer in exponential form. (3 marks)

(e) The complex number \(2 + i\) is a zero of the polynomial
\[ P(z) = z^4 - 3z^3 + cz^2 + dz - 30 \]
where \(c\) and \(d\) are real numbers.

(i) Explain why \(2 - i\) is also a zero of the polynomial \(P(z)\). (1 mark)

(ii) Find the remaining zeros of the polynomial \(P(z)\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Proof by contradiction showing \(\sqrt{23}\) is irrational. (b) Proof showing \(\frac{(x+y)^2}{x^2+y^2} \le 2\). (c)(i) \(\underset{\sim}{F} = -(mg \sin \theta) \underset{\sim}{i}\). (c)(ii) \(\underset{\sim}{v} = -gt \sin \theta \underset{\sim}{i}\). (d) \(z = \sqrt{2}e^{-\frac{i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{7i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{5i\pi}{4}}\). (e)(i) Since \(P(z)\) has real coefficients, zeros occur in complex conjugate pairs, so \(2-i\) is also a zero. (e)(ii) Other zeros are \(z=2\) and \(z=-3\).
workingLatex: (a) Assume the contradiction, that \(\sqrt{23}\) is rational. Let \(\sqrt{23} = \frac{p}{q}\) where \(p, q \in \mathbb{Z}\) and \(q \ne 0\). Assume \(p\) and \(q\) are coprime. Then \(23q^2 = p^2\). Since 23 is prime, 23 divides \(p\). Let \(p = 23m\), then \(q^2 = 23m^2\), so 23 divides \(q\). This contradicts the assumption that \(p, q\) are coprime.

(c)(i) Resolving parallel and perpendicular to the slope gives \(\underset{\sim}{R} - (mg \cos \theta)\underset{\sim}{j} = 0\) and \(\underset{\sim}{F} - (mg \sin \theta)\underset{\sim}{i} = 0\). Therefore \(\underset{\sim}{F} = -(mg \sin \theta)\underset{\sim}{i}\).

(c)(ii) \(ma = -mg \sin \theta \underset{\sim}{i} \implies a = -g \sin \theta \underset{\sim}{i}\). Integrating with respect to \(t\), \(\underset{\sim}{v} = -gt \sin \theta \underset{\sim}{i} + C\). Since \(\underset{\sim}{v} = 0\) at \(t=0\), \(\underset{\sim}{v} = -gt \sin \theta \underset{\sim}{i}\).

(d) Let \(z^3 = 2-2i = \sqrt{8}e^{-\frac{i\pi}{4}}\). Then \(r^3 = \sqrt{8} \implies r = \sqrt{2}\). The arguments are \(3\theta = -\frac{\pi}{4} + 2k\pi\), giving \(\theta = -\frac{\pi}{12}, \frac{7\pi}{12}, \frac{15\pi}{12}\). Thus \(z = \sqrt{2}e^{-\frac{i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{7i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{5i\pi}{4}}\).
(e)(i) Since \(P(z)\) has real coefficients, zeros occur in complex conjugate pairs. Thus \(2+i\) being a zero implies \(\overline{2+i} = 2-i\) is also a zero.

(e)(ii) Sum of roots \(= 3\), product of roots \(= -30\). Let other zeros be \(\alpha, \beta\). \((2+i)+(2-i)+\alpha+\beta = 3 \implies \alpha+\beta = -1\). \((2+i)(2-i)\alpha\beta = -30 \implies 5\alpha\beta = -30 \implies \alpha\beta = -6\). Solving gives \(z=2, z=-3\).

betterResponses: (a) assume by contradiction that \(\sqrt{23}\) is rational and let \(\sqrt{23} = \frac{p}{q}\), where \(p\) and \(q\) are integers with a highest common factor of 1, rearrange the equation to find \(p^2 = 23q^2\), state that 23 divides \(p^2\) and hence 23 divides \(p\), since 23 is prime, let \(p= 23m\) for \(m \in \mathbb{Z}\) and show that \(q^2 = 23m^2\), and so similarly 23 divides \(q\), state that this contradicts \(p\) and \(q\) having a highest common factor of 1 and so \(\sqrt{23}\) must be irrational.

(b) start with \((x-y)^2 \geq 0\), expand the LHS and add \(x^2 + y^2\) to both sides then rearrange successfully, start with a result known to be true and proceed until the proof is completed, choose an efficient method of proof.

(c) (i) use a diagram, show in algebra or in words why the resultant force is negative, has a magnitude of \(mg\sin\theta\), and why there is no net force in the \(\vec{j}\) direction, construct a diagram with \(\theta\) at the appropriate angle, with \(mg\) on the hypotenuse and \(-mg\sin\theta\) and \(-mg\cos\theta\) on the other sides, and with an arrow showing the normal reactive force, show that the net force is \(\vec{F} = \binom{-mg\sin\theta}{|\vec{R}|-mg\cos\theta}\) and state that \(|\vec{R}| = mg\cos\theta\).

(c) (ii) divide \(\vec{F}\) by \(m\) to find the acceleration vector and use integration to find the velocity vector, use definite or indefinite integrals to show that the constant of integration is zero, find \(v(t)\) as a vector in the \(\vec{i}\) direction rather than a scalar.

(d) convert \(2-2i\) into exponential form, find the modulus of the cube root by finding the cube root of the modulus, find three arguments for the cube roots by either dividing the argument by 3 then adding or subtracting multiples of \(\frac{2\pi}{3}\), or adding multiples of \(2\pi\) to the argument then dividing by 3, show the three cube roots in exponential form.
(e) (i) show that since all coefficients are real that the conjugate root theorem applies, show that \(2-i\) is the conjugate of \(2+i\) so must also be a zero.
(e) (ii) represent the two unknown roots as \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\), find the sum and product of the roots, find the values of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) by inspection.
improvementAreas: (a) defining any pronumerals they use in the proofs, stating all the successive implications used in reaching conclusions rather than skipping steps, stating the conclusion of the proof.

(b) understanding that when a proof starts with a result that is not yet known to be true a logical fallacy is created, understanding the significance of \(x\) and \(y\) being real rather than positive, especially when dividing an inequality by \(2xy\).
(c) (i) identifying the important components of a result to be proved and stating how each can be derived.

(c) (ii) reading the question carefully to find the answer using all pronumerals required in the question, using a definite integral to integrate both sides, or show that the LHS is \(\vec{v}-0\), to show that the constant of integration is zero, using an indefinite integral clearly to find the value of the constant \(c\) rather than assuming it is zero.
(d) finding the cube root of a surd, finding three appropriate arguments for the cube roots, ensuring that complex numbers in exponential form have \(i\) included in the exponent.
(e) (i) understanding that the conjugate root theorem only applies when the coefficients are real, stating that \(2-i\) is a zero because it is the conjugate of the zero given in the question.

(e) (ii) understanding that the two unknown roots could either both be real, or could be a pair of complex conjugates, writing the roots in the form \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) rather than \(a+ib\) and \(a-ib\).

questions.title: Integration, Induction, and Projectile Motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find \(\int \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} dx\). (3 marks)

(b) (i) Show that \(k^2-2k-3 \ge 0\) for \(k \ge 3\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, use mathematical induction to prove that \(2^n \ge n^2-2\), for all integers \(n \ge 3\). (3 marks)

(c) A particle of mass \(1 \text{ kg}\) is projected from the origin with speed \(40 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle \(30^\circ\) to the horizontal plane.

(i) Use the information above to show that the initial velocity of the particle is \(\mathbf{v}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3} \\ 20 \end{pmatrix}\). (1 mark)

The forces acting on the particle are gravity and air resistance. The air resistance is proportional to the velocity vector with a constant of proportionality \(4\). Let the acceleration due to gravity be \(10 \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

The position vector of the particle, at time \(t\) seconds after the particle is projected, is \(\mathbf{r}(t)\) and the velocity vector is \(\mathbf{v}(t)\).

(ii) Show that \(\mathbf{v}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3}e^{-4t} \\ \frac{45}{2}e^{-4t} - \frac{5}{2} \end{pmatrix}\). (3 marks)

(iii) Show that \(\mathbf{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 5\sqrt{3}(1-e^{-4t}) \\ \frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t}) - \frac{5}{2}t \end{pmatrix}\). (2 marks)

(iv) The graphs \(y = 1 - e^{-4x}\) and \(y = \frac{4x}{9}\) are given in the diagram below.

Using the diagram, find the horizontal range of the particle, giving your answer rounded to one decimal place. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (c)\((iv) \text{Set } y=0: \frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t}) - \frac{5}{2}t = 0. \text{ Solving } t \approx 2.25. \text{ Range } = x(2.25) = 5\sqrt{3}(1-e^{-4\times 2.25}) \approx 8.7 \text{ m}.\)

betterResponses: (a) complete the square in the denominator, split the linear numerator into correct algebraic parts, use the reference sheet to apply the reverse chain rule and standard integrals.
(b) (i) apply correct mathematical structures for proofs, factorise or complete the square to prove a result.
(b) (ii) state clearly the inductive step in the proof for \(n=k+1\), use the assumption to complete the inequality proof.
(c) (i) use appropriate mathematics to show the initial velocities.
(c) (ii) state the components of the force equations, use the appropriate time equations and definite integrals.
(c) (iii) separate variables and apply a definite integral.
(c) (iv) solve for \(y=0\) to obtain the time for finding the range, use the graphs provided in order to obtain the correct time value.
improvementAreas: (a) completing the square when quadratics have a negative coefficient of \(x^2\), applying the reverse chain rule as stated in the reference sheet.
(b) (i) identifying the difference between solving an inequation and proving a result, completing a proof for all values of a variable, not specific values.
(b) (ii) including the use of required to prove statements at all levels of the proof, connecting the result from part (i) in part (ii).
(c) (i) reasoning of trigonometric connections between angle and velocity at any point on a trajectory.
(c) (ii) understanding that air resistance applies to both components of the motion, using initial conditions to find constants of integration.
(c) (iii) using constants of integration.
(c) (iv) connecting horizontal and vertical components of displacement in order to find the range.

questions.title: Complex numbers, SHM, and projectile motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(z\) be the complex number \(z = e^{\frac{i\pi}{6}}\) and \(w\) be the complex number \(w = e^{\frac{3i\pi}{4}}\).

(i) By first writing \(z\) and \(w\) in Cartesian form, or otherwise, show that \(|z + w|^2 = \frac{4 - \sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{2}\). (3 marks)

(ii) The complex numbers \(z\), \(w\) and \(z + w\) are represented in the complex plane by the vectors \(\vec{OA}\), \(\vec{OB}\) and \(\vec{OC}\) respectively, where \(O\) is the origin. Show that \(\angle AOC = \frac{7\pi}{24}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Deduce that \(\cos \frac{7\pi}{24} = \frac{\sqrt{8 - 2\sqrt{6} + 2\sqrt{2}}}{4}\). (1 mark)

(b) The point \(P\) is 4 metres to the right of the origin \(O\) on a straight line.

A particle is released from rest at \(P\) and moves along the straight line in simple harmonic motion about \(O\), with period \(8\pi\) seconds.

After \(2\pi\) seconds, another particle is released from rest at \(P\) and also moves along this straight line in simple harmonic motion about \(O\), with period \(8\pi\) seconds.

Find when and where the two particles first collide. (3 marks)

(c) A projectile of mass \(M\) kg is launched vertically upwards from the origin with an initial speed \(v_0 \text{ m s}^{-1}\). The acceleration due to gravity is \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

The projectile experiences a resistive force of magnitude \(kMv^2\) newtons, where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(v\) is the speed of the projectile at time \(t\) seconds.

(i) The maximum height reached by the particle is \(H\) metres. Show that \(H = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{g} \right)\). (3 marks)

(ii) When the projectile lands on the ground, its speed is \(v_1 \text{ m s}^{-1}\), where \(v_1\) is less than the magnitude of the terminal velocity. Show that \(g(v_0^2 - v_1^2) = kv_0^2v_1^2\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) \frac{1}{2}[4-\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}] (a)(ii) \frac{7\pi}{24} (a)(iii) \frac{\sqrt{8-2\sqrt{6}+2\sqrt{2}}}{4} (b) \text{Collide } 2\sqrt{2} \text{ metres left of } 0 \text{ when } t=5\pi (c)(i) H = \frac{1}{2k}\ln\left(\frac{kv_0^2+g}{g}\right) (c)(ii) g(v_0^2-v_1^2) = kv_0^2v_1^2\)
workingLatex: \(OACB\) is a rhombus, so \(\angle AOC = \frac{1}{2}\angle AOB = \frac{7\pi}{24}\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) find the cartesian form for the complex numbers as the arguments gave exact values, apply the formula for \(|z+w|^2\), use algebraic techniques involving surds.
(a) (ii) apply geometry results involving the properties of a rhombus, realise that the addition of complex numbers yields a diagonal of a parallelogram.
(a) (iii) apply the cosine rule and part (i) to find the exact value of \(\cos\frac{7\pi}{24}\).

(b) draw a diagram showing the related positions of both particles, realise that the time of colliding \(t=5\pi\) could be obtained through symmetry, use trigonometric equations correctly to solve to find \(t=5\pi\).
(c) (i) calculate vertical resisted motion, apply separation of variable techniques and definite integrals to generate related equations of motion.
(c) (ii) establish two equivalent expressions for the height H in terms of the original velocity and final velocity, use the absolute values involved in log expressions.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) understanding of the modulus of complex numbers.
(a) (ii) considering geometric approaches for complex numbers, working mathematically with vectors.
(a) (iii) using a diagram to convey a message.
(b) generating the time equations for simple harmonic motion, applying the sine or cosine graph for certain starting conditions.
(c) (i) choosing the resultant force vector for upwards motion involving gravity and resistance.
(c) (ii) setting the downwards direction as positive for downwards motion, generating the correct direction for gravity and resistance for downwards motion.

questions.title: Calculus, Vectors, and Parametric Curves
questions.subtopic: Integration, Vector Geometry, and Parametric Modelling
questions.promptLatex:
(a) (i) Let \(J_n = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^n \theta \, d\theta\) where \(n \ge 0\) is an integer.

Show that \(J_n = \frac{n-1}{n} J_{n-2}\) for all integers \(n \ge 2\). (3 marks)

(ii) Let \(I_n = \int_{0}^{1} x^n (1-x)^n \, dx\) where \(n\) is a positive integer.

By using the substitution \(x = \sin^2 \theta\), or otherwise,

show that \(I_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1} \theta \, d\theta\). (4 marks)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, show that \(I_n = \frac{n}{4n+2} I_{n-1}\), for all integers \(n \ge 1\). (2 marks)

(b) On the triangular pyramid \(ABCD\), \(L\) is the midpoint of \(AB\), \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AC\), \(N\) is the midpoint of \(AD\), \(P\) is the midpoint of \(CD\), \(Q\) is the midpoint of \(BD\) and \(R\) is the midpoint of \(BC\).

Let \(\underset{\sim}{b} = \vec{AB}\), \(\underset{\sim}{c} = \vec{AC}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{d} = \vec{AD}\).

(i) Show that \(\vec{LP} = \frac{1}{2}(-\underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} + \underset{\sim}{d})\). (1 mark)

(ii) It can be shown that

\(\vec{MQ} = \frac{1}{2}(\underset{\sim}{b} - \underset{\sim}{c} + \underset{\sim}{d})\)

and \(\vec{NR} = \frac{1}{2}(\underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} - \underset{\sim}{d})\). (Do NOT prove these.)

Prove that

\(|\vec{AB}|^2 + |\vec{AC}|^2 + |\vec{AD}|^2 + |\vec{BC}|^2 + |\vec{BD}|^2 + |\vec{CD}|^2 = 4(|\vec{LP}|^2 + |\vec{MQ}|^2 + |\vec{NR}|^2)\). (3 marks)

(c) A curve \(\mathcal{C}\) spirals 3 times around the sphere centred at the origin and with radius 3, as shown.

A particle is initially at the point \((0, 0, -3)\) and moves along the curve \(\mathcal{C}\) on the surface of the sphere, ending at the point \((0, 0, 3)\).

By using the diagram below, which shows the graphs of the functions \(f(x) = \cos(\pi x)\) and \(g(x) = \sqrt{9 - x^2}\), and considering the graph \(y = f(x)g(x)\), give a possible set of parametric equations that describe the curve \(\mathcal{C}\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(J_n = \frac{n-1}{n}J_{n-2}\) (a)(ii) \(I_n = \frac{1}{2^n} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1} \theta \, d\theta\) (a)(iii) \(I_n = \frac{n}{4n+2} I_{n-1}\) (b)(i) \(\overline{LP} = \frac{1}{2}(-\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{d})\) (b)(ii) Proof (c) \(x = \cos(\pi t)\sqrt{9-t^2}\), \(y = -\sin(\pi t)\sqrt{9-t^2}\), \(z = t\)
workingLatex: (a)(i) \(J_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^n \theta \, d\theta = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin \theta \sin^{n-1} \theta \, d\theta = [-\cos \theta \sin^{n-1} \theta]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} - \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} (-\cos \theta)(n-1)\sin^{n-2} \theta \cos \theta \, d\theta = 0 - 0 + (n-1)\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos^2 \theta \sin^{n-2} \theta \, d\theta = (n-1)\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} (1-\sin^2 \theta)\sin^{n-2} \theta \, d\theta = (n-1)J_{n-2} - (n-1)J_n \implies nJ_n = (n-1)J_{n-2} \implies J_n = \frac{n-1}{n}J_{n-2}\)

(a)(ii) \(I_n = \int_0^1 x^n(1-x)^n \, dx\). Let \(x = \sin^2 \theta\), \(dx = \sin 2\theta \, d\theta\). \(I_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n} \theta \cos^{2n} \theta \sin 2\theta \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) \, d\theta\). Let \(\phi = 2\theta\), \(d\phi = 2d\theta\). \(I_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n+1}} \int_0^{\pi} \sin^{2n+1} \phi \, d\phi = \frac{2}{2^{2n+1}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1} \phi \, d\phi = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1} \theta \, d\theta\)
(c) Projecting onto \(xz\)-plane gives \(x = \cos(\pi t)\sqrt{9-t^2}\) and \(z=t\). Projecting onto \(yz\)-plane gives \(y = -\sin(\pi t)\sqrt{9-t^2}\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) use integration by parts with \(\sin^{n-1}\theta \times \sin\theta\), rewrite the result in terms of \(J_n\) and \(J_{n-2}\) and then rearrange to prove the result.

(a) (ii) use the given substitution and trigonometric identities to find the integrand in terms of \(2\theta\), use the substitution \(u=2\theta\) to find the integral from 0 to \(\pi\) in terms of \(u\), use the symmetry of \(\sin^n u\) around \(u=\frac{\pi}{2}\) to show that it is twice the integral from 0 to \(\frac{\pi}{2}\), then show that since the integral is independent of the variable used the result is proved.

(b) (i) use the vector form \(\vec{XY}\) to clearly show \(\vec{LL}\) as the sum or difference of vectors, convert their expression into vectors in terms of \(\vec{b}\), \(\vec{c}\) and \(\vec{d}\), simplify their expression in logical steps to achieve the result to be proved.

(b)(i) convert their expression into vectors in terms of \(\tilde{b}\), \(\tilde{c}\) and \(\tilde{d}\)
(b)(i) simplify their expression in logical steps to achieve the result to be proved
(b)(ii) use scalar products to correctly expand and simplify either the LHS or RHS
(b)(ii) correctly expand and simplify the other side and show they are equal or rearrange the expression until it is equal to the other side
(c) realise that \(z\) increases in a linear manner and let \(z=t\)
(c) clearly draw the graph of \(y=f(x)g(x)\) or consider \(\sqrt{9-t^2} \cos(\pi t)\)
(c) find the relevance to the parametric equations of \(x\) and \(y\), taking careful note of the starting and end points of the spiral, plus the direction in which it travels
(c) find possible parametric equations

(a) (ii) differentiating \(\sin^2\theta\) when integrating by parts, converting integrands from \(\sin\theta\cos\theta\) to \(\sin 2\theta\), operating with exponents in the form \(an+b\), considering second substitutions and symmetry when working with harder integrals.

questions.title: Complex numbers, inequalities, and geometry
questions.subtopic: Geometry of complex numbers and mathematical proof
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(w\) be the complex number \(w = e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}}\).

(i) Show that \(1 + w + w^2 = 0\). (2 marks)

The vertices of a triangle can be labelled \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) in anticlockwise or clockwise direction, as shown.

(ii) Show that if triangle \(ABC\) is anticlockwise and equilateral, then \(a + bw + cw^2 = 0\). (2 marks)

(iii) It can be shown that if triangle \(ABC\) is clockwise and equilateral, then \(a + bw^2 + cw = 0\). (Do NOT prove this.)

Show that if \(ABC\) is an equilateral triangle, then \[a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = ab + bc + ca.\] (2 marks)

(b) (i) Prove that \(x > \ln x\), for \(x > 0\). (2 marks)

(ii) Using part (i), or otherwise, prove that for all positive integers \(n\), \[e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2.\] (3 marks)

(c) The complex numbers \(w\) and \(z\) both have modulus \(1\), and \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}\left(\frac{z}{w}\right) < \pi\), where \(\text{Arg}\) denotes the principal argument.

For real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), consider the complex number \(\frac{xz + yw}{z}\).

On an \(xy\)-plane, clearly sketch the region that contains all points \((x, y)\) for which \[\frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}\left(\frac{xz + yw}{z}\right) < \pi.\] (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(1+w+w^2=0\) (a)(ii) \(a+bw+w^2c=0\) (a)(iii) \(a^2+b^2+c^2=ab+bc+ac\) (b)(i) \(x>\ln x\) (b)(ii) \(e^{n^2+n}>(n!)^2\) (c) Region in the complex plane defined by \(y>-\sec(\theta)x\) and \(y<0\)
workingLatex: (a)(ii) Rotating \(\overline{AC}\) by \(\frac{2\pi}{3}\) brings it onto \(\overline{BA}\) so \(w(c-a) = a-b\). Then \(a(1+w)-b-wc=0\), which implies \(-aw^2-b-wc=0\) (by part (i)), and thus \(a+bw+w^2c=0\) (multiplying by \(-w\)).

(a)(iii) Suppose \(ABC\) is equilateral. It is either anticlockwise or clockwise, so \(a+bw+w^2c=0\) or \(a+bw^2+cw=0\). This is equivalent to \((a+bw+cw^2)(a+bw^2+cw)=0\). Expanding gives \(a^2+bw^3+cw^3+ab(w^2+w)+bc(w^2+w^4)+ac(w+w^2)=0\). Since \(w^3=1\) and \(w+w^2=-1\), this simplifies to \(a^2+b^2+c^2-(ab+bc+ac)=0\), so \(a^2+b^2+c^2=ab+bc+ac\).

(b)(i) Let \(f(x) = x-\ln x\) for \(x>0\). \(f'(x) = 1-\frac{1}{x}\). For \(x>1\), \(f'(x)>0\) and for \(0<x<1\), \(f'(x)<0\). The minimum is at \(f(1)=1\). Thus \(f(x)\ge 1\) for all \(x>0\), so \(x-\ln x \ge 1 > 0\), which implies \(x>\ln x\).

(b)(ii) Assume \(n\) is a positive integer. By part (i), \(n>\ln n, n-1>\ln(n-1), \dots, 1>\ln 1\). Adding these inequalities gives \(1+2+\dots+n > \ln(n!) + \ln((n-1)!) + \dots + \ln 1\), which is \(\frac{n(n+1)}{2} > \ln(n!)\). Thus \(n^2+n > 2\ln(n!) = \ln((n!)^2)\). Taking the exponential of both sides gives \(e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2\).

(c) Let \(\theta = \text{Arg}\left(\frac{z}{w}\right)\), then \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi\). As \(\left|\frac{z}{w}\right| = \left|\frac{w}{z}\right| = 1\), \(\text{Arg}\left(\frac{w}{z}\right) = -\theta\). The expression is \(x+y\frac{w}{z}\). For the argument to be in the second quadrant, the real part must be negative: \(x+y\cos(-\theta) < 0\). Since \(\cos\) is even, \(x < -\cos(\theta)y\). Given \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi\), \(-1 < \cos(\theta) < 0\), so \(0 < -\cos(\theta) < 1\). This leads to \(y > -\sec(\theta)x\) and \(y < 0\).

betterResponses: (a)(i) use the factorisation of the difference of two cubes
(a)(i) substitute \(e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}}\) and simplify accurately
(a)(ii) associate AC as a rotation of AB, that is \(\vec{AC} = e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}} \times \vec{AB}\)
(a)(ii) use the result from part (i), as \(1 + w = -w^2\)
(a)(ii) use vector notation correctly, \((c-a) = e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}} \times (b-a)\)
(a)(iii) identify that the product of the two expressions given in parts (i) and (ii) could possibly provide a solution if expanded and simplified using the result from part (i)
(b)(i) prove the given result using meticulous detail and not omit any cases

(b)(ii) recognising that multiplying exponentials leads to a sum of 1 to \(n\)

Electricity provider \(A\) charges 25 cents per kilowatt hour (kWh) for electricity, plus a fixed monthly charge of $40.

| Electricity used in a month (kWh) | 0 | 400 | 1000 |

questions.answerLatex: (a) Electricity used: 0, 400, 1000; Monthly charge: 40, 140, 290. (b) A line starting at (0, 40) passing through (400, 140) and (1000, 290). (c) They charge the same amount at 400 kWh. (d) Provider A is cheaper by $40.

(c) The lines intersect at \(400\) kWh, meaning they charge the same amount at this usage.
(d) Provider \(B\) at \(800\) kWh charges \(\$280\). Provider \(A\) at \(800\) kWh charges \(\$240\). \(\therefore\) Provider \(A\) would be the cheaper option by \(\$40\).

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| *Interest rate* (per annum) | *Term of loan* (years) | | | | | |
| | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
| 6.5\% | 19.57 | 11.35 | 8.71 | 7.46 | 6.75 | 6.32 |
| 7.0\% | 19.80 | 11.61 | 8.99 | 7.75 | 7.07 | 6.65 |
| 7.5\% | 20.04 | 11.87 | 9.27 | 8.06 | 7.39 | 6.99 |
| 8.0\% | 20.28 | 12.13 | 9.56 | 8.36 | 7.72 | 7.34 |

(a) A couple borrows \$520\,000 to buy a house at 8\% per annum over 25 years. (3 marks)

How much does the couple repay in total for this loan?

(b) Chris borrows some money at 7\% per annum. Chris will repay the loan over 15 years, paying \$3596 per month. (1 mark)

betterResponses: recognise the table interest factors were ‘per $1000’ borrowed and accordingly divided $520 000 by $1000
(b) understand the amount being repaid per month needed to be divided by 8.99 (not multiplied by 8.99) and multiply the result by 1000 (not by 12 × 15 = 180 months)
improvementAreas: (a) knowing monthly repayments must be multiplied by 12 then the number of years (12 × 25 ) to get correct final answer
not subtracting the original amount borrowed at the end of the question because this calculates the amount of interest paid rather than the total repaid for the loan
interpreting what the table values are representing.

questions.title: Critical path analysis of tasks
questions.promptLatex:
A function centre employs staff so that all necessary tasks can be completed between the end of one function and the beginning of the next function.

The network diagram shows the time taken in hours for the tasks that need to be completed.

(a) Find the TWO critical paths. (2 marks)

(b) The function centre wants to decrease the length of each critical path by 3 hours. They can do this by hiring more staff to do ONE of the tasks so it takes less time to complete.

For which task should the centre hire more staff, and how long should that task take to ensure all tasks can be completed in 14 hours? (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Critical paths are \(H, I, G, C\) and \(H, I, K\). (b) Decrease one task on the critical path by 3 hours; since \(H\) is only 2 hours, task \(I\) should be reduced by 3 hours (or equivalent).
workingLatex: (a) Critical paths are \(H, I, G, C\) and \(H, I, K\).
(b) \(H\) and \(I\) are common to both paths.
(b) \(H\) is only 2 hours, so task \(I\) should be reduced by 3 hours.

betterResponses: find the critical paths
draw the route on the diagram with a highlighter
use forward and backward scanning to determine the earliest starting time (EST) and latest starting time (LST) for each activity and use boxes on the network to clearly show this
understand that if a question states that there are two critical paths then there will be two paths of the same length
name the task, from part (a) and give the time with reasoning
identify the need to reduce an activity by 3 means the activity had to be greater than 3.
improvementAreas: recalling how to find the EST and LST
understanding when to use minimum cut or spanning tree or LST / EST
understanding the task needed to be greater than 3.

criteria.criterion: Identifies a critical path, or equivalent merit
Shortens a task on the critical path by 3 hours, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Least-squares regression line analysis
questions.promptLatex:
A university uses gas to heat its buildings. Over a period of 10 weekdays during winter, the gas used each day was measured in megawatts (MW) and the average outside temperature each day was recorded in degrees Celsius (\(^\circ\text{C}\)).

Using \(x\) as the average daily outside temperature and \(y\) as the total daily gas usage, the equation of the least-squares regression line was found.

The equation of the regression line predicts that when the temperature is \(0^\circ\text{C}\), the daily gas usage is \(236 \text{ MW}\).

The ten temperatures measured were: \(0^\circ, 0^\circ, 0^\circ, 2^\circ, 5^\circ, 7^\circ, 8^\circ, 9^\circ, 9^\circ, 10^\circ\).

The total gas usage for the ten weekdays was \(1840 \text{ MW}\).

In any bivariate dataset, the least-squares regression line passes through the point \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\), where \(\bar{x}\) is the sample mean of the \(x\)-values and \(\bar{y}\) is the sample mean of the \(y\)-values.

(a) Using the information provided, plot the point \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\) and the \(y\)-intercept of the least-squares regression line on the grid. (3 marks)

(b) What is the equation of the regression line? (2 marks)

(c) In the context of the dataset, identify ONE problem with using the regression line to predict gas usage when the average outside temperature is \(23^\circ\text{C}\). (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\bar{x} = 5, \bar{y} = 184\), point plotted at \((5, 184)\). (b) \(y = 236 - 10.4x\). (c) Extrapolation leads to physically impossible results (e.g., negative gas usage).
workingLatex: (c) \(\text{When temperature is } 23^\circ\text{C, the regression equation provides a negative answer, which is not physically possible (negative gas usage).}\)

betterResponses: (a) calculate both means showing all calculations
(a) accurately plot the coordinates of the means and y-intercept
(a) use correct mathematical notation.
(b) calculate the gradient using \(\frac{rise}{run}\)
(b) recognise the y-intercept
(b) correctly substitute into \(y = mx+ c\).
(b) correctly substitute into \(y = mx + c\).
(c) comment on the prediction of gas usage being negative, which is not possible
(c) recognise and explain that using extrapolation is inaccurate.
improvementAreas: (a) reading the question carefully to determine the means for each quantity
(a) accurately plotting coordinates on the intersection of the grid lines
(a) recognising that the initial quantity is the y-intercept
(a) reading the grid scale correctly.
(b) writing an equation not an expression
(b) not interpreting the data as bivariate and attempting to use the calculator to find the equation of the regression line
(b) using the two coordinates from their graph to find the gradient of the line and not reading two inaccurate coordinates off their regression line.
(c) relating their answer to the context of the question, that is, gas usage and temperatures
(c) providing an accurate explanation relevant to the regression line.

criteria.criterion: Correctly plots both points on the graph
Calculates \(\bar{x}\) and \(\bar{y}\), and plots this point on the grid, or equivalent merit
Finds the slope of the regression line, or equivalent merit
Identifies one problem with predicting using the regression line
