questions.title: Inequalities, Number Sequences, and Projectile Motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) For all non-negative real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), \(\sqrt{xy} \le \frac{x + y}{2}\). (Do NOT prove this.)

(i) Using this fact, show that for all non-negative real numbers \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\), \(\sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2 + b^2 + 2c}{4}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Using part (i), or otherwise, show that for all non-negative real numbers \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\), \(\sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + a + b + c}{6}\). (2 marks)

(b) For integers \(n \ge 1\), the triangular numbers \(t_n\) are defined by \(t_n = \frac{n(n + 1)}{2}\), giving \(t_1 = 1\), \(t_2 = 3\), \(t_3 = 6\), \(t_4 = 10\) and so on.

For integers \(n \ge 1\), the hexagonal numbers \(h_n\) are defined by \(h_n = 2n^2 - n\), giving \(h_1 = 1\), \(h_2 = 6\), \(h_3 = 15\), \(h_4 = 28\) and so on.

(i) Show that the triangular numbers \(t_1\), \(t_3\), \(t_5\), and so on, are also hexagonal numbers. (2 marks)

(ii) Show that the triangular numbers \(t_2\), \(t_4\), \(t_6\), and so on, are not hexagonal numbers. (1 mark)

(c) An object of mass \(1 \text{ kg}\) is projected vertically upwards with an initial velocity of \(u \text{ m/s}\). It experiences air resistance of magnitude \(kv^2 \text{ newtons}\) where \(v\) is the velocity of the object, in \(\text{m/s}\), and \(k\) is a positive constant. The height of the object above its starting point is \(x \text{ metres}\). The time since projection is \(t \text{ seconds}\) and acceleration due to gravity is \(g \text{ m/s}^2\).

(i) Show that the time for the object to reach its maximum height is \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \arctan\left(u \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}}\right) \text{ seconds}\). (3 marks)

(ii) Find an expression for the maximum height reached by the object, in terms of \(k\), \(g\) and \(u\). (3 marks)

(d) Prove that \(2^n + 3^n \ne 5^n\) for all integers \(n \ge 2\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2+b^2+2c}{4} (a)(ii) \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2+b^2+c^2+a+b+c}{6} (b)(i) t_{2m-1} = h_m (b)(ii) \text{The original statement is false} (c)(i) t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( u \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right) (c)(ii) x = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{g+ku^2}{g} \right) (d) 2^n + 3^n \ne 5^n \text{ for } n \ge 2\)
workingLatex: (b)\((ii) t_{2m} = \frac{2m(2m+1)}{2} = 2m^2+m. \text{ Assume } 2m^2+m = 2k^2-k \implies m+k = 2(k^2-m^2) = 2(k-m)(k+m) \implies 1 = 2(k-m), \text{ which is impossible as 1 is odd.}\)

(c)\((i) ma = -mg-kv^2 \implies \frac{dv}{dt} = -g-kv^2 \implies \int dt = \int \frac{-dv}{g+kv^2} \implies t = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( v \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right) + c. \text{ At } t=0, v=u, c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( u \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right). \text{ At } v=0, t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( u \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right)\)

(c)\((ii) v \frac{dv}{dx} = -(g+kv^2) \implies \int dx = \int \frac{-v dv}{g+kv^2} \implies x = -\frac{1}{2k} \ln(g+kv^2) + c. \text{ At } x=0, v=u, c = \frac{1}{2k} \ln(g+ku^2). \text{ At } v=0, x = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{g+ku^2}{g} \right)\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) know what substitutions for \(x\) and \(y\) would be most beneficial, for example \(x=\frac{a^2+b^2}{2}\) and \(y= c\) or similar.
(a) (ii) use the extended version; \(\sqrt[6]{abcdef} \leq \frac{a+b+c+d+e+f}{6}\)
(a) (ii) use the result from part (i) recognising that \(\sqrt{abc} \leq \frac{a^2+c^2+2b}{4}\) and \(\sqrt{abc} \leq \frac{b^2+c^2+2a}{4}\) are also true.

(b) (i) substitute \(n= 2k-1\) or \(n= 2k+ 1\) into \(t_n\), students using \(n= 2k-1\) were more successful as it simplified easily. Those substituting \(n= 2k+ 1\) had an extra step to show that \(t_{2k+1} = 2k^2 + 3k+ 1 = 2(k+ 1)^2 -(k+ 1) = h_{k+1}.\)
(b) (ii) prove that \(t_{2k}= h_n\) was only possible if one solution was negative and the other was a positive fraction
(b) (ii) show from part (i) that the odd indexed triangular numbers covered all hexagonal numbers and so there are no hexagonal numbers that map to the even indexed triangular numbers.
(c) (i) use a definite integral approach, which was generally quicker and more successful
(c) (i) reverse the limits of integration to eliminate the negative from the equation
(c) (i) use the limit method or substituted \(v= 0\) to find the given equation for \(t\).
(c) (ii) integrate using \(v \frac{dv}{dx}\) using definite or indefinite integrals with definite integrals providing the solution quicker and easier
(c) (ii) draw a force diagram and indicate the positive direction to be used.
(d) state that \(5^n= (2 + 3)^n\) then use a binomial expansion and noting the middle terms in the expansion are all positive, so \(2^n+ 3^n \neq 5^n\)
(d) successfully show that \(2^n+ 3^n < 5^n\) using mathematical induction, and so \(2^n+ 3^n \neq 5^n\)

(d) use contradiction, showing that \(5^n-3^n= (5 -3)(5^{n-1} + 5^{n-2}3 + 5^{n-3}3^2 + \dots+ 3^{n-1})\) which gives a statement like \(2^{n-1} = 5^{n-1} + 5^{n-2}3 + 5^{n-3}3^2 + \dots+ 3^{n-1}\), for \(n \geq 2\).
improvementAreas: (a) (i) showing all steps in the proof, using the given inequality as required
(a) (i) using the relationship between the arithmetic mean and geometric mean in its many forms.
(a) (ii) using part (i) to assist in understanding and solving this part
(a) (ii) knowing the different forms of the relationship between the arithmetic mean and geometric mean.
(b) (i) understanding that proving for all odd values of \(n\) does not mean simply trying a few odd numbers, like the ones given in the question
(b) (i) reducing simple algebraic errors by writing every step of the proof and not skipping steps.
(b) (ii) understanding how to disprove statements and that providing counter-examples does not disprove a statement for all even \(n\)
(b) (ii) showing every step to avoid careless and costly arithmetic and algebraic errors.
(c) (i) drawing a force diagram and determining a correct equation of motion
(c) (i) knowing what is required as a result, \(t\) or \(x\), which then determines the use of \(\frac{dv}{dt}\) or \(v \frac{dv}{dx}\).

(c) (ii) taking care with the algebra and not losing constants

questions.title: Vectors, Projectile Motion, and Complex Regions
questions.subtopic: Vector geometry, projectile motion, and complex number regions
questions.promptLatex:
(a) (i) The point \(P(x, y, z)\) lies on the sphere of radius 1 centred at the origin \(O\).

Using the position vector of \(P\), \(\vec{OP} = xi + yj + zk\), and the triangle inequality, or otherwise, show that \(|x| + |y| + |z| \ge 1\). (2 marks)

(ii) Given the vectors \(\underline{a} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\underline{b} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{pmatrix}\), show that \(|a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \le \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2}\). (3 marks)

(iii) As in part (i), the point \(P(x, y, z)\) lies on the sphere of radius 1 centred at the origin \(O\).

Using part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(|x| + |y| + |z| \le \sqrt{3}\). (2 marks)

(b) A particle which is projected from the origin with initial speed \(u \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle of \(\theta\) to the positive \(x\)-axis lands on the \(x\)-axis, as shown in the diagram. The particle is subject to an acceleration due to gravity of \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

The position vector of the particle, \(\underset{\sim}{r}(t)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after the particle is projected, is given by

\[ \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ -\frac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta \end{pmatrix}.  (\text{Do NOT prove this.}) \]

For some value(s) of \(\theta\) there will be two times during the time of flight when the particle's position vector is perpendicular to its velocity vector.

Find the value(s) of \(\theta\) for which this occurs, justifying that both times occur during the time of flight. (5 marks)

(c) Sketch the region of the complex plane defined by \(\text{Re}(z) \ge \text{Arg}(z)\) where \(\text{Arg}(z)\) is the principal argument of \(z\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) (i) \(|x| + |y| + |z| \ge 1\) (ii) \(|a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \le \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2}\) (iii) \(|x| + |y| + |z| \le \sqrt{3}\) (b) \(\theta > 1.23\) (c) Region bounded by \(y = x \tan x\) in the Argand plane
workingLatex: (a) (i) Point \(P\) is on the unit sphere so \(1 = |\vec{OP}| = |x\underset{\sim}{i} + y\underset{\sim}{j} + z\underset{\sim}{k}| \le |x\underset{\sim}{i}| + |y\underset{\sim}{j}| + |z\underset{\sim}{k}| = |x| + |y| + |z|\), so \(|x| + |y| + |z| \ge 1\).

(a) (ii) \(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}| \cos \theta\). Since \(-1 \le \cos \theta \le 1\), \(-|\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}| \le \underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} \le |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}|\), so \(|a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \le |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}| = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2}\).

(b) Position \(\underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ -\frac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta \end{pmatrix}\), Velocity \(\dot{\underset{\sim}{r}}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ -gt + u \sin \theta \end{pmatrix}\). Perpendicular if \(\underset{\sim}{r} \cdot \dot{\underset{\sim}{r}} = 0\), leading to \(g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2 = 0\). For real \(t > 0\), discriminant \(\Delta > 0\) leads to \(\sin^2 \theta > \frac{8}{9}\), so \(\sin \theta > \frac{\sqrt{8}}{3}\), \(\theta > 1.23\).

(c) If \(z = x + iy\) and \(\text{Arg}(z) = \theta\), then \(\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}\). For \(0 \le x < \frac{\pi}{2}\), \(\text{Re}(z) \ge \text{Arg}(z)\) implies \(x \ge \theta\), so \(\tan x \ge \tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}\), hence \(x \tan x \ge y\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) state and apply the triangle inequality as an identity and use it show the lower bound to the expression \(|x| + |y| + |z|\) is 1.

(a) (ii) identify a correct relationship initially, typically \(\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|} = \cos \theta\), leading to a correct inequality involving \(|\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}|\) and \(|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|\).
(a) (iii) carefully choose vectors from part (ii) to apply in this part, leading to the required upper bound for \(|x| + |y| + |z|\) of \(\sqrt{3}\).
(b) link the ideas of projectile motion, dot products, discriminant theory and trigonometry to find a range of angles
(b) justify their answers.
(c) realise a functional relationship, \(y= x \tan x\), and use it to guide them to a correct region satisfying the required relationship in \(z\).
improvementAreas: (a) (i) understanding the difference between \(|x\mathbf{i} + y\mathbf{j} + z\mathbf{k}|\) and \(|x+ y+ z|\).
(a) (ii) using absolute value or modulus symbols across vectors and scalars.
(a) (iii) understanding inequalities using vectors.
(b) manipulating algebraic expansions
(b) applying discriminant theory to quadratic equations.
(c) using the Cartesian form when dealing with graphs involving complex numbers.

questions.title: Regression analysis of temperature data
questions.promptLatex:
For a sample of 17 inland towns in Australia, the height above sea level, \(x\) (metres), and the average maximum daily temperature, \(y\) (\(^\circ\text{C}\)), were recorded.

The graph shows the data as well as a regression line.

[Graph of Average maximum daily temperature vs Height above sea level]

The equation of the regression line is \(y = 29.2 - 0.011x\).

The correlation coefficient is \(r = -0.494\).

(a) (i) By using the equation of the regression line, predict the average maximum daily temperature, in degrees Celsius, for a town that is \(540\text{ m}\) above sea level. Give your answer correct to one decimal place. (1 mark)

(ii) The gradient of the regression line is \(-0.011\). Interpret the value of this gradient in the given context. (2 marks)

(b) The graph below shows the relationship between the latitude, \(x\) (degrees south), and the average maximum daily temperature, \(y\) (\(^\circ\text{C}\)), for the same 17 towns, as well as a regression line.

[Graph of Average maximum daily temperature vs Latitude]

The equation of the regression line is \(y = 45.6 - 0.683x\).

The correlation coefficient is \(r = -0.897\).

Another inland town in Australia is \(540\text{ m}\) above sea level. Its latitude is \(28\) degrees south.

Which measurement, height above sea level or latitude, would be better to use to predict this town's average maximum daily temperature? Give a reason for your answer. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(23.3^\circ\text{C}\) (a)(ii) For a one metre increase in the height above sea level, we expect a \(0.011^\circ\text{C}\) drop in average maximum daily temperature. (b) It would be better to use the latitude, as the average maximum daily temperature has a stronger correlation with latitude than with height above sea level.
workingLatex: (a)(ii) For a one metre increase in the height above sea level, we expect a \(0.011^\circ\text{C}\) drop in average maximum daily temperature.
(b) It would be better to use the latitude. From the information given, the average maximum daily temperature has a stronger correlation with latitude than with height above sea level.

betterResponses: (a) (i) correctly substitute the correct values into the given equation and evaluate it.
(a) (ii) state that as the height above sea level increases, the temperature decreases (or gets colder)
(a) (ii) interpret the meaning of the gradient within the context of the question.
(b) make a clear and concise statement. For example, ‘Latitude, because the correlation coefficient is stronger for latitude than for height above sea level’.
(b) identify that when given two negative correlation coefficients, the value closer to -1 is stronger.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) writing down their substitution before solving
(a) (i) evaluating algebraic expressions.
(a) (ii) keeping their answer simple and concise
(a) (ii) providing an interpretation of data
(a) (ii) improving their understanding of what gradient means in practical situations.
(b) making mathematical interpretations rather than geographical answers
(b) thinking that only +1 means strong correlation.

criteria.criterion: Provides a correct relevant statement relating temperature to height above sea level or equivalent merit

A graph of this quadratic model for revenue is shown. A dashed line is used for values of \(x\) which are not relevant to the practical context of this problem.

(a) By first finding a suitable value of \(x\), find the price the publisher should charge for each book to maximise the revenue raised from sales of the book. (2 marks)

betterResponses: interpret the question and correctly apply the context to a quadratic model
calculate the x-value for the axis of symmetry of the parabola
calculate the intercept of a parabola with the vertical axis
recognise that they had to add 10 to the x-value found for the maximum revenue.

questions.promptLatex: Sketch the graph of the curve \(y = -x^3 + 3x^2 - 1\), labelling the stationary points and point of inflection. Do NOT determine the \(x\) intercepts of the curve. (4 marks)
questions.answerLatex: Stationary points are \((0, -1)\) and \((2, 3)\); point of inflection is \((1, 1)\).
workingLatex: Set \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\) to find stationary points: \(3x(2 - x) = 0\), which gives \(x = 0\) and \(x = 2\).

Set \(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 0\) to find the point of inflection: \(6(1 - x) = 0\), which gives \(x = 1\).

questions.title: Modelling populations of mice and cats
questions.subtopic: Trigonometric modelling and rates of change
questions.promptLatex:
The population of mice on an isolated island can be modelled by the function

\[ m(t) = a \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{26} t \right) + b, \]

where \( t \) is the time in weeks and \( 0 \le t \le 52 \). The population of mice reaches a maximum of 35 000 when \( t = 13 \) and a minimum of 5000 when \( t = 39 \). The graph of \( m(t) \) is shown.

(a) What are the values of \( a \) and \( b \)? (2 marks)

(b) On the same island, the population of cats can be modelled by the function

\[ c(t) = -80 \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{26} (t - 10) \right) + 120. \]

Consider the graph of \( m(t) \) and the graph of \( c(t) \).

Find the values of \( t \), \( 0 \le t \le 52 \), for which both populations are increasing. (3 marks)

(c) Find the rate of change of the mice population when the cat population reaches a maximum. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(a = 15000\), \(b = 20000\) (b) \(10 < t < 13\) (c) The mice population is decreasing at \(643\) mice per week.
workingLatex: (b) The population of mice increases for \(0 < t < 13\) and \(39 < t < 52\).
(b) The population of cats increases for \(10 < t < 36\).
(b) Therefore both populations are increasing for \(10 < t < 13\).
(c) The cat population reaches a maximum at \(t = 36\).
(c) Therefore the mice population is decreasing at \(643\) mice per week.

questions.title: Mathematical Induction, Probability, and Calculus
questions.subtopic: Mathematical induction, binomial distribution, and differential equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Use the principle of mathematical induction to show that for all integers \(n \ge 1\),

\[ 1 \times 2 + 2 \times 5 + 3 \times 8 + \dots + n(3n - 1) = n^2(n + 1). \] (3 marks)

(b) When a particular biased coin is tossed, the probability of obtaining a head is \(\frac{3}{5}\).

This coin is tossed 100 times.

Let \(X\) be the random variable representing the number of heads obtained. This random variable will have a binomial distribution.

(i) Find the expected value, \(E(X)\). (1 mark)

(ii) By finding the variance, \(\text{Var}(X)\), show that the standard deviation of \(X\) is approximately 5. (1 mark)

(iii) By using a normal approximation, find the approximate probability that \(X\) is between 55 and 65. (1 mark)

(c) To complete a course, a student must choose and pass exactly three topics. (2 marks)

There are eight topics from which to choose.

Last year 400 students completed the course.

Explain, using the pigeonhole principle, why at least eight students passed exactly the same three topics.

(d) Find \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos 5x \sin 3x \, dx\). (3 marks)

(e) Find the curve which satisfies the differential equation \(\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{x}{y}\) and passes through the point \((1, 0)\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Proof by induction (b)(i) \(60\) (ii) \(5\) (iii) \(68\%\) (c) At least one combination has \(8\) or more students (d) \(-\frac{1}{2}\) (e) \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\)
workingLatex: (c) There are \(\binom{8}{3} = 56\) possible choices of 3 topics and \(\frac{400}{56} = 7.14\). As there are 56 possible combinations, we can have at most 392 students without exceeding 7 students per combination. But we have 400 students, so at least one combination has 8 or more students.

(e) \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-x}{y}\). Separating variables, \(\int y \, dy = \int -x \, dx\). \(\frac{y^2}{2} = \frac{-x^2}{2} + c\), \(x^2 + y^2 = d\), where \(d = 2c\). The curve passes through \((1, 0)\), so \(d = 1^2 + 0^2 = 1\). Hence the equation of \(D\) is \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\).

betterResponses: (a) deal with the LHS and RHS separately for base case \(n = 1\) and for \(n = k + 1\), clearly articulate the inductive hypothesis for \(n = k\), state what they were required to prove when \(n = k + 1\), use factorisation rather than expansion when proving true for \(n = k + 1\).
(b)(i) use the correct formula to arrive at the answer.
(b)(ii) substitute correctly in the formula for variance, show that the standard deviation is \(\sqrt{24}\).
(b)(iii) use the empirical rule found in the Reference Sheet rather than attempting to calculate probabilities from \(z\)-scores.

(c) work out that a minimum of 393 students are needed to ensure that at least eight students pass exactly the same three topics, establish that 392 pigeons are needed to equally fill each pigeonhole with 7 pigeons and the 8 remaining pigeons could be added to any one or more of the pigeonholes.
(d) use the appropriate transformation and provide the correct solution in concise manner.

(e) use definite integrals to arrive at the solution, thereby obviating the need to work with constants of integration, use only one constant of integration, realise that the solution does not have to be a function.
improvementAreas: (a) factorising algebraic expressions, exercising care when working with brackets, ensuring that each step of the proof is algebraically correct, correctly use sigma notation, should they choose to use it, setting out proofs in a logical sequence, working from one side to the other when attempting proofs, and not manipulate both sides simultaneously.
(b)(i) familiarising themselves with the Reference Sheet.
(b)(ii) focusing on using the Reference Sheet correctly.
(b)(iii) reading the question carefully to ascertain when and how normal distribution approximation should be used.

(c) clearly explaining the pigeonhole principle, understanding the ceiling function (or why we round up) when using the pigeonhole principle, solving the problem using logic and sound reasoning, instead of simply using a formula with the hope of getting the correct answer, practicing interpreting worded problems, thoroughly revising Year 11 content.
(d) recognising when to use trigonometric identities, ensuring accuracy when working with negatives and fractions.

(e) understanding that differential equations should not be integrated where there are multiple variables on one side, avoiding placing constants of integration on both sides as this can cause confusion when evaluating the constant of the primitive, focusing on algebraic skills when dealing with negative numbers, particularly when substituting and solving equations.

questions.title: Combinatorics and Trigonometric Cubic Equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) (i) Use the identity \((1 + x)^{2n} = (1 + x)^n (1 + x)^n\) to show that
\[ \binom{2n}{n} = \binom{n}{0}^2 + \binom{n}{1}^2 + \dots + \binom{n}{n}^2, \]
where \(n\) is a positive integer. (2 marks)

(ii) A club has \(2n\) members, with \(n\) women and \(n\) men.

A group consisting of an even number \((0, 2, 4, \dots, 2n)\) of members is chosen, with the number of men equal to the number of women.

Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to do this is \(\binom{2n}{n}\). (2 marks)

(iii) From the group chosen in part (ii), one of the men and one of the women are selected as leaders.

Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to choose the even number of people and then the leaders is
\[ 1^2 \binom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 \binom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 \binom{n}{n}^2. \] (2 marks)

(iv) The process is now reversed so that the leaders, one man and one woman, are chosen first. The rest of the group is then selected, still made up of an equal number of women and men.

By considering this reversed process and using part (ii), find a simple expression for the sum in part (iii). (2 marks)

(b) (i) Show that \(\sin^3 \theta - \frac{3}{4} \sin \theta + \frac{\sin(3\theta)}{4} = 0\). (2 marks)

(ii) By letting \(x = 4 \sin \theta\) in the cubic equation \(x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0\).

Show that \(\sin(3\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Prove that \(\sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18} = \frac{3}{2}\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) The left hand side is \(\binom{2n}{n}\) which is the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^{2n}\). This means that \(\binom{n}{0}\binom{n}{0} + \binom{n}{1}\binom{n}{1} + \dots + \binom{n}{n}\binom{n}{n}\) should be the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\). The first term, \(\binom{n}{0}\) is the constant term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) and so should be multiplied by an \(x^n\) term. But \(\binom{n}{0} = \binom{n}{n-0} = \binom{n}{n}\) is equal to the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion \((1+x)^n\). Thus \(\binom{n}{0}^2\) is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term that comes from the constant in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) times the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\). Similarly \(\binom{n}{1}^2\) is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term that comes from the coefficient of \(x\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) times the coefficient of the \(x^{n-1}\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\). Therefore, the right hand side is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\).

(a)(ii) We can choose 0 men and 0 women in \(\binom{n}{0}\binom{n}{0} = \binom{n}{0}^2\) ways, or 1 man and 1 woman in \(\binom{n}{1}^2\) ways, ..., or \(n\) men and \(n\) women in \(\binom{n}{n}^2\) ways. Hence the total number of ways is \(\binom{n}{0}^2 + \dots + \binom{n}{n}^2 = \binom{2n}{n}\) from part (i).

(a)(iii) We can choose 1 woman and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{1} \times 1\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{1} \times 1 \times \binom{n}{1} \times 1 = \binom{n}{1}^2 \times 1^2\) for this case. We can choose 2 women and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{2} = \binom{n}{2} \times 2\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{2} \times 2 \times \binom{n}{2} \times 2 = \binom{n}{2}^2 \times 2^2\) for this case. We can choose \(n\) women and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{n} = \binom{n}{n} \times n\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{n} \times n \times \binom{n}{n} \times n = \binom{n}{n}^2 \times n^2\) for this case. And so the total is \(1^2 \binom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 \binom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 \binom{n}{n}^2\).

(a)(iv) There are \(n\) ways to choose a woman leader, leaving \((n-1)\) women from which to choose. There are \(n\) ways to choose a man leader, leaving \((n-1)\) men from which to choose. By part (ii) there are \(\binom{2(n-1)}{n-1}\) ways to choose the \((n-1)\) women and \((n-1)\) men. Hence \(1^2 \binom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 \binom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 \binom{n}{n}^2 = n^2 \binom{2n-2}{n-1}\).

(b)(ii) \(x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0\). Let \(x = 4\sin \theta\), so \(64\sin^3 \theta - 48\sin \theta + 8 = 0\). Dividing by 64 gives \(\sin^3 \theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin \theta + \frac{8}{64} = 0\). Comparing with (i), \(\frac{8}{64} = \frac{\sin 3\theta}{4} \implies \sin 3\theta = \frac{32}{64} = \frac{1}{2}\).

(b)(iii) From \(\sin 3\theta = \frac{1}{2}\), we have \(3\theta = \frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5\pi}{6}, \frac{13\pi}{6}, \dots\) so \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{18}, \frac{5\pi}{18}, \frac{13\pi}{18}, \dots\). Let \(\alpha = 4\sin \frac{\pi}{18}\), \(\beta = 4\sin \frac{5\pi}{18}\), \(\gamma = 4\sin \frac{25\pi}{18}\). Then \(\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = (\alpha + \beta + \gamma)^2 - 2(\alpha\beta + \alpha\gamma + \beta\gamma) = 0^2 + 2 \times 12 = 24\). Thus \(16(\sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18}) = 24\), so the sum is \(\frac{24}{16} = \frac{3}{2}\).

questions.title: Mechanics and Projectile Motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A 50-kilogram box is initially at rest. The box is pulled along the ground with a force of 200 newtons at an angle of \(30^\circ\) to the horizontal. The box experiences a resistive force of \(0.3R\) newtons, where \(R\) is the normal force, as shown in the diagram.

Take the acceleration \(g\) due to gravity to be \(10 \text{ m/s}^2\).

(i) By resolving the forces vertically, show that \(R = 400\). (2 marks)

(ii) Show that the net force horizontally is approximately \(53.2\) newtons. (2 marks)

(iii) Find the velocity of the box after the first three seconds. (2 marks)

(b) A particle is projected from the origin with initial velocity \(u \text{ m/s}\) at an angle \(\theta\) to the horizontal. The particle lands at \(x = R\) on the \(x\)-axis. The acceleration vector is given by \(a = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -g \end{pmatrix}\), where \(g\) is the acceleration due to gravity. (Do NOT prove this.)

(i) Show that the position vector \(r(t)\) of the particle is given by
\[ r(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2} gt^2 \end{pmatrix}. \] (3 marks)

(ii) Show that the Cartesian equation of the path of flight is given by
\[ y = \frac{-gx^2}{2u^2} \left( \tan^2 \theta - \frac{2u^2}{gx} \tan \theta + 1 \right). \] (3 marks)

(iii) Given \(u^2 > gR\), prove that there are 2 distinct values of \(\theta\) for which the particle will land at \(x = R\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) 400 \text{ newtons} (a)(ii) \simeq 53.2 \text{ newtons} (a)(iii) 3.192 \text{ m/s} (b)(i) \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix} (b)(ii) y = x \tan \theta - \frac{gx^2}{2u^2}(1 + \tan^2 \theta) (b)(iii) \Delta > 0 \text{ implies two distinct values for } \theta\)
workingLatex: (a)\((iii) \text{From part (b), using } F = ma, 50a = 53.2 \implies \frac{dV}{dt} = 1.064. \text{ Hence } V = \int_0^3 1.064 dt = 1.064 \times 3 = 3.192 \text{ m/s}\)

(b)\((i) \underset{\sim}{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -g \end{pmatrix}, \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} c_1 \\ -gt + c_2 \end{pmatrix}. \text{ At } t = 0, \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ u \sin \theta \end{pmatrix} \implies \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ u \sin \theta - gt \end{pmatrix}. \text{ Integrating, } \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta + c_3 \\ ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 + c_4 \end{pmatrix}. \text{ Since } \underset{\sim}{r}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, c_3 = c_4 = 0, \text{ so } \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix}\)

(b)\((ii) x = ut \cos \theta \implies t = \frac{x}{u \cos \theta}. \text{ Substituting into } y = ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2: y = x \tan \theta - \frac{1}{2}g \frac{x^2}{u^2 \cos^2 \theta} = x \tan \theta - \frac{gx^2}{2u^2}(1 + \tan^2 \theta)\)

(b)\((iii) \text{We require the quadratic to have two distinct real roots when } x = R. \Delta = \frac{4u^4}{g^2 R^2} - 4 = \frac{4(u^4 - g^2 R^2)}{g^2 R^2}. \text{ Given } u^2 > gR \implies u^4 > g^2 R^2, \text{ so } \Delta > 0. \text{ Hence there are two distinct values for } \tan \theta, \text{ and thus two distinct values for } \theta\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) write an equation in which the sum of the vertical forces is equal to zero, ensure that their calculation was the same as the given result, resolve the vertical and horizontal forces separately.
(a) (ii) write an equation in which the sum of the horizontal forces is equal to \(F_{net}\).
(a) (iii) write an integration statement, understand that when \(t=3\), this implies to use \(\frac{dv}{dt}\) for acceleration, find the acceleration and integrate to find \(v\).

(b) (i) justify all constants with reference to \(t=0\), use a diagram to assist them find initial velocity components, perform vector calculations so that both components were solved simultaneously, use definite integrals as a means of defining constants.

(b) (ii) substitute \(t\) into the equation for \(y\) and simplify the resulting expression with clarity and precision, factor out the common factor, \(-\frac{gx^2}{2u^2}\) very clearly, without losing squares on the \(u\) or \(x\).

(b) (iii) use the simplest quadratic in \(\tan \theta\) to find the discriminant, simplify the discriminant into a form which made it easier to apply the given condition \(u^2 > gx\), construct a logically ordered proof that showed ‘If \(u^2 > gx\) then \(\Delta > 0\)’.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) not getting confused with the \(50g\) (thinking 50 grams) when \(g\) was given in the question to be \(10 \text{ m/s}^2\), writing down relevant calculations.
(a) (ii) writing with clarity.

(a) (iii) understanding that when the box was moving there was no vertical velocity, understanding there is no diagonal velocity, knowing when to use \(\ddot{x} = \frac{dv}{dt}\) and/or \(\ddot{x} = \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2}v^2)\), knowing \(F_{net} = ma\) and so \(a = \frac{F}{m}\), knowing when to include resistive motion.

(b) (i) using vector notation properly, if they choose to go that way, improving vector notation and understanding, that if used correctly, was more succinct and clearer, refraining from simply quoting \(\dot{x} = u \cos \theta\) with no justification or making errors in notation such as \(t=0, \dot{x}=0, \therefore \dot{x} = u \cos \theta\), refraining from the use of rote learnt responses and leaving out critical details, finding velocity and the various constants rather than writing acceleration and displacement components/vectors which were given in the question, knowing what is important to include in a ‘show that’ question in order to score the allocated marks.

(b) (ii) taking care with details, ensuring that squares do not go missing, negative signs appear as required, and pronumerals are currently included or omitted, improving handwriting for ease of reading, showing sufficient steps to justify progression, making changes to working clear.

(b) (iii) taking care to be precise with their notation. It was common to see \(\Delta \ge 0\) or \(\Delta\) is real, reducing algebraic errors, realising that substituting \(u^2 > gx\) into the quadratic does not result in the quadratic expression being positive, writing larger to avoid error in powers and fractions, reading the clues in the question: this part required only the discriminant and so time and effort spent on simplifying the quadratic created opportunities for more errors, keeping powers in fractions legible resulting in less omissions or unintended sign changes.

questions.title: SHM, 3D Lines, Inequalities, and Trigonometric Integrals
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A particle is undergoing simple harmonic motion with period \( \frac{\pi}{3} \). The central point of motion of the particle is at \( x = \sqrt{3} \). When \( t = 0 \) the particle has its maximum displacement of \( 2\sqrt{3} \) from the central point of motion.

Find an equation for the displacement, \( x \), of the particle in terms of \( t \). (3 marks)

(b) Consider the two lines in three dimensions given by
\[ \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_1 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \text{ and } \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_2 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}. \]

By equating components, find the point of intersection of the two lines. (3 marks)

(c) (i) By considering the right-angled triangle below, or otherwise, prove that \( \frac{a + b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab} \), where \( a > b \ge 0 \). (2 marks)

(ii) Prove that \( p^2 + 4q^2 \ge 4pq \). (1 mark)

(d) (i) Show that for any integer \( n \), \( e^{in\theta} + e^{-in\theta} = 2\cos(n\theta) \). (1 mark)

(ii) By expanding \( (e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4 \), show that
\[ \cos^4 \theta = \frac{1}{8} (\cos(4\theta) + 4\cos(2\theta) + 3). \] (3 marks)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, find \( \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos^4 \theta \, d\theta \). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) The equation has the form \(x(t) = A\cos(nt) + \sqrt{3}\). Now \(A = 2\sqrt{3}\) and \(\frac{2\pi}{n} = \frac{\pi}{3}\), so \(n = 6\). \(\therefore x(t) = 2\sqrt{3} \cos(6t) + \sqrt{3}\).

(b) \(r = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_1 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\), \(r = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_2 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\). Equating components: \(3 + \lambda_1 = 3 - 2\lambda_2\) (1), \(-1 + 2\lambda_1 = -6 + \lambda_2\) (2), \(7 + \lambda_1 = 2 + 3\lambda_2\) (3). (1) \(\Rightarrow \lambda_1 = -2\lambda_2\). (2) \(\Rightarrow -1 - 4\lambda_2 = -6 + \lambda_2 \Rightarrow \lambda_2 = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = -2\). As a check, using equation (3), Left hand side = 5, Right hand side = 5. \(\therefore\) Point of intersection is \(r = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}\).
(c)(i) By Pythagoras, \((a+b)^2 = x^2 + (a-b)^2\). \(\therefore x = 2\sqrt{ab}\). Now the hypotenuse is the longest side so \(a+b \ge 2\sqrt{ab}\). \(\therefore \frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab}\).
(d)(i) \(e^{in\theta} + e^{-in\theta} = (\cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta) + (\cos n\theta - i\sin n\theta) = 2\cos(n\theta)\).

(d)(ii) \((e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4 = e^{4i\theta} + 4e^{3i\theta} \cdot e^{-i\theta} + 6e^{2i\theta} \cdot e^{-2i\theta} + 4e^{i\theta} \cdot e^{-3i\theta} + e^{-4i\theta} = (e^{4i\theta} + e^{-4i\theta}) + 4(e^{2i\theta} + e^{-2i\theta}) + 6 = 2\cos 4\theta + 8\cos 2\theta + 6\). Also \((e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4 = 2^4 \cos^4 \theta\). \(\therefore \cos^4 \theta = \frac{1}{8}(\cos 4\theta + 4\cos 2\theta + 3)\).

betterResponses: (a) use the equation found on the Reference Sheet and replace each of the variables to suit this question, interpret the information in the question correctly, use a diagram to assist in finding all the missing parts to the equation.
(b) equate the components in order to then find the point of intersection.
(c) (i) use the diagram and show the connection between the diagram and the inequality, use another method to prove the result.
(c) (ii) substitute into the result in part (i) to prove the additional inequality, use another method to prove the result.
(d) (i) show the relationship between the complex number and its conjugate.
(d) (ii) expand carefully and then use the result in the part (i) to show the desired result.
(d) (iii) use the result in part (ii) and then integrate correctly.
improvementAreas: (a) knowing what is on the Reference Sheet, knowing that simple harmonic motion questions can be asked in Mathematics Extension 2, knowing the difference between when to use the \(x = a \cos nt + x_0\) or \(x = a \cos(nt + \alpha) + x_0\) forms and when to use the sine equations, what the phase change means, how to find the amplitude, and how the period gives the constant, \(n\).
(b) remembering to find the point of intersection once the variables are found, checking the solution to make sure the same point of intersection can be found using either one of the variables.
(c) (i) learning a standard method to prove this result.

(c) (ii) understanding a variety of methods that can be used to prove inequalities. These could be substitution into a known result, using \(LHS-RHS\) approach and proving positive, using proof by contradiction, or starting with a true positive inequality and building an argument from there.
(d) (i) showing the result by writing the complex number in modulus-argument form.
(d) (ii) being careful with the use of the result from part (i) to ensure that the final answer was correct, being careful not to make simple errors such as stating that \(2^4 = 8\).

(d) (iii) making use of formulae given in examinations, such as the Reference Sheet results, being aware that this question could have been attempted even if the previous part was not attempted. Students should look out for the types of questions where a previous result can be used.

questions.title: Complex numbers, motion, induction, and irrationality
questions.subtopic: Complex geometry, resisted motion, and mathematical induction
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(z_1\) be a complex number and let \(z_2 = e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}} z_1\).

The diagram shows points \(A\) and \(B\) which represent \(z_1\) and \(z_2\), respectively, in the Argand plane.

(i) Explain why triangle \(OAB\) is an equilateral triangle. (2 marks)

(ii) Prove that \(z_1^2 + z_2^2 = z_1 z_2\). (3 marks)

(b) A particle starts from rest and falls through a resisting medium so that its acceleration, in \(\text{m/s}^2\), is modelled by
\[a = 10(1 - (kv)^2),\]
where \(v\) is the velocity of the particle in \(\text{m/s}\) and \(k = 0.01\).

Find the velocity of the particle after 5 seconds. (4 marks)

(c) Prove by mathematical induction that, for \(n \ge 2\),
\[\frac{1}{2^2} + \frac{1}{3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{n^2} < \frac{n - 1}{n}.\] (4 marks)

(d) Prove that for any integer \(n > 1\), \(\log_n(n + 1)\) is irrational. (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) \triangle OAB is equilateral. (a)(ii) z_1^2 + z_2^2 = z_1z_2 . (b) v = 100\left(\frac{e-1}{e+1}\right) \approx 46.2 \text{ m/s} . (c) Proof by induction. (d) Proof by contradiction.\)
workingLatex: (a)(i) Multiplying \(z_1\) by \(e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}}\) rotates it anticlockwise through an angle \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), giving \(z_2\). Also \(|z_1| = |z_2|\). So \(\triangle OAB\) is isosceles giving all angles equal to \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) and hence \(\triangle OAB\) is equilateral.

(b) \(\frac{dv}{dt} = 10(1 - (kv)^2)\). \(t = \frac{1}{10} \int \frac{1}{(1-kv)(1+kv)} dv = \frac{1}{20} \int \left(\frac{1}{1-kv} + \frac{1}{1+kv}\right) dv = \frac{1}{20k} \ln\left(\frac{1+kv}{1-kv}\right) + C\). When \(t=0, v=0\), so \(C=0\). When \(t=5\), \(\ln\left(\frac{1+kv}{1-kv}\right) = 5 \times 20 \times 0.01 = 1\). \(\therefore 1 + 0.01v = e(1 - 0.01v)\). \(v = 100\left(\frac{e-1}{e+1}\right) \approx 46.2 \text{ m/s}\).

(c) Let \(P(n)\) be the given proposition. \(P(2)\) is true since \(\frac{1}{2^2} < \frac{1}{2}\). Assume \(\frac{1}{2^2} + \frac{1}{3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2} < \frac{k-1}{k}\). Consider \(\frac{1}{2^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2} + \frac{1}{(k+1)^2} < \frac{k-1}{k} + \frac{1}{(k+1)^2} = \frac{(k-1)(k+1)^2 + k}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k^3+k^2-k-1+k}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k^3+k^2-1}{k(k+1)^2} < \frac{k^3+k^2}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k^2(k+1)}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k}{k+1}\). \(\therefore P(k+1)\) is true. \(\therefore P(n)\) true for all integers \(n \ge 2\) by induction.

(d) Suppose \(\log_n(n+1)\) is rational. Then \(\log_n(n+1) = \frac{p}{q}\), where \(p, q\) are integers and \(q \ne 0\). \(\therefore n+1 = n^{\frac{p}{q}}\), so \((n+1)^q = n^p\). If \(n\) is even then RHS is even, LHS is odd - a contradiction. If \(n\) is odd then RHS is odd, LHS is even - a contradiction. Hence in either case we have a contradiction, so \(\log_n(n+1)\) is irrational.

betterResponses: (a) (i) clearly state that \(z_2\) is the rotation of \(z_1\) by \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) with no change of length, so that the included angle is \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) and the moduli are equal, state that the triangle is isosceles with an apex angle of \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) so the triangle is equilateral.

(a) (ii) cube both sides of \(z_2 = e^{i\pi/3} z_1\), rearrange and factorise to gain the result, take out a factor of \(z_1^2\) from LHS, convert \(1 + e^{i2\pi/3}\) into mod-arg form then convert back to \(e^{i\pi/3}\), and rearrange to form the RHS, take out a factor of \(z_1^2 e^{i\pi/3}\) from the LHS then use conjugate results.

(b) substitute \(k=0.01\) at the start and rearrange the equation of motion into a simpler form such as \(\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{10000-v^2}{1000}\), invert the fraction and form the partial fractions, integrate the partial fractions including evaluating \(c\) then rearrange to make \(v\) the subject and substitute given conditions
(b) substitute \(k= 0.01\) at the start and rearrange the equation of motion into a simpler form such as \(\frac{dv}{dt}=\frac{10000-v^2}{1000}\)
(b) invert the fraction and form the partial fractions
(b) integrate the partial fractions including evaluating \(c\) then rearrange to make \(v\) the subject and substitute given conditions
(c) prove the base case for \(n= 2\)
(c) clearly show the inductive step using the correct assumption for \(n= k+ 1\) case
(c) work from the true result (LHS) to gain the result to be proved (RHS) with clear working and appropriate use of < and = signs
(d) let \(\log_n(n+ 1) = \frac{p}{q}\) as the contradiction
(d) remove \(\log_n\) to gain \(n^p= (n+ 1)^q\)
(d) clearly state that if \(n\) was even then \(n^p\) was even, but that \(n+ 1\) must be odd and so \((n+ 1)^q\) must be odd or vice versa which is a contradiction
(d) take out a factor of \(n\) on one side and on the other do the same by expanding \((n+ 1)^q\) and factorising to leave a remainder of 1 to prove the contradiction
improvementAreas: (a) (i) understanding the geometrical significance of a complex number in polar form without converting to other form, giving a clear explanation of why moduli are equal and angle is \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), practising questions where they need to explain using a mixture of words, diagrams and mathematical language, knowing the most appropriate form of the complex number to use in particular situations, knowing the properties of an equilateral triangle.

(a) (ii) choosing which complex number form is most appropriate depending on the type of calculation to be performed, making sure all necessary steps are included in a proof, understanding that \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) are complex not real numbers so using the cosine rule is invalid.

(b) recognising appropriate and most efficient integration techniques to use for \(\int \frac{dv}{1-a^2x^2}\), integrating expressions involving a variable in the denominator with an unknown constant coefficient, recognising when to use partial fractions in applied questions, rather than standalone integration questions, avoiding the use of memorised formulas for integrating partial fractions, taking care with the primitive of \(\int \frac{1}{1-kv} dv\) to not forget the \(-\frac{1}{k}\) factor in front of \(\ln|1-kv|\), thinking about whether the answer they gain is reasonable.
(b) recognising appropriate and most efficient integration techniques to use for \(\int \frac{dv}{1-a^2x^2}\)
(b) integrating expressions involving a variable in the denominator with an unknown constant coefficient
(b) recognising when to use partial fractions in applied questions, rather than standalone integration questions

(b) taking care with the primitive of \(\int \frac{1}{1-kx}dx\) to not forget the \(-\frac{1}{k}\) factor in front of \(\ln|1 -kx|\)
(b) thinking about whether the answer they gain is reasonable
(c) practising induction where \(n= 1\) is not the base case
(c) taking care with algebra manipulation involving algebraic fractions
(c) being able to write clear and logical reasoning for an inequality proof

(d) taking care when choosing variables to avoid using the same pronumeral for two different variables eg \(\log_n(n+ 1) = \frac{m}{n}\)

questions.title: Logical Propositions and Vector Geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) In the set of integers, let \(P\) be the proposition:

‘If \(k + 1\) is divisible by 3, then \(k^3 + 1\) is divisible by 3’.

(i) Prove that the proposition \(P\) is true. (2 marks)

(ii) Write down the contrapositive of the proposition \(P\). (1 mark)

(iii) Write down the converse of the proposition \(P\) and state, with reasons, whether this converse is true or false. (3 marks)

(b) The point \(C\) divides the interval \(AB\) so that \(\frac{CB}{AC} = \frac{m}{n}\). The position vectors of \(A\) and \(B\) are \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) respectively, as shown in the diagram.

(i) Show that \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m + n}(\underset{\sim}{b} - \underset{\sim}{a})\). (2 marks)

(ii) Prove that \(\vec{OC} = \frac{m}{m + n}\underset{\sim}{a} + \frac{n}{m + n}\underset{\sim}{b}\). (1 mark)

Let \(OPQR\) be a parallelogram with \(\vec{OP} = \underset{\sim}{p}\) and \(\vec{OR} = \underset{\sim}{r}\). The point \(S\) is the midpoint of \(QR\) and \(T\) is the intersection of \(PR\) and \(OS\), as shown in the diagram.

(iii) Show that \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}\underset{\sim}{r} + \frac{1}{3}\underset{\sim}{p}\). (3 marks)

(iv) Using parts (ii) and (iii), or otherwise, prove that \(T\) is the point that divides the interval \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\). (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(k^3+1 = (k+1)(k^2-k+1) = 3j(k^2-k+1)\), hence divisible by 3. (a)(ii) If \(k^3+1\) is not divisible by 3, then \(k+1\) is not divisible by 3. (a)(iii) The converse is 'If \(k^3+1\) is divisible by 3, then \(k+1\) is divisible by 3', which is true. (b)(i) \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m+n}(\vec{b}-\vec{a})\). (b)(ii) \(\vec{OC} = \frac{m}{m+n}\vec{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}\vec{b}\). (b)(iii) \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}\vec{r} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{p}\). (b)(iv) \(T\) divides the interval \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\).
workingLatex: (a)(i) Suppose \(k+1\) is divisible by 3, then \(k+1 = 3j\), for some integer \(j\). Now \(k^3+1 = (k+1)(k^2-k+1) = 3j(k^2-k+1)\). Hence \(k^3+1\) is divisible by 3.
(a)(ii) If \(k^3+1\) is not divisible by 3, then \(k+1\) is not divisible by 3.

(a)(iii) The converse states that: 'If \(k^3+1\) is divisible by 3, then \(k+1\) is divisible by 3'. This statement is true. The integer \(k\) must be of the form \(3j\), \(3j+1\) or \(3j-1\), for some integer \(j\). Then \(k^3+1 = 27j^3+1\) or \((3j+1)^3+1\) or \((3j-1)^3+1\). Only the third case gives \(k^3+1\) divisible by 3.

(b)(iii) \(\vec{OS} = \vec{OR} + \vec{SR} = \vec{r} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{p}\). Equation of line \(OS\) is \(\vec{x} = (\vec{r} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{p})\lambda\). Also equation of line \(PR\) is \(\vec{x} = \vec{p} + (\vec{r}-\vec{p})\mu\). Equating, we have \(\vec{p}(\frac{1}{2}\lambda) + \vec{r}\lambda = \vec{p}(1-\mu) + \vec{r}\mu\). \(\therefore \frac{1}{2}\lambda = 1-\mu\) and \(\lambda = \mu \implies \lambda = \frac{2}{3}\). Hence point of intersection is \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}(\vec{r} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{p}) = \frac{2}{3}\vec{r} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{p}\).

(b)(iv) By part (ii) the point which divides \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\) is given by \(\frac{2}{3}\vec{r} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{p}\), but this is \(\vec{OT}\). \(\therefore T\) divides the interval \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\).

betterResponses: (a)(i) utilise the factorisation of \(k^3 + 1 = (k+ 1)(k^2 -k+ 1)\) requiring a simple substitution
(a)(ii) write the contrapositive clearly and concisely
(a)(ii) understand truth tables and implications

(a)(iii) use algebraic techniques effectively: \(k^3 + 1 = 3M \rightarrow 3M-k^3 = 1\) or \(k+ 1 = k-k^3 + 3M \rightarrow k(1 -k)(1 + k) + 3M\) and used the fact that one of three consecutive integers is a multiple of three

questions.title: Dynamics and Integral Calculus Proofs
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Two masses, \(2m \text{ kg}\) and \(4m \text{ kg}\), are attached by a light string. The string is placed over a smooth pulley as shown.

The two masses are at rest before being released and \(v\) is the velocity of the larger mass at time \(t\) seconds after they are released.

(i) Show that \(\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{gm - kv}{3m}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Given that \(v < \frac{gm}{k}\), show that when \(t = \frac{3m}{k} \ln 2\), the velocity of the larger mass is \(\frac{gm}{2k}\). (3 marks)

(b) Let \(I_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) \, d\theta\), \(n = 0, 1, \dots\).

(i) Prove that \(I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} I_{n-1}\), \(n \ge 1\). (3 marks)

(ii) Deduce that \(I_n = \frac{2^{2n}(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}\). (3 marks)

Let \(J_n = \int_0^1 x^n(1-x)^n \, dx\), \(n = 0, 1, 2, \dots\).

(iii) Using the result of part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(J_n = \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}\). (3 marks)

(iv) Prove that \((2^n n!)^2 \le (2n+1)!\). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) (i) Let \( a \) be the acceleration of the masses. Comparing forces, \( 4gm - kv - 2gm - kv = 2ma + 4ma \), \( \therefore 2gm - 2kv = 6ma \), \( \therefore a = \frac{gm - kv}{3m} \)

(a) (ii) From part (i), \( \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{gm - kv}{3m} \), \( \therefore t = \int_0^v \frac{3m}{gm - kv} dv = -\frac{3m}{k} \ln(gm - kv) |_0^v = -\frac{3m}{k} [\ln(gm - kv) - \ln(gm)] = \frac{3m}{k} \ln(\frac{gm}{gm - kv}) \). When \( t = \frac{3m}{k} \ln 2 \), we have \( \frac{gm}{gm - kv} = 2 \), \( \Rightarrow 1 - \frac{kv}{gm} = \frac{1}{2} \), \( \Rightarrow v = \frac{gm}{2k} \)

(b) (i) \( I_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) d\theta = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin(2\theta) \sin^{2n}(2\theta) d\theta \). Let \( u = \sin^{2n}(2\theta) \), \( dv = \sin(2\theta) d\theta \), \( du = 4n \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) \cos(2\theta) d\theta \), \( v = -\frac{\cos(2\theta)}{2} \). \( I_n = \frac{1}{2} \sin^{2n}(2\theta) \times -\cos(2\theta) |_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} + 2n \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) \cos^2(2\theta) d\theta \). \( \therefore I_n = 2n [\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) d\theta - \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) d\theta] \). \( I_n(2n+1) = 2n I_{n-1} \), \( \therefore I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} I_{n-1} \)

(b) (ii) \( I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} \times \frac{2n-2}{2n-1} \times \dots \times \frac{2}{3} I_0 = \frac{2^n (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} I_0 \). Now \( I_0 = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin(2\theta) d\theta = -\frac{1}{2} \cos(2\theta) |_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} = 1 \). \( \therefore I_n = \frac{2^n (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \)

(b) (iii) \( J_n = \int_0^1 x^n (1-x)^n dx \). Put \( x = \sin^2 \theta \), \( \frac{dx}{d\theta} = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta \). \( \therefore J_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 2 \sin^{2n+1} \theta \cos^{2n+1} \theta d\theta = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{2^{2n}} (2 \sin \theta \cos \theta)^{2n+1} d\theta = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \sin^{2n+1} (2\theta) d\theta = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} I_n = \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \)

(b) (iv) The quadratic expression \( x(1-x) \) has maximum value of \( \frac{1}{4} \) when \( x = \frac{1}{2} \). \( \therefore J_n = \int_0^1 x^n (1-x)^n dx \le \int_0^1 (\frac{1}{4})^n dx = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \). \( \therefore \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \le \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \), \( \Rightarrow (2^n n!)^2 \le (2n+1)! \)

betterResponses: (a)(i) handle the resolution of forces when more than one object (mass) is involved

(a)(i) calculate the overall mass as being \(6m\)
(a)(ii) use definite integrals and separation of variables to efficiently solve the problem with minimal working
(a)(ii) obtain an expression for \(t\) in terms of \(v\), or \(v\) in terms of \(t\) and then substitute \(t=\frac{3m}{k}\ln 2\) to the derived expression for \(v\)
(a)(ii) execute the appropriate integration technique successfully and simplify the expression using logarithm laws
(b)(i) explain the logic behind their proof when it may not be immediately obvious what they had done moving from one line to another in their proof
(b)(i) indicate clearly the values for \(u\), \(u'\), \(v\) and \(v'\)
(b)(ii) find \(I_0\) correctly
(b)(ii) show clearly the final step to get the denominator \((2n+ 1)\)

questions.title: Scatterplot analysis and linear modelling
questions.promptLatex:

Use the line of best fit drawn in part (c) to estimate Meg's score on this test. (1 mark)

(e) John also did not sit the test and he missed 16 lessons.

questions.answerLatex: (a) The linear association is strong and the direction is negative. (b) 20 (c) A line of best fit drawn on the scatter plot. (d) Between 41 and 46 depending on the line of best fit drawn. (e) No. Extrapolation beyond the range of the data is usually not appropriate.
workingLatex: (a) The linear association is strong and the direction is negative.
(c) A line of best fit drawn on the scatter plot.
(d) Between 41 and 46 depending on the line of best fit drawn.
(e) No. Extrapolation beyond the range of the data is usually not appropriate.

draw a line of best fit which passing through points in the centre of the scatterplot and read information from their graph
express the understanding that extrapolation beyond the scope of the data set was inappropriate.

drawing a line of best fit using a ruler
recognising that the range of a dataset is a single value, and how to calculate it from a set of given data.

questions.title: Mean, median, and standard deviation analysis
questions.subtopic: Measures of spread and central tendency
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The ages, in years, of ten people at the local cinema last Saturday afternoon are shown.

\[ 38  25  38  46  55  68  72  55  36  38 \]

The mean of this dataset is \(47.1\) years.

How many of the ten people were aged between the mean age and the median age? (2 marks)

(b) On Wednesday, ten people all aged \(70\) went to this same cinema.

Would the standard deviation of the age dataset from Wednesday be larger than, smaller than or equal to the standard deviation of the age dataset given in part (a)? Briefly explain your answer without performing any calculations. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) The median is 42. There is one person aged between the mean and the median. (b) The standard deviation of the Wednesday dataset will be smaller because the spread of the data is very low (zero) compared to the Saturday data.
workingLatex: (a) The scores in ascending order are: 25, 36, 38, 38, 38, 46, 55, 55, 68, 72.
(a) There is one person aged between the mean and the median.
(b) On Wednesday, all the ages were the same, so the spread of the data is very low (in fact zero) compared to the spread of the Saturday data.
(b) Therefore the standard deviation of the Wednesday dataset will be smaller.

betterResponses: arrange scores in ascending order and select the two middle scores to average for the median
show their understanding of the concept of standard deviation by linking it to the spread of the ages in the dataset.
improvementAreas: understanding the meaning and process for calculating the median
reading questions carefully and selecting the key words, then checking they have answered what has been asked.

questions.title: Normal distribution and z-scores
questions.promptLatex:
The Intelligence Quotient (IQ) scores for adults in City \(A\) are normally distributed with a mean of \(108\) and a standard deviation of \(10\).

The IQ scores for adults in City \(B\) are normally distributed with a mean of \(112\) and a standard deviation of \(16\).

(a) Yin is an adult who lives in City \(A\) and has an IQ score of \(128\).

What percentage of the adults in this city have an IQ score higher than Yin's? (2 marks)

(b) There are \(1\,000\,000\) adults living in City \(B\).

Calculate the number of adults in City \(B\) that would be expected to have an IQ score lower than Yin's. (2 marks)

(c) Simon, an adult who lives in City \(A\), moves to City \(B\). The \(z\)-score corresponding to his IQ score in City \(A\) is the same as the \(z\)-score corresponding to his IQ score in City \(B\).

By first forming an equation, calculate Simon's IQ score. Give your answer correct to one decimal place. (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(128 \text{ corresponds to } 2 \text{ standard deviations above the mean}\)
(a) \(\therefore \frac{5\%}{2} = 2.5\% \text{ have an IQ score higher than Yin's.}\)
(b) \(1 \text{ standard deviation above the mean for City } B = 112 + 16 = 128\)
(b) \(\text{So Yin's IQ score is } 1 \text{ standard deviation above the mean of IQ scores in City } B.\)
(b) \(\therefore \text{percentage of population that have IQ score lower than Yin's is } 50\% + \frac{1}{2} \times 68\% = 84\%\)

betterResponses: calculate the 𝑧-score by formula
use the normal distribution diagram, indicating the mean and the 𝑧-scores clearly
accurately apply the percentage breakdown
set up and equate the two 𝑧-score equations and solve these equations simultaneously.
improvementAreas: using the 𝑧-score formula and calculating the corresponding percentages to the 𝑧-score
recognising that IQ referred to the score and it was the same in both cities
interpreting the need to form an equation relating to both cities as opposed to separate equations that could not be connected
solving equations involving fractions.

questions.title: Cricket chirps and temperature regression
questions.promptLatex:
A cricket is an insect. The male cricket produces a chirping sound.

A scientist wants to explore the relationship between the temperature in degrees Celsius and the number of cricket chirps heard in a 15-second time interval.

Once a day for 20 days, the scientist collects data. Based on the 20 data points, the scientist provides the information below.

* A box-plot of the temperature data is shown.

[Box-plot diagram showing temperature range from 13 to 28 degrees Celsius, with the median at 22, first quartile at 19, and third quartile at 25.]

* The mean temperature in the dataset is \(0.525^{\circ}\text{C}\) below the median temperature in the dataset.

* A total of 684 chirps was counted when collecting the 20 data points.

The scientist fits a least-squares regression line using the data \((x, y)\), where \(x\) is the temperature in degrees Celsius and \(y\) is the number of chirps heard in a 15-second time interval. The equation of this line is

\[y = -10.6063 + bx,\]

where \(b\) is the slope of the regression line.

The least-squares regression line passes through the point \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\) where \(\bar{x}\) is the sample mean of the temperature data and \(\bar{y}\) is the sample mean of the chirp data.

Calculate the number of chirps expected in a 15-second interval when the temperature is \(19^{\circ}\) Celsius. Give your answer correct to the nearest whole number.
workingLatex: Substituting \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\) into the regression equation: \(34.2 = -10.6063 + b(21.475)\)

carry through the value of an answer, for example, 𝑏= 2.08644 … for a more precise answer
provide an equation and calculate the answer from information they provided.

understanding that different pronumerals can have different meanings according to their application, for example, 𝑏 in this question.

questions.title: Present value of annuity withdrawals
questions.promptLatex:
Wilma deposited a lump sum into a new bank account which earns \(2\%\) per annum compound interest.

Present value interest factors for an annuity of \$1 for various interest rates (\(r\)) and numbers of periods (\(N\)) are given in the table.

| | \(r\) | Interest rate per period as a decimal | | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| \(N\) | \(0.01\) | \(0.015\) | \(0.02\) | \(0.025\) |
| \(10\) | \(9.471\) | \(9.222\) | \(8.983\) | \(8.752\) |
| \(20\) | \(18.046\) | \(17.169\) | \(16.351\) | \(15.589\) |
| \(30\) | \(25.808\) | \(24.016\) | \(22.396\) | \(20.930\) |

Wilma was able to make the following withdrawals from this account.

* \$1000 at the end of each year for twenty years (starting one year after the account is opened)
* \$3000 each year for ten years starting 21 years after the account is opened.

Calculate the minimum lump sum Wilma must have deposited when she opened the new account.
workingLatex: Present value of $1000 annuity for 20 years: \(16.351 \times 1000 = \$16 351\)
Present value of $3000 annuity for 30 years: \(22.396 \times 3000 = \$67 188\)
Present value of $3000 annuity for 20 years: \(16.351 \times 3000 = \$49 053\)

use only values in the 0.02 column of the table
add two ‘present value of an annuity’ calculations to give a lump sum
show structured working.

knowing how to use a present value interest table as opposed to the compound interest formula

questions.title: Coordinate Geometry, Series, Modelling, and Integration
questions.subtopic: Rates of change, integration, and arithmetic series
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The line \(\ell\), with equation \(x - 2y + 4 = 0\), passes through the point \(A(8, 6)\) and intersects the \(x\)-axis at the point \(B\).

The line through \(A\) perpendicular to \(\ell\) intersects the \(x\)-axis at the point \(C\).

(i) Find the equation of the line \(AC\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find the area of triangle \(ABC\). (2 marks)

(b) In an arithmetic series, the fourth term is \(6\) and the sum of the first \(16\) terms is \(120\).

Find the common difference. (3 marks)

(c) The number of leaves, \(L(t)\), on a tree \(t\) days after the start of autumn can be modelled by

\[L(t) = 200\,000\,e^{-0.14t}\]

(i) What is the number of leaves on the tree when \(t = 31\)? (1 mark)

(ii) What is the rate of change of the number of leaves on the tree when \(t = 31\)? (2 marks)

(iii) For what value of \(t\) are there \(100\) leaves on the tree? (2 marks)

(d) The diagram shows the graph of \(y = \frac{3x}{x^2 + 1}\).

The region enclosed by the graph, the \(x\)-axis and the line \(x = 3\) is shaded.

Calculate the exact value of the area of the shaded region. (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) (i) \(2y = x + 4 \implies y = \frac{1}{2}x + 2\). Slope of line \(AC\) is \(\frac{-1}{\frac{1}{2}} = -2\). \(y = -2x + K\). Passes through \(A(8, 6)\): \(6 = -2 \times 8 + K \implies K = 22\). Thus \(y = -2x + 22\).

(a) (ii) Height of \(\triangle ABC\) is \(6\). Base is distance between \(B\) and \(C\). \(B: x\text{-int } y = 0 \implies x - 2(0) + 4 = 0 \implies x = -4 \implies B(-4, 0)\). \(C: x\text{-int } y = 0 \implies 2x + 0 - 22 = 0 \implies x = 11 \implies C(11, 0)\). \(BC\) length is \(15\). Area \(\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times 6 = 45\).

(b) \(T_n = a + (n-1)d\). \(6 = T_4 = a + 3d\). \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(2a + (n-1)d)\). \(120 = S_{16} = \frac{16}{2}(2a + 15d) \implies 15 = 2a + 15d\). Solving the system: \(6 = a + 3d \implies 12 = 2a + 6d\). Subtracting: \(15 - 12 = 15d - 6d \implies 3 = 9d \implies d = \frac{1}{3}\).

(aii) recognise that the distance between two points along the \(x\)-axis does not require the distance formula
(b) use the Reference Sheet to obtain the correct rules for arithmetic series
(b) solve \(9d = 3\) to obtain \(d = 1/3\) and not \(d = 3\)

(ciii) achieve accuracy of calculations particularly when small decimals or fractions are involved

questions.title: Calculus, Geometry, and Tangent Analysis
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A particle is moving along a straight line. The particle is initially at rest. The acceleration of the particle at time \(t\) seconds is given by \(a = e^{2t} - 4\), where \(t \ge 0\).

Find an expression, in terms of \(t\), for the velocity of the particle. (2 marks)

(b) The derivative of a function \(y = f(x)\) is given by \(f'(x) = 3x^2 + 2x - 1\).

(i) Find the \(x\)-values of the two stationary points of \(y = f(x)\), and determine the nature of the stationary points. (2 marks)

(ii) The curve passes through the point \((0, 4)\).

Find an expression for \(f(x)\). (2 marks)

(iii) Hence sketch the curve, clearly indicating the stationary points. (2 marks)

(iv) For what values of \(x\) is the curve concave down? (1 mark)

(c) The regular hexagon \(ABCDEF\) has sides of length \(1\). The diagonal \(AE\) and the side \(CD\) are produced to meet at the point \(X\).

Copy or trace the diagram into your writing booklet.

Find the exact length of the line segment \(EX\), justifying your answer. (3 marks)

(d) The equation of the tangent to the curve \(y = x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 4\) at the point where \(x = 2\) is \(y = x - 4\).

Find the values of \(a\) and \(b\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (b)(i) \(f'(x) = 3x^2 + 2x - 1 = 0 \implies (3x - 1)(x + 1) = 0 \implies x = -1, x = \frac{1}{3}\). Testing values shows maximum at \(x = -1\) and minimum at \(x = \frac{1}{3}\).
(b)(iii) Plot points \((-1, 5)\) and \((\frac{1}{3}, \frac{103}{27})\) and draw the cubic curve.

(c) Interior angle of regular hexagon is \(120^\circ\). \(\triangle AEF\) is isosceles with \(\angle AEF = 30^\circ\). \(\angle AED = 120^\circ - 30^\circ = 90^\circ\). \(\angle DEX = 90^\circ\) (supplementary). In \(\triangle DEX\), \(\tan 60^\circ = \frac{EX}{1} \implies EX = \sqrt{3}\).

(d) \(y' = 3x^2 + 2ax + b\). At \(x = 2\), \(y' = 1 \implies 1 = 3(2)^2 + 2a(2) + b \implies 4a + b = -11\). At \(x = 2\), \(y = 2 - 4 = -2\). Substituting into \(y = x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 4\): \(-2 = 8 + 4a + 2b + 4 \implies 4a + 2b = -14\). Solving the system gives \(b = -3, a = -2\).

questions.title: Algebra, Geometry, Optimisation, and Probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Solve \(e^{2\ln x} = x + 6\). (2 marks)

(b) The triangle \(ABC\) is a right-angled triangle with the right angle at \(C\). The point \(D\) is chosen on \(AB\) so that \(CD\) is perpendicular to \(AB\).

The length of \(AD\) is \(p\), the length of \(BD\) is \(q\) and the length of \(CD\) is \(h\).

[Diagram showing a right-angled triangle ABC with altitude CD to the hypotenuse AB, where AD=p, BD=q, and CD=h.]

Show that \(h = \sqrt{pq}\). (3 marks)

(c) The entry points, \(R\) and \(Q\), to a national park can be reached via two straight access roads. The access roads meet the national park boundaries at right angles. The corner, \(P\), of the national park is \(8\text{ km}\) from \(R\) and \(1\text{ km}\) from \(Q\). The boundaries of the national park form a right angle at \(P\).

A new straight road is to be built joining these roads and passing through \(P\).

Points \(A\) and \(B\) on the access roads are to be chosen to minimise the distance, \(D\text{ km}\), from \(A\) to \(B\) along the new road.

Let the distance \(QA\) be \(x\text{ km}\).

[Diagram showing the national park, access roads, and the new road forming a triangle with points R, B, P, Q, A.]

(i) Show that \(D^2 = (x + 8)^2 + \left(\frac{8}{x} + 1\right)^2\). (3 marks)

(ii) Show that \(x = 2\) gives the minimum value of \(D^2\). (3 marks)

(d) The probability that a person chosen at random has red hair is \(0.02\).

(i) Two people are chosen at random.

What is the probability that at least ONE has red hair? (2 marks)

(ii) What is the smallest number of people that can be chosen at random so that the probability that at least ONE has red hair is greater than \(0.4\)? (2 marks)

(c)(ii) So has a stationary point at \(x = 2\)

questions.title: Financial modelling, kinematics, and calculus
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A person wins \$1\,000\,000 in a competition and decides to invest this money in an account that earns interest at \(6\%\) per annum compounded quarterly. The person decides to withdraw \$80\,000 from this account at the end of every fourth quarter. Let \(A_n\) be the amount remaining in the account after the \(n\)th withdrawal.

(i) Show that the amount remaining in the account after the withdrawal at the end of the eighth quarter is
\[A_2 = 1\,000\,000 \times 1.015^8 - 80\,000(1 + 1.015^4).\] (2 marks)

(ii) For how many years can the full amount of \$80\,000 be withdrawn? (3 marks)

(b) A particle moves in a straight line, starting at the origin. Its velocity, \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\), is given by \(v = e^{\cos t} - 1\), where \(t\) is in seconds.

The diagram shows the graph of the velocity against time.

Using one application of Simpson's rule, estimate the position of the particle when it first comes to rest. Give your answer correct to two decimal places. (3 marks)

(c) The diagram shows the region \(R\), bounded by the curve \(y = x^r\), where \(r \ge 1\), the \(x\)-axis and the tangent to the curve at the point \((1, 1)\).

(i) Show that the tangent to the curve at \((1, 1)\) meets the \(x\)-axis at \(\left(\frac{r-1}{r}, 0\right)\). (2 marks)

(ii) Using the result of part (i), or otherwise, show that the area of the region \(R\) is \(\frac{r-1}{2r(r+1)}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Find the exact value of \(r\) for which the area of \(R\) is a maximum. (3 marks)
workingLatex: (b) Particle comes to rest when \( v = 0 \), \( e^{\cos t} = 1 \), \( \cos t = 0 \), \( t = \frac{\pi}{2} \), Distance = \( \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} (e^{\cos t} - 1) dt \), Using Simpson's Rule: \( \frac{1}{6} \left( \frac{\pi}{2} - 0 \right) \left[ (e^{\cos 0} - 1) + 4(e^{\cos \frac{\pi}{4}} - 1) + (e^{\cos \frac{\pi}{2}} - 1) \right] \), \( = \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ e - 1 + 4e^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} - 4 + 0 \right] = \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ e + 4e^{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} - 5 \right] \approx 1.53 \)

(c) (ii) Area of \( R = \int_{0}^{1} x^r dx - \text{area of triangle} \), \( = \left[ \frac{x^{r+1}}{r+1} \right]_0^1 - \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{r-1}{r} \right) \times 1 \), \( = \frac{1}{r+1} - \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{r - (r - 1)}{r} \right) = \frac{1}{r+1} - \frac{1}{2r} = \frac{2r - (r + 1)}{2r(r+1)} = \frac{r-1}{2r(r+1)} \)

questions.title: Algebra, Geometry, Calculus, and Probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find the acute angle between the lines \(x - 2y + 1 = 0\) and \(y = 3x - 4\). (2 marks)

(b) For what values of \(x\) is \(\frac{x}{x + 1} < 2\)? (3 marks)

(c) The line segment \(AB\) is a diameter of the circle, centred at \(O\). The line \(CD\) is the tangent to the circle at \(C\) and \(BD\) is perpendicular to \(CD\), as shown in the diagram.

Prove that \(\angle ABC = \angle CBD\). (3 marks)

(d) Find the polynomial \(Q(x)\) that satisfies \(x^3 + 2x^2 - 3x - 7 = (x - 2)Q(x) + 3\). (2 marks)

(e) Find \(\int 2\sin^2 4x \, dx\). (2 marks)

(f) Prize-winning symbols are printed on \(5\%\) of ice-cream sticks. The ice-creams are randomly packed into boxes of \(8\).

(i) What is the probability that a box contains no prize-winning symbols? (1 mark)

(ii) What is the probability that a box contains at least \(2\) prize-winning symbols? (2 marks)
workingLatex: (c) Let \(\angle BCD = \alpha\). \(\angle CAB = \alpha\) (angle in alternate segment). \(\angle ACB = 90^\circ\) (angle in a semi-circle). \(\angle ABC = 90^\circ - \alpha\) (angle sum of \(\triangle\)). \(\angle CBD = 90^\circ - \alpha\) (angle sum of \(\triangle\)). Thus \(\angle ABC = \angle CBD\).
(d) Perform polynomial division of \(x^3 + 2x^2 - 3x - 7\) by \(x - 2\). The quotient is \(x^2 + 4x + 5\) with remainder \(3\). Thus \(Q(x) = x^2 + 4x + 5\).

questions.title: Rates, SHM, Parabola Geometry, and Cooling
questions.subtopic: Related rates, differential equations, and harmonic motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Distance \(A\) is inversely proportional to distance \(B\), such that \(A = \frac{9}{B}\), where \(A\) and \(B\) are measured in metres. The two distances vary with respect to time. Distance \(B\) is increasing at a rate of \(0.2 \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

What is the value of \(\frac{dA}{dt}\) when \(A = 12\)? (3 marks)

(b) A particle is moving along the \(x\)-axis in simple harmonic motion. The position of the particle is given by

\[x = \sqrt{2} \cos 3t + \sqrt{6} \sin 3t, \text{ for } t \ge 0.\]

(i) Write \(x\) in the form \(R \cos(3t - \alpha)\), where \(R > 0\) and \(0 < \alpha < \frac{\pi}{2}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find the two values for \(x\) where the particle comes to rest. (1 mark)

(iii) When is the first time that the speed of the particle is equal to half of its maximum speed? (2 marks)

(c) The point \(P(2ap, ap^2)\) lies on the parabola \(x^2 = 4ay\). The focus of the parabola is at \(S(0, a)\) and the directrix is the line \(y = -a\). The vertical line through \(P\) meets the directrix at \(M\). The tangent at \(P\) meets the \(y\)-axis at \(R\).

Show that \(\angle SPR = \angle SRP\). (3 marks)

(d) A refrigerator has a constant temperature of \(3^\circ\text{C}\). A can of drink with temperature \(30^\circ\text{C}\) is placed in the refrigerator.

After being in the refrigerator for 15 minutes, the temperature of the can of drink is \(28^\circ\text{C}\).

The change in the temperature of the can of drink can be modelled by \(\frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - 3)\), where \(T\) is the temperature of the can of drink, \(t\) is the time in minutes after the can is placed in the refrigerator and \(k\) is a constant.

(i) Show that \(T = 3 + Ae^{kt}\), where \(A\) is a constant, satisfies \(\frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - 3)\). (1 mark)

(ii) After 60 minutes, at what rate is the temperature of the can of drink changing? (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{dA}{dt} = -3.2 (b)(i) x = 2\sqrt{2} \cos(3t - \frac{\pi}{3}) (b)(ii) x = \pm 2\sqrt{2} (b)(iii) t = \frac{\pi}{18} (c) \text{Proof} (d)(i) \frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - 3) (d)(ii) \text{Temperature is decreasing at } 0.102^\circ \text{ per min.}\)
workingLatex: (b)\((i) R\cos(3t - \alpha) = R\cos 3t \cos \alpha + R\sin 3t \sin \alpha = \sqrt{2} \cos 3t + \sqrt{6} \sin 3t, R\cos \alpha = \sqrt{2}, R\sin \alpha = \sqrt{6}, R^2 = 2 + 6 = 8, R = 2\sqrt{2}, \tan \alpha = \sqrt{3}, \alpha = \frac{\pi}{3}, x = 2\sqrt{2} \cos(3t - \frac{\pi}{3})\)

(b)\((iii) x = 2\sqrt{2} \cos(3t - \frac{\pi}{3}), v = -6\sqrt{2} \sin(3t - \frac{\pi}{3}), \text{Max speed} = 6\sqrt{2}, \text{Half max speed} = 3\sqrt{2}, \frac{1}{2} = \sin(3t - \frac{\pi}{3}), 3t - \frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{\pi}{6}, 3t = \frac{\pi}{6}, t = \frac{\pi}{18}\)

(c) \(SP = PM = ap^2 + a, \text{tangent at } P \text{ is } y = px - ap^2, \text{at } R, x = 0 \implies y = -ap^2, R(0, -ap^2), SR = a + ap^2, SR = SP, \text{In } \triangle SPR, SP = SR, \angle SPR = \angle SRP\)

questions.title: Calculus, Binomial Expansion, Kinematics, and Projectile Motion
questions.subtopic: Integration, Projectile Motion, and Binomial Expansion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Use the substitution \(u = \cos^2 x\) to evaluate \[ \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin 2x}{4 + \cos^2 x} dx. \] (3 marks)

(b) In the expansion of \((5x + 2)^{20}\), the coefficients of \(x^k\) and \(x^{k+1}\) are equal.

What is the value of \(k\)? (3 marks)

(c) A particle moves in a straight line. At time \(t\) seconds the particle has a displacement of \(x\) m, a velocity of \(v\) m s\(^{-1}\) and acceleration \(a\) m s\(^{-2}\). Initially the particle has displacement \(0\) m and velocity \(2\) m s\(^{-1}\). The acceleration is given by \(a = -2e^{-x}\). The velocity of the particle is always positive.

(i) Show that \(v = 2e^{-\frac{x}{2}}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find an expression for \(x\) as a function of \(t\). (2 marks)

(d) The point \(O\) is on a sloping plane that forms an angle of \(45^\circ\) to the horizontal. A particle is projected from the point \(O\). The particle hits a point \(A\) on the sloping plane as shown in the diagram.

The equation of the line \(OA\) is \(y = -x\). The equations of motion of the particle are
\[ x = 18t \]
\[ y = 18\sqrt{3}t - 5t^2, \]
where \(t\) is the time in seconds after projection. Do NOT prove these equations.

(i) Find the distance \(OA\) between the point of projection and the point where the particle hits the sloping plane. (2 marks)

(ii) What is the size of the acute angle that the path of the particle makes with the sloping plane as the particle hits the point \(A\)? (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) Let \(u = \cos^2 x\), then \(\frac{du}{dx} = -2\cos x \sin x\), so \(du = -\sin 2x \, dx\). When \(x=0, u=1\); when \(x=\frac{\pi}{4}, u=\frac{1}{2}\). The integral is \(\int_{1}^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{-du}{4+u} = \int_{\frac{1}{2}}^{1} \frac{du}{4+u} = [\ln(4+u)]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{1} = \ln 5 - \ln \frac{9}{2} = \ln\left(\frac{10}{9}\right)\).

(d)(i) We want \(y = -x\). \(18\sqrt{3}t - 5t^2 = -18t \implies 5t^2 = (18\sqrt{3}+18)t\). \(t = \frac{18(\sqrt{3}+1)}{5}\). At this time \(x = 18 \cdot \frac{18(\sqrt{3}+1)}{5}\). \(OA = \sqrt{x^2+x^2} = \sqrt{2}x = \frac{18^2\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}+1)}{5} \approx 250.37 \text{ m}\).
