questions.title: Induction, Newton's Method, and Trigonometric Tangents
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove by mathematical induction that, for all integers \(n \ge 1\),

\[1(1!) + 2(2!) + 3(3!) + \dots + n(n!) = (n + 1)! - 1.\]

(b) The diagram shows the graph of \(y = \frac{1}{x - k}\), where \(k\) is a positive real number.

(i) By considering the graphs of \(y = x^2\) and \(y = \frac{1}{x - k}\), explain why the function \(f(x) = x^3 - kx^2 - 1\) has exactly one real zero. (2 marks)

Let this zero be \(\alpha\) and let \(x_1 = k\) be a first approximation to \(\alpha\).

(ii) Show that using one application of Newton's method gives a second approximation, \(x_2 = k + \frac{1}{k^2}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Show that \(x_1 < \alpha < x_2\). (3 marks)

(c) The diagram shows the two curves \(y = \sin x\) and \(y = \sin(x - \alpha) + k\), where \(0 < \alpha < \pi\) and \(k > 0\). The two curves have a common tangent at \(x_0\) where \(0 < x_0 < \frac{\pi}{2}\).

(i) Explain why \(\cos x_0 = \cos(x_0 - \alpha)\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(\sin x_0 = -\sin(x_0 - \alpha)\). (2 marks)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, find \(k\) in terms of \(\alpha\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Proof by induction. (b)(i) Exactly one solution. (b)(ii) \(x_2 = k + \frac{1}{k^2}\). (b)(iii) Proof that \(x_1 < \alpha < x_2\). (c)(i) \(\cos x_0 = \cos(x_0 - \alpha)\). (c)(ii) \(\sin x_0 = -\sin(x_0 - \alpha)\). (c)(iii) \(k = 2\sin\frac{\alpha}{2}\).
workingLatex: (b)(i) Adding \(y = x^2\) to the sketch shows exactly one point of intersection, so \(x^2 = \frac{1}{x-k}\) has exactly one solution.
(c)(i) Curves have common tangent at \(x_0\), so \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \frac{d}{dx}(\sin(x-\alpha) + k)\). Thus \(\cos x_0 = \cos(x_0 - \alpha)\).

(c)(ii) \(\sin^2 x_0 = 1 - \cos^2 x_0 = 1 - \cos^2(x_0 - \alpha) = \sin^2(x_0 - \alpha)\). So \(\sin x_0 = \pm \sin(x_0 - \alpha)\). Since \(k > 0\), \(\sin x_0 \ne \sin(x_0 - \alpha)\), hence \(\sin x_0 = -\sin(x_0 - \alpha)\).

(c)(iii) \(\sin x_0 = -\sin(x_0 - \alpha) + k \implies 2\sin x_0 = k \implies \sin x_0 = \frac{k}{2}\). Using \(x_0 - \alpha = -x_0\), we get \(2x_0 = \alpha\), so \(x_0 = \frac{\alpha}{2}\). Thus \(k = 2\sin\frac{\alpha}{2}\).

questions.title: Complex numbers, ellipses, and integration
questions.subtopic: Complex arithmetic, polar form, and integration techniques
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(z = 1 + 3i\) and \(w = 2 - i\).

(i) Find \(z + \bar{w}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Express \(\frac{z}{w}\) in the form \(x + iy\), where \(x\) and \(y\) are real numbers. (2 marks)

(b) Sketch the ellipse \(\frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1\), showing the intercepts, foci and directrices. (3 marks)

(c) Find \(\int \frac{dx}{x^2 + 10x + 29}\). (2 marks)

(d) Find \(\int \frac{6}{x^2 - 9} dx\). (3 marks)

(e) Let \(z = -1 + i\sqrt{3}\).

(i) Write \(z\) in modulus-argument form. (2 marks)

(ii) Find \(z^3\), giving your answer in the form \(x + iy\), where \(x\) and \(y\) are real numbers. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(3+4i\) (a)(ii) \(\frac{-1}{5} + \frac{7i}{5}\) (b) Foci at \(x = \pm \sqrt{3}\), directrices at \(x = \pm \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\) (c) \(\frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{x+5}{2}\right) + c\) (d) \(\ln|x-3| - \ln|x+3| + c\) (e)(i) \(2\left(\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) + i\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)\right)\) (e)(ii) \(x=8, y=0\)
workingLatex: (b) \(\frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1\), \(a=2, b=1\). \(b^2 = a^2(1-e^2) \implies 1 = 4(1-e^2) \implies e^2 = \frac{3}{4} \implies e = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\). Foci at \(x = \pm ae = \pm \sqrt{3}\). Directrices at \(x = \pm \frac{a}{e} = \pm \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\).

(d) \(\frac{6}{x^2-9} = \frac{a}{x+3} + \frac{b}{x-3} \implies a(x-3) + b(x+3) = 6\). Letting \(x=3 \implies b=1\), letting \(x=-3 \implies a=-1\). \(\int \left(\frac{1}{x-3} - \frac{1}{x+3}\right) dx = \ln|x-3| - \ln|x+3| + c\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) use basic complex number arithmetic to write down the answer.

(a) Sketch the region defined by \(\frac{\pi}{4} \le \arg(z) \le \frac{\pi}{2}\) and \(\text{Im}(z) \le 1\). (2 marks)

(b) The diagram shows two straight railway tracks that meet at an angle of \(\frac{2\pi}{3}\) at the point \(P\).

Trains \(A\) and \(B\) are joined by a cable which is 70 m long.

At time \(t\) seconds, train \(A\) is \(x\) metres from \(P\) and train \(B\) is \(y\) metres from \(P\).

Train \(B\) is towing train \(A\) and is moving at a constant speed of \(4 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) away from \(P\).

(i) Show that \(x^2 + xy + y^2 = 70^2\). (1 mark)

(ii) What is the value of \(\frac{dx}{dt}\) when train \(A\) is 30 metres from \(P\) and train \(B\) is 50 metres from \(P\)? (3 marks)

(c) Following a volcanic eruption, fine ash is deposited in a circular pattern around the point of eruption.

The depth in kilometres, \(D(x)\), of the fine ash can be modelled by
\[D(x) = Ae^{-kx},\]
where \(x\) is the distance in kilometres from the point of eruption and \(k > 0\).

Using the method of cylindrical shells, find an expression for the volume of fine ash deposited between 10 km and 40 km from the point of eruption. (4 marks)

(d) Consider the function \(f(x) = x^3 - 1\).

(i) Sketch the graph \(y = |f(x)|\). (1 mark)

(ii) Sketch the graph \(y = \frac{1}{f(x)}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Without using calculus, sketch the graph \(y = \frac{x}{f(x)}\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) A region in the complex plane bounded by the real axis and the line \(y=x\) for \(x>0\), with the angle \(\frac{\pi}{4}\) indicated. (b)(i) \(x^2 + y^2 + xy = 70^2\) (b)(ii) \(-4.7\) (c) \(2A\pi \left[ \left( -\frac{40}{k}e^{-40k} + \frac{10}{k}e^{-10k} \right) - \frac{1}{k^2}(e^{-40k} - e^{-10k}) \right]\) (d)(i) Sketch of \(y=|f(x)|\) (d)(ii) Sketch of \(y=\frac{1}{f(x)}\) (d)(iii) Sketch of curve passing through origin with vertical asymptote at \(x=1\)

questions.title: Hyperbolas, Solids of Revolution, and Projectile Motion
questions.subtopic: Projectile motion, volumes by integration, and conic tangents
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Two points \(P(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta)\) and \(Q(a \sec \phi, b \tan \phi)\) are on the hyperbola 
\[ \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1. \]
Let \(O\) be the origin and let \(M\) be the midpoint of \(PQ\). 
Tangents to the hyperbola are drawn at points \(P\) and \(Q\) as shown.

(i) Show that the equation of the tangent at \(P\) is 
\[ bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta = ab. \] (2 marks)

(ii) The tangents at \(P\) and \(Q\) meet at the point \(T(x_0, y_0)\). 
Show that \(x_0 = \frac{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi}\). (2 marks)

(iii) It is given that \(y_0 = \frac{b(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi}\). (Do NOT prove this.) 
By considering gradients, or otherwise, show that \(O, T\) and \(M\) are collinear. (3 marks)

(b) One end of a solid is the region bounded by \(y = 1 - x^2\) and the \(x\)-axis. The base of the solid is a rectangle perpendicular to the \(x,y\)-plane with one side lying on the \(x\)-axis. A flat face of the solid is formed by the plane passing through the point \((0, 1)\) making an angle of \(45^\circ\) to the base, as shown in the diagram.
Vertical cross-sections perpendicular to the \(x\)-axis are trapezia. One trapezium \(ABCD\) is shown, where \(A\) is the point \((x, 0)\).

(i) Show that the area of \(ABCD\) is \(\frac{1 - x^4}{2}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Find the volume of the solid. (1 mark)

(c) Two objects are projected from the same point on a horizontal surface. Object 1 is projected with an initial velocity of \(20 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) directed at an angle of \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) to the horizontal. Object 2 is projected 2 seconds later.
The equations of motion of an object projected from the origin with initial velocity \(v\) at an angle \(\theta\) to the \(x\)-axis are 
\[ x = vt \cos \theta \]
\[ y = -4.9t^2 + vt \sin \theta, \]
where \(t\) is the time after the projection of the object. Do NOT prove these equations.

(i) Show that Object 1 will land at a distance \(\frac{100\sqrt{3}}{4.9} \text{ m}\) from the point of projection. (2 marks)

(ii) The two objects hit the horizontal plane at the same place and time. 
Find the initial speed and the angle of projection of Object 2, giving your answer correct to 1 decimal place. (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) \(\frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1\), \(\frac{d}{dx}(\frac{x^2}{a^2}) - \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{y^2}{b^2}) = \frac{d}{dx}(1)\), \(\frac{2x}{a^2} - \frac{2y}{b^2}(\frac{dy}{dx}) = 0\), \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{xb^2}{ya^2}\). At \(P(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta)\), \(m = \frac{b \sec \theta}{a \tan \theta}\). Equation: \(y - b \tan \theta = \frac{b \sec \theta}{a \tan \theta}(x - a \sec \theta)\), \(ay \tan \theta - ab \tan^2 \theta = bx \sec \theta - ab \sec^2 \theta\), \(ab(\sec^2 \theta - \tan^2 \theta) = bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta\), \(\therefore ab = bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta\).

(a)(ii) \(y = \frac{bx \sec \theta - ab}{a \tan \theta}\) (tangent at \(P\)), \(y = \frac{bx \sec \phi - ab}{a \tan \phi}\) (tangent at \(Q\)). Equating: \(\frac{bx \sec \theta - ab}{a \tan \theta} = \frac{bx \sec \phi - ab}{a \tan \phi}\), \(a \tan \phi(bx \sec \theta - ab) = a \tan \theta(bx \sec \phi - ab)\), \(abx \tan \phi \sec \theta - a^2b \tan \phi = abx \tan \theta \sec \phi - a^2b \tan \theta\), \(a^2b(\tan \theta - \tan \phi) = abx(\tan \theta \sec \phi - \sec \theta \tan \phi)\), \(x_0 = \frac{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}{\tan \theta \sec \phi - \sec \theta \tan \phi}\).

(a)(iii) \(M = (\frac{a(\sec \theta + \sec \phi)}{2}, \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)}{2})\). Gradient \(OM = \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)}{a(\sec \theta + \sec \phi)}\). Gradient \(OT = \frac{b(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi} \div \frac{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi} = \frac{b(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}\). Showing gradients are equal: \(\frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)}{a(\sec \theta + \sec \phi)} \times \frac{\sec \theta - \sec \phi}{\sec \theta - \sec \phi} = \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{a(\sec^2 \theta - \sec^2 \phi)} = \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{a(\tan^2 \theta - \tan^2 \phi)} = \frac{b(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}\).

betterResponses: (a)(i) correctly rearrange the equation in a minimum numbers of steps, while still covering all important aspects.
(a)(ii) multiply the equations by \(\tan \theta\) and \(\tan \phi\) respectively, to eliminate the \(y\), or make \(y\) the subject of both equations.
(a)(iii) choose \(OM\) and \(OT\) as the two pairs involving the simplest algebraic manipulation

(a)(iii) equate the two gradients by: multiplying one of the gradients by the conjugate of either the numerator or denominator, equating the two gradients then showing the left and right hand sides are equal, proving that one gradient divided by the other equals one.

(c)(ii) then divide the equations to find \(\tan\alpha\), and substitute back in.

(a)(ii) determining whether the elimination or substitution method will lead to an easier solution, again with reference to equations with trigonometric ratios and where fractions could be avoided for most of the solution if a more efficient method was chosen.

questions.title: Mechanics, Calculus, and Trigonometric Induction
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A cyclist rides around a track. The track is a circle of radius \(r\) metres, banked at an angle of \(\theta\) to the horizontal.

Let \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\) be the velocity at which the cyclist experiences no net force parallel to the track.

By resolving forces, show that \(v\) satisfies \(v^2 = gr \tan \theta\) where \(g\) is the acceleration due to gravity. (3 marks)

(b) A parachutist jumps from a plane, falls freely for a short time and then opens the parachute. Let \(t\) be the time in seconds after the parachute opens, \(x(t)\) be the distance in metres travelled after the parachute opens, and \(v(t)\) be the velocity of the parachutist in \(\text{m s}^{-1}\).

The acceleration of the parachutist after the parachute opens is given by
\[\ddot{x} = g - kv,\]
where \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\) is the acceleration due to gravity and \(k\) is a positive constant.

(i) With an open parachute the parachutist has a terminal velocity of \(w \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

Show that \(w = \frac{g}{k}\). (1 mark)

At the time the parachute opens, the speed of descent is \(1.6w \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

(ii) Show that it takes \(\frac{1}{k} \log_e 6\) seconds to slow down to a speed of \(1.1w \text{ m s}^{-1}\). (4 marks)

(iii) Let \(D\) be the distance the parachutist travels between opening the parachute and reaching the speed \(1.1w \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

Show that \(D = \frac{g}{k^2} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \log_e 6 \right)\). (3 marks)

(c) (i) Show that \(\cot x - \cot 2x = \text{cosec } 2x\). (2 marks)

(ii) Use mathematical induction to prove that, for all \(n \ge 1\),
\[\sum_{r=1}^{n} \text{cosec}(2^r x) = \cot x - \cot(2^n x).\] (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(F + mg \sin \theta = \frac{mv^2}{r} \cos \theta\). Since there are no net forces parallel to the track, \(F = 0\). Thus, \(mg \sin \theta = \frac{mv^2}{r} \cos \theta\), which simplifies to \(v^2 = \frac{gr \sin \theta}{\cos \theta} = gr \tan \theta\).
(b)(i) Terminal velocity is a solution of \(\ddot{x} = 0\). So \(0 = g - kv\), which gives \(v = \frac{g}{k}\). Thus \(w = \frac{g}{k}\).

(b)(ii) \(\ddot{x} = \frac{dv}{dt} = g - kv\). Separating variables: \(\int \frac{dv}{g - kv} = \int dt\). Integrating: \(-\frac{1}{k} \ln |g - kv| = t + c\). At \(t = 0\), \(v = 1.6 \frac{g}{k}\), so \(c = -\frac{1}{k} \ln |g - 1.6g| = -\frac{1}{k} \ln | -0.6g |\). Substituting back and solving for \(t\) when \(v = 1.1w = 1.1 \frac{g}{k}\): \(-\frac{1}{k} \ln |g - 1.1g| = t - \frac{1}{k} \ln | -0.6g |\), which leads to \(t = \frac{1}{k} \ln |\frac{-0.6g}{-0.1g}| = \frac{1}{k} \ln 6\).

(b)(iii) From \(-\frac{1}{k} \ln |g - kv| = t - \frac{1}{k} \ln | -0.6g |\), we rearrange to find \(v = \frac{g}{k} [0.6e^{-kt} + 1]\). Integrating \(v = \frac{dx}{dt}\) with \(x(0) = 0\) gives \(x = \frac{g}{k} [-\frac{1}{k} 0.6e^{-kt} + t] + \frac{g}{k^2} (0.6)\). Substituting \(t = \frac{1}{k} \ln 6\) and \(v = 1.1w\) into the expression for \(x\) yields \(D = \frac{g}{k^2} (\frac{1}{2} + \ln 6)\).

(c)(i) \(\cot x - \cot 2x = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} - \frac{\cos 2x}{\sin 2x} = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} - \frac{\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x}{2 \sin x \cos x} = \frac{2 \cos^2 x - (\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x)}{2 \sin x \cos x} = \frac{\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x}{\sin 2x} = \frac{1}{\sin 2x} = \csc 2x\).

(c)(ii) Base case: \(n=1\), \(\csc(2x) = \cot x - \cot 2x\) (true from (i)). Assume true for \(n=k\): \(\sum_{j=1}^k \csc(2^j x) = \cot x - \cot(2^k x)\). For \(n=k+1\): \(\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} \csc(2^j x) = \cot x - \cot(2^k x) + \csc(2^{k+1} x)\). Using \(\csc(2^{k+1} x) = \cot(2^k x) - \cot(2^{k+1} x)\), the sum becomes \(\cot x - \cot(2^k x) + \cot(2^k x) - \cot(2^{k+1} x) = \cot x - \cot(2^{k+1} x)\). True for \(n=k+1\), so true for all \(n \ge 1\).

betterResponses: (a) balance horizontally and vertically or parallel to the track and resolve.

questions.title: Integrals, Probability, and Recurrence Relations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) (i) Show that \(\int_{-a}^{a} \frac{f(x)}{f(x) + f(-x)} \, dx = \int_{-a}^{a} \frac{f(-x)}{f(x) + f(-x)} \, dx\). (2 marks)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \(\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{e^x}{e^x + e^{-x}} \, dx\). (2 marks)

(b) An urn contains \(w\) white marbles and \(y\) yellow marbles. Two players, \(A\) and \(B\), play a game in which they take turns to randomly draw a marble from the urn.

If player \(A\) draws a white marble then player \(A\) wins. If player \(B\) draws a yellow marble then player \(B\) wins.

Each player replaces the marble if their draw is unsuccessful. The game stops as soon as one player wins.

Player \(A\) draws first.

(i) Explain why, in the case where \(w = y\), player \(A\) has a greater chance of winning than player \(B\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that for player \(B\) to have a greater chance of winning than player \(A\), \(\frac{y}{w} > \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}\). (3 marks)

(c) (i) Show that \(\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{(x+1)^2} \, dx = \ln 2 - \frac{1}{2}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Let \(I_n = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^n}{(x+1)^2} \, dx\).

Show that \(I_n = \frac{1}{2(n-1)} - \frac{n}{n-1} I_{n-1}\) for \(n \ge 2\). (3 marks)

(iii) Evaluate \(I_3\). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) Let \(I = \int_{-a}^{a} \frac{f(x)}{f(x)+f(-x)} dx\). Let \(u = -x\) so that \(du = -dx\). \(I = \int_{a}^{-a} \frac{f(-u)}{f(-u)+f(u)}(-du) = \int_{-a}^{a} \frac{f(-u)}{f(u)+f(-u)} du = \int_{-a}^{a} \frac{f(-x)}{f(x)+f(-x)} dx\).

(b)(i) The probability that \(A\) wins on the first draw is \(\frac{1}{2}\). If \(A\) doesn't win on the first draw then \(A\) still has a further chance of winning. \(\therefore P(A \text{ wins}) > \frac{1}{2}\). \(\therefore P(B \text{ wins}) < \frac{1}{2}\).

(b)(ii) \(P(B) = \frac{y}{w+y} \times \frac{y}{w+y} + \frac{y}{w+y} \times \frac{w}{w+y} \times \frac{y}{w+y} \times \frac{y}{w+y} + \dots\). Limiting sum \(= \frac{\frac{y^2}{(w+y)^2}}{1 - \frac{wy}{(w+y)^2}} = \frac{y^2}{(w+y)^2 - wy}\). For \(B\) to have a greater chance, \(P(B) > \frac{1}{2}\). \(\therefore \frac{y^2}{(w+y)^2 - wy} > \frac{1}{2}\). \(2y^2 > w^2 + 2wy + y^2 - wy\). \(y^2 - wy - w^2 > 0\). \((\frac{y}{w})^2 - \frac{y}{w} - 1 > 0\). Roots of quadratic are \(\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}\). \(\frac{y}{w} > \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\) (Positive root of quadratic).

(c)(ii) \(I_n = \int_{0}^{1} x^n (x-1)^{-2} dx = \int_{0}^{1} x^n \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{-1}{x+1}) dx = [\frac{-x^n}{x+1}]_{0}^{1} + n \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}}{x+1} dx = \frac{-1}{2} + n \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}(x+1-x)}{(x+1)^2} dx = \frac{-1}{2} + n \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}}{x+1} dx - n \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^n}{(x+1)^2} dx\). This approach requires adjustment to match the provided sample: \(I_n = \frac{-1}{2} + n \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}(x+1)}{(x+1)^2} dx = \frac{-1}{2} + n \int_{0}^{1} (\frac{x^n}{(x+1)^2} + \frac{x^{n-1}}{(x+1)^2}) dx = \frac{-1}{2} + nI_n + nI_{n-1}\). \(\therefore I_n(1-n) = \frac{-1}{2} + nI_{n-1}\). \(\therefore I_n = \frac{1}{2(n-1)} - \frac{n}{n-1}I_{n-1}\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) use the substitution \(u = -x\) and substitute correctly clearly showing the substitution step.
(a)(i) use the substitution \(u = -x\) and substitute correctly clearly showing the substitution step.
(a)(ii) use part (i) to gain \(\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{e^x}{e^x+e^{-x}} dx = \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{e^{-x}}{e^{-x}+e^x} dx\), then solve to gain \(I= 1\).

(b)(ii) recognise that the question involved a limiting sum not a sum to \(n\) terms then solve the inequality \(y^2-wy-w^2 > 0\) in terms of \(\frac{y}{w}\).
(c)(i) use integration by parts, write the integral \(\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{(x+1)^2} dx = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x+1-1}{(x+1)^2} dx\), or substitute \(x= u+ 1\) to gain \(\ln 2 - \frac{1}{2}\).

(c)(ii) use integration by parts to gain an expression in \(I_{n-1}\) then split \(n\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^{n-1}}{x+1} dx\) into \(n\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^n}{(x+1)^2} + \frac{x^{n-1}}{(x+1)^2} dx\) then rearrange to gain the correct answer.
(c)(iii) use \(I_2 = \frac{1}{2} - 2(\ln 2 - \frac{1}{2})\) combining parts (i) and (ii) then find \(I_3\).

questions.title: Polynomial roots, complex numbers, and geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) (i) Consider the equation \(x^3 - px + q = 0\), where \(p\) and \(q\) are real numbers and \(p > 0\).

Let \(r = \sqrt{\frac{4p}{3}}\) and \(\cos 3\theta = \frac{-4q}{r^3}\).

Show that \(r \cos \theta\) is a root of \(x^3 - px + q = 0\).

You may use the result \(4\cos^3 \theta - 3 \cos \theta = \cos 3\theta\). (Do NOT prove this.) (2 marks)

(ii) Let \(\alpha\), \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) be the roots of the equation

\[x^3 + 9x^2 + 15x - 17 = 0.\]

Show that \(\alpha + 3\), \(\beta + 3\), \(\gamma + 3\) are the roots of \(x^3 - 12x - 8 = 0\). (2 marks)

(iii) Using parts (i) and (ii), solve

\[x^3 + 9x^2 + 15x - 17 = 0.\] (3 marks)

(b) Let \(P(z) = z^4 - 2kz^3 + 2k^2z^2 + mz + 1\), where \(k\) and \(m\) are real numbers.

The roots of \(P(z)\) are \(\alpha\), \(\bar{\alpha}\), \(\beta\), \(\bar{\beta}\).

It is given that \(|\alpha| = 1\) and \(|\beta| = 1\).

(i) Show that \((\text{Re}(\alpha))^2 + (\text{Re}(\beta))^2 = 1\). (3 marks)

(ii) The diagram shows the position of \(\alpha\).

Copy or trace the diagram into your writing booklet.

On the diagram, accurately show all possible positions of \(\beta\). (2 marks)

(c) The vertices \(A\), \(B\), \(C\), \(D\) and \(E\) of a pentagon lie on a circle. The lengths of the sides opposite \(A\), \(B\), \(C\), \(D\) and \(E\) are \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(e\), respectively, as shown.

Copy or trace the diagram into your writing booklet.

Using the same convention as in the sine rule, the vertex name also represents the size of the angle at that vertex. For example, \(A\) is used to represent the size of \(\angle BAE\).

Show that \(\frac{a}{\sin(B + E)} = \frac{d}{\sin(C + E)}\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) Let \(x = r\cos\theta\). Then \(r = \sqrt{\frac{4p}{3}}\) and \(r^3 = \frac{4p}{3}r\). Substituting into \(x^3 - px + q = 0\) gives \(r^3\cos^3\theta - pr\cos\theta + q = \frac{4p}{3}r\cos^3\theta - pr\cos\theta + q = \frac{pr}{3}(4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta) + q = \frac{pr}{3}\cos 3\theta + q\). Setting this to zero gives \(\cos 3\theta = \frac{-3q}{pr} = \frac{-3q}{p\sqrt{4p/3}} = \frac{-4q}{r^3}\), which confirms \(r\cos\theta\) is a root.

(a)(ii) The roots are \(\alpha+3, \beta+3, \gamma+3\). Substituting \(x-3\) into the original cubic \(0 = (x-3)^3 + 9(x-3)^2 + 15(x-3) - 17\) yields \(x^3 - 9x^2 + 27x - 27 + 9x^2 - 54x + 81 + 15x - 45 - 17 = x^3 - 12x - 8\).

(a)(iii) Using \(p=12, q=-8\), \(r = \sqrt{\frac{4(12)}{3}} = 4\). Then \(\cos 3\theta = \frac{-4(-8)}{4^3} = \frac{1}{2}\). Thus \(3\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5\pi}{3}, \frac{7\pi}{3}\), so \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{9}, \frac{5\pi}{9}, \frac{7\pi}{9}\). The roots are \(4\cos\frac{\pi}{9}-3, 4\cos\frac{5\pi}{9}-3, 4\cos\frac{7\pi}{9}-3\).

(b)(i) Given \(\alpha+\bar{\alpha}+\beta+\bar{\beta} = 2k\), we have \(2\text{Re}(\alpha) + 2\text{Re}(\beta) = 2k \implies \text{Re}(\alpha) + \text{Re}(\beta) = k\). Expanding \(\alpha\bar{\alpha} + \alpha\beta + \alpha\bar{\beta} + \bar{\alpha}\beta + \bar{\alpha}\bar{\beta} + \beta\bar{\beta} = 2k^2\) leads to \(2\text{Re}(\alpha)\text{Re}(\beta) = k^2 - 1\). Substituting \(\text{Re}(\beta) = k - \text{Re}(\alpha)\) into the second equation gives \(\text{Re}(\alpha)^2 + \text{Re}(\beta)^2 = 1\).
(b)(ii) The sketch shows \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) on the unit circle in the complex plane, with \(\text{Re}(\alpha)^2 + \text{Re}(\beta)^2 = 1\).

(c) By the sine rule in \(\triangle ABC\), \(\frac{d}{\sin ACB} = \frac{e}{\sin BAC}\). In \(\triangle DBC\), \(\frac{a}{\sin DBC} = \frac{e}{\sin BDC}\). Since \(\angle BDC = \angle BAC\), \(\frac{a}{\sin DBC} = \frac{d}{\sin ACB}\). Since \(ABDE\) is cyclic, \(\angle DBC = B+E-\pi\), so \(\sin DBC = -\sin(B+E)\). Similarly \(\sin ACB = -\sin(C+E)\). Thus \(\frac{a}{\sin(B+E)} = \frac{d}{\sin(C+E)}\).

betterResponses: (a)(i) show, via direct substitution, that \(x= r\cos \theta\) satisfies the equation \(x^3 -px+ q= 0\), making use of the given result, \(4 \cos^3 \theta-3 \cos \theta= \cos 3\theta\) (and the assumptions given in the question for \(r\) and \(\cos 3\theta\))

(a)(i) commence their proof with the given result, \(4 \cos^3 \theta-3 \cos \theta= \cos 3\theta\), and then proceed to obtain the equation \((r\cos \theta)^3 -p(r\cos \theta) + q= 0\), thus showing that \(r\cos \theta\) is a root of the equation \(x^3 -px+ q= 0\).
(a)(ii) show, by expansion, that the equation \((x-3)^3 + 9(x-3)^2 + 15(x-3) -17 = 0\) (whose roots are \(\alpha+ 3, \beta+ 3\) and \(\gamma+ 3\)) is equivalent to the equation \(x^3 -12x-8 = 0\)
(a)(ii) show, by expansion, that the equation \((\alpha+ 3)^3 -12(\alpha+ 3) -8 = 0\) is equivalent to \(\alpha^3 + 9\alpha^2 + 15\alpha-17 = 0\) to reach the appropriate conclusion
(a)(ii) find the sum, sum of products and product of the roots \(\alpha+ 3, \beta+ 3\) and \(\gamma+ 3\).
(a)(iii) use the results of both part (i) and part (ii), to solve the equation
(a)(iii) first solve the equation \(x^3 -12x-8 = 0\) by putting \(p= 12, q= -8\) in part (i), followed by finding the values of \(r\) and \(\theta\)
(a)(iii) use part (ii) to write down the solution in terms of the roots of the equation \(x^3 -12x-8 = 0\).
(b)(i) find the sum, \(Re(\alpha) + Re(\beta)\), from the sum of roots of \(P(z) = 0\)
(b)(i) find the product, \(Re(\alpha) \times Re(\beta)\), from the sum of the products of pairs of roots of \(P(z) = 0\)
(b)(i) find both the sum and the product of \(Re(\alpha)\) and \(Re(\beta)\) in order to find the sum of their squares
(b)(i) find \((Re(\alpha))^2 + (Re(\beta))^2\) by expanding and finding \((\frac{\alpha+\bar{\alpha}}{2})^2 + (\frac{\beta+\bar{\beta}}{2})^2\).

(b)(ii) show all four possible positions of \(\beta\) in relation to the position of \(\alpha\), with reference to either \(\arg \alpha\) or the coordinates of \(\alpha\): \((Re(\alpha), Im(\alpha))\).

(c) apply the sine rule in two different triangles such as \(\Delta ABC\) and \(\Delta BCD\) to establish a key result \(\frac{a}{\sin DBC} = \frac{d}{\sin BCA}\) (or something similar) using the geometric property of equal angles in the same segment of a circle
(c) establish, through geometrical reasoning, applicable results involving angles \(B+ E\) and \(C+ E\) such as \(\angle DBC= B+ E-\pi\) and \(\angle BCA= C+ E-\pi\).
improvementAreas: (a)(i) guarding against making the mistake of assuming true, the statement which is required to be proven
(a)(i) showing all necessary working in their solution
(a)(i) justifying any substitution of variables.
(a)(ii) providing all necessary algebraic working such as expanding cubic expressions in order to prove a given result
(a)(ii) finding all three items (sum, sum of products and product of roots) to establish the appropriate cubic equation.
(a)(iii) finding all possible roots in a guided question involving cubic equations.
(b)(i) knowing and applying basic complex number identities such as \(Re(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}(\alpha+ \bar{\alpha})\) and \(\alpha\bar{\alpha} = |\alpha|^2\).
(b)(ii) responding appropriately to key directions in an examination question such as ‘accurately show’.
(c) making use of appropriate circle geometry theorems and properties
(c) stating the reasoning used in making geometrical assertions.

questions.title: Rabbit population growth modelling
questions.promptLatex:
Five rabbits were introduced onto a farm at the start of 2018. At the start of 2019 there were 10 rabbits on the farm. It is predicted that the number of rabbits on the farm will continue to double each year.

(a) Complete the following table. (1 mark)

| Start of year | 2018 | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of rabbits | 5 | 10 | | 40 | | |

(b) Complete the scale on the vertical axis and then plot the data from part (a) on the grid. (2 marks)

(c) Would a linear model or an exponential model better fit this graph? Explain briefly the reason for your answer. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) 2021: 40, 2022: 80, 2023: 160 (b) Points plotted at (2021, 40), (2022, 80), (2023, 160) (c) Exponential; it is a curve not a straight line.
workingLatex: (a) The number of rabbits doubles each year: \(20 \times 2 = 40\) for 2021, \(40 \times 2 = 80\) for 2022, and \(80 \times 2 = 160\) for 2023.
(b) Plot the points \((2021, 40)\), \((2022, 80)\), and \((2023, 160)\) on the provided grid.
(c) The relationship is exponential because the graph forms a curve rather than a straight line.

betterResponses: (c) identify the model as an exponential
complete the scale on the \(y\)-axis and plot all correct points.
improvementAreas: completing scales on relevant axis
plotting all points from their table of values
identifying that a non-linear model does not go in a straight line.

criteria.criterion: Writes correct scale and plots all three points
Writes correct scale or plots one correct point
Provides correct answer with correct reason

betterResponses: calculate the percentage for the number 3
calculate the number of times the number 6 or 2 was rolled and then the total percentage.
improvementAreas: calculating percentages
understanding the term ‘bias’.

criteria.criterion: Finds correct relative frequency for rolling a 3, or equivalent merit
Attempts to multiply 20 by a relative frequency, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Calculating total shed construction costs
questions.promptLatex:
Scott decided to have a shed built at his home. He agreed to the following costs:

* Materials
  - Timber: \$5400
  - Roof: \$1800
  - Nails: \$160
  - Paint: \$375
* \$70 per hour for the builder when working Monday to Friday
* \$30 per hour for the labourer when working Monday to Friday
* Builder and labourer paid time-and-a-half when working on Saturday.

It took six days to build the shed. The builder and labourer both worked from 8 am until 4 pm each day from Monday to Friday. On each of these days, they both took a 1 hour unpaid lunch break at 12 noon.

The builder and labourer also both worked 4 hours on Saturday, without a break, to finish the job.

What was the total cost to build the shed?

betterResponses: take account of the number of hours worked at normal pay and at time-and-a-half in order to calculate wages
calculate the normal pay for five days, rather than one.
improvementAreas: calculating overtime payments and sum all components to determine total cost
calculating the number of hours worked and subtracting the lunch hour
presenting their working to indicate what figures are used in calculations, especially necessary in the event of the final, or partial, answers being incorrect.

criteria.criterion: Provides an expression for the cost of builder and labourer, or equivalent merit
Provides an expression for the cost of builder and/or labourer working from Monday to Friday, or equivalent merit
Provides an expression for the cost of materials, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Scatterplot and line of best fit

questions.answerLatex: (a) Line of best fit drawn on the graph. (b) \(151 \text{ cm}\)
workingLatex: (a) A straight line is drawn through the data points such that it represents the trend of the data.
(b) Using the line of best fit, locate the value on the horizontal axis and read the corresponding height on the vertical axis, which is \(151 \text{ cm}\).

betterResponses: (a) draw a correct line of best fit and (b) read the correct coordinate.
improvementAreas: drawing an accurate line of best fit for the data given
using their line of best fit to state the correct prediction.

criteria.criterion: Provides correct answer based on their line of best fit

questions.title: Minimum spanning tree and shortest path
questions.subtopic: Minimum spanning tree and shortest path
questions.promptLatex:
The network diagram shows the tracks connecting 8 picnic sites in a nature park. The vertices \(A\) to \(H\) represent the picnic sites. The weights on the edges represent the distances along the tracks between the picnic sites, in kilometres.

(a) Each picnic site needs to provide drinking water. The main water source is at site \(A\).

By drawing a minimum spanning tree in the space below, calculate the minimum length of water pipes required to supply water to all the sites if the water pipes can only be laid along the tracks.

Minimum length = ...................................

(b) One day, the track between \(C\) and \(H\) is closed. State the vertices that identify the shortest path from \(C\) to \(E\) that avoids the closed track. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) 25 kilometres (b) C to G to H to E
workingLatex: (a) The minimum length is calculated by summing the edges of the minimum spanning tree: \(4 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 5 + 5 = 25\).
(b) The path is identified as \(C \to G \to H \to E\).

betterResponses: highlight the minimum spanning tree on their question which was helpful for them to successfully transfer this to the answer box
use the diagram to cross out path \(CH\) to visually understand what was being asked.
improvementAreas: interpreting the closed section of the network
looking at a variety of routes to find the shortest if they do not recall the algorithms.

criteria.criterion: Provides a correct minimum spanning tree and correct minimum length
Calculates the minimum length from an incorrect network diagram, or equivalent merit

assets.label: Network diagram of picnic sites
assets.alt: A graph with 8 nodes labeled A through H connected by edges with weights 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7.

Triangle \(ADC\) has a right angle at \(D\), with side \(AD = 2.5 \text{ cm}\) and side \(DC = 6 \text{ cm}\). Angle \(\theta\) is located at vertex \(A\) within triangle \(ADC\).)

Two right-angled triangles, \(ABC\) and \(ADC\), are shown.

Calculate the size of angle \(\theta\), correct to the nearest minute.

betterResponses: find \(AB\) by using either Pythagoras theorem or the cosine rule
use right-angled trigonometry (\(cos \theta = A/H\)) to find \(\theta\).
improvementAreas: remembering to square root their answer when using Pythagoras’ theorem or the cosine rule
practising with two triangles of different orientations and identifying the correct trig ratio
avoiding rounding too soon when solving problems with several steps
understanding that if angles are alternate then the appropriate parallel lines would have been marked on the diagram.

criteria.criterion: Uses a trigonometric ratio with the correct value of \(AC\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Bivariate data scatterplot and regression
questions.promptLatex:
A set of bivariate data is collected by measuring the height and arm span of seven children. The graph shows a scatterplot of these measurements.

(a) Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient for the data, correct to two decimal places. (\(1\) mark)

(b) Identify the direction and the strength of the linear association between height and arm span. (\(1\) mark)

(c) The equation of the least-squares regression line is shown.

\[ \text{Height} = 0.866 \times (\text{arm span}) + 23.7 \]

A child has an arm span of \(143 \text{ cm}\).

Calculate the predicted height for this child using the equation of the least-squares regression line. (\(1\) mark)

betterResponses: calculate the correct coefficient
use terminology involving both strength and direction.
improvementAreas: accurately substituting the data presented in the graph into their calculator
(c) only rounding answers that need to be rounded.

criteria.criterion: Provides correct answer with or without correct rounding

assets.label: Scatterplot of height vs arm span
assets.alt: A scatterplot with seven points showing a positive linear trend between arm span and height.

questions.title: Energy expenditure and conversion
questions.promptLatex:
Amanda uses \(80\) kilocalories of energy per kilometre while she is running.

She eats a burger that contains \(2180\) kilojoules of energy. How many kilometres will she need to run to use up all the energy from the burger? Give your answer correct to one decimal place. (\(1\) kilocalorie = \(4.184\) kilojoules)

betterResponses: make one of the conversions, such as kilojoules to kilocalories or kilocalories/km to kilojoules/km.
improvementAreas: solving using actual conversions rather than using guess and check as it is not reliable enough
identifying the difference between kilojoules and kilocalories.

criteria.criterion: Provides one correct conversion of energy, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Probability of selecting same coloured apples
questions.promptLatex:
A bowl of fruit contains 17 apples of which 9 are red and 8 are green.

Dennis takes one apple at random and eats it. Margaret also takes an apple at random and eats it.

By drawing a probability tree diagram, or otherwise, find the probability that Dennis and Margaret eat apples of the same colour. (3 marks)
workingLatex: Draw a probability tree diagram with the first apple being Red (\(P = \frac{9}{17}\)) or Green (\(P = \frac{8}{17}\)).

For the second apple, if the first was Red, the probabilities are Red (\(P = \frac{8}{16}\)) or Green (\(P = \frac{8}{16}\)). If the first was Green, the probabilities are Red (\(P = \frac{9}{16}\)) or Green (\(P = \frac{7}{16}\)).
Calculate the probability of the same colour: \(P(\text{same colour}) = P(RR) + P(GG)\)

betterResponses: determine the probabilities of drawing one and then a second apple in a non-replacement scenario
draw a useful probability tree and use it to answer the question
understand the concept of two-stage probability involving non-replacement.
improvementAreas: understanding when to add and when to multiply in probability
practising labelling tree diagrams with consistency
showing working as a bald answer here was awarded only one mark since this was one of the initial probabilities.

criteria.criterion: Provides correct probability tree diagram, or equivalent merit
Draws a relevant tree diagram, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Calculating time to zero BAC
questions.promptLatex:
The formula below is used to calculate an estimate for blood alcohol content (\(BAC\)) for females.

\[ BAC_{\text{Female}} = \frac{10N - 7.5H}{5.5M} \]

The number of hours required for a person to reach zero \(BAC\) after they stop consuming alcohol is given by the following formula.

\[ \text{Time} = \frac{BAC}{0.015} \]

The number of standard drinks in a glass of wine and a glass of spirits is shown.

(Diagram showing a wine glass labeled "Wine 1.2 standard drinks" and a glass of spirits labeled "Spirits 1 standard drink")

Hannah weighs \(60\text{ kg}\). She consumed \(3\) glasses of wine and \(4\) glasses of spirits between \(6:15\text{ pm}\) and \(12:30\text{ am}\) the following day. She then stopped drinking alcohol.

Using the given formulae, calculate the time in the morning when Hannah's \(BAC\) should reach zero.
workingLatex: \(\text{Time to zero } BAC = \frac{0.088257...}{0.015} = 5 \text{ h } 53 \text{ min (to nearest minute)}\)
\(\text{Time to get to zero } BAC = 12:30 \text{ am} + 5 \text{ hours } 53 \text{ min} = 6:23 \text{ am}\)

betterResponses: calculate the number of standard drinks
divide by 0.015 and convert this result to hours and minutes as required.
improvementAreas: avoiding rounding too early and giving answers that are truncated
showing all working steps clearly, so an earlier error can be tracked
making sure they finish the question by calculating the time asked for in the question.

criteria.criterion: Provides correct answer with appropriate calculations
Calculates the correct length of time for \(BAC\) to reach zero, or equivalent merit
Calculates the correct \(BAC\), or correct numerical expression
Provides correct value for \(N\) or \(H\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Minimum spanning tree and shortest path
questions.subtopic: Minimum spanning tree and shortest path
questions.promptLatex:
The network diagram shows the tracks connecting 8 picnic sites in a nature park. The vertices \(A\) to \(H\) represent the picnic sites. The weights on the edges represent the distances along the tracks between the picnic sites, in kilometres.

(a) Each picnic site needs to provide drinking water. The main water source is at site \(A\).

By drawing a minimum spanning tree in the space below, calculate the minimum length of water pipes required to supply water to all the sites if the water pipes can only be laid along the tracks. (2 marks)

Minimum length = ...................................

(b) One day, the track between \(C\) and \(H\) is closed. State the vertices that identify the shortest path from \(C\) to \(E\) that avoids the closed track. (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) 25 kilometres (b) Path goes from C to G to H to E
workingLatex: (a) The minimum length of water pipes needed is calculated by summing the edges of the minimum spanning tree: \(4 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 5 + 5 = 25\).
(b) The path goes from C to G to H to E.

betterResponses: show the minimum spanning tree
interpret the closed section of the network and give a clear guideline of where they were going
highlight the minimum spanning tree on the question which was helpful for them to successfully transfer this to the answer box
(b) use the diagram to cross out path \(CD\) to obtain a visual understanding of what was being asked.
improvementAreas: applying Prim’s algorithm correctly to find the minimum spanning tree
being able to supply some form of spanning tree which is a minimum.

criteria.criterion: Provides a correct minimum spanning tree and correct minimum length
Calculates the minimum length from an incorrect network diagram, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Area and perimeter of a rectangle
questions.promptLatex:
A rectangle has width \(w\) centimetres. The area of the rectangle, \(A\), in square centimetres, is \(A = 2w^2 + 5w\).

The graph of \(A = 2w^2 + 5w\) is shown.

(a) Explain why, in this context, the model \(A = 2w^2 + 5w\) only makes sense for the bold section of the graph. (1 mark)

(b) The area of the rectangle is \(18 \text{ cm}^2\). Calculate the perimeter of the rectangle. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) The width must be a positive number. (b) 22 cm
workingLatex: (a) The width of the rectangle must be a positive number.
(b) From the graph, if \(A = 18\) we have \(w = 2\).
(b) If the width of the rectangle is \(2 \text{ cm}\) the length must be \(9 \text{ cm}\).

betterResponses: successfully explain that the values below the \(x\)-axis and to the left of the \(y\)-axis related to a negative width or area, which was not possible
relate the model to the graph of a parabola
use guess and check (into \(18 = 2w^2 + 5w\)) to find the width
identify (2, 18) on the parabola and established the link between the graph and the model.
improvementAreas: using correct mathematical terminology or notation
writing brief explanations
identifying what the variables refer to (in this case width and area of a rectangle).

criteria.criterion: Provides correct width, or equivalent merit

assets.label: Graph of A = 2w^2 + 5w
assets.alt: A Cartesian coordinate system showing the parabola A = 2w^2 + 5w, where the portion of the curve for w > 0 is drawn with a thicker, bold line.

questions.title: Calculating income tax from Medicare levy

| \$37 001 to \$90 000 | \$3 572 plus 32.5c for each \$1 over \$37 000 |
| \$90 001 to \$180 000 | \$20 797 plus 37c for each \$1 over \$90 000 |
| \$180 001 and over | \$54 097 plus 45c for each \$1 over \$180 000 |

The Medicare levy is calculated as 2\% of taxable income.

For the 2018–2019 financial year, Charlie pays a Medicare levy of \$1934.80.

Calculate the tax payable on Charlie’s taxable income.

identify the correct line in the tax table and find the tax payable.
improvementAreas: reviewing the terms Medicare Levy and taxable income
given a percentage of the total amount, being able to find the total amount
understanding how to use the tax table and when to add and when to multiply.

betterResponses: identify they needed to use \(Speed = Distance / Time\) to solve the question
plot four points and sketch the correct curve smoothly.

questions.title: Geometry, Calculus, and Kinematics Problems
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A ship travels from Port \(A\) on a bearing of \(050^\circ\) for \(320 \text{ km}\) to Port \(B\). It then travels on a bearing of \(120^\circ\) for \(190 \text{ km}\) to Port \(C\).

(i) What is the size of \(\angle ABC\)? (1 mark)

(ii) What is the distance from Port \(A\) to Port \(C\)? Answer to the nearest \(10 \text{ kilometres}\). (2 marks)

(b) Find the equation of the tangent to the curve \(y = \cos 2x\) at \(x = \frac{\pi}{6}\). (3 marks)

(c) The diagram shows the square \(ABCD\). The point \(E\) is chosen on \(BC\) and the point \(F\) is chosen on \(CD\) so that \(EC = FC\).

(i) Prove that \(\triangle ADF\) is congruent to \(\triangle ABE\). (2 marks)

(ii) The side length of the square is \(14 \text{ cm}\) and \(EC\) has length \(4 \text{ cm}\). Find the area of \(AECF\). (2 marks)

(d) The displacement of a particle moving along the \(x\)-axis is given by
\[x = \frac{t^3}{3} - 2t^2 + 3t,\]
where \(x\) is the displacement from the origin in metres and \(t\) is the time in seconds, for \(t \ge 0\).

(i) What is the initial velocity of the particle? (1 mark)

(ii) At which times is the particle stationary? (2 marks)

(iii) Find the position of the particle when the acceleration is zero. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (c)(i) In \(\triangle ADF\) and \(\triangle ABE\), \(AD = AB\) (equal sides of square \(ABCD\)), \(\angle ADF = \angle ABE = 90^\circ\) (equal angles of square \(ABCD\)), \(DF = DC - FC = BC - EC = BE\) (equal sides of square \(ABCD\), \(FC = EC\) given), \(\therefore \triangle ADF \equiv \triangle ABE\) (SAS)
(c)(ii) Area square \(= 14^2 = 196 \text{ cm}^2\), Area triangle \(ABE = \frac{1}{2} \times 14 \times 10 = 70 \text{ cm}^2\), Area \(AECF = 196 - (2 \times 70) = 56 \text{ cm}^2\)

questions.title: Calculus, Geometry, and Exponential Modelling
questions.subtopic: Applications of differentiation and exponential modelling
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the curve \(y = 6x^2 - x^3\).

(i) Find the stationary points and determine their nature. (3 marks)

(ii) Given that the point \((2, 16)\) lies on the curve, show that it is a point of inflexion. (2 marks)

(iii) Sketch the curve, showing the stationary points, the point of inflexion and the \(x\) and \(y\) intercepts. (2 marks)

(b) In \(\triangle ABC\), sides \(AB\) and \(AC\) have length \(3\), and \(BC\) has length \(2\). The point \(D\) is chosen on \(AB\) so that \(DC\) has length \(2\).

(i) Prove that \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle CBD\) are similar. (2 marks)

(ii) Find the length \(AD\). (2 marks)

(c) The population of a country grew exponentially between 1910 and 2010. This population can be modelled by the equation \(P(t) = 92e^{kt}\), where \(P(t)\) is the population of the country in millions, \(t\) is the time in years after 1910 and \(k\) is a positive constant. The population of the country in 1960 was 184 million.

(i) Show that the value of \(k\) is \(0.0139\), correct to 4 decimal places. (2 marks)

(ii) Assuming that this model continues to be valid after 2010, estimate the population of the country in 2020 to the nearest million. (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) Stationary points at \((0, 0)\) and \((4, 32)\). (a)(ii) Point of inflexion at \((2, 16)\). (a)(iii) Sketch of curve with stationary points at \((0, 0)\) and \((4, 32)\) and point of inflexion at \((2, 16)\). (b)(i) Proof of similarity. (b)(ii) \(AD = \frac{5}{3}\). (c)(i) \(k \approx 0.0139\).
workingLatex: (a)(i) \(y = 6x^2 - x^3\), \(y' = 12x - 3x^2\), \(y'' = 12 - 6x\). Stationary points occur when \(y' = 0\). \(12x - 3x^2 = 0 \implies 3x(4 - x) = 0\). Thus \(x = 0\) or \(x = 4\). Stationary points at \((0, 0)\) and \((4, 32)\). At \(x = 0, y'' = 12 > 0\) (minimum). At \(x = 4, y'' = -12 < 0\) (maximum).
(a)(ii) Point of inflexion when \(y'' = 0\). \(12 - 6x = 0 \implies x = 2\). When \(x = 2 - \epsilon, y'' > 0\) and when \(x = 2 + \epsilon, y'' < 0\). Thus \((2, 16)\) is a point of inflexion.
(a)(iii) Sketch showing the curve passing through the origin, with a local minimum at \((0, 0)\), a point of inflexion at \((2, 16)\), and a local maximum at \((4, 32)\).

(b)(i) In \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle CBD\), angle \(B\) is common. Both triangles are isosceles so \(\angle CDB = \angle CBD = \angle ABC = \angle ACB\), hence \(\triangle ABC\) is similar to \(\triangle CBD\) (AA).

betterResponses: (ai) showing numerical results in their testing of stationary points using either the first or second derivative, using tables with labelled rows, indicating \(x\), \(dy/dx\) and slope, when using the first derivative test, factorising fully before solving \(dy/dx = 0\), showing their numerical results when completing the testing of points, stating conclusions by classifying their stationary points

(aii) using a table of values to show the change in sign of the second derivative and hence a change in concavity, indicating that they were substituting into the second derivative, showing numerical results to verify their test, stating a conclusion after their test
(aiii) drawing a smooth curve of at least 1/3 of a page, clearly showing important features, labelling all important features, including intercepts, labelling the axes

(bi) drawing the diagram given in the question onto their answer sheet, annotating their diagram with information provided in the question and geometric facts used in their proof, using correct geometric reasons, using an appropriate test for similarity and stated a conclusion, clearly showing the pairs of equal matching angles

(bii) drawing the triangles separately to help determine matching sides, clearly showing the ratio of matching sides, for example, \(CD/AD = BD/BC\), before substituting the lengths given in the question, correctly calculating the length of \(BD\) then \(AD\)
(ci) showing the substitution of \(P(t) = 184\) and \(t= 50\) into \(P(t) = 92e^{kt}\), using logarithms to solve for \(k\), understanding that the variable for population was given ‘in millions’
(cii) showing the substitution of \(k= 0.0139\) and \(t= 110\) into \(P(t) = 92e^{kt}\), calculating the population using a calculator
improvementAreas: (aii) remembering that the second derivative is used to test concavity not slope, realising that the \(y\)-coordinate is given in the question, so there is no need to show it

(aiii) understanding the difference between a point of inflexion and a horizontal point of inflexion, sketching the curve beyond the domain of the features to be shown, including passing through the \(x\)-intercept of 6

(bi) understanding the different tests for similar triangles, stating matching angle pairs correctly, for example, \(\angle ABD = \angle CBD\), setting out a proof, providing statements, in a logical order stating reasons at each step
(bii) defining variables used that are not given in the question, understanding that if using the SAS test for similarity that the included angle is required
(ci) recalculating the value for \(k\) if their result does not match value given in question, if using 184 000 000 also use 92 000 000
(cii) understanding that \(t\) is the number of years after 1910, so 2020 is represented by \(t= 110\)

questions.promptLatex:
(a) In \(\triangle KLM\), \(KL\) has length 3, \(LM\) has length 6 and \(\angle KLM = 60^\circ\). The point \(N\) is chosen on side \(KM\) so that \(LN\) bisects \(\angle KLM\). The length \(LN\) is \(x\).

(i) Find the exact value of the area of \(\triangle KLM\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, find the exact value of \(x\). (2 marks)

(b) The shaded region shown in the diagram is bounded by the curve \(y = x^4 + 1\), the \(y\)-axis and the line \(y = 10\). Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed when the shaded region is rotated about the \(y\)-axis. (3 marks)

(c) Let \(f(x) = x^3 + kx^2 + 3x - 5\), where \(k\) is a constant. Find the values of \(k\) for which \(f(x)\) has NO stationary points. (3 marks)

(d) An artist posted a song online. Each day there were \(2^n + n\) downloads, where \(n\) is the number of days after the song was posted.

(i) Find the number of downloads on each of the first 3 days after the song was posted. (1 mark)

(ii) What is the total number of times the song was downloaded in the first 20 days after it was posted? (2 marks)

(e) Two machines, \(A\) and \(B\), produce pens. It is known that 10% of the pens produced by machine \(A\) are faulty and that 5% of the pens produced by machine \(B\) are faulty.

(i) One pen is chosen at random from each machine. What is the probability that at least one of the pens is faulty? (1 mark)

(ii) A coin is tossed to select one of the two machines. Two pens are chosen at random from the selected machine. What is the probability that neither pen is faulty? (2 marks)

questions.title: Calculus, Trigonometry, and Coordinate Geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The length of daylight, \(L(t)\), is defined as the number of hours from sunrise to sunset, and can be modelled by the equation

\[L(t) = 12 + 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{366}\right),\]

where \(t\) is the number of days after 21 December 2015, for \(0 \le t \le 366\).

(i) Find the length of daylight on 21 December 2015. (1 mark)

(ii) What is the shortest length of daylight? (1 mark)

(iii) What are the two values of \(t\) for which the length of daylight is 11? (2 marks)

(b) The diagram shows the region bounded by the curve \(y = \frac{1}{x + 3}\) and the lines \(x = 0\), \(x = 45\) and \(y = 0\). The region is divided into two parts of equal area by the line \(x = k\), where \(k\) is a positive integer. (3 marks)

What is the value of the integer \(k\), given that the two parts have equal areas?

(c) The shaded region is enclosed by the curve \(y = x^3 - 7x\) and the line \(y = 2x\), as shown in the diagram. The line \(y = 2x\) meets the curve \(y = x^3 - 7x\) at \(O(0, 0)\) and \(A(3, 6)\). Do NOT prove this.

(i) Use integration to find the area of the shaded region. (2 marks)

(ii) Verify that one application of Simpson's rule gives the exact area of the shaded region. (2 marks)

The point \(P\) is chosen on the curve \(y = x^3 - 7x\) so that the tangent at \(P\) is parallel to the line \(y = 2x\) and the \(x\)-coordinate of \(P\) is positive.

(iii) Show that the coordinates of \(P\) are \((\sqrt{3}, -4\sqrt{3})\). (2 marks)

(iv) Find the area of \(\triangle OAP\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) 14 hours (ii) 10 hours (iii) 122 days after 21 December and 244 days after 21 December (b) k = 9 (c)(i) \frac{81}{4} (ii) \frac{81}{4} (iii) (\sqrt{3}, -4\sqrt{3}) (iv) 9\sqrt{3}\)
workingLatex: (a)(ii) \(-1 \le \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{366}\right) \le 1\), so \(-2 \le 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{366}\right) \le 2\). Shortest length is \(12 - 2 = 10\) hours.

(a)(iii) \(11 = 12 + 2\cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{366}\right) \implies \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{366}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}\). Thus \(\frac{2\pi t}{366} = \frac{2\pi}{3}\) or \(\frac{4\pi}{3}\), giving \(t = 122\) or \(t = 244\).

(c)(iv) Perpendicular distance from \((\sqrt{3}, -4\sqrt{3})\) to \(2x - y = 0\) is \(d = \frac{|2\sqrt{3} + 4\sqrt{3}|}{\sqrt{2^2 + (-1)^2}} = \frac{6\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}}\). Base of triangle is distance from \((0,0)\) to \((\sqrt{3}, 2\sqrt{3})\) which is \(\sqrt{3+12} = \sqrt{15}\). Area \(= \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{15} \times \frac{6\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{5}} = 9\sqrt{3}\).

betterResponses: (ai) showing the substitution of \(t= 0\) into \(L(t) = 12 + 2\cos(2\pi t/366)\)
(aii) using the fact that \(-1 \leq \cos x \leq 1\) to find the range of \(L(t)\) and hence the solution, drawing a graph to find the minimum value
(aiii) understanding how to work with trigonometric functions in terms of \(\pi\)
(b) equating the two parts of equal area using definite integrals, finding primitive functions and solving the resulting logarithmic equation, knowing that \(k > 0\) and discarding \(k= -15\)

(ci) knowing the required area could be represented by \(A = \int (f(x) - g(x)) dx\) where \(a\) and \(b\) are \(x\)-values, simplifying \(f(x) - g(x)\) before finding the primitive function and calculating the definite integral

(cii) using the correct formula for Simpson’s rule and understanding that three function values are required for one application of the rule, using their simplified function from (ci) to calculate their function values, using a table to show their three functions values
(ciii) equating the gradient function for the tangent at \(P\) and the gradient of the line \(y = 2x\) and solving for \(x\), finding the \(y\)-coordinate by substitution
(civ) understanding that the triangle is not right angled, using the formula for perpendicular distance provided in the Reference Sheet
improvementAreas: (ai) knowing that \(\cos 0 = 1\)
(aii) using the mark allocation as a guide to the amount of working expected

(aiii) understanding how to find ‘angles of any magnitude’, changing the subject of the equation to \(t\), understanding the difference between radians and degrees when solving trigonometric equations
(b) using the correct order when substituting limits into the primitive function, solving logarithmic equations
(ci) writing correct statements, involving definite integrals, to use in area problems, applying absolute value of functions correctly
(cii) using the formula from the reference sheet and understanding the meaning of \((b-a)/6\), finding the correct function to use in Simpson’s rule
(ciii) showing substitutions used to find the \(y\)-coordinate, performing calculations with surds
(civ) understanding that the equation of the line used in the perpendicular distance formula needs to be in general form, finding the correct angle at the origin to use in the sine rule

questions.title: Cones, Probability, and Compound Interest
questions.subtopic: Applications of differentiation, financial modelling, and probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A sector with radius \(10 \text{ cm}\) and angle \(\theta\) is used to form the curved surface of a cone with base radius \(x \text{ cm}\), as shown in the diagram.

The volume of a cone of radius \(r\) and height \(h\) is given by \(V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h\).

(i) Show that the volume, \(V \text{ cm}^3\), of the cone described above is given by \[V = \frac{1}{3}\pi x^2 \sqrt{100 - x^2}\]. (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{\pi x(200 - 3x^2)}{3\sqrt{100 - x^2}}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Find the exact value of \(\theta\) for which \(V\) is a maximum. (3 marks)

(b) A game involves rolling two six-sided dice, followed by rolling a third six-sided die. To win the game, the number rolled on the third die must lie between the two numbers rolled previously. For example, if the first two dice show \(1\) and \(4\), the game can only be won by rolling a \(2\) or \(3\) with the third die.

(i) What is the probability that a player has no chance of winning before rolling the third die? (2 marks)

(ii) What is the probability that a player wins the game? (2 marks)

(c) Kara deposits an amount of \(\$300\,000\) into an account which pays compound interest of \(4\%\) per annum, added to the account at the end of each year. Immediately after the interest is added, Kara makes a withdrawal for expenses for the coming year. The first withdrawal is \(\$P\). Each subsequent withdrawal is \(5\%\) greater than the previous one.

Let \(\$A_n\) be the amount in the account after the \(n\)th withdrawal.

(i) Show that \(A_2 = 300\,000(1.04)^2 - P[(1.04) + (1.05)]\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(A_3 = 300\,000(1.04)^3 - P[(1.04)^2 + (1.04)(1.05) + (1.05)^2]\). (1 mark)

(iii) Show that there will be money in the account when \[\left(\frac{105}{104}\right)^n < 1 + \frac{3000}{P}\]. (3 marks)

questions.title: Acceleration of a particle given velocity

What is the acceleration of the particle at \(x = 1\)?

B. \(3 \text{ m s}^{-2}\)
C. \(6 \text{ m s}^{-2}\)
D. \(12 \text{ m s}^{-2}\)

B. \( x = \frac{n\pi}{2} + (-1)^n \frac{\pi}{12} \)

questions.title: Simple harmonic motion displacement function
questions.promptLatex:
A particle is moving in simple harmonic motion. The displacement of the particle is \(x\) and its velocity, \(v\), is given by the equation \(v^2 = n^2 (2kx - x^2)\), where \(n\) and \(k\) are constants. The particle is initially at \(x = k\).

Which function, in terms of time \(t\), could represent the motion of the particle?

A. \(x = k \cos(nt)\)
B. \(x = k \sin(nt) + k\)
C. \(x = 2k \cos(nt) - k\)
D. \(x = 2k \sin(nt) + k\)

questions.title: Polynomials, Logarithms, Trigonometry, Geometry, and Calculus
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the polynomial \(P(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6\).

(i) Show that \(x = 1\) is a zero of \(P(x)\). (1 mark)

(ii) Find the other zeros. (2 marks)

(b) Solve \(\log_2 5 + \log_2 (x - 2) = 3\). (2 marks)

(c) Write \(\sqrt{3} \sin x + \cos x\) in the form \(R \sin(x + \alpha)\) where \(R > 0\) and \(0 \le \alpha \le \frac{\pi}{2}\). (2 marks)

(d) Two secants from the point \(C\) intersect a circle as shown in the diagram. What is the value of \(x\)? (2 marks)

(e) Consider the function \(f(x) = \frac{1}{4x - 1}\).

(i) Find the domain of \(f(x)\). (1 mark)

(ii) For what values of \(x\) is \(f(x) < 1\)? (2 marks)

(f) Evaluate \(\int_{-3}^{0} \frac{x}{\sqrt{1 - x}} \, dx\), using the substitution \(u = 1 - x\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (c) \(\sqrt{3}\sin x + \cos x = R\sin(x + \alpha) = R\sin x \cos \alpha + R\cos x \sin \alpha\). Equating coefficients: \(R\cos \alpha = \sqrt{3}\), \(R\sin \alpha = 1\). \(\tan \alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \implies \alpha = \frac{\pi}{6}\). \(R^2 = (\sqrt{3})^2 + 1^2 = 4 \implies R = 2\). Result: \(2\sin(x + \frac{\pi}{6})\).

(f) Let \(u = 1 - x\), \(du = -dx\). Limits: \(x = -3 \to u = 4\), \(x = 0 \to u = 1\). \(I = \int_4^1 \frac{1 - u}{\sqrt{u}}(-du) = \int_1^4 (u^{\frac{1}{2}} - u^{-\frac{1}{2}}) du = [\frac{2}{3}u^{\frac{3}{2}} - 2u^{\frac{1}{2}}]_1^4 = (\frac{2}{3}(8) - 2(2)) - (\frac{2}{3} - 2) = \frac{16}{3} - 4 - \frac{2}{3} + 2 = \frac{14}{3} - 2 = \frac{8}{3} = 2\frac{2}{3}\). Note: The sample answer provided in the image contains a calculation error in the final step, but the steps shown are: \(I = \int_1^4 (u^{-\frac{1}{2}} - u^{\frac{1}{2}}) du = [2u^{\frac{1}{2}} - \frac{2}{3}u^{\frac{3}{2}}]_1^4 = (2(2) - \frac{2}{3}(8)) - (2(1) - \frac{2}{3}(1)) = 4 - \frac{16}{3} - 2 + \frac{2}{3} = 2 - \frac{14}{3} = -\frac{8}{3} = -2\frac{2}{3}\).

(ai) use long division to show that \(x-1\) is a factor and hence that \(x=1\) is a zero of \(P(x)\)
(aii) divide the polynomial by the factor \(x-1\) to generate the quotient \(x^2 -x-6\); then factorised the quotient correctly to arrive at the other zeroes \(x=3\) and \(x=-2\)

questions.title: Calculus, Trigonometry, Probability, and Parabola Geometry
questions.topic: Calculus, Statistical Analysis, and Trigonometric Functions
questions.subtopic: Integration, Related Rates, Binomial Distribution, and Inverse Trig
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find \(\int \cos^2(3x)dx\). (2 marks)

(b) A ferris wheel has a radius of 20 metres and is rotating at a rate of 1.5 radians per minute. The top of a carriage is \(h\) metres above the horizontal diameter of the ferris wheel. The angle of elevation of the top of the carriage from the centre of the ferris wheel is \(\theta\).

(i) Show that \(\frac{dh}{d\theta} = 20 \cos \theta\). (1 mark)

(ii) At what speed is the top of the carriage rising when it is 15 metres higher than the horizontal diameter of the ferris wheel? Give your answer correct to one decimal place. (2 marks)

(c) Let \(f(x) = \sin^{-1}x + \cos^{-1}x\).

(i) Show that \(f'(x) = 0\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, prove \(\sin^{-1}x + \cos^{-1}x = \frac{\pi}{2}\). (1 mark)

(iii) Hence, sketch \(f(x) = \sin^{-1}x + \cos^{-1}x\). (1 mark)

(d) A group of 12 people sets off on a trek. The probability that a person finishes the trek within 8 hours is 0.75.

Find an expression for the probability that at least 10 people from the group complete the trek within 8 hours. (2 marks)

(e) The points \(P(2ap, ap^2)\) and \(Q(2aq, aq^2)\) lie on the parabola \(x^2 = 4ay\). The focus of the parabola is \(S(0, a)\) and the tangents at \(P\) and \(Q\) intersect at \(T(a(p + q), apq)\). (Do NOT prove this.)

The tangents at \(P\) and \(Q\) meet the \(x\)-axis at \(A\) and \(B\) respectively, as shown.

(i) Show that \(\angle PAS = 90^\circ\). (2 marks)

(ii) Explain why \(S, B, A, T\) are concyclic points. (1 mark)

(iii) Show that the diameter of the circle through \(S, B, A\) and \(T\) has length \(a\sqrt{(p^2 + 1)(q^2 + 1)}\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{1}{12}\sin(6x) + \frac{x}{2} + c (b)(i) \frac{dh}{d\theta} = 20\cos\theta (ii) 19.8 (c)(i) f'(x) = 0 (ii) \frac{\pi}{2} (iii) \text{Sketch with domain } -1 \le x \le 1 \text{ and constant value } \frac{\pi}{2} (d) \binom{12}{10}(0.75)^{10}(0.25)^2 + \binom{12}{11}(0.75)^{11}(0.25) + \binom{12}{12}(0.75)^{12} (e)(i) \angle PAS = 90^\circ (ii) \text{Concyclic points proof} (iii) a\sqrt{(p^2+1)(q^2+1)}\)
workingLatex: (c)\((iii) \text{Domain is } -1 \le x \le 1 \text{, graph is a horizontal line at } y = \frac{\pi}{2}\)

(e)\((i) \text{Tangent at } P \text{ is } y = px - ap^2 \implies A = (ap, 0) \implies \text{slope } AS = \frac{a-0}{0-ap} = -\frac{1}{p} \implies \text{slope } AS \times \text{slope } PT = -\frac{1}{p} \times p = -1 \implies \angle PAS = 90^\circ\)
(e)\((ii) \angle PAS = 90^\circ \implies \angle TAS = 90^\circ \text{ and } \angle QBS = 90^\circ \implies \angle TBS = 90^\circ \text{, so } A, B, S, T \text{ are concyclic}\)

questions.title: Induction, Inverse Functions, and Projectile Motion
questions.subtopic: Induction, Inverse Functions, and Projectile Motion
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove by mathematical induction that, for \(n \ge 1\),

\[ 2 - 6 + 18 - 54 + \dots + 2(-3)^{n-1} = \frac{1 - (-3)^n}{2} \]

(b) The diagram shows the graph \(y = \frac{x}{x^2 + 1}\), for all real \(x\).

Consider the function \(f(x) = \frac{x}{x^2 + 1}\), for \(x \ge 1\).

The function \(f(x)\) has an inverse. (Do NOT prove this.)

(i) State the domain and range of \(f^{-1}(x)\). (2 marks)

(ii) Sketch the graph \(y = f^{-1}(x)\). (1 mark)

(iii) Find an expression for \(f^{-1}(x)\). (3 marks)

(c) An object is projected from the origin with an initial velocity of \(V\) at an angle \(\theta\) to the horizontal. The equations of motion of the object are

\[ x(t) = Vt \cos\theta \]
\[ y(t) = Vt \sin\theta - \frac{gt^2}{2} \]. (Do NOT prove these.)

(i) Show that when the object is projected at an angle \(\theta\), the horizontal range is \(\frac{V^2}{g} \sin 2\theta\). (2 marks)

(ii) Show that when the object is projected at an angle \(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\), the horizontal range is also \(\frac{V^2}{g} \sin 2\theta\). (1 mark)

(iii) The object is projected with initial velocity \(V\) to reach a horizontal distance \(d\), which is less than the maximum possible horizontal range. There are two angles at which the object can be projected in order to travel that horizontal distance before landing. Let these angles be \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\), where \(\beta = \frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha\).

Let \(h_\alpha\) be the maximum height reached by the object when projected at the angle \(\alpha\) to the horizontal.

Let \(h_\beta\) be the maximum height reached by the object when projected at the angle \(\beta\) to the horizontal.

Show that the average of the two heights, \(\frac{h_\alpha + h_\beta}{2}\), depends only on \(V\) and \(g\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Proof by induction. (b)(i) Domain: \(0 < x \le \frac{1}{2}\), Range: \(y \ge 1\). (b)(ii) Sketch of \(y = f^{-1}(x)\) starting at \((\frac{1}{2}, 1)\) and curving upwards. (b)(iii) \(f^{-1}(x) = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 - 4x^2}}{2x}\). (c)(i) Range = \(\frac{V^2}{g} \sin 2\theta\). (c)(ii) Proof showing \(\sin 2(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta) = \sin 2\theta\). (c)(iii) \(\frac{h_\alpha + h_\beta}{2} = \frac{V^2}{4g}\).
workingLatex: (b)(iii) Let \(y = \frac{x}{x^2 + 1} \implies x^2y - x + y = 0\). Using quadratic formula: \(x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4y^2}}{2y}\). Since \(x=2, y=\frac{2}{5}\), we select the positive root: \(f^{-1}(x) = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 - 4x^2}}{2x}\).

(c)(iii) Max height \(h = \frac{V^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}\). Thus \(h_\alpha = \frac{V^2 \sin^2 \alpha}{2g}\) and \(h_\beta = \frac{V^2 \sin^2 \beta}{2g}\). Since \(\beta = \frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha\), \(\sin \beta = \cos \alpha\). Then \(h_\alpha + h_\beta = \frac{V^2}{2g}(\sin^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \alpha) = \frac{V^2}{2g}\). Therefore \(\frac{h_\alpha + h_\beta}{2} = \frac{V^2}{4g}\).

betterResponses: (a) establish \(\frac{1-(-3)(-3)^k}{2} = \frac{1-(-3)^{k+1}}{2}\) correctly
(a) reach a successful conclusion for \(S_{k+1}\)
(bi) determine the correct domain as \(0 < x \leq \frac{1}{2}\)
(bii) obtain a correct graph in (bi) coming from the correct domain and range
(biii) solve the equation by using quadratic formula or completing the square method
(biii) use the domain restriction correctly to provide final solution
(ci) complete the proof by using the appropriate double angle identity
(cii) establish the result from (ci) by using the range determined
(ciii) link the working from the previous two parts to establish equations in terms of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)
(ciii) correctly use the given substitution \(\beta= \frac{\pi}{2} -\alpha\) to eliminate \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)
improvementAreas: (a) accurately manipulating algebra
(a) managing the connection between LHS and RHS effectively
(bi) considering \(f^{-1}(x)\) as more than the interchange between \(x\) of \(y\) only
(bii) checking the graph with a vertical line test
(bii) enhancing the diagram with more detail such as the line \(x= y\) or the point (½, 1)
(biii) moving on algebraically from \(x= \frac{y}{y^2+1}\)
(biii) using the domain restriction to establish the final solution \(f^{-1}(x) = \frac{1+\sqrt{1-4x^2}}{2x}\)

(ci) not using the Cartesian equation of the projectile unless necessary
(cii) considering the aim of this problem and why this proof might be needed or linked to
(ciii) using the trigonometric identities more efficiently and effectively to simplify the algebra
(ciii) finding an average for the two range equations

questions.title: Cyclic quadrilateral, binomial identity, geometric series
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The diagram shows quadrilateral \(ABCD\) and the bisectors of the angles at \(A\), \(B\), \(C\) and \(D\). The bisectors at \(A\) and \(B\) intersect at the point \(P\). The bisectors at \(A\) and \(D\) meet at \(Q\). The bisectors at \(C\) and \(D\) meet at \(R\). The bisectors at \(B\) and \(C\) meet at \(S\).

The diagram shows quadrilateral \(ABCD\) with angle bisectors meeting at points \(P\), \(Q\), \(R\), \(S\) as described.

Copy or trace the diagram into your writing booklet.

Show that \(PQRS\) is a cyclic quadrilateral. (3 marks)

(b)

(i) By considering the expansions of \((1+(1+x))^n\) and \((2+x)^n\), show that
\[
\binom{n}{r}\binom{r}{r} + \binom{n}{r+1}\binom{r+1}{r} + \binom{n}{r+2}\binom{r+2}{r} + \cdots + \binom{n}{n}\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n}{r}2^{n-r}.
\]
(3 marks)

(ii) There are 23 people who have applied to be selected for a committee of 4 people.

The selection process starts with Selector \(A\) choosing a group of at least 4 people from the 23 people who applied.

Selector \(B\) then chooses the 4 people to be on the committee from the group Selector \(A\) has chosen.

In how many ways could this selection process be carried out? (2 marks)

(c) In triangle \(ABC\), \(BC\) is perpendicular to \(AC\). Side \(BC\) has length \(a\), side \(AC\) has length \(b\) and side \(AB\) has length \(c\). A quadrant of a circle of radius \(x\), centred at \(C\), is constructed. The arc meets side \(BC\) at \(E\). It touches the side \(AB\) at \(D\), and meets side \(AC\) at \(F\). The interval \(CD\) is perpendicular to \(AB\).

The diagram shows triangle \(ABC\) with the described quadrant of radius \(x\) centred at \(C\), meeting \(BC\) at \(E\), touching \(AB\) at \(D\), and meeting \(AC\) at \(F\).

(i) Show that \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle ACD\) are similar. (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(x = \dfrac{ab}{c}\). (1 mark)

From \(F\), a line perpendicular to \(AC\) is drawn to meet \(AB\) at \(G\), forming the right-angled triangle \(GFA\). A new quadrant is constructed in triangle \(GFA\) touching side \(AB\) at \(H\). The process is then repeated indefinitely.

The diagram shows the repeated construction with quadrants of radii \(x\), \(x_1\), \(\ldots\) inside successive right-angled triangles.

(iii) Show that the limiting sum of the areas of all the quadrants is \(\dfrac{\pi ab^2}{4(2c-a)}\). (4 marks)

(iv) Hence, or otherwise, show that \(\dfrac{\pi}{2} < \dfrac{2c-a}{b}\). (1 mark)
questions.answerLatex: (a) Proof shown. (b)(i) Proof shown. (b)(ii) 4 642 570 240. (c)(i) Proof shown. (c)(ii) \(x = \frac{ab}{c}\). (c)(iii) \(A = \frac{\pi ab^2}{4(2c - a)}\). (c)(iv) Proof shown.
workingLatex: (a) Let \(\alpha = \frac{1}{2}\angle A\), \(\beta = \frac{1}{2}\angle B\), \(\gamma = \frac{1}{2}\angle C\), and \(\delta = \frac{1}{2}\angle D\). Then \(2\alpha + 2\beta + 2\gamma + 2\delta = 360^\circ\) (\(\angle\) sum quadrilateral). \(\therefore \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 180^\circ\). \(\angle PSR = \angle BSC = 180^\circ - (\beta + \gamma)\) (\(\angle\) sum of \(\triangle BSC\)). \(\angle PQR = \angle AQD = 180^\circ - (\alpha + \delta)\) (\(\angle\) sum of \(\triangle AQD\)). \(\therefore \angle PSR + \angle PQR = 2 \times 180^\circ - (\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta) = 2 \times 180^\circ - 180^\circ = 180^\circ\). \(\therefore PQRS\) is cyclic.

(b)(i) Consider the coefficients of \(x^r\) on both sides of \((2 + x)^n = (1 + (1 + x))^n\). LHS: \(\binom{n}{r} 2^{n-r}\). RHS: \(\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(1+x)^k\). The coefficient of \(x^r\) is \(\sum_{k=r}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \binom{k}{r}\). Equating these gives the result.

(c)(i) In \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle ACD\), \(\angle ACB = \angle ADC\) (\(CD \perp AB\), \(\triangle ABC\) right-angled at \(C\)), \(\angle BAC = \angle CAD\) (common). \(\therefore \triangle ABC \parallel\parallel \triangle ACD\).

(c)(iii) \(\frac{GF}{a} = \frac{b-x}{b} \implies GF = \frac{b}{b}(1 - \frac{a}{c})a\). \(x_1 = (1 - \frac{a}{c})x\). Area \(A = \frac{1}{4}\pi x^2(1 + (1 - \frac{a}{c})^2 + (1 - \frac{a}{c})^4 + \dots) = \frac{1}{4}\pi x^2 \frac{1}{1 - (1 - \frac{a}{c})^2} = \frac{1}{4}\pi x^2 \frac{c^2}{a(2c - a)} = \frac{\pi ab^2}{4(2c - a)}\).
(c)(iv) Area of quadrants < area of \(\triangle ABC\) \(\implies \frac{\pi ab^2}{4(2c - a)} < \frac{ab}{2} \implies \frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{2c - a}{2b} \implies \frac{\pi}{2} < \frac{2c - a}{b}\).

betterResponses: (a) quickly determine the 3 main geometrical relationships, namely \(2\alpha+ 2\beta+ 2\gamma+ 2\delta= 360^\circ\) (angle sum of quadrilateral \(ABCD\)), \(\angle PRQ= \angle BRC= 180^\circ -(\beta+ \gamma)\) (angle sum of \(\Delta BRC\)), \(\angle PSQ= \angle ASD= 180^\circ -(\alpha+ \delta)\) (angle sum of \(\Delta ASD\)) which formed the basis of the proof
(bi) determine the need to find the terms in \(x^r\) from the outset of the question
(bii) simply state that from (bi) the counting could be done in \(\binom{23}{4} \binom{21}{9}\) ways
(ci) show the common angle and one other angle for the proof
(cii) show a simple relationship between the corresponding sides for a correct solution
(cii) use equivalent expressions for the area of the triangle to show the result
(ciii) realise that the successive radii need to be written in terms of \(a, b\) or \(c\) so that the areas would also be in these terms
(ciii) apply appropriate geometric series formula with a correct ratio, typically \(r= (1 -\frac{a}{c})^2\)
(civ) recognise that the inequality connected the area of the triangle with the area of the quadrants
improvementAreas: (a) seeking to find angles directly related to the cyclic quadrilateral in question
(a) following the instructions within the question which asked them to copy or trace the diagram, as many did not do this
(bi) recognising the need to search for the coefficients of a particular power of \(x\) in an expansion
(bi) expanding \((2 + x)^n\) and \((1 + (1 + x))^n\) correctly with the powers in the correct order
(bi) using the \(\binom{n}{r}\) expression in binomial expansions
(bii) avoiding making huge arithmetic calculations
(bii) avoiding misinterpreting the question as a probability question

questions.title: Complex Numbers, Polynomials, Integration, and Geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(z = 2 + 3i\) and \(w = 1 - i\).

(i) Find \(zw\). (1 mark)

(ii) Express \(\overline{z} - \frac{2}{w}\) in the form \(x + iy\), where \(x\) and \(y\) are real numbers. (2 marks)

(b) The polynomial \(p(x) = x^3 + ax^2 + b\) has a zero at \(r\) and a double zero at \(4\).
Find the values of \(a\), \(b\) and \(r\). (3 marks)

(c) By writing \(\frac{x^2 - x - 6}{(x + 1)(x^2 - 3)}\) in the form \(\frac{a}{x + 1} + \frac{bx + c}{x^2 - 3}\),
find \(\int \frac{x^2 - x - 6}{(x + 1)(x^2 - 3)} dx\). (4 marks)

(d) The points \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) on the Argand diagram represent the complex numbers \(u\), \(v\) and \(w\) respectively.
The points \(O\), \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) form a square as shown on the diagram.
It is given that \(u = 5 + 2i\).

(i) Find \(w\). (1 mark)

(ii) Find \(v\). (1 mark)

(iii) Find \(\arg\left(\frac{w}{v}\right)\). (1 mark)

(e) The circle centred at \(O\) with radius \(r\) has a diameter \(AB\). Points \(C\) and \(D\) are chosen on the circle as shown in the diagram. The chord \(AC\) has length \(d\).
Show that \(d = 2r \sin D\). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (e) \(\angle ABC = \angle ADC = \theta\) (angles standing on same arc are equal). \(\angle BCA = 90^\circ\) (angle in a semi-circle). \(\sin \theta = \frac{d}{2r}\), \(\sin D = \frac{d}{2r} \implies d=2r\sin D\)

questions.title: Calculus, Implicit Differentiation, and Graph Sketching
questions.subtopic: Integration, implicit differentiation, and curve sketching
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The base of a solid is the region enclosed by the parabola \(x = 1 - y^2\) and the \(y\)-axis. Each cross-section perpendicular to the \(y\)-axis is an equilateral triangle, as shown in the diagram.

Find the volume of the solid. (3 marks)

(b) A curve has equation \(x^2 + xy + y^2 = 3\).

(i) Use implicit differentiation to show that \(\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{2x + y}{x + 2y}\). (2 marks)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, find the coordinates of the points on the curve where \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\). (2 marks)

(c) Find \(\int \frac{x^2 + 2x}{x^2 + 2x + 5} \, dx\). (3 marks)

(d) The diagram shows the graph of the function \(f(x) = \frac{x}{x - 1}\).

Draw a separate half-page graph for each of the following functions, showing all asymptotes and intercepts.

(i) \(y = |f(x)|\) (1 mark)

(ii) \(y = (f(x))^2\) (2 marks)

(iii) \(y = x + f(x)\) (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{15} (b)(i) \frac{-(2x+y)}{x+2y} (b)(ii) (1, -2) \text{ and } (-1, 2) (c) x - \frac{5}{2}\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right) + c (d)(i)-(iii) Sketches provided in marking guidelines.\)

betterResponses: (a) show the ability to obtain the cross-sectional area of a general slice, \(A = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}x^2\), before obtaining an integral in terms of \(y\) for the volume of the solid. Once established, the majority of responses correctly evaluated the integral
(bi) show the ability to differentiate implicitly before solving for \(dy/dx\)
(bii) obtain a quadratic equation in one variable by deducing that \(y = -2x\) since \(dy/dx = 0\)

(bii) solving this quadratic equation and substituting the solutions into \(y = -2x\) provided the coordinates of the points on the curve where \(dy/dx = 0\). The better responses did not substitute the quadratic’s solutions into the equation of the curve, an approach that leads to extra and unwanted points

(c) evaluate the integral correctly. The majority of successful responses added and subtracted the number 5 into the numerator to rewrite the integrand equal to \(1 - \frac{5}{x^2+2x+5}\). Completing the square in the denominator and applying standard integral results complete the integration
(di) provide a sketch showing the asymptotes and the cusp at the origin
(dii) provide a sketch showing the asymptotes and the turning point at the origin
(diii) write the equation of the curve \(y = x + f(x)\) in the form \(y = x + 1 + \frac{1}{x-1}\) which aids in producing the correct sketch with oblique asymptote \(y = x + 1\)
