(a) simplification of algebraic expressions involving squares
(bi) practising implicit differentiation for a variety of curves written implicitly
(bii) practising their algebraic skills as a significant number did not solve \(2x + y = 0\) correctly, instead getting \(y = 2x\)

(bii) checking their solutions; in this case by substituting the coordinates of the points found into both the equation of the curve and into the equation \(dy/dx = 0\), to ensure they have the correct solution
(c) making use of formulae given in examinations, such as standard integral results
(di) clearly showing key features of graphs, such as cusps and asymptotes

(diii) clearly showing key features of graphs, such as turning points and vertical and oblique asymptotes

questions.title: Calculus, Complex Numbers, Mechanics, and Geometry
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The graph \(y^2 = x(1 - x)^2\) is shown.

Use the method of cylindrical shells to find the volume of the solid formed when the shaded region is rotated about the line \(x = 1\). (3 marks)

(b) Let \(z = 1 - \cos 2\theta + i \sin 2\theta\), where \(0 < \theta \le \pi\).

(i) Show that \(|z| = 2 \sin \theta\). (2 marks)

(ii) Show that \(\arg(z) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\). (2 marks)

(c) A particle of mass \(m\) is attached to a light inextensible string of length \(\ell\). The string makes an angle \(\theta\) to the vertical.

The particle is moving in a circle of radius \(r\) on a smooth horizontal surface. The particle is moving with uniform angular velocity \(\omega\). The forces on the particle are the tension \(T\) in the string; the normal reaction \(N\) to the horizontal surface; and the gravitational force \(mg\).

The particle remains in contact with the horizontal surface.

By resolving the forces on the particle in the horizontal and vertical directions, show that \(\omega^2 \le \frac{g}{\ell \cos \theta}\). (3 marks)

(d) The points \(P\left(cp, \frac{c}{p}\right)\) and \(Q\left(cq, \frac{c}{q}\right)\) lie on the rectangular hyperbola \(xy = c^2\).
The line \(PQ\) has equation \(x + pqy = c(p + q)\). (Do NOT prove this.)
The \(x\) and \(y\) intercepts of \(PQ\) are \(R\) and \(S\) respectively, as shown in the diagram.

(i) Show that \(PS = QR\). (3 marks)

The point \(T(2at, at^2)\) lies on the parabola \(x^2 = 4ay\). The tangent to the parabola at \(T\) intersects the rectangular hyperbola \(xy = c^2\) at \(A\) and \(B\) and has equation \(y = tx - at^2\). (Do NOT prove this.) The point \(M\) is the midpoint of the interval \(AB\). One such case is shown in the diagram.

(ii) Using part (i), or otherwise, show that \(M\) lies on the parabola \(2x^2 = -ay\). (2 marks)

betterResponses: (a) develop an expression for \(\partial x\) from a slice which leads to the integral \(4\pi \int_{0}^{1} (1-x) \sqrt{x(1-x)} dx\) and then integrated correctly
(bi) find \(\sqrt{(1-\cos 2\theta)^2 + (\sin 2\theta)^2}\) and simplify for the correct expression
(bi) write \(2\sin \theta(\sin \theta + i\cos \theta)\) in mod-arg form and achieve the correct answer
(bii) use \(arg(z) = \tan^{-1}(\frac{Im(z)}{Re(z)})\) to gain \(\tan^{-1}(\cot \theta)\) and hence \(arg z = (\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta)\)
(bii) use the mod-arg answer from part (i) to express as \(2\sin \theta(\cos (\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta) + i\sin (\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta))\)
(c) find correct vertical and horizontal forces, eliminate \(T\) to find an expression in \(N\), then state \(N \ge 0\) and solved to gain the correct inequality
(di) find the points \(S\) and \(R\) then use the distance formula to show \(PQ = QR\)
(di) find the midpoint of \(PQ\) and \(SR\) and show they had a shared midpoint
(dii) find the midpoint of the \(x\) and \(y\)-intercepts of the tangent and show that it satisfies the locus equation
improvementAreas: (a) careful development of the \(\partial x\) expression for cylindrical shells
(a) simplifying algebraic expressions involving squares and square roots
(bi) using the most efficient trigonometric substitutions to simplify expressions in order to reduce errors
(bii) using different variables for different angles. Solutions such as \(\tan \theta = \cos \theta\) should have read \(\tan (arg(z)) = \cot \theta\) or \(\tan (\alpha) = \cot \theta\)
(bii) learning and recognising the complementary angle results
(c) not quoting standard circular motion formulas but considering the context of the question
(c) setting out solutions that involve inequalities with clear explanations of why the inequality has been introduced
(di) learning the correct formulas for distance and midpoint
(di) taking care with algebraic simplification

(dii) looking for the most efficient method to gain an answer. Students who solved \(xy = c^2\) and \(y = tx - at^2\) simultaneously then used the quadratic formula found it difficult to obtain the correct result

questions.title: Calculus, Mechanics, and Probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Using the substitution \(t = \tan\frac{\theta}{2}\) evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{d\theta}{2 - \cos\theta}\). (3 marks)

(b) A falling particle experiences forces due to gravity and air resistance. The acceleration of the particle is \(g - kv^2\), where \(g\) and \(k\) are positive constants and \(v\) is the speed of the particle. (Do NOT prove this.)

Prove that, after falling from rest through a distance, \(h\), the speed of the particle will be \(\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}(1 - e^{-2kh})}\). (3 marks)

(c) Let \(I_n = \int_{-3}^{0} x^n \sqrt{x + 3} \, dx\) for \(n = 0, 1, 2, \dots\)

(i) Show that, for \(n \ge 1\), \(I_n = \frac{-6n}{3 + 2n} I_{n-1}\). (3 marks)

(ii) Find the value of \(I_2\). (2 marks)

(d) Three people, \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\), play a series of \(n\) games, where \(n \ge 2\). In each of the games there is one winner and each of the players is equally likely to win.

(i) What is the probability that player \(A\) wins every game? (1 mark)

(ii) Show that the probability that \(A\) and \(B\) win at least one game each but \(C\) never wins, is \(\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^n - 2\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n\). (1 mark)

(iii) Show that the probability that each player wins at least one game is \(\frac{3^{n-1} - 2^n + 1}{3^{n-1}}\). (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) Let \(t = \tan\frac{\theta}{2}\), then \(d\theta = \frac{2dt}{1+t^2}\). The integral becomes \(I = \int_0^1 \frac{2dt}{1+3t^2} = \frac{2}{3} \int_0^1 \frac{dt}{(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}})^2 + t^2} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \sqrt{3} [\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3}t)]_0^1 = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{9}\pi\).

(b) Given \(\ddot{x} = g - kv^2\), we have \(v \frac{dv}{dx} = g - kv^2\), so \(\frac{dx}{dv} = \frac{v}{g-kv^2}\). Integrating, \(x = \int \frac{v}{g-kv^2} dv = -\frac{1}{2k} \ln(g-kv^2) + C\). Using \(x=0, v=0\), \(C = \frac{1}{2k}\ln(g)\). Thus \(x = -\frac{1}{2k}\ln(\frac{g-kv^2}{g}) = -\frac{1}{2k}\ln(1-\frac{k}{g}v^2)\). Rearranging gives \(v = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}(1 - e^{-2kh})}\).

(c)(i) Using integration by parts with \(u = x^n\) and \(dv = (x+3)^{1/2} dx\), \(I_n = [\frac{2x^n(x+3)^{3/2}}{3}]_{-3}^0 - \int_{-3}^0 \frac{2nx^{n-1}(x+3)^{3/2}}{3} dx = 0 - \frac{2n}{3} \int_{-3}^0 x^{n-1}(x+3)\sqrt{x+3} dx = -\frac{2n}{3} I_n - 2n I_{n-1}\). Rearranging gives \(I_n(1 + \frac{2n}{3}) = -2n I_{n-1}\), so \(I_n = \frac{-6n}{3+2n}I_{n-1}\).
(d)(ii) \(P(C \text{ never wins}) = (\frac{2}{3})^n\). Subtracting cases where only \(A\) wins or only \(B\) wins: \((\frac{2}{3})^n - 2(\frac{1}{3})^n\).

(d)(iii) \(P(\text{each wins at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{one person wins all}) - P(\text{two people win at least one but not the third}) = 1 - [3(\frac{1}{3})^n + 3((\frac{2}{3})^n - 2(\frac{1}{3})^n)] = 1 - \frac{2^n-2+3}{3^{n-1}} = \frac{3^{n-1} - 2^n + 1}{3^{n-1}}\).

betterResponses: (a) substitute \(t\) for \(\theta\) and were able to integrate the expression correctly
(ci) make sure they use the correct choices for \(u\) and \(dv/dx\) in integration by parts questions, that will lead to the required expression
(ci) use integration by parts to gain an expression in \(x^{n-1}\) then split \(x^{n-1}(x+3)^{3/2}\) into \(x^n(x+3)^{1/2} + 3x^{n-1}(x+3)^{1/2}\) to gain a correct expression
(cii) use the reduction formula then calculated \(I_0\)
(dii) use clear and concise language, clearly interpreting \(2 \times (1/3)^n\) as ‘either A or B winning’ every game and \((2/3)^n\) as ‘C never wins’, to gain the correct expression
(diii) look at previous parts to see how they relate to the question
(diii) consider the probability of one person winning every game (part i) and the probability of one person never winning (part ii) then simplify their answer to give correct expression
improvementAreas: (a) taking care when manipulating the constant \(a\) in the integration of expressions of the form \(\int_{0}^{1} \frac{2}{1+at^2} dt\)
(b) making sure they include \(+c\) when integrating
(b) reading the initial conditions in mechanics questions more carefully
(b) choosing the correct expression of acceleration to use in different situations
(ci) taking care with the limits of integration
(ci) following through algebra steps carefully from one line to the next so as not to lose negative signs, \(n\) or fractions
(cii) taking care with negative signs
(cii) using the reduction formula down to \(I_0\), not stopping at \(I_1\)
(dii) writing clear and concise answers, especially when the question is only worth 1 mark
(diii) using correct reasoning to obtain the given answer

criteria.criterion: Obtains correct integral, or equivalent merit
Correctly applies integration by parts, or equivalent merit
Attempts to apply integration by parts or equivalent merit
Finds \(I_2\) in terms of \(I_0\), or equivalent merit
Correctly deals with the case where one player wins all the games, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Ellipse Geometry, De Moivre's Theorem, and Inequalities
questions.subtopic: De Moivre's theorem and algebraic proof
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The point \(P(a \cos \theta, b \sin \theta)\), where \(0 < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}\), lies on the ellipse 
\[\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1, \text{ where } a > b.\] 
The point \(A(a \cos \theta, a \sin \theta)\) lies vertically above \(P\) on the auxiliary circle \(x^2 + y^2 = a^2\). The point \(B\) lies on the auxiliary circle such that \(\angle AOB = \frac{\pi}{2}\) and the point \(Q\) lies on the ellipse vertically below \(B\), as shown.

(i) Show that \(Q\) has coordinates \((-a \sin \theta, b \cos \theta)\). (2 marks)

The line \(QO\) meets the ellipse again at \(Q'(a \sin \theta, -b \cos \theta)\). (Do NOT prove this.)

(ii) Show that the minimum size of \(\angle POQ'\) is \(\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2ab}{a^2 - b^2}\right)\). (3 marks)

(b) (i) Use De Moivre's theorem and the expansion of \((\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^8\) to show that 
\[\sin 8\theta = \binom{8}{1} \cos^7 \theta \sin \theta - \binom{8}{3} \cos^5 \theta \sin^3 \theta + \binom{8}{5} \cos^3 \theta \sin^5 \theta - \binom{8}{7} \cos \theta \sin^7 \theta.\] (2 marks)

(ii) Hence, show that 
\[\frac{\sin 8\theta}{\sin 2\theta} = 4(1 - 10 \sin^2 \theta + 24 \sin^4 \theta - 16 \sin^6 \theta).\] (3 marks)

(c) Let \(n\) be a positive integer and let \(x\) be a positive real number.

(i) Show that \(x^n - 1 - n(x - 1) = (x - 1)(1 + x + x^2 + \dots + x^{n-1} - n)\). (1 mark)

(ii) Hence show that \(x^n \ge 1 + n(x - 1)\). (2 marks)

(iii) Deduce that for positive real numbers \(a\) and \(b\), 
\[a^n b^{1-n} \ge na + (1 - n)b.\] (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(y = b \cos \theta\) (a)(ii) \(\sin 2\theta = 1\) (b)(i) \(\sin 8\theta = \binom{8}{1} \cos^7 \theta \sin \theta - \binom{8}{3} \cos^5 \theta \sin^3 \theta + \binom{8}{5} \cos^3 \theta \sin^5 \theta - \binom{8}{7} \cos \theta \sin^7 \theta\) (b)(ii) \(4(1 - 10 \sin^2 \theta + 24 \sin^4 \theta - 16 \sin^6 \theta)\) (c)(i) Show result (c)(ii) Show result (c)(iii) Show result
workingLatex: (a)(i) Since \(\angle AOB = \frac{\pi}{2}\), \(B\) has coordinates \(\left(a \cos(\theta + \frac{\pi}{2}), a \sin(\theta + \frac{\pi}{2})\right)\) i.e. \((-a \sin \theta, a \cos \theta)\). \(Q\) has same x-coordinate. \(\frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1 - \frac{x^2}{a^2} = 1 - \frac{a^2 \sin^2 \theta}{a^2} = \cos^2 \theta\). \(\therefore y^2 = b^2 \cos^2 \theta \implies y = b \cos \theta\) (since \(y > 0\)).

(a)(ii) \(M_{OP} = \frac{b \sin \theta}{a \cos \theta}\) (\(\cos \theta \ne 0\)), \(M_{OQ'} = -\frac{b \cos \theta}{a \sin \theta}\) (\(\sin \theta \ne 0\)). \(\therefore \tan \angle POQ' = \left| \frac{\frac{b \sin \theta}{a \cos \theta} - (-\frac{b \cos \theta}{a \sin \theta})}{1 + (\frac{b \sin \theta}{a \cos \theta})(-\frac{b \cos \theta}{a \sin \theta})} \right| = \left| \frac{ab \sin^2 \theta + ab \cos^2 \theta}{a^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta - b^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} \right| = \frac{2ab}{(a^2 - b^2) \sin 2\theta}\). This expression is least when \(\sin 2\theta\) is greatest, i.e. when \(\sin 2\theta = 1\).

(b)(i) \((\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^8 = \cos 8\theta + i \sin 8\theta\) (De Moivre). Also \((\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^8 = \cos^8 \theta + \binom{8}{1} \cos^7 \theta (i \sin \theta) + \binom{8}{2} \cos^6 \theta (i^2 \sin^2 \theta) + \binom{8}{3} \cos^5 \theta (i^3 \sin^3 \theta) + \binom{8}{4} \cos^4 \theta (i^4 \sin^4 \theta) + \dots + i^8 \sin^8 \theta\). Equating imaginary parts: \(\sin 8\theta = \binom{8}{1} \cos^7 \theta \sin \theta - \binom{8}{3} \cos^5 \theta \sin^3 \theta + \binom{8}{5} \cos^3 \theta \sin^5 \theta - \binom{8}{7} \cos \theta \sin^7 \theta\).

(c)(ii) When \(x = 1\), \(x^n = 1\) and \(1 + n(x - 1) = 1\), so \(x^n \ge 1 + n(x - 1)\). Suppose \(0 < x < 1\), then \(x^k < 1\), so \(1 + x + x^2 + \dots + x^{n-1} < n\), thus \(1 + x + x^2 + \dots + x^{n-1} - n < 0\). Since \(x - 1 < 0\), the product is \(> 0\). Suppose \(x > 1\), then \(x^k > 1\), so \(1 + x + x^2 + \dots + x^{n-1} > n\), thus \(1 + x + x^2 + \dots + x^{n-1} - n > 0\). Since \(x - 1 > 0\), the product is \(> 0\). Thus \(x^n - 1 - n(x - 1) \ge 0\) so \(x^n \ge 1 + n(x - 1)\).

(c)(iii) Let \(x = \frac{a}{b} > 0\). Then \((\frac{a}{b})^n \ge 1 + n(\frac{a}{b} - 1) = 1 + \frac{na}{b} - n\). Multiplying by \(b^n\): \(a^n \ge b^n + na b^{n-1} - n b^n = b^n(1 - n) + na b^{n-1}\). This is equivalent to \(a^n b^{1-n} \ge na + b(1 - n)\).

betterResponses: (ai) recognise that the parametric coordinates of \(Q\) are equal to \((a\cos (\theta + \pi/2), b\sin (\theta + \pi/2))\) to show that \(Q\) has coordinates \((-a\sin \theta, b\cos \theta)\)

(ai) first establish the \(x\)-coordinate of \(Q\). The \(y\)-coordinate of \(Q\) is then found by substituting the \(x\)-coordinate into the equation of the ellipse and solving for \(y\), or more simply, by multiplying the \(x\)-coordinate by \(b/a\)
(aii) show that \(\tan \angle P O Q'\) is equal to \(\frac{2ab}{(a^2-b^2) \sin 2\theta}\) and that its minimum value occurs when \(\sin 2\theta\) is a maximum
(aii) use the formula for the angle between two straight lines to find \(\tan \angle P O Q'\)
(bi) use sigma notation to provide the binomial expansion of \((\cos \theta + i\sin \theta)^8\) before applying De Moivre’s theorem

(bii) carefully show all working in their solutions, dividing each term of the given expression for \(\sin^8 \theta\) by \(2\sin \theta \cos \theta\) before replacing each occurrence of \(\cos^2 \theta\) with \(1 - \sin^2 \theta\), followed by expanding and collecting like terms
(ci) establish the equivalence of the “left hand side” with the “right hand side” or vice versa
(cii) recognise that, in order to prove the result using the identity in part (c)(i), separate cases for \(x\) (such as \(x < 1, x \ge 1\)) need to be considered
(cii) use the result in part (c)(ii), replacing \(x\) with \(a/b\) before completing the proof
improvementAreas: (ai) providing all necessary working in solving problems that require a stated result to be shown or proven
(aii) providing all necessary working in solving problems that require a stated result to be shown or proven
(aii) correctly applying fundamental formulae such as the formula for the angle between two straight lines
(bi) eliminating basic errors in algebraic manipulation
(bii) clearly showing all necessary steps in their responses
(bii) reviewing their solution if they find they do not reach the given result
(ci) showing exactly which terms cancel when expanding and simplifying expressions involving many terms
(cii) providing justification for key assertions in mathematical proofs
(cii) working on algebraic skills and using index laws/properties correctly

Finds the minimum value of \(\tan(\angle POQ')\), or equivalent merit
Finds an expression for \(\tan(\angle POQ')\), or equivalent merit
Correctly uses binomial theorem, or equivalent merit
Obtains an expression for \(\frac{\sin 8\theta}{\sin 2\theta}\) in terms of \(\sin \theta\), or equivalent merit
Correctly divides by \(\sin 2\theta\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Induction, Geometry, and Cubic Zeros
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Use mathematical induction to prove that, for \(n \ge 1\),

\[x^{(3^n)} - 1 = (x - 1)(x^2 + x + 1)(x^6 + x^3 + 1) \dots (x^{(2 \times 3^{n-1})} + x^{(3^{n-1})} + 1).\]

(b) In \(\triangle ABC\), point \(D\) is chosen on side \(AB\) and point \(E\) is chosen on side \(AC\) so that \(DE\) is parallel to \(BC\) and \(\frac{BC}{DE} = \sqrt{2}\).

The process is repeated two more times. Point \(F\) is chosen on \(BC\) and point \(G\) on \(BA\) so that \(FG\) is parallel to \(CA\) and \(\frac{CA}{FG} = \sqrt{2}\).

Point \(H\) is chosen on side \(CB\) and \(I\) on \(CA\) so that \(HI\) is parallel to \(BA\) and \(\frac{BA}{HI} = \sqrt{2}\).

The segments \(FG\) and \(HI\) intersect at \(X\), \(DE\) and \(HI\) intersect at \(Y\), and \(DE\) and \(FG\) intersect at \(Z\).

(i) Prove that \(DY = ZE\). (3 marks)

(ii) Find the exact value of the ratio \(\frac{YZ}{BC}\). (2 marks)

(c) Let \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) be the zeros of \(p(x) = x^3 + px + q\), where \(p\) and \(q\) are real and \(q 
e 0\).

(i) Show that \((\beta - \gamma)^2 = \alpha^2 + \frac{4q}{\alpha}\). (2 marks)

(ii) By considering the constant term of a cubic equation with roots \((\alpha - \beta)^2, (\beta - \gamma)^2\) and \((\gamma - \alpha)^2\), or otherwise, show that

\[(\alpha - \beta)^2 (\beta - \gamma)^2 (\gamma - \alpha)^2 = -(27q^2 + 4p^3).\] (3 marks)

(iii) Deduce that if \(27q^2 + 4p^3 < 0\), then the equation \(p(x) = 0\) has 3 distinct real roots. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) For \( n=1 \), \( LHS = x^3 - 1 = (x-1)(x^2+x+1) = RHS \). Assume true for \( n=k \). For \( n=k+1 \), \( x^{3^{k+1}} - 1 = (x^{3^k})^3 - 1 = (x^{3^k}-1)((x^{3^k})^2 + x^{3^k} + 1) \). Substitute the inductive hypothesis to complete the proof.

(b)(i) \( \triangle BGF \sim \triangle BAC \) as \( GF \parallel AC \). Thus \( \frac{AC}{GF} = \sqrt{2} \implies \frac{BC}{BF} = \sqrt{2} \implies BF = \frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} \). Similarly \( HC = \frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} \), so \( BF = HC \). Since \( DYHB \) and \( ZECF \) are parallelograms, \( DY = BH = BC - HC = BC - FB = FC = ZE \).
(b)(ii) \( YZ = DE - DY - ZE = DE - 2DY = \frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} - 2(BC - HC) = \frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} - 2BC + 2\frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} = 3\frac{BC}{\sqrt{2}} - 2BC = BC(\frac{3-2\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}) \).

(c)(ii) Let \( y = x^2 + \frac{4q}{x} \). Then \( xy = x^3 + 4q = -px - q + 4q \). \( x(y+p) = 3q \implies x = \frac{3q}{y+p} \). Substituting into \( x^3 + px + q = 0 \) and multiplying by \( (y+p)^3 \) yields \( 27q^2 + 3pq(y+p)^2 + q(y+p)^3 = 0 \). The product of roots is \( -(27q^2 + 4p^3) \).
(c)(iii) If \( 27q^2 + 4p^3 < 0 \), then the product of the squares of the differences of the roots is positive. If roots were not all real, the product would be negative, leading to a contradiction.

betterResponses: (a) work from the RHS to show that \((x^{3k}-1)(x^{2 \times 3k}+ x^{3k}+ 1)= x^{3k+1} -1\)
(a) work from the LHS and express \(x^{3k+1} -1\) as \(x^{3 \times 3k}-1= (x^{3k})^3 -1\) and then use the difference of two cubes to obtain the RHS
(bi) identify the triangles required to obtain a connection between \(BD\), \(BE\) and \(BH\), before using parallelograms to complete the proof
(bi) use the fact that parallel lines preserve the ratio of intercepts on transversals
(bii) use the result from part (i) to show that \(YF= DF-2DF\) and \(DF= BE-BH\) before using the ratios connecting \(DF\) and \(BH\) to \(BE\)
(ci) obtain the connection between \((\beta+ \gamma)^2\) and \((\beta- \gamma)^2\) and combine these with the result \(\alpha\beta\gamma= -q\) to achieve the desired outcome
(ci) use one of a number of valid approaches to achieve the appropriate result

(cii) use either \(\alpha= \frac{3p}{x+p}\) which was substituted into \(\alpha^3 + p\alpha+ q= 0\), or \(\alpha^3 = -p\alpha-q\) which was substituted into \(\frac{1}{\alpha\beta\gamma}(\alpha^3 + 4q)(\beta^3 + 4q)(\gamma^3 + 4q)\) to achieve the desired result
(ciii) realise that the cubic equation with real coefficients could have all real roots, or one real root and 2 complex roots that were conjugates of each other
(ciii) find the expression for the product of the \(y\)-coordinates of the stationary points and show that it was negative
improvementAreas: (a) practising the application of index laws when multiplying terms and when raising terms to a power to eliminate errors such as \((x^{3k})^3 = x^{3k+1}\) and not \(x^{3k+3}\)

(a) practising algebraic manipulation of complex expressions. For example, recognising that \((x^{3k})^3 -1 = (x^{3k}-1)(x^{2 \times 3k}+ x^{3k}+ 1)\) is a case of factorising the difference of two cubes

(bi) understanding that geometric claims must be supported with deductive reasoning as students: used ratios of corresponding sides in similar triangles without proof of similarity; claimed a ratio of lengths without proof; claimed equality of intervals without proof
(bi) practising how to construct geometrical proofs
(bii) practising algebraic manipulation involving fractions and ratios as students were using incorrect ratios and incorrect interval lengths
(ci) making use of the relationships between the roots and coefficients of polynomials and connecting this to the question
(ci) using an efficient approach, rather than changing the LHS and RHS of the given equation and making errors in the in the algebraic manipulation required

(cii) expanding and then simplifying complex algebraic expressions, as students were attempting to multiply expressions like \((\alpha-\beta)^2(\alpha-\gamma)^2(\beta-\gamma)^2\) or \((\alpha^2 + \frac{4q}{\alpha}) (\beta^2 + \frac{4q}{\beta})(\gamma^2 + \frac{4q}{\gamma})\) and were unable to progress to the solution

(cii) practising solving problems involving cubic equations involving roots, as students failed to make use of \(\alpha^3 + p\alpha+ q= 0\) to enable an effective substitution for \(\alpha\) or to simplify \(\frac{1}{\alpha\beta\gamma}(\alpha^3 + 4q)(\beta^3 + 4q)(\gamma^3 + 4q)\)
(ciii) practising the use of the conjugate root theorem as many students did not consider the existence of complex conjugate roots

(ciii) working with the existence of one, two or three distinct real roots using the \(y\)-coordinates of the 2 turning points as students managed to find 2 turning points but did not show a knowledge of what to do with them

Lets \( y = x^2 + \frac{4q}{x} \), or equivalent merit
Observes that \( p(x) \) has distinct roots which are all real or 1 real and 2 complex, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Mathematics Standard Mixed Topics
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Jeremy rolled a biased \(6\)-sided die a number of times. He recorded the results in a table.

| Number | \(1\) | \(2\) | \(3\) | \(4\) | \(5\) | \(6\) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency | \(23\) | \(19\) | \(48\) | \(20\) | \(21\) | \(19\) |

What is the relative frequency of rolling a \(3\)? (\(1\) mark)

(b) Clark's formula, given below, is used to determine the dosage of medicine for children.

\[ \text{Dosage} = \frac{\text{weight in kg} \times \text{adult dosage}}{70} \]

For a particular medicine, the adult dosage is \(325 \text{ mg}\) and the correct dosage for a specific child is \(90 \text{ mg}\).

How much does the child weigh, to the nearest kg? (\(2\) marks)

(c) Ali made monthly deposits of \($100\) into an annuity for \(5\) years.

Calculate the total amount Ali deposited into the annuity over this period. (\(1\) mark)

(d) The graph displays the mean monthly rainfall in Sydney and Perth.

(i) For how many months is the mean monthly rainfall higher in Perth than in Sydney? (\(1\) mark)

(ii) For which of the two cities is the standard deviation of the mean monthly rainfall smaller? Justify your answer WITHOUT calculations. (\(1\) mark)

(e) A cumulative frequency table for a data set is shown.

| Score | Cumulative frequency |
| :--- | :--- |
| \(1\) | \(5\) |
| \(2\) | \(9\) |
| \(3\) | \(16\) |
| \(4\) | \(20\) |
| \(5\) | \(34\) |
| \(6\) | \(42\) |

What is the interquartile range of this data set? (\(2\) marks)

(f) A toy shop sells buckets and spades separately. Buckets are available in one of six colours. Spades are also available in one of the same six colours.

Abdul wants to buy a bucket-and-spade set where the bucket and spade are of different colours.

How many different bucket-and-spade sets are possible for Abdul to buy? (\(1\) mark)

(g) A field diagram of a block of land has been drawn to scale. The shaded region \(ABFG\) is covered in grass.

The actual length of \(AG\) is \(24 \text{ m}\).

(i) Show that the scale of the diagram is \(1 \text{ cm} = 3 \text{ m}\). (\(1\) mark)

(ii) How much fertiliser would be needed to fertilise the grassed area \(ABFG\) at the rate of \(26.5 \text{ g/m}^2\)? (\(3\) marks)

(h) A car is purchased for \($23 900\).

The value of the car is depreciated by \(11.5\%\) each year using the declining-balance method.

What is the value of the car after three years? (\(2\) marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \( \frac{48}{150} \) (b) \( 19 \text{ kg} \) (c) \( \$6000 \) (d)(i) \( 3 \) (d)(ii) Sydney has the smaller standard deviation because it has less variation in mean monthly rainfall than Perth. (e) \( 2 \) (f) \( 30 \) (g)(i) \( 1 \text{ cm} = 3 \text{ m} \) (g)(ii) \( 5008.5 \text{ grams} \) (h) \( \$16 566.38 \)
workingLatex: (b) \(\text{Let } x \text{ be the weight of the child.} 90 = \frac{x \times 325}{70} x = \frac{90 \times 70}{325} = 19 \text{ kg}\)
(d)\((ii) \text{Sydney has the smaller standard deviation because it has less variation in mean monthly rainfall than Perth.}\)
(e) \(\text{Total of 42 scores split into two halves of 21 scores. We need the 11th and 32nd scores (when arranged in ascending order). IQ range} = 5 - 3 = 2\)
(g)\((i) \text{The measured length of } AG \text{ is } 8 \text{ cm. Therefore, } 8 \text{ cm} = 24 \text{ m. Dividing by } 8, 1 \text{ cm} = 3 \text{ m.}\)

(g)\((ii) AB \text{ measures } 3 \text{ cm and therefore the actual length of } AB \text{ is } 9 \text{ m. } BF \text{ measures } 6 \text{ cm and therefore the actual length of } BF \text{ is } 18 \text{ m. Area } ABFG = \frac{9}{2}(18 + 24) = 189 \text{ m}^2. \text{Fertiliser needed} = 26.5 \times 189 = 5008.5 \text{ grams}\)

betterResponses: (b) presented more than one ‘guess and check’ possibility
(a) understood the term ‘relative frequency’
used clear and concise explanations of standard deviation or spread of data.
(g i) correctly used ratios or fractions to show a comparison
were able to calculate the required areas and find the quantity of fertiliser by multiplication by 26.5.

entering data into their calculator

questions.title: Mixed Mathematics Standard Applications
questions.subtopic: Linear equations, z-scores, and surface area
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Jenny used her mobile phone while she was overseas for one month.

Her mobile phone plan has a base monthly cost of \$50. While overseas, she is also charged 33 cents per SMS message sent and 26 cents per MB of data used.

During her month overseas, Jenny sent 120 SMS messages and used 1400 MB of data.

What was her mobile phone bill for the month overseas? (2 marks)

(b) Find the values of \(x\) and \(y\) which satisfy the following equations simultaneously.
\[ y = x + 5 \]
\[ 3y - x = 7 \] (3 marks)

(c) A shade shelter is to be constructed in the shape of half a cylinder with open ends. The diameter is \(3.8 \text{ m}\) and the length is \(10 \text{ m}\).

The curved roof is to be made of plastic sheeting.

What area of plastic sheeting is required, to the nearest \(\text{m}^2\)? (2 marks)

(d) The graph displays the cost (\(\$c\)) charged by two companies for the hire of a minibus for \(x\) hours.

Both companies charge \(\$360\) for the hire of a minibus for 3 hours.

(i) What is the hourly rate charged by Company \(A\)? (1 mark)

(ii) Company \(B\) charges an initial booking fee of \(\$75\).

Write a formula, in the form of \(c = mx + b\), for the cost of hiring a minibus from Company \(B\) for \(x\) hours. (2 marks)

(iii) A minibus is hired for 5 hours from Company \(B\).

Calculate how much cheaper this is than hiring from Company \(A\). (2 marks)

(e) Joanna sits a Physics test and a Biology test.

(i) Joanna's mark in the Physics test is 70. The mean mark for this test is 58 and the standard deviation is 8.

Calculate the \(z\)-score for Joanna's mark in this test. (1 mark)

(ii) In the Biology test, the mean mark is 64 and the standard deviation is 10.

Joanna's \(z\)-score is the same in both the Physics test and the Biology test.

What is her mark in the Biology test? (2 marks)
workingLatex: (d)(iii) \(\text{Company } A \text{ cost is } 5 \times 120 = \$600\), \(\text{Company } B \text{ cost is } 95 \times 5 + 75 = \$550\), \(\therefore \text{Company } B \text{ is } \$50 \text{ cheaper than Company } A\)

betterResponses: (b) solved simultaneous equations using the elimination method
(a) converted cents to dollars at the start of a calculation
(d) correctly interpreted information from a graph
improvementAreas: calculating the radius, given the diameter
calculating the curved surface area of a cylinder
writing a formal equation given the gradient and \(y\)-intercept
reviewing the concept of \(z\)-scores.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A field is bordered on one side by a straight road and on the other side by a river, as shown. Measurements are taken perpendicular to the road every \(7.5 \text{ metres}\) along the road.

Use TWO applications of Simpson's Rule to find an approximation to the area of the field. (3 marks)

(b) Solve the equation \(\frac{2x}{5} + 1 = \frac{3x + 1}{2}\), leaving your answer as a fraction. (3 marks)

(c) Every day, a \(1200\text{-watt}\) microwave oven is used for \(45 \text{ minutes}\) at \(40\% \text{ power}\). Electricity is charged at \(\$0.25 \text{ per kWh}\).

What is the cost of running this microwave oven for \(180 \text{ days}\)? (3 marks)

(d) Yanika opens a new credit card account, with interest and fees as shown.

Interest
- Flat rate of \(12.3\% \text{ per annum}\)
- No interest-free period

Fees
- \(\$0 \text{ for online repayments}\)
- \(\$3 \text{ for repayments in cash}\)
(fee added to balance immediately after repayment)

Yanika makes a single purchase of \(\$849\) with the credit card.

(i) Show that the balance owing on the credit card \(24 \text{ days}\) after making the purchase is \(\$855.87\). (2 marks)

(ii) Yanika makes her first repayment \(24 \text{ days}\) after making the purchase. She makes a cash repayment of \(\$450\).

What is the balance owing on the credit card immediately after her repayment is made and the repayment fee has been charged? (1 mark)

questions.title: Time, Data, and Financial Modelling
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The time in Brisbane is \(4\frac{1}{2}\) hours ahead of the time in New Delhi. John flew from New Delhi to Brisbane via Singapore. His plane left New Delhi at 11.30 am (New Delhi time), stopped for 3 hours in Singapore, and arrived in Brisbane at 9.00 am the following day (Brisbane time).

What was the plane's total flying time? (3 marks)

(b) A TV program which has a file size of \(495\) MB (megabytes) is to be downloaded. The download speed is \(82.7\) megabits per second.

How long will it take to download this program? Give your answer correct to the nearest second. (Note: \(1\) megabit = \(1\,000\,000\) bits) (2 marks)

(c) When people walk in snow, the depth (\(D\) cm) of each footprint depends on both the area (\(A\) cm\(^2\)) of the shoe sole and the weight of the person.

The graph shows the relationship between the area of the shoe sole and the depth of the footprint in snow, for a group of people of the same weight.

(i) The graph is a hyperbola because \(D\) is inversely proportional to \(A\). The point \(P\) lies on the hyperbola.

Find the equation relating \(D\) and \(A\). (2 marks)

(ii) A man from this group walks in snow and the depth of his footprint is \(4\) cm.

Use your equation from part (i) to calculate the area of his shoe sole. (1 mark)

(d) Data for life expectancy (expected remaining years of life) for females at selected ages are given in the table.

| Age (\(x\) years) | Life expectancy (\(y\) years) for females |
| :--- | :--- |
| \(0\) | \(84.6\) |
| \(5\) | \(79.9\) |
| \(10\) | \(74.9\) |
| \(15\) | \(69.9\) |
| \(20\) | \(65.0\) |
| \(25\) | \(60.1\) |
| \(30\) | \(55.1\) |
| \(35\) | \(50.2\) |
| \(40\) | \(45.4\) |
| \(\bar{x} = 20\) | \(\bar{y} = 65.01\) (to 2 decimal places) |

(i) A student is finding the equation of the least-squares line of best fit relating the life expectancy (\(y\)) for females and their age (\(x\)).

The student has calculated the gradient to be \(-0.984\).

Use this value and the given values for \(\bar{x}\) and \(\bar{y}\) to show that the \(y\)-intercept of the line is \(84.69\). (1 mark)

(ii) A student uses the least-squares line of best fit from part (i) to estimate the life expectancy of her grandmother who is currently aged \(87\).

Explain why this does NOT give a valid estimate. (1 mark)

(iii) Sally is a female aged \(37\).

Use the least-squares line of best fit for females, \(y = -0.984x + 84.69\), to calculate her life expectancy. (1 mark)

(iv) For males, the least-squares line of best fit relating life expectancy (\(y\)) and age (\(x\)) has the equation \(y = -0.972x + 80.44\).

James is a male. He marries Sally who is aged \(37\). On their wedding day, they have the same life expectancy.

Calculate James's age on their wedding day. Give your answer in years. (2 marks)

(e) Andrew borrowed \(\$20\,000\) to be repaid in equal monthly repayments of \(\$243\) over \(10\) years. Having made this monthly repayment for \(4\) years, he increased his monthly repayment to \(\$281\). As a result, Andrew paid off the loan one year earlier.

How much less did he repay altogether by making this change? (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \( 14 \text{ hours} \) (b) \( 50 \text{ seconds} \) (c)(i) \( D = \frac{4500}{A} \) (c)(ii) \( 1125 \text{ cm}^2 \) (d)(i) \( 84.69 \) (d)(ii) Extrapolation beyond the range of data (d)(iii) \( 48.282 \text{ years} \) (d)(iv) \( 33 \text{ years} \) (e) \( \$636 \text{ less} \)
workingLatex: (d)\((ii) \text{This involves extrapolation beyond the range of the data and therefore does not give a valid estimate.}\)

questions.title: Cylindrical Tank, Tax, Trigonometry, and Probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A cylindrical water tank has a radius of \(9 \text{ metres}\) and a capacity of \(1.26 \text{ megalitres}\).

[Diagram of a cylinder with radius 9 m and height h]

What is the height of the water tank? Give your answer in metres, correct to two decimal places. (3 marks)

(b) Last year, Luke's taxable income was \(\$87\,000\) and the tax payable on this income was \(\$19\,822\). This year, Luke's taxable income has increased by \(\$16\,800\).

(i) Use the table to calculate the tax payable by Luke this year. (2 marks)

| Taxable income (\$) | Tax payable |
| :--- | :--- |
| \(\$0 - \$18\,200\) | Nil |
| \(\$18\,201 - \$37\,000\) | \(19\text{c}\) for each \(\$1\) over \(\$18\,200\) |
| \(\$37\,001 - \$87\,000\) | \(\$3572\) plus \(32.5\text{c}\) for each \(\$1\) over \(\$37\,000\) |
| \(\$87\,001 - \$180\,000\) | \(\$19\,822\) plus \(37\text{c}\) for each \(\$1\) over \(\$87\,000\) |
| \(\$180\,001 \text{ and over}\) | \(\$54\,232\) plus \(45\text{c}\) for each \(\$1\) over \(\$180\,000\) |

(ii) How much extra money will Luke have this year, after paying tax, as a result of the increase in his taxable income? Ignore the Medicare levy. (2 marks)

(c) The diagram shows two triangles.

Triangle \(ABC\) is right-angled, with \(AB = 13 \text{ cm}\) and \(\angle ABC = 62^\circ\).

In triangle \(ACD\), \(AD = x \text{ cm}\) and \(\angle DAC = 40^\circ\). The area of triangle \(ACD\) is \(30 \text{ cm}^2\).

[Diagram of two triangles sharing side AC]

What is the value of \(x\), correct to one decimal place? (3 marks)

(d) A game consists of two tokens being drawn at random from a barrel containing \(20\) tokens. There are \(17\) tokens labelled \(10 \text{ cents}\) and \(3\) tokens labelled \(\$2\). The player wins the total value of the two tokens drawn.

(i) Complete the probability tree by writing the missing probabilities in the boxes. (2 marks)

[Probability tree diagram]

(ii) Considering that the game costs \(\$1\) to play, calculate the financial expectation of the game. (3 marks)

questions.title: Calculus, Trigonometry, and Algebraic Equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Using the cosine rule, find the value of \(x\) in the following diagram.

(3 marks)

(b) Consider the curve \(y = 2x^3 + 3x^2 - 12x + 7\).

(i) Find the stationary points of the curve and determine their nature. (4 marks)

(ii) Sketch the curve, labelling the stationary points. (2 marks)

(iii) Hence, or otherwise, find the values of \(x\) for which \(\frac{dy}{dx}\) is positive. (1 mark)

(c) By letting \(m = t^{\frac{1}{3}}\), or otherwise, solve \(t^{\frac{2}{3}} + t^{\frac{1}{3}} - 6 = 0\). (2 marks)

(d) The rate at which water flows into a tank is given by

\[\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{2t}{1 + t^2},\]

where \(V\) is the volume of water in the tank in litres and \(t\) is the time in seconds.

Initially the tank is empty.

Find the exact amount of water in the tank after 10 seconds. (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(x = 11\); (b)(i) \((-2, 27)\) is a local maximum, \((1, 0)\) is a local minimum; (b)(ii) [Sketch of cubic curve]; (b)(iii) \(x > 1\) or \(x < -2\); (c) \(t = 8, t = -27\); (d) \(\ln(101)\) litres
workingLatex: (b)(i) For stationary points, \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies x^2 + x - 2 = 0 \implies (x+2)(x-1) = 0 \implies x = 1, -2\)

(c) \(m = t^{\frac{1}{3}}, m^2 = t^{\frac{2}{3}}\). Equation becomes \(m^2 + m - 6 = 0\)

questions.title: Calculus, Exponential Decay, and Area
questions.subtopic: Trigonometric graphs, integration, and exponential modelling
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Sketch the curve \(y = 4 + 3 \sin 2x\) for \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\). (3 marks)

(b) (i) Find the exact value of \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \cos x \, dx\). (1 mark)

(ii) Using Simpson's rule with one application, find an approximation to the integral \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \cos x \, dx\), leaving your answer in terms of \(\pi\) and \(\sqrt{3}\). (2 marks)

(iii) Using parts (i) and (ii), show that \(\pi \approx \frac{18\sqrt{3}}{3 + 4\sqrt{3}}\). (1 mark)

(c) Carbon-14 is a radioactive substance that decays over time. The amount of carbon-14 present in a kangaroo bone is given by \(C(t) = Ae^{kt}\), where \(A\) and \(k\) are constants, and \(t\) is the number of years since the kangaroo died.

(i) Show that \(C(t)\) satisfies \(\frac{dC}{dt} = kC\). (1 mark)

(ii) After 5730 years, half of the original amount of carbon-14 is present. Show that the value of \(k\), correct to 2 significant figures, is \(-0.00012\). (2 marks)

(iii) The amount of carbon-14 now present in a kangaroo bone is 90% of the original amount. Find the number of years since the kangaroo died. Give your answer correct to 2 significant figures. (2 marks)

questions.title: Geometry, Financial Modelling, and Kinematics
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The triangle \(ABC\) is isosceles with \(AB = AC\) and the size of \(\angle BAC\) is \(x^\circ\). Points \(D\) and \(E\) are chosen so that \(\triangle ABC\), \(\triangle ACD\) and \(\triangle ADE\) are congruent, as shown in the diagram.

Find the value of \(x\) for which \(AB\) is parallel to \(ED\), giving reasons. (3 marks)

(b) Anita opens a savings account. At the start of each month she deposits \(\$X\) into the savings account. At the end of each month, after interest is added into the savings account, the bank withdraws \(\$2500\) from the savings account as a loan repayment. Let \(M_n\) be the amount in the savings account after the \(n^{\text{th}}\) withdrawal.

The savings account pays interest of \(4.2\%\) per annum compounded monthly.

(i) Show that after the second withdrawal the amount in the savings account is given by
\[M_2 = X(1.0035^2 + 1.0035) - 2500(1.0035 + 1).\] (2 marks)

(ii) Find the value of \(X\) so that the amount in the savings account is \(\$80\,000\) after the last withdrawal of the fourth year. (3 marks)

(c) Two particles move along the \(x\)-axis.
When \(t = 0\), particle \(P_1\) is at the origin and moving with velocity \(3\).
For \(t \ge 0\), particle \(P_1\) has acceleration given by \(a_1 = 6t + e^{-t}\).

(i) Show that the velocity of particle \(P_1\) is given by \(v_1 = 3t^2 + 4 - e^{-t}\). (2 marks)

When \(t = 0\), particle \(P_2\) is also at the origin.
For \(t \ge 0\), particle \(P_2\) has velocity given by \(v_2 = 6t + 1 - e^{-t}\).

(ii) When do the two particles have the same velocity? (2 marks)

(iii) Show that the two particles do not meet for \(t > 0\). (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(\Delta ABC, \Delta ACD, \Delta ADE\) are congruent (given). So angles \(\angle BAC, \angle CAD, \angle DAE\) are equal (corresponding angles in congruent triangles). \(\angle AED\) is the base angle of an isosceles triangle. \(2 \times \angle AED + x = 180\) (angle sum of a triangle). So \(\angle AED = \frac{180 - x}{2}\). \(\angle AED + \angle EAB = 180\) (cointerior angle - \(AB \parallel ED\)). \(\frac{180 - x}{2} + 3x = 180\). \(180 - x + 6x = 360\). \(5x = 180\). \(x = 36\).

betterResponses: (a) students used clear logic and provided geometric reasons for each step.
(b) (iii) students obtained the correct exponential equation usually obtained the correct answer.

(b) (i) omitting brackets or not closing brackets

(b) (i) writing the given statement for \(M_2\) without any working
(b) (i) not checking their answer for (b)(i) was the same as the one required before continuing onto (b)(ii).
(b) (ii) not using the appropriate value for \(n\) (48 months)
(b) (ii) using incorrect values for \(a\) and \(n\) for each series
(b) (ii) incorrectly applying the \(S_n\) formula for a geometric series.
(c) (i) not evaluating the constant of integration
(c) (i) substituting the given values to prove they satisfy the equation without integrating
(c) (i) incorrectly integrating \(e^{-t}\)
(c) (i) not correctly evaluating \(e^0\).
(c) (ii) not obtaining a quadratic equation after equating \(v_1\) and \(v_2\)

questions.title: Optimisation, Geometric Series, and Geometry
questions.subtopic: Applications of differentiation and geometric series
questions.promptLatex:
(a) John's home is at point \(A\) and his school is at point \(B\). A straight river runs nearby.

The point on the river closest to \(A\) is point \(C\), which is \(5 \text{ km}\) from \(A\).
The point on the river closest to \(B\) is point \(D\), which is \(7 \text{ km}\) from \(B\).
The distance from \(C\) to \(D\) is \(9 \text{ km}\).

To get some exercise, John cycles from home directly to point \(E\) on the river, \(x \text{ km}\) from \(C\), before cycling directly to school at \(B\), as shown in the diagram.

[Diagram showing points A, C, E, D, B with distances AC=5, BD=7, CD=9, CE=x, ED=9-x, and angles alpha and beta]

The total distance John cycles from home to school is \(L \text{ km}\).

(i) Show that \(L = \sqrt{x^2 + 25} + \sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that if \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0\), then \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\). (3 marks)

(iii) Find the value of \(x\) that makes \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\). (2 marks)

(iv) Explain why this value of \(x\) gives a minimum for \(L\). (1 mark)

(b) A geometric series has first term \(a\) and limiting sum \(2\). (3 marks)

Find all possible values for \(a\).

(c) In the triangle \(ABC\), the point \(M\) is the mid-point of \(BC\). The point \(D\) lies on \(AB\) and \(BD = DA + AC\).

The line that passes through the point \(C\) and is parallel to \(MD\) meets \(BA\) produced at \(E\).

[Diagram showing triangle ABC, points M, D, E, and parallel lines MD and CE]

Copy or trace this diagram into your writing booklet.

(i) Prove that \(\triangle ACE\) is isosceles. (3 marks)

(ii) The point \(F\) is chosen on \(BC\) so that \(AF\) is parallel to \(DM\).

[Diagram showing triangle ABC, points M, D, E, F, and parallel lines MD, CE, and AF]

Show that \(AF\) bisects \(\angle BAC\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(L = \sqrt{x^2 + 25} + \sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}\) (a)(ii) \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 25}} = \frac{9 - x}{\sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}}\) (a)(iii) \(x = 3\frac{3}{4}\) (a)(iv) Minimum confirmed by derivative sign change (b) \(0 < a < 4\) (c)(i) Proof that \(\triangle ACE\) is isosceles (c)(ii) Proof that \(AF\) bisects \(\angle BAC\)
workingLatex: (a)(i) By Pythagoras' theorem \(AE = \sqrt{x^2 + 25}\), \(DE = 9 - x\), so \(BE = \sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}\). \(\therefore L = \sqrt{x^2 + 25} + \sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}\).

(a)(ii) \(\frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 25}} - \frac{9 - x}{\sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}}\). Setting \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 25}} = \frac{9 - x}{\sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}}\). This implies \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\).

(a)(iv) From parts (ii) and (iii) there is only one value of \(x\) for which \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0\) for \(0 < x < 9\). For \(x = 0\), \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0 - \frac{9}{\sqrt{130}} < 0\). For \(x = 9\), \(\frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{9}{\sqrt{106}} > 0\). Hence, since \(x = 3\frac{3}{4}\) lies between \(x = 0\) and \(x = 9\) then this must be a minimum.

(b) \(S_\infty = \frac{a}{1 - r} = 2 \implies \frac{a}{2} = 1 - r \implies r = 1 - \frac{a}{2}\). Now \(|r| < 1\) so \(|1 - \frac{a}{2}| < 1\). \(-1 < 1 - \frac{a}{2} < 1 \implies -2 < 2 - a < 2 \implies 2 > a - 2 > -2 \implies 4 > a > 0\). \(\therefore 0 < a < 4\).

(c)(i) Since \(BM = MC\) (given), then \(BD = DE\) (equal intercept). \(\therefore BD = DA + AE\) and \(BD = DA + AC\) (given). ie \(DA + AE = DA + AC \implies AE = AC\). Hence, \(\triangle ACE\) is isosceles (two equal sides).

(c)(ii) Since \(AF \parallel DM\) and \(EC \parallel DM\) then \(AF \parallel EC\). \(\angle BAF = \angle AEC\) (corresponding angles, parallel lines \(AF \parallel EC\)). \(\angle AEC = \angle ACE\) (base angles of isosceles triangle \(ACE\)). \(\angle ACE = \angle CAF\) (alternate angles, parallel lines \(AF\) and \(EC\)). So \(\angle BAF = \angle CAF\), ie \(AF\) bisects \(\angle BAC\).

betterResponses: (a) (ii) students expanded \((9 - x)^2\) and then differentiated, or identified ratios for \(\sin \alpha\) and \(\sin \beta\).
(a) (iii) students recognised if \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\) the triangles were similar, or if \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\) then \(\tan \alpha = \tan \beta\) (\(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) acute).

(a) (iv) students responded correctly to the direction “Explain” by demonstrating a deeper engagement. Using a table to test \(x\) values above and below \(x = 15/4\) and explain why their \(x\) value gave a minimum proved to be the easier method.
(b) students stated incorrectly that \(|r| \le 1\) or \(0 < r < 1\).
improvementAreas: (a) (i) not all referred to the sides of the given triangles but instead used the generic \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\).
(a) (ii) differentiating incorrectly
(a) (ii) integrating instead of differentiating
(a) (ii) not recognising the relationship between \(dL/dx = 0\) and \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\)
(a) (ii) incorrectly expanding \(49 + (9 - x)^2\)
(a) (iii) making algebraic errors when solving the equation
(a) (iii) using \(x/5 = (9-x)/7\) without explanation.
(a) (iv) Students who attempted to find \(d^2L/dx^2\) and substitute their value for \(x\) found that method difficult.
(b) incorrect algebraic manipulation when making \(r\) the subject of \(2 = a/(1-r)\)
(b) solving inequations incorrectly.
(c) (i) attempting to prove \(\Delta ACE\) is isosceles without using the information provided
(c) (i) stating that \(D\) is the midpoint of \(BE\) without justification.
(c) (ii) proving extra triangles were similar (using \(\Delta BDM\), \(\Delta BAF\) and \(\Delta BEC\)) and hence manipulated many equal angles to achieve the correct result
(c) (ii) naming angles incorrectly
(c) (ii) providing incorrect geometric reasons.

criteria.criterion: Obtains \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 25}} = \frac{9 - x}{\sqrt{49 + (9 - x)^2}}\), or equivalent merit
Shows \(\triangle BDM\) is similar to \(\triangle BEC\), or equivalent merit
Shows \(\angle FAC = \angle ACE\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Calculus, Coordinate Geometry, and Probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The point \(P\) divides the interval from \(A(-4, -4)\) to \(B(1, 6)\) internally in the ratio \(2:3\).

Find the \(x\)-coordinate of \(P\). (1 mark)

(b) Differentiate \(\tan^{-1}(x^3)\). (2 marks)

(c) Solve \(\frac{2x}{x+1} > 1\). (3 marks)

(d) Sketch the graph of the function \(y = 2 \cos^{-1}x\). (2 marks)

(e) Evaluate \(\int_{0}^{3} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x+1}} dx\), using the substitution \(x = u^2 - 1\). (3 marks)

(f) Find \(\int \sin^2 x \cos x dx\). (1 mark)

(g) The probability that a particular type of seedling produces red flowers is \(\frac{1}{5}\). Eight of these seedlings are planted.

(i) Write an expression for the probability that exactly three of the eight seedlings produce red flowers. (1 mark)

(ii) Write an expression for the probability that none of the eight seedlings produces red flowers. (1 mark)

(iii) Write an expression for the probability that at least one of the eight seedlings produces red flowers. (1 mark)
workingLatex: (d) A graph of \( y = 2\cos^{-1}x \) starting at \( (-1, 2\pi) \) and ending at \( (1, 0) \) passing through \( (0, \pi) \).

betterResponses: (a) The large majority of responses showed correct substitution into the formula \(x=\frac{kx_2+lx_1}{k+l}\) given on the Reference Sheet to achieve the correct result \(x= -2\).
(b) In most responses, students were able to differentiate \(\tan^{-1}(x)\) correctly.
(d) Many responses correctly graphed \(y= 2 \cos^{-1} x\), showing or stating the correct domain and range.
(e) Those students who were able to differentiate implicitly to arrive at \(dx= 2u du\) and change the bounding limits correctly were able to execute the correct solution.
(f) Approximately half of the responses demonstrated the use of mental strategies to obtain the answer to \(\frac{\sin^3 x}{3}\).
(g)(i) In many responses students were able to write down the correct expression.
(g)(iii) The large majority of students demonstrated that the probability sum of complementary events is 1. Even if they were unable to answer (g) (ii) correctly, most answered this part correctly.
improvementAreas: (a) In some responses the answer was unsimplified, for example \(x=\frac{-10}{5}\), and others showed the \(y\)-coordinate as well.

(b) A substantial number relied on the Reference Sheet formula \(\int \frac{1}{a^2+x^2} dx = \frac{1}{a} \tan^{-1} \frac{x}{a} + C\) and were unable to identify the appropriate relationship between the formula and the \(x^3\). Common errors were algebraic, such as \((x^3)^2 = x\) or \(x^9\). In a few responses, students changed the subject to \(x\) and then attempted to find \(\frac{dx}{dy}=\frac{d}{dy}((\tan y)^{1/3})\). In some responses, students incorrectly wrote \(\tan^{-1}(x^3) = \frac{1}{\tan(x^3)}\).

(c) Some responses showed invalid conclusions such as: \(1 < x < -1\). In many responses students wrote \(x < -1\) and \(x > 1\) or \(x < -1, x > 1\) rather than the correct solution \(x < -1\) or \(x > 1\).
(d) In some responses, students confused the shape, included asymptotes or wrote incorrect domain and/or range.

(e) Common errors were: \(x= u^2 + 1, dx=\frac{du}{2u}\) and \(\frac{1}{u} \times 2u = u\); in some responses, students found the limits \(u= \pm 2u\) and \(u= \pm 1\) which led to dual solutions or confused the \(x\) with the \(u\) when calculating the limits. In many responses students changed the subject to \(u\) such that \(u= \sqrt{x+1}\) or \(u= \pm \sqrt{x+1}\), introducing roots and cases. In other responses students found \([\frac{u^3}{3} - 2u]_1^2\) correctly but then confounded the fraction calculations.
(f) The other half used the \(\cos^2 \theta\) results and substituted to arrive at \(\int(1 -\cos^2 x) \cos x dx\). Most then proceeded to integrate both factors together or to keep substituting.
(g)(i) Some omitted the binomial coefficient \(^8C_3\), that is, \(\binom{8}{3}\) or wrote \(^8P_3\). In some responses the indices were swapped in error.
(g)(ii) Omission of brackets was common, such as \(\frac{4}{5}^8\) meaning \((\frac{4}{5})^8\).
(g)(iii) In some responses the words ‘at least one’ were confused with either ‘one’ or ‘more than one’.

questions.title: Geometry, Absolute Values, Calculus, and Kinematics
questions.promptLatex:
(a) The points \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) lie on a circle with centre \(O\), as shown in the diagram. The size of \(\angle AOC\) is \(100^\circ\).

Find the size of \(\angle ABC\), giving reasons. (2 marks)

(b) (i) Carefully sketch the graphs of \(y = |x + 1|\) and \(y = 3 - |x - 2|\) on the same axes, showing all intercepts. (3 marks)

(ii) Using the graphs from part (i), or otherwise, find the range of values of \(x\) for which
\[|x + 1| + |x - 2| = 3.\] (1 mark)

(c) The region enclosed by the semicircle \(y = \sqrt{1 - x^2}\) and the \(x\)-axis is to be divided into two pieces by the line \(x = h\), where \(0 \le h < 1\).

The two pieces are rotated about the \(x\)-axis to form solids of revolution. The value of \(h\) is chosen so that the volumes of the solids are in the ratio \(2:1\).

(i) Show that \(h\) satisfies the equation \(3h^3 - 9h + 2 = 0\). (3 marks)

(ii) Given \(h_1 = 0\) as the first approximation for \(h\), use one application of Newton's method to find a second approximation for \(h\). (1 mark)

(d) At time \(t\) the displacement, \(x\), of a particle satisfies \(t = 4 - e^{-2x}\).

Find the acceleration of the particle as a function of \(x\). (3 marks)

(e) Evaluate \(\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{x^2}\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(130^\circ (b)(i) Sketch of two intersecting V-shaped graphs (b)(ii) -1 \le x \le 2 (c)(i) 3h^3 - 9h + 2 = 0 (c)(ii) \frac{2}{9} (d) \ddot{x} = \frac{1}{2}e^{4x} (e) 2\)
workingLatex: (a) \(\angle ABC = \frac{1}{2} \text{reflex } \angle AOC = \frac{1}{2}(260^\circ) = 130^\circ \text{ (angle at centre equals twice the angle at circumference standing in the same arc)}\)
(b)\((ii) \therefore -1 \le x \le 2 \text{ when } |x+1| = 3-|x-2| \text{ the graphs have points in common, ie } |x+1| + |x-2| = 3\)
(c)\((i) V_1 = \pi \int_{h}^{1} y^2 dx = \pi \int_{h}^{1} (1-x^2) dx = \pi \left[ x - \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{h}^{1} = \pi \left( \frac{2}{3} - h + \frac{h^3}{3} \right)\)

betterResponses: (a) In better responses, the students found the reflex angle \(\angle AOC= 260^\circ\) before proceeding to the correct value.
(b)(i) In the better responses, students used different colours to distinguish their two sketches and showed the sharp vertex point on both curves.
(b)(ii) The better responses referenced the graphs and the points of intersection.
(c)(i) In better responses students correctly equated the volumes in the given ratio 2:1 before simplifying their equation to show \(3h^3 -9h+ 2 = 0\).

(d) In better responses, students found \(\frac{dt}{dx}\) then \(\frac{dx}{dt}\) first, then used the Reference sheet for the acceleration \(\ddot{x} = \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{v^2}{2})\) to achieve the result.
(e) In better responses, students used trigonometric substitution to gain the answer of 2.
improvementAreas: (a) Common errors were: misquoting reasons, for example stating that the angle at the centre is half the angle at the circumference; assuming that the chords \(AB\) and \(BC\) were equal in length.

(b)(i) Common errors were: not showing a scale on their axes; not showing any intercepts even though they were asked; not using a ruler to draw the straight line segments; not realising the translations necessary for drawing both curves.

(b)(ii) Common errors were: excluding the endpoints and writing \(-1 < x < 2\); attempting to algebraically solve the equation; mistaking the word ‘range’ in the question to mean finding a set of \(y\) values, not a ‘range of values of \(x\)’.

(c)(i) Common errors were: not equating the volumes in the correct ratio, interchanging the ratio as 1:2; omitting \(\pi\) from their definite integral and/or not squaring the function; difficulty with fractions and negative signs; incorrectly using areas and distances to attempt to find \(h\).
(c)(ii) Common errors were: mixing negative signs to arrive at \(h^2 = -\frac{2}{9}\); incorrectly transcribing the formula from the reference sheet.

(d) Common errors were: not substituting back for \(t= 4 -e^{-2x}\) in their acceleration/time function; not expressing \(x= -\frac{1}{2} \ln(4 -t)\) correctly and thus not arriving at the correct solution; not being able to differentiate the above equation correctly; mistakenly thinking acceleration was \(\frac{dv}{dx}\).

(e) Common errors were: mistakenly giving \(\sqrt{2}\) as their answer; not using a double angle formula or choosing an inappropriate version; not knowing the fundamental limit \(\lim_{x \to 0} (\frac{\sin x}{x}) = 1\).

criteria.criterion: Attempts to relate two angles in the diagram, or equivalent merit
Provides correct sketch of \(y = 3 - |x - 2|\), or equivalent merit
Provides correct sketch of \(y = |x + 1|\), or equivalent merit
Equates the volumes in the correct ratio and attempts to evaluate an integral, or equivalent merit
Provides correct integral for the volume of one solid, or equivalent merit
Attempts to use \(\ddot{x} = \frac{d}{dx}(\frac{1}{2}v^2)\), or equivalent merit
Correctly uses double angle result, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Simple Harmonic Motion, Binomial Expansions, and Projectile Motion
questions.subtopic: Projectile motion, SHM, and binomial identities
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A particle is moving along the \(x\)-axis in simple harmonic motion centred at the origin.

When \(x = 2\) the velocity of the particle is \(4\).

When \(x = 5\) the velocity of the particle is \(3\).

Find the period of the motion. (3 marks)

(b) Let \(n\) be a positive EVEN integer.

(i) Show that \((1 + x)^n + (1 - x)^n = 2 \left[ \binom{n}{0} + \binom{n}{2} x^2 + \dots + \binom{n}{n} x^n \right]\). (2 marks)

(ii) Hence show that 
\[ n \left[ (1 + x)^{n-1} - (1 - x)^{n-1} \right] = 2 \left[ 2 \binom{n}{2} x + 4 \binom{n}{4} x^3 + \dots + n \binom{n}{n} x^{n-1} \right] \]. (1 mark)

(iii) Hence show that \(\binom{n}{2} + 2 \binom{n}{4} + 3 \binom{n}{6} + \dots + \frac{n}{2} \binom{n}{n} = n 2^{n-3}\). (2 marks)

(c) A golfer hits a golf ball with initial speed \(V \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle \(\theta\) to the horizontal. The golf ball is hit from one side of a lake and must have a horizontal range of \(100 \text{ m}\) or more to avoid landing in the lake.

Neglecting the effects of air resistance, the equations describing the motion of the ball are
\[ x = Vt \cos \theta \]
\[ y = Vt \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2} gt^2 \]
where \(t\) is the time in seconds after the ball is hit and \(g\) is the acceleration due to gravity in \(\text{m s}^{-2}\). Do NOT prove these equations.

(i) Show that the horizontal range of the golf ball is \(\frac{V^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}\) metres. (2 marks)

(ii) Show that if \(V^2 < 100g\) then the horizontal range of the ball is less than \(100 \text{ m}\). (1 mark)

It is now given that \(V^2 = 200g\) and that the horizontal range of the ball is \(100 \text{ m}\) or more.

(iii) Show that \(\frac{\pi}{12} \le \theta \le \frac{5\pi}{12}\). (2 marks)

(iv) Find the greatest height the ball can achieve. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (b)(i) \(\text{(odd terms have different sign and so cancel)}\)
(c)(iv) \(\text{Maximum height occurs when } 0 = \frac{dy}{dt} = V\sin\theta - gt \implies t = \frac{V}{g}\sin\theta\)
(c)(iv) \(\text{For } 0 \le \theta \le \frac{\pi}{2}, \sin^2\theta \text{ is an increasing function, so maximum occurs when } \theta = \frac{5\pi}{12}\)

betterResponses: (a) In better responses, students recognised and used the formula \(v^2 = n^2(a^2 -x^2)\) and correctly arrived at the answer by first establishing a pair of simultaneous equations.
(b)(iii) The responses that substituted \(x= 1\) in part (ii) were generally successful.
(c)(i) In better responses, students used \(y= 0\) to find the time \(t\) and substitute \(t\) into \(x= vt \cos \theta\).
(c)(iii) In better responses, students used a graphical approach.
(c)(iv) In better responses students solved \(\frac{dy}{dt}= 0\) for \(t\) and used it to establish a correct expression for maximum height.
improvementAreas: (a) Common errors were: starting with \(x= b+ a \cos(nt+ \alpha)\); starting with integrating \(\ddot{x} = n^2(x-b)\).
(b)(i) A common error was ignoring the odd terms in the binomial expansions of \((1 + x)^n\) and \((1 -x)^n\).
(b)(ii) A common error was failing to multiply by -1 when applying the chain rule to find the derivative of \((1 -x)^n\).
(b)(iii) A common error was incorrect setting out of LHS and RHS.
(c)(i) A common error was using the Cartesian equation of the projectile and let \(y= 0\) to show the horizontal range; this took too much time.
(c)(ii) A common error was assuming that the horizontal range is less than 100 m and showing \(V^2 < 100g\). Some students were not sure how to use the fact that \(\sin 2\theta \leq 1\).
(c)(iii) A common error was incorrectly justifying \(\frac{\pi}{12} \leq \theta \leq \frac{5\pi}{12}\) from \(\sin 2\theta \geq \frac{1}{2}\).

(c)(iv) Common errors were: leaving out \(\sin \theta\) (1st term) in the expression of \(y= V(\frac{V \sin \theta}{g}) \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2} g(\frac{V \sin \theta}{g})^2\); using \(\theta= \frac{\pi}{4}\) instead of \(\theta= \frac{5\pi}{12}\) to establish the maximum.

criteria.criterion: Correctly finds the value of \(n^2\), or equivalent merit
Correctly uses \(v^2 = n^2(a^2 - x^2)\), or equivalent merit
Correctly applies binomial theorem to one binomial expression, or equivalent merit
Attempts to solve \(y = 0\), or equivalent merit
Provides an inequality in \(\sin 2\theta\), or equivalent merit
Obtains correct expression for the maximum height, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Mathematical Induction, Parabolas, and Differential Equations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove by mathematical induction that \(8^{2n+1} + 6^{2n-1}\) is divisible by \(7\), for any integer \(n \ge 1\). (3 marks)

(b) Let \(P(2p, p^2)\) be a point on the parabola \(x^2 = 4y\).

The tangent to the parabola at \(P\) meets the parabola \(x^2 = -4ay\), \(a > 0\), at \(Q\) and \(R\). Let \(M\) be the midpoint of \(QR\).

(i) Show that the \(x\) coordinates of \(R\) and \(Q\) satisfy
\[x^2 + 4apx - 4ap^2 = 0.\] (2 marks)

(ii) Show that the coordinates of \(M\) are \((-2ap, -p^2(2a+1))\). (2 marks)

(iii) Find the value of \(a\) so that the point \(M\) always lies on the parabola \(x^2 = -4y\). (2 marks)

(c) The concentration of a drug in a body is \(F(t)\), where \(t\) is the time in hours after the drug is taken.

Initially the concentration of the drug is zero. The rate of change of concentration of the drug is given by
\[F'(t) = 50e^{-0.5t} - 0.4F(t).\]

(i) By differentiating the product \(F(t)e^{0.4t}\) show that
\[\frac{d}{dt}(F(t)e^{0.4t}) = 50e^{-0.1t}.\] (2 marks)

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, show that \(F(t) = 500(e^{-0.4t} - e^{-0.5t})\). (2 marks)

(iii) The concentration of the drug increases to a maximum.

For what value of \(t\) does this maximum occur? (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a) Let \(P(n)\) be the given proposition. \(P(1)\) is true since \(8^3 + 6 = 518 = 7 \times 74\), which is divisible by \(7\). Let \(k\) be an integer for which \(P(k)\) is true, so \(8^{2k+1} + 6^{2k-1} = 7m\). Consider \(8^{2(k+1)+1} + 6^{2(k+1)-1} = 8^2 \times 8^{2k+1} + 6^2 \times 6^{2k-1} = 8^2 \times 8^{2k+1} + 8^2 \times 6^{2k-1} - 8^2 \times 6^{2k-1} + 6^2 \times 6^{2k-1} = 8^2(8^{2k+1} + 6^{2k-1}) + (6^2 - 8^2)6^{2k-1} = 64 \times 7m - 28 \times 6^{2k-1} = 7(64m - 4 \times 6^{2k-1})\). Thus \(P(k+1)\) is true, and \(P(n)\) is true for all \(n \ge 1\).
(b)(i) The tangent at \(P\) is \(y = px - p^2\). Substituting into \(x^2 = -4ay\), we get \(-\frac{x^2}{4a} = px - p^2\), so \(x^2 + 4apx - 4ap^2 = 0\).
(b)(ii) The sum of the roots is \(-4ap\), so the \(x\)-coordinate of \(M\) is \(-2ap\). Substituting into the tangent, \(y = p(-2ap) - p^2 = -p^2(2a+1)\). Thus \(M = (-2ap, -p^2(2a+1))\).

betterResponses: (a) In better responses, students who used a rearrangement of their assumption also drew attention to this rearrangement (eg ‘using the assumption’).

(b)(i) In better responses, students used the most straightforward method, which was to substitute the equation of the tangent, which was easily found using the Reference Sheet, and eliminate the \(y\) variable by substituting into the equation of the parabola.
(b)(ii) In better responses, students used the sum of the roots of the equation given in part (i) for the \(x\)-coordinate then substituted into the tangent to find the \(y\)-coordinate.

(c)(i) In better responses, students took note of the instruction ‘by differentiating the product’, correctly went on to substitute the given expression for \(F'(t)\), and recognised the simple cancellation that was then needed.
improvementAreas: (a) Common errors were: poor use of algebraic techniques including factorisation; simple arithmetical errors; not carrying over the letter used to indicate the multiple of 7. A significant number of students were unable to execute the proof because of difficulty handling/manipulating the powers of 8 and 6.
(b)(i) Many students spent time and effort deriving the equation of a tangent to a parabola. A common error was carelessness with signs.
(b)(ii) Much time was wasted attempting to find the \(y\)-coordinates of \(P\) and \(Q\), and then applying the mid-point formula once more.

(b)(iii) Many students did not realise that, as the equation of the locus had been given to them, all that was required was to substitute the given coordinates into the given equation. Of those students who did manage to arrive at the correct quadratic \(a^2 = 2a+ 1\), a number could not correctly solve this equation. A significant number did not go on to impose the condition that \(a > 0\).
(c)(i) A common error was not using the product rule, instead making a statement such as \(\frac{d}{dt}[F(t)e^{0.4t}] = 0.4F(t)e^{0.4t}\).
(c)(ii) Many responses ignored the integration of the LHS.
(c)(iii) The most common errors were related to division of terms with base \(e\), converting exponentials to logarithms and using basic log laws.

criteria.criterion: Correctly shows \(P(n) \implies P(n+1)\), or equivalent merit
Establishes result for \(n=1\), or equivalent merit
Obtains equation of tangent at \(P\), or equivalent merit
Eliminates the parameter, or equivalent merit
Correctly applies the product rule, or equivalent merit
Correctly integrates the expression given in part (i), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Roots of unity polygon
questions.promptLatex:
The complex number \(z\) is chosen so that \(1\), \(z\), ..., \(z^7\) form the vertices of the regular polygon shown.

[Diagram of a regular octagon in the complex plane with vertices at \(1, z, z^2, z^3, z^4, z^5, z^6, z^7\)]

Which polynomial equation has all of these complex numbers as roots?

B. \(x^7 + 1 = 0\)

questions.title: Locus of a point defined by distances
questions.promptLatex:
Suppose \(\ell\) is a line and \(S\) is a point NOT on \(\ell\).

The point \(P\) moves so that the distance from \(P\) to \(S\) is half the perpendicular distance from \(P\) to \(\ell\).

Which conic best describes the locus of \(P\)?

A. A circle

Which complex number lies in the region \(2 < |z - 1| < 3\)?

B. \(1 + 3i\)

questions.title: Identifying a transformed function graph
questions.promptLatex:
The graph of the function \(y = f(x)\) is shown.

A second graph is obtained from the function \(y = f(x)\).

Which equation best represents the second graph?

questions.title: Sum of cubes of polynomial roots
questions.promptLatex:
The polynomial \(p(x) = x^3 - 2x + 2\) has roots \(\alpha\), \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\).

What is the value of \(\alpha^3 + \beta^3 + \gamma^3\)?

What is the value of \(a\)?

questions.title: Integral of even and odd functions
questions.promptLatex:
It is given that \(f(x)\) is a non-zero even function and \(g(x)\) is a non-zero odd function.

Which expression is equal to \(\int_{-a}^{a} f(x) + g(x) dx\)?

A. \(2 \int_{0}^{a} f(x) dx\)

B. \(2 \int_{0}^{a} g(x) dx\)

C. \(\int_{-a}^{a} g(x) dx\)

D. \(2 \int_{0}^{a} f(x) + g(x) dx\)

questions.title: Properties of odd functions
questions.promptLatex:
Suppose that \(f(x)\) is a non-zero odd function.

Which of the functions below is also odd?

A. \(f(x^2)\cos x\)

B. \(f(f(x))\)

C. \(f(x^3)\sin x\)

D. \(f(x^2) - f(x)\)

questions.title: Particle motion on a circle
questions.promptLatex:
A particle is travelling on the circle with equation \(x^2 + y^2 = 16\).

It is given that \(\frac{dx}{dt} = y\).

Which statement about the motion of the particle is true?

A. \(\frac{dy}{dt} = x\) and the particle travels clockwise

B. \(\frac{dy}{dt} = x\) and the particle travels anticlockwise

C. \(\frac{dy}{dt} = -x\) and the particle travels clockwise

D. \(\frac{dy}{dt} = -x\) and the particle travels anticlockwise

questions.title: Convexity and Jensen's Inequality
questions.promptLatex:
Suppose \(f(x)\) is a differentiable function such that
\[ \frac{f(a) + f(b)}{2} \ge f\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right), \text{ for all } a \text{ and } b. \]
Which statement is always true?

A. \( \int_{0}^{1} f(x) dx \ge \frac{f(0) + f(1)}{2} \)

B. \( \int_{0}^{1} f(x) dx \le \frac{f(0) + f(1)}{2} \)

C. \( f'\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \ge 0 \)

D. \( f'\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \le 0 \)

questions.title: Complex numbers, hyperbolas, and integration
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(z = 1 - \sqrt{3}i\) and \(w = 1 + i\).

(i) Find the exact value of the argument of \(z\). (1 mark)

(ii) Find the exact value of the argument of \(\frac{z}{w}\). (2 marks)

(b) An asymptote to the hyperbola \(\frac{x^2}{12} - \frac{y^2}{4} = 1\) makes an angle \(\alpha\) with the positive \(x\)-axis, as shown.

Find the value of \(\alpha\). (2 marks)

(c) Sketch the region in the Argand diagram where
\[-\frac{\pi}{4} \le \arg z \le 0 \text{ and } |z - 1 + i| \le 1.\] (2 marks)

(d) Using the substitution \(t = \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\), or otherwise, evaluate
\[\int_{0}^{\frac{2\pi}{3}} \frac{1}{1 + \cos \theta} d\theta.\] (3 marks)

(e) The region bounded by the lines \(y = 3 - x\), \(y = 2x\) and the \(x\)-axis is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.

Use the method of cylindrical shells to find an integral whose value is the volume of the solid of revolution formed. Do NOT evaluate the integral. (2 marks)

(f) Using the substitution \(x = \sin^2 \theta\), or otherwise, evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{x}{1 - x}} dx\). (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(z = 1 - i\sqrt{3}\) (a)(ii) \(-\frac{7\pi}{12}\) (b) \(\alpha = \frac{\pi}{6}\) (c) Region defined by \(-\frac{\pi}{4} \le \arg z \le 0\) and \(|z - (1 - i)| \le 1\) (d) \(\sqrt{3}\) (e) \(V = 2\pi \int_{0}^{2} y \left( 3 - \frac{3}{2}y \right) dy\) (f) \(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{2}\)
workingLatex: (c) Sketch showing the intersection of the sector defined by \(-\frac{\pi}{4} \le \arg z \le 0\) and the disk \(|z - (1 - i)| \le 1\).

(f) \(Let x = \sin^2 \theta , dx = 2\sin \theta \cos \theta d\theta . \int_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{x}{1-x}} dx = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta} \cdot 2\sin \theta \cos \theta d\theta = 2 \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sin^2 \theta d\theta = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} (1 - \cos 2\theta) d\theta = [\theta - \frac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} = \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{2}"\)

betterResponses: (a) (ii) Students knew to subtract arg(w) from arg(z).
(b) Students found the equation of the asymptote of the hyperbola, then correctly found the inverse tangent of their gradient.
(c) Students drew a sketch with a ruler and compass, and ensured their sketch covered one-third of a page so as to more clearly show the details.
(d) Students used the suggested substitution, found the value of dθ and the new limits, and then found the correct primitive function and solution.
(e) Students found the height of shells in terms of y then rotated their shells around the x-axis using the radius values of 0 and 2 as required.
(f) Students used the suggested substitution and found the value of dx and the new limits, or used double angles and methods for integrating sin^2 θ.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) Giving a positive value; finding the modulus instead of the argument.
(a) (ii) Attempting to ‘realise’ the division of the two complex numbers but then failing to find the argument of the answer.
(d) Using a different substitution or another method (integration by parts), making it difficult to find a reasonable primitive function or complete the solution.
(e) Students that rotated around the y-axis, or attempted methods not involving cylindrical shells found difficulty in obtaining a working integral.

criteria.criterion: Finds argument of \(w\), or equivalent merit
Finds the slope of the asymptote, or equivalent merit
Sketches one correct region, or equivalent merit
Obtains correct primitive, or equivalent merit
Correctly deals with \(d\theta\), or equivalent merit
