questions.title: Functions, Complex Numbers, Integration, and Polynomials
questions.subtopic: Integration, curve sketching, and complex roots
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Consider the function \(f(x) = \frac{e^x - 1}{e^x + 1}.\)

(i) Show that \(f(x)\) is increasing for all \(x\). (1 mark)

(ii) Show that \(f(x)\) is an odd function. (1 mark)

(iii) Describe the behaviour of \(f(x)\) for large positive values of \(x\). (1 mark)

(iv) Hence sketch the graph of \(f(x) = \frac{e^x - 1}{e^x + 1}\). (1 mark)

(v) Hence, or otherwise, sketch the graph of \(y = \frac{1}{f(x)}\). (1 mark)

(b) Solve the quadratic equation \(z^2 + (2 + 3i)z + (1 + 3i) = 0\), giving your answers in the form \(a + bi\), where \(a\) and \(b\) are real numbers. (3 marks)

(c) Find \(\int x \tan^{-1} x \, dx\). (3 marks)

(d) Let \(P(x)\) be a polynomial.

(i) Given that \((x - \alpha)^2\) is a factor of \(P(x)\), show that \(P(\alpha) = P'(\alpha) = 0\). (2 marks)

(ii) Given that the polynomial \(P(x) = x^4 - 3x^3 + x^2 + 4\) has a factor \((x - \alpha)^2\), find the value of \(\alpha\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \(f'(x) = \frac{2e^x}{(e^x+1)^2} > 0\), so increasing. (a)(ii) \(f(-x) = -f(x)\), odd. (a)(iii) \(f(x) \to 1\) as \(x \to \infty\). (a)(iv) Sketch with \((0,0)\) and horizontal asymptotes \(y=1, y=-1\). (a)(v) Sketch with vertical asymptote \(x=0\) and horizontal asymptotes \(y=1, y=-1\). (b) \(z = -1, z = -1-3i\). (c) \(\frac{1}{2}(x^2 \tan^{-1} x - x + \tan^{-1} x) + c\). (d)(i) Proof shown. (d)(ii) \(\alpha = 2\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) Students successfully calculated and simplified f'(x) = 2e^x / (e^x + 1)^2 from which they recognised and stated that f'(x) > 0 for all x and so f(x) is increasing; a small number of better solutions avoided the derivative and used algebraic manipulation to show f(x) is increasing.
(a) (iii) Some based this on a correct limit argument; others came to the same conclusion based on numerical evidence for large x.
(b) A direct factorisation could be: z^2 + (2 + 3i)z + (1 + 3i) = (z + 1)(z + 1 + 3i), so the roots are -1 and -1 - 3i.
(c) This question was very well done with many students producing the standard solution.
(d) (i) A large number of students produced the standard solution.
(d) (ii) Many students produced the standard solution.
improvementAreas: (a) (ii) Some students had difficulty proving definition of an odd function f(-x) = -f(x) for all x with a clear intermediate step.
(a) (iv) Sketching only for x >= 0 (ignoring f(x) is defined for all x and is an odd function); making a sketch including x < 0 which did not satisfy the oddness property.
(a) (v) Converting the limit behaviour of f(x) to the corresponding behaviour for 1/f(x) in the graph; sketching the graph for the inverse function f^-1(x) instead of that for 1/f(x).
(b) There were many errors in complex number arithmetic in the quadratic formula or the equivalent direct ‘completing-the-square method’; some students used inefficient or overly complex methods.
(c) Use of an incorrect primitive for tan^-1(x); incorrect substitution into a correct integration by parts formula or an incorrect integration by parts formula; confusing tan^-1(x) with [tan(x)]^-1.

(d) (i) A small number of students were familiar with the result but merely restated it without proof; a number of students started with P(x) = (x-α)^2 Q(x) + R(x) but needed R(x) = 0 to recover the standard solution.
(d) (ii) A number of students derived a new polynomial that a double root α of P(x) must satisfy but often failed to then show that 2 is in fact a double root of P(x).

criteria.criterion: Correctly evaluates the discriminant, or equivalent merit
Uses quadratic formula, or equivalent merit
Correctly uses parts, or equivalent merit
Attempts to use parts, or equivalent merit
Recognises that \(P(x) = (x-\alpha)^2 q(x)\), or equivalent merit
Attempt to solve \(P'(x) = 0\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Inequalities, polynomials, projectile motion, volume, complex numbers
questions.promptLatex:
(15 marks) Use a SEPARATE writing booklet.

(a) Show that \( \dfrac{r+s}{2} \ge \sqrt{rs} \) for \( r \ge 0 \) and \( s \ge 0 \). (1 mark)

(b) Leta, \(b\) and \(c\) be real numbers. Suppose that \( P(x) = x^4 + ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + 1 \) has roots \( \alpha,\ \dfrac{1}{\alpha},\ \beta,\ \dfrac{1}{\beta} \), where \( \alpha > 0 \) and \( \beta > 0 \).

(i) Prove that \( a = c \). (2 marks)

(ii) Using the inequality in part (a), show that \( b \ge 6 \). (2 marks)

(c) A particle is projected upwards from ground level with initial velocity \( \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{k}} \) m s\(^{-1}\), where \( g \) is the acceleration due to gravity and \( k \) is a positive constant. The particle moves through the air with speed \( v \) m s\(^{-1}\) and experiences a resistive force.

The acceleration of the particle is given by \( \ddot{x} = -g - kv^2 \) m s\(^{-2}\). Do NOT prove this.

The particle reaches a maximum height, \( H \), before returning to the ground.

Using \( \ddot{x} = v\dfrac{dv}{dx} \), or otherwise, show that \( H = \dfrac{1}{2k}\log_e\!\left(\dfrac{5}{4}\right) \) metres. (4 marks)

(d) The trapezium whose vertices are \( A(0, -1) \), \( B(0, 1) \), \( C(4, 3) \) and \( D(4, -3) \) forms the base of a solid.

Each cross-section perpendicular to the \( x \)-axis is an isosceles triangle. The height of the isosceles triangle \( ABE \) with base \( AB \), is 1. The height of the isosceles triangle \( DCF \) with base \( DC \), is 2. The cross-section through the point \( (x, 0) \) is the isosceles triangle \( PQR \), where \( R \) lies on the line \( EF \), as shown in the diagram.

Find the volume of the solid. (4 marks)

(e) The points\(A\), \(B\), \(C\) and \(D\) on the Argand diagram represent the complex numbers \( a \), \( b \), \( c \) and \( d \) respectively. The points form a square as shown on the diagram.

By using vectors, or otherwise, show that \( c = (1 + i)d - ia \). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a) \(\frac{r+s}{2} \ge \sqrt{rs} (b)(i) a=c (b)(ii) b \ge 6 (c) H = \frac{1}{2k} \log_e \left( \frac{5}{4} \right) (d) \frac{38}{3} \text{ units}^3 (e) c = (1+i)d - ia\)

betterResponses: (a) This question was well attempted and by a variety of correct methods including one by contradiction.
(b) (i) Responses demonstrated a good grasp of the relationships between the sum and products of roots and the coefficients of the polynomial.
(c) The excellent responses to this part indicated that students were well prepared in this mechanics question on resisted motion.
(d) The use of similar triangles and linear expressions were recognised as methods to answer this volume question. Diagrams assisted students’ solutions.
(e) Responses indicated that students recognised a multiplication by i was required.
improvementAreas: (a) A common error was starting from the given inequality, squaring it in an attempt to ‘show that’, proving nothing.
(b) (i) Neglecting the minus signs; confusing the coefficients of the general polynomial with the polynomial P(x).
(b) (ii) A common error was not seeing the connection between part (a) and this part.
(d) The inability to correctly manipulate the algebra; integration and expansion of expressions with fractions.
(e) Using the answer and trying to work backwards; incorrect labelling of vectors; not showing all lines of working.

questions.title: Calculus, Geometry, and Mechanics Problems
questions.promptLatex:
(a) It is given that
\[ x^4 + 4 = (x^2 + 2x + 2)(x^2 - 2x + 2). \]
(i) Find \(A\) and \(B\) so that
\[ \frac{16}{x^4 + 4} = \frac{A + 2x}{x^2 + 2x + 2} + \frac{B - 2x}{x^2 - 2x + 2}. \] (1 mark)
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, show that for any real number \(m\),
\[ \int_{0}^{m} \frac{16}{x^4 + 4} dx = \ln\left( \frac{m^2 + 2m + 2}{m^2 - 2m + 2} \right) + 2\tan^{-1}(m + 1) + 2\tan^{-1}(m - 1). \] (2 marks)
(iii) Find the limiting value as \(m \to \infty\) of
\[ \int_{0}^{m} \frac{16}{x^4 + 4} dx. \] (1 mark)

(b) Two circles, \(\mathscr{C}_1\) and \(\mathscr{C}_2\), intersect at the points \(A\) and \(B\). Point \(C\) is chosen on the arc \(AB\) of \(\mathscr{C}_2\) as shown in the diagram.
The line segment \(AC\) produced meets \(\mathscr{C}_1\) at \(D\).
The line segment \(BC\) produced meets \(\mathscr{C}_1\) at \(E\).
The line segment \(EA\) produced meets \(\mathscr{C}_2\) at \(F\).
The line segment \(FC\) produced meets the line segment \(ED\) at \(G\).
Copy or trace the diagram into your writing booklet.
(i) State why \(\angle EAD = \angle EBD\). (1 mark)
(ii) Show that \(\angle EDA = \angle AFC\). (1 mark)
(iii) Hence, or otherwise, show that \(B\), \(C\), \(G\) and \(D\) are concyclic points. (3 marks)

(c) A smooth double cone with semi-vertical angle \(\theta < \frac{\pi}{2}\) is rotating about its axis with constant angular velocity \(w\).
Two particles, each of mass \(m\), are sitting on the cone as it rotates, as shown in the diagram.
Particle 1 is inside the cone at vertical distance \(h\) above the apex, \(A\), and moves in a horizontal circle of radius \(r\).
Particle 2 is attached to the apex \(A\) by a light inextensible string so that it sits on the cone at vertical distance \(h\) below the apex. Particle 2 also moves in a horizontal circle of radius \(r\).
The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\).
(i) The normal reaction force on Particle 1 is \(R\).
By resolving \(R\) into vertical and horizontal components, or otherwise, show that \(w^2 = \frac{gh}{r^2}\). (2 marks)
(ii) The normal reaction force on Particle 2 is \(N\) and the tension in the string is \(T\).
By considering horizontal and vertical forces, or otherwise, show that
\[ N = mg\left( \sin\theta - \frac{h}{r}\cos\theta \right). \] (2 marks)
(iii) Show that \(\theta \ge \frac{\pi}{4}\). (2 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) A=4, B=4; (a)(ii) \ln\left(\frac{m^2+2m+2}{m^2-2m+2}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}(m+1) + 2\tan^{-1}(m-1); (a)(iii) 2\pi; (b)(i) \angle EAD = \angle EBD; (b)(ii) \angle EDA = \angle EBA = \angle CFA = \angle AFC; (b)(iii) \angle EGF = \angle EBD = \beta, \text{so } BCGD \text{ is a cyclic quadrilateral}; (c)(i) w^2 = \frac{gh}{r^2}; (c)(ii) N = mg\sin\theta - mrw^2\cos\theta; (c)(iii) \theta \ge \frac{\pi}{4}\)
workingLatex: (a)(ii) \(\int_0^m \frac{16}{x^4+4} dx = \int_0^m \frac{4+2x}{x^2+2x+2} dx + \int_0^m \frac{4-2x}{x^2-2x+2} dx = \int_0^m \frac{2}{x^2+2x+2} dx + \int_0^m \frac{2x+2}{x^2+2x+2} dx + \int_0^m \frac{2}{x^2-2x+2} dx - \int_0^m \frac{2x-2}{x^2-2x+2} dx\). This evaluates to \(\ln\left(\frac{m^2+2m+2}{m^2-2m+2}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}(m+1) + 2\tan^{-1}(m-1)\).
(b)(i) Construct \(DB\). \(\angle EAD = \angle EBD\) as they stand on the same arc \(ED\) of \(C_1\).

(b)(ii) Construct \(AB\). \(\angle EDA = \angle EBA\) (angles standing on arc \(EA\) of \(C_1\)). \(\angle EBA = \angle CBA = \angle CFA\) (angles standing on arc \(AC\) in circle \(C_2\)). So \(\angle EDA = \angle EBA = \angle CFA = \angle AFC\).

(b)(iii) Let \(\angle AFC = \angle EDA = \alpha\) and \(\angle EAD = \angle EBD = \beta\). In \(\triangle EAD\), \(\angle AED = \pi - \alpha - \beta\). In \(\triangle EFG\), \(\angle EGF = \pi - (\alpha + (\pi - \alpha - \beta)) = \beta\). So \(\angle EGF = \angle EBD = \beta\). Exterior angle \(\angle EGF\) equals opposite interior angle \(\angle CBD\) and so \(BCGD\) is a cyclic quadrilateral.
(c)(ii) Horizontal: \(T\sin\theta - N\cos\theta = mrw^2\). Vertical: \(T\cos\theta + N\sin\theta = mg\). Solving for \(N\) gives \(N = mg\sin\theta - mrw^2\cos\theta\).

(c)(iii) As particle 2 moves in a circle of radius \(r\), it must have \(N \ge 0\). \(mg(\sin\theta - \frac{h}{r}\cos\theta) \ge 0 \implies \sin\theta - \cot\theta\cos\theta \ge 0 \implies \tan\theta \ge \cot\theta\). In the first quadrant, \(\theta \ge \frac{\pi}{4}\).

betterResponses: (a) (i) Most students were able to correctly find the values of A and B; students who attempted this part by expanding the result and equating coefficients were largely successful.
(a) (ii) In better responses, students used the result in (a)(i), algebra and techniques of integration to achieve the result.
(a) (iii) In better responses, students showed the limiting process and fully explained their solution.
(b) (ii) Better responses saw the link to (b)(i) and used it in their solution.
(b) (iii) Better responses saw students using the link to (b)(i) and (b)(ii) and then making use of appropriate circle geometry theorems and properties in their solution.
(c) (i) A significant number of students were able to correctly provide the required solution after correctly resolving force R into horizontal and vertical components.
(c) (ii) A significant number of students were able to provide the required solution after correctly resolving the forces on the second particle into horizontal and vertical components.
(c) (iii) Better responses provided justification throughout the solution.
improvementAreas: (a) (ii) Incorrect values in (a)(i); incorrect algebraic manipulation; simply writing down the correct primitive implied by the result supplied in the question.
(a) (iii) A number of students simply used a calculator to determine an approximate value.
(b) (i) A common error was stating an incorrect reason such as ‘angle in the alternate segment’ or ‘alternate angles’.
(b) (iii) Students are reminded to provide a labelled diagram as part of their solution.
(c) (i) Incorrect resolution of force R; incorrect algebraic manipulation.

(c) (ii) Incorrect resolution of the forces acting on the second particle; manipulation of the derived expressions; algebraic manipulation required to achieve the desired result was found very difficult.
(c) (iii) Incorrect manipulation of the expression from (c) (ii); incorrect trigonometric results being used.

questions.title: Integration, Tangents, and Conic Sections
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(I_n = \int_0^1 x^n \sqrt{1 - x^2} \, dx\), for \(n = 0, 1, 2, \dots\).

(i) Find the value of \(I_1\). (1 mark)

(ii) Using integration by parts, or otherwise, show that for \(n \ge 2\)
\[I_n = \left( \frac{n - 1}{n + 2} \right) I_{n - 2}.\] (3 marks)

(iii) Find the value of \(I_5\). (1 mark)

(b) Consider the curve \(\sqrt{x} + \sqrt{y} = \sqrt{a}\), for \(x \ge 0\) and \(y \ge 0\), where \(a\) is a positive constant.

(i) Show that the equation of the tangent to the curve at the point \(P(c, d)\) is given by \(y\sqrt{c} + x\sqrt{d} = d\sqrt{c} + c\sqrt{d}\). (2 marks)

(ii) The tangent to the curve at the point \(P\) meets the \(x\) and \(y\) axes at \(A\) and \(B\) respectively. Show that \(OA + OB = a\), where \(O\) is the origin. (3 marks)

(c) The ellipse with equation \(\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1\), where \(a > b\), has eccentricity \(e\).

The hyperbola with equation \(\frac{x^2}{c^2} - \frac{y^2}{d^2} = 1\), has eccentricity \(E\).

The value of \(c\) is chosen so that the hyperbola and the ellipse meet at \(P(x_1, y_1)\), as shown in the diagram.

(i) Show that \(\frac{x_1^2}{y_1^2} = \frac{a^2c^2}{(a^2 - c^2)} \times \frac{(b^2 + d^2)}{b^2d^2}\). (2 marks)

(ii) If the two conics have the same foci, show that their tangents at \(P\) are perpendicular. (3 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) \frac{1}{3} (a)(ii) I_n = \frac{n-1}{n+2} I_{n-2} (a)(iii) \frac{8}{105} (b)(i) y\sqrt{c} + x\sqrt{d} = d\sqrt{c} + c\sqrt{d} (b)(ii) a (c)(i) \frac{x_1^2}{y_1^2} = \frac{a^2c^2}{(a^2-c^2)} \times \frac{(b^2+d^2)}{b^2d^2} (c)(ii) -1\)
workingLatex: (b)\((i) x^{\frac{1}{2}} + y^{\frac{1}{2}} = a^{\frac{1}{2}}. \frac{1}{2}x^{-\frac{1}{2}} + \frac{1}{2}y^{-\frac{1}{2}} \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = -\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}. \text{ At } P(c,d), m = -\sqrt{\frac{d}{c}}. y-d = -\sqrt{\frac{d}{c}}(x-c) \implies y\sqrt{c} - d\sqrt{c} = -x\sqrt{d} + c\sqrt{d} \implies y\sqrt{c} + x\sqrt{d} = d\sqrt{c} + c\sqrt{d}\)

(b)\((ii) \text{At } A(y=0), x = \frac{d\sqrt{c}+c\sqrt{d}}{\sqrt{d}} = d\sqrt{\frac{c}{d}} + c. \text{At } B(x=0), y = \frac{d\sqrt{c}+c\sqrt{d}}{\sqrt{c}} = d + c\sqrt{\frac{d}{c}}. OA+OB = \frac{d\sqrt{c}+c\sqrt{d}}{\sqrt{d}} + \frac{d\sqrt{c}+c\sqrt{d}}{\sqrt{c}} = \frac{(\sqrt{c}+\sqrt{d})(d\sqrt{c}+c\sqrt{d})}{\sqrt{cd}} = (\sqrt{c}+\sqrt{d})^2 = a\)

(c)\((ii) \text{Ellipse slope } m_1 = -\frac{x_1b^2}{a^2y_1}. \text{Hyperbola slope } m_2 = \frac{x_1d^2}{c^2y_1}. m_1m_2 = -\frac{x_1^2b^2d^2}{a^2c^2y_1^2} = -\frac{b^2d^2}{a^2c^2} \cdot \frac{a^2c^2(b^2+d^2)}{b^2d^2(a^2-c^2)} = -\frac{b^2+d^2}{a^2-c^2}. \text{Since } a^2-b^2 = c^2+d^2, b^2+d^2 = a^2-c^2. \text{Product } = -\frac{a^2-c^2}{a^2-c^2} = -1\)

betterResponses: (a) (i) Students displayed sound knowledge of the integration process.
(a) (ii) More capable students were able to recognise the identity that enabled them to achieve the required result.
(a) (iii) More capable students were able to use the identity from part (ii) and their result from part (i) to obtain an appropriate result.
(b) (i) Almost all students displayed sound knowledge of implicit differentiation.
(b) (ii) More capable students used the fact that P(c, d) was on the curve to then show that OA + OB = a.
(c) (i) The most successful students were able to see the equality of the two expressions.
(c) (ii) More capable students were then able to use the fact that the foci were equal to show that the tangents at P were perpendicular.
improvementAreas: (a) (i) Less successful responses used incorrect limits of integration after using the integration by substitution approach.
(b) (ii) A considerable number of students, after finding the required intercepts on the axes, proceeded to find the length of AB rather than OA + OB.
(c) (i) The second approach was often characterised by a lack of attention to detail that resulted in incorrect signs.
(c) (ii) A number of students incorrectly assumed that because the foci were equal then the eccentricities were equal.

questions.title: Complex numbers, hyperbolas, and combinatorics
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(\alpha = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta\), where \(0 < \theta < 2\pi\).

(i) Show that \(\alpha^k + \alpha^{-k} = 2 \cos k\theta\), for any integer \(k\). (1 mark)

Let \(C = \alpha^{-n} + \dots + \alpha^{-1} + 1 + \alpha + \dots + \alpha^n\), where \(n\) is a positive integer.

(ii) By summing the series, prove that \(C = \frac{\alpha^n + \alpha^{-n} - (\alpha^{n+1} + \alpha^{-(n+1)})}{(1 - \alpha)(1 - \bar{\alpha})}\). (3 marks)

(iii) Deduce, from parts (i) and (ii), that
\[1 + 2(\cos \theta + \cos 2\theta + \dots + \cos n\theta) = \frac{\cos n\theta - \cos(n+1)\theta}{1 - \cos \theta}\]. (2 marks)

(iv) Show that \(\cos \frac{\pi}{n} + \cos \frac{2\pi}{n} + \dots + \cos \frac{n\pi}{n}\) is independent of \(n\). (1 mark)

(b) The hyperbola with equation
\[\frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1\]
has eccentricity 2.

The distance from one of the foci to one of the vertices is 1.

What are the possible values of \(a\)? (2 marks)

(c) A 2 by \(n\) grid is made up of two rows of \(n\) square tiles, as shown.

The tiles of the 2 by \(n\) grid are to be painted so that tiles sharing an edge are painted using different colours. There are \(x\) different colours available, where \(x \ge 2\).

It is NOT necessary to use all the colours.

Consider the case of the 2 by 2 grid with tiles labelled \(A, B, C\) and \(D\), as shown.

There are \(x(x - 1)\) ways to choose colours for the first column containing tiles \(A\) and \(B\). Do NOT prove this.

(i) Assume the colours for tiles \(A\) and \(B\) have been chosen. There are two cases to consider when choosing colours for the second column. Either tile \(C\) is the same colour as tile \(B\), or tile \(C\) is a different colour from tile \(B\).

By considering these two cases, show that the number of ways of choosing colours for the second column is
\[x^2 - 3x + 3\]. (2 marks)

(ii) Prove by mathematical induction that the number of ways in which the 2 by \(n\) grid can be painted is \(x(x - 1)(x^2 - 3x + 3)^{n-1}\), for \(n \ge 1\). (2 marks)

(iii) In how many ways can a 2 by 5 grid be painted if 3 colours are available and each colour must now be used at least once? (2 marks)

betterResponses: (a) (i) The majority of responses correctly applied De Moivre’s theorem.
(a) (ii) The most successful responses involved finding the sum of all (2n + 1) terms in the series, C, followed by multiplying by (1-α)/(1-α).
(a) (iii) This part was well done by most students.
(a) (iv) Most students correctly substituted θ = π/n into the identity in part (iii).
(a) (i) The majority of responses correctly applied De Moivre’s theorem to determine \(a^k\) and \(a^{-k}\) before evaluating the sum of \(a^k + a^{-k}\).

(a) (ii) The most successful responses to this part involved finding the sum of all \((2n+1)\) terms in the series, C, followed by multiplying by \(\frac{1-\alpha}{1-\alpha}\). Many students first split the series into two smaller series before combining each sum.
(a) (iv) Most students correctly substituted \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{n}\) into the identity in part (iii).

(c) (i) The more successful responses considered the two cases provided in the question, while some responses presented sub-cases by considering whether tile \(D\) is a different colour or not from tile \(A\). All successful responses avoided using fallacious reasoning to arrive at the given answer.
(c) (ii) The successful responses included clear reasons when presenting the base case (for \(n=1\)) and the inductive step.

(c) (iii) Many students substituted \(x=3\) and \(n=5\) into the given formula in part (ii) to find the number of ways of painting the grid if all colours need not be used. The correct responses subtracted the number of ways of painting with just two different colours.
improvementAreas: (a) (ii) Incorrectly counting the number of terms in the series; not simplifying terms; not providing complete solutions; making errors in algebraic manipulation.
(a) (iii) Not clearly showing all necessary steps in the solutions; having difficulty with simplifying the denominator term.

(a) (ii) Common errors were: incorrectly counting the number of terms in the series, not simplifying terms such as \(\alpha\alpha^n\), not providing complete solutions, and making errors in algebraic manipulation.
(a) (iii) Common errors included: not clearly showing all necessary steps in the solutions and having difficulty with simplifying the denominator term \((1-\alpha)(1-\alpha)\).
(a) (iv) A common error was not applying basic trigonometric identities correctly to show that \(\cos \frac{\pi}{n} + \cos \frac{2\pi}{n} + \dots + \cos \frac{n\pi}{n}\) is independent of \(n\).
(b) Common errors included finding just one possible value for \(a\) or finding non-numeric values for \(a\) in terms of \(b\).

(c) (ii) Common errors were: treating the base case with \(n=2\) rather than \(n=1\) and not realizing that the number of ways of choosing colours for any column after the second column is the same as the number of ways of choosing colours for the second column, and applying this to prove the inductive step.
(c) (iii) A common error was subtracting the number of ways of painting with exactly two given colours rather than the number of ways of painting with any two colours chosen from three colours.

questions.title: Future value of compound interest investment
questions.promptLatex:
A single amount of \$10\,000 is invested for 4 years, earning interest at the rate of 3\% per annum, compounded monthly.

questions.title: Financial, Statistical, and Geometric Modelling
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Electricity costs \(\$0.27\) per kWh.

How much does \(20\) kWh cost? (1 mark)

(b) Toby's mobile phone plan costs \(\$20\) per month, plus the cost of all calls. Calls are charged at the rate of \(70\) cents per \(30\) seconds, or part thereof. There is also a call connection fee of \(50\)c per call.

Here is a record of all his calls in July.

| Date | Call duration |
| :--- | :--- |
| 5 July | 20 seconds |
| 12 July | 40 seconds |
| 23 July | 2 minutes 15 seconds |

How much is Toby's mobile phone bill for July? (2 marks)

(c) A farmer needed to estimate the number of goats on his property. He tagged \(80\) of his goats. Later, he collected a random sample of \(45\) goats and found that \(16\) of these had tags.

Estimate the number of goats the farmer has on his property. (2 marks)

(d) A sewer pipe needs to be placed into the ground so that it has a \(2^\circ\) angle of depression. The length of the pipe is \(15\ 000\) mm.

How much deeper should one end of the pipe be compared to the other end? Answer to the nearest mm. (2 marks)

(e) Sam purchased \(500\) company shares at \(\$3.20\) per share. Brokerage fees were \(1.5\%\) of the purchase price.

Sam is paid a dividend of \(26\) cents per share, then immediately sells the shares for \(\$4.80\) each.

If he pays no further brokerage fees, what is Sam's total profit? (3 marks)

(f) The area chart shows the number of goals scored by three hockey teams, \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\), in the first \(4\) rounds.

(i) How many goals were scored by team \(C\) in round \(1\)? (1 mark)

(ii) In which round did all three teams score the same number of goals? (1 mark)

(g) Rachel bought a motorcycle advertised for \(\$7990\). She paid a \(\$500\) deposit and took out a flat-rate loan to repay the balance. Simple interest was charged at a rate of \(7\%\) per annum on the amount borrowed. She repaid the loan over \(2\) years, making equal weekly repayments.

Calculate the weekly repayment. (3 marks)
workingLatex: (e) \(\text{Share cost} = 500 \times 3.20 = \$1600, \text{Brokerage} = 1.5\% \times 1600 = \$24, \text{Total cost} = \$1624, \text{Share selling price} = 500 \times 4.80 = \$2400, \text{Dividend} = 0.26 \times 500 = \$130, \text{Total proceeds} = 2400 + 130 = \$2530, \text{Total profit} = 2530 - 1624 = \$906\)

betterResponses: (a) calculate the costs involved using electricity
(d) use right angled trigonometry to calculate a side
(e) calculate share cost, brokerage, selling price and dividend
improvementAreas: (b) understand the term 'part thereof’ and ‘for each call’
(c) capture/recapture using a proportion statement rather than a memorised formula
(f) learn to read and interpret an area chart

questions.title: Statistics, Data Storage, Annuities, Navigation, and BAC
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Jamal surveyed eight households in his street. He asked them how many kilolitres (kL) of water they used in the last year. Here are the results.

\(220, 105, 101, 450, 37, 338, 151, 205\)

(i) Calculate the mean of this set of data. (1 mark)

(ii) What is the standard deviation of this set of data, correct to one decimal place? (1 mark)

(b) How many \(20\) megabyte files can fit on a \(3\) terabyte external hard disc? (2 marks)

(c) A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) is shown.

| Period | Interest rate per period | | | | |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| | \(1\%\) | \(2\%\) | \(3\%\) | \(4\%\) | \(5\%\) |
| \(3\) | \(3.0301\) | \(3.0604\) | \(3.0909\) | \(3.1216\) | \(3.1525\) |
| \(4\) | \(4.0604\) | \(4.1216\) | \(4.1836\) | \(4.2465\) | \(4.3101\) |
| \(5\) | \(5.1010\) | \(5.2040\) | \(5.3091\) | \(5.4163\) | \(5.5256\) |
| \(6\) | \(6.1520\) | \(6.3081\) | \(6.4684\) | \(6.6330\) | \(6.8019\) |

An annuity involves contributions of \(\$12\,000\) per annum for \(5\) years. The interest rate is \(4\%\) per annum, compounded annually.

(i) Calculate the future value of this annuity. (1 mark)

(ii) Calculate the interest earned on this annuity. (1 mark)

(d) Island \(A\) and island \(B\) are both on the equator. Island \(B\) is west of island \(A\). The longitude of island \(A\) is \(5^\circ E\) and the angle at the centre of Earth (\(O\)), between \(A\) and \(B\), is \(30^\circ\).

(i) What is the longitude of island \(B\)? (1 mark)

(ii) What time is it on island \(B\) when it is \(10\) am on island \(A\)? (1 mark)

(iii) A ship leaves island \(A\) and travels west along the equator to island \(B\). It travels at a constant speed of \(40\) km/h.

How long will the ship take to arrive at island \(B\)? Give your answer in days and hours to the nearest hour. (3 marks)

(e) Rhys is drinking low alcohol beer at a party over a five-hour period. He reads on the label of the low alcohol beer bottle that it is equivalent to \(0.8\) of a standard drink.

Rhys weighs \(90\) kg.

What is the maximum number of complete bottles of the low alcohol beer he can drink to remain under a Blood Alcohol Content (BAC) of \(0.05\)? (4 marks)
questions.answerLatex: (a)\((i) Mean = 200.875 kL (a)(ii) Standard deviation = 127.4 kL (b) 157 286 files (c)(i) $64 995.60 (c)(ii) $4995.60 (d)(i) 25^\circ W (d)(ii) 8 am (d)(iii) 3 days 12 hours (e) 8 complete bottles\)
workingLatex: (b) 3 terabytes = \( 3 \times 2^{40} \) bytes; 20 megabytes = \( 20 \times 2^{20} \) bytes; Number of files = \( \frac{3 \times 2^{40}}{20 \times 2^{20}} = 157 286.4 \); \( \therefore \) 157 286 files can fit.
(d)\((ii) Time difference = \frac{30}{15} = 2 hours; \therefore Time on island B = 10 am - 2 hours = 8 am\)

(d)\((iii) Distance = \frac{30}{360} \times 2\pi \times 6400 = 3351.0... km; Time = \frac{3351.0...}{40} = 83.7... hours = \frac{83.7...}{24} days = 3.490658504 days = 3 days 12 hours (nearest hour)\)

(e) \( \text{BAC}_{\text{male}} = \frac{10N - 7.5H}{6.8M} \); \( 0.05 = \frac{10N - 7.5 \times 5}{6.8 \times 90} \); \( 6.8 \times 90 \times 0.05 = 10N - 7.5 \times 5 \); \( 10N = 7.5 \times 5 + 6.8 \times 90 \times 0.05 \); \( N = 6.81 \); Number of bottles = \( \frac{6.81}{0.8} = 8.5125 \); \( \therefore \) Rhys can drink a maximum of 8 complete bottles.

betterResponses: (a) calculate the mean of a data set
(d) (i) calculate the longitude of a place from a diagram
(d) (ii) show a conversion from degrees in longitude to hours

questions.title: Temperature, Probability, Finance, and Optimisation
questions.subtopic: Algebraic manipulation, loan balances, and probability
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Temperature can be measured in degrees Celsius (\(C\)) or degrees Fahrenheit (\(F\)). The two temperature scales are related by the equation \(F = \frac{9C}{5} + 32\).

(i) Calculate the temperature in degrees Fahrenheit when it is \(-20\) degrees Celsius. (1 mark)

(ii) Solve the following equations simultaneously, using either the substitution method or the elimination method.
\[F = \frac{9C}{5} + 32\]
\[F = C\] (2 marks)

(iii) The graphs of \(F = \frac{9C}{5} + 32\) and \(F = C\) are shown below.

What does the result from part (ii) mean in the context of the graph? (1 mark)

(b) Five people are in a team. Two of them are selected at random to attend a competition.

(i) How many different groups of two can be selected? (1 mark)

(ii) If Mary is one of the five people in the team, what is the probability that she is selected to attend the competition? (1 mark)

(c) Michelle borrows \(\$100\,000\). The interest rate charged is \(12\%\) per annum compounded monthly. The monthly payment is \(\$1029\) and the first repayment is made after one month.

What is the amount outstanding immediately after the SECOND monthly repayment is made? (3 marks)

(d) Make \(y\) the subject of the equation \(x = \sqrt{yp - 1}\). (2 marks)

(e) A movie theatre has \(200\) seats. Each ticket currently costs \(\$8\).

The theatre owners are currently selling all \(200\) tickets for each session. They decide to increase the price of tickets to see if they can increase the income earned from each movie session.

It is assumed that for each one dollar increase in ticket price, there will be \(10\) fewer tickets sold.

A graph showing the relationship between an increase in ticket price and the income is shown below.

(i) What ticket price should be charged to maximise the income from a movie session? (1 mark)

(ii) What is the number of tickets sold when the income is maximised? (1 mark)

(iii) The cost to the theatre owners of running each session is \(\$500\) plus \(\$2\) per ticket sold.

Calculate the profit earned by the theatre owners when the income earned from a session is maximised. (2 marks)
workingLatex: (a)(i) \( F = \frac{9C}{5} + 32, C = -20 \), \( F = \frac{9 \times (-20)}{5} + 32 \), Temperature = -4 degrees Fahrenheit

(c) Amount owing after first month, \( A_1 = 100\,000 \times \left( 1 + \frac{0.12}{12} \right) - 1029 = \$99\,971 \), Amount owing after second month, \( A_2 = 99\,971 \times \left( 1 + \frac{0.12}{12} \right) - 1029 = \$99\,941.71 \)
(e)(i) From the graph, the income is maximised when the increase in ticket price is \( \$6 \). \( \therefore \) the ticket price should be \( \$8 + \$6 = \$14 \).

betterResponses: (b) write down sample spaces
(e) read and interpret a graph
(e) calculate the profit earned after costs are calculated and subtracted
improvementAreas: (a) (ii) and (iii) solve simultaneous equations and explain the context of the solution
(c) be competent with \(P+I-R\) using more than one application
(d) change the subject of a formula involving the inverse of a square root

Provides balance after one month, or equivalent merit
Provides one correct step, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Integrated Statistics, Finance, and Measurement
questions.topic: Measurement, Statistical Analysis, and Financial Mathematics
questions.subtopic: Simpson's rule, Bivariate data, and Tax calculations
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A new 200-metre long dam is to be built.

The plan for the new dam shows evenly spaced cross-sectional areas.

(i) Using TWO applications of Simpson's rule, show that the volume of the dam is approximately \(44\,333\text{ m}^3\). (2 marks)

(ii) It is known that the catchment area for this dam is \(2\text{ km}^2\). (2 marks)

Calculate how much rainfall is needed, to the nearest mm, to fill the dam.

(b) Sabrina's taxable income is \(\$86\,725\) in a particular year.

The table below is used to calculate her tax payable. In addition, she pays the Medicare levy, which is \(2\%\) of her taxable income. (3 marks)

| Taxable income (\$) | Tax payable |
| :--- | :--- |
| \(\$0 - \$18\,200\) | Nil |
| \(\$18\,201 - \$37\,000\) | 19c for each \(\$1\) over \(\$18\,200\) |
| \(\$37\,001 - \$87\,000\) | \(\$3572\) plus 32.5c for each \(\$1\) over \(\$37\,000\) |
| \(\$87\,001 - \$180\,000\) | \(\$19\,822\) plus 37c for each \(\$1\) over \(\$87\,000\) |
| \(\$180\,001\) and over | \(\$54\,232\) plus 45c for each \(\$1\) over \(\$180\,000\) |

Calculate Sabrina's net income in that year.

(c) A group of Year 12 students was surveyed. The students were asked whether they live in the city or the country. They were also asked if they have ever waterskied.

The results are recorded in the table.

| | Have waterskied | Have never waterskied |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Live in the city | 150 | 2500 |
| Live in the country | 70 | 800 |

(i) A person is selected at random from the group surveyed. (2 marks)

Calculate the probability that the person lives in the city and has never waterskied.

(ii) A newspaper article claimed that Year 12 students who live in the country are more likely to have waterskied than those who live in the city. (2 marks)

Is this true, based on the survey results? Justify your answer with relevant calculations.

(d) All the students in a class of 30 did a test.

The marks, out of 10, are shown in the dot plot.

(i) Find the median test mark. (1 mark)

(ii) The mean test mark is 5.4. The standard deviation of the test marks is 4.22. (2 marks)

Using the dot plot, calculate the percentage of the marks which lie within one standard deviation of the mean.

(iii) A student states that for any data set, \(68\%\) of the scores should lie within one standard deviation of the mean. (1 mark)

With reference to the dot plot, explain why the student's statement is NOT relevant in this context.
questions.answerLatex: (a)(i) \( 44 333 \text{ m}^3 \) (ii) \( 22 \text{ mm} \) (b) \( \$65 257.87 \) (c)(i) \( 125/176 \) (ii) True (d)(i) \( 6 \) (ii) \( 43.3\% \) (iii) The dot plot does not show a normally distributed data set, so the statement is not relevant.
workingLatex: (c)(ii) \(\text{Country students who have water skied}: \frac{70}{870} \times 100 = 8.04...\%\), \(\text{City students who have water skied}: \frac{150}{2650} \times 100 = 5.66...\%\), \(\therefore \text{The claim in the newspaper article is true.}\)

(d)(ii) \(\text{Mean minus standard deviation} = 5.4 - 4.22 = 1.18\), \(\text{Mean plus standard deviation} = 5.4 + 4.22 = 9.62\), \(\text{Percentage} = \frac{2+2+2+1+6}{30} = \frac{13}{30} = 43.3\%\)
(d)(iii) The dot plot does not show a normally distributed data set, so the statement is not relevant.

betterResponses: (c) calculate probability using a two-way table and finding two relevant probabilities and comparing them
(b) calculate the Medicare levy, and taxable income from a given table
(d) find the median from a dot plot
improvementAreas: (a) verify a given solution by showing calculations on how the result was obtained
(b) convert \(km^2\) to \(m^2\)
(d) (ii) and (iii) recognise when data does not fit a normal distribution /bell curve

questions.title: Statistics, Variation, Trigonometry, and Geometry
questions.subtopic: Bivariate data, non-right-angled trigonometry, and variation
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A set of data has a lower quartile \(Q_L\) of 10 and an upper quartile \(Q_U\) of 16. What is the maximum possible range for this set of data if there are no outliers? (2 marks)

(b) The cost of a jewellery box varies directly with the cube of its height. A jewellery box with a height of \(10 \text{ cm}\) costs \(50\). Calculate the cost of a jewellery box with a height of \(12 \text{ cm}\). (2 marks)

(c) The diagram shows the location of three schools. School \(A\) is \(5 \text{ km}\) due north of school \(B\), school \(C\) is \(13 \text{ km}\) from school \(B\) and \(\angle ABC\) is \(135^\circ\).

(i) Calculate the shortest distance from school \(A\) to school \(C\), to the nearest kilometre. (2 marks)

(ii) Determine the bearing of school \(C\) from school \(A\), to the nearest degree. (3 marks)

(d) In an investigation, students used different numbers of identical small solar panels to power model cars. The cars were then tested and their speed measured in \(\text{km/h}\). The results are summarised in the table.

| | Mean | Standard deviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of solar panels \((x)\) | \(2.9\) | \(0.8\) |
| Speed \((y)\) | \(8.2\) | \(2\) |

The equation of the least-squares line of best fit, relating the speed and the number of solar panels, has been calculated to be \[y = 2.125x + 2.0375.\]

(i) What would be the speed of a car powered by \(5\) solar panels, based on this equation? (1 mark)

(ii) Calculate the correlation coefficient, \(r\), between the number of solar panels and the speed of a car. (2 marks)

(e) A solid is made up of a sphere sitting partially inside a cone. The sphere, centre \(O\), has a radius of \(4 \text{ cm}\) and sits \(2 \text{ cm}\) inside the cone. The solid has a total height of \(15 \text{ cm}\). The solid and its cross-section are shown. What is the volume of the cone, correct to the nearest \(\text{cm}^3\)? (3 marks)
workingLatex: (a) \(IQR = Q_U - Q_L = 16 - 10 = 6\). \(Lower bound = Q_L - 1.5 \times IQR = 10 - 1.5 \times 6 = 1\). \(Upper bound = Q_U + 1.5 \times IQR = 16 + 1.5 \times 6 = 25\). \(Range = 25 - 1 = 24\).
(b) Let \(C\) be the cost and \(h\) be the height. \(C = kh^3\). \(50 = k \times 10^3 \implies k = 0.05\). When \(h=12\), \(C = 0.05 \times 12^3 = 86.40\).
(d)(ii) \(gradient = r \times \frac{\text{standard deviation of } y \text{ scores}}{\text{standard deviation of } x \text{ scores}}\). \(2.125 = r \times \frac{2}{0.8} \implies r = 0.85\).

betterResponses: (a) find the IQR
(c) apply the cosine rule to find the side length of a triangle and sine rule for an angle
(d) substitute into a given formula
(e) selecting the appropriate formula from the reference sheet provided to calculating the volume of a cone and area of a circle
improvementAreas: (a) understand the difference between the range and IQR for a data set
(b) realise variation is not always linear
(c) understand a bearing as opposed to an angle

Substitutes correctly into cosine rule, or equivalent merit
Calculates an angle in the triangle, or equivalent merit
Substitutes correctly into sine rule or cosine rule, or equivalent merit
